## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The concept of Span is deceptively simple but has multiple layers. Let us break down the engineering logic:

**Step 1 — Identifying the Lower Range Value (LRV):**
The LRV is the **lowest input stimulus** at which the sensor is specified to operate with its stated accuracy. Below the LRV, the sensor output is *undefined* or *non-guaranteed*.

**Step 2 — Identifying the Upper Range Value (URV):**
The URV is the **highest input stimulus** at which the sensor is specified to operate with its stated accuracy. Above the URV, the sensor may saturate, become non-linear, or suffer permanent damage.

**Step 3 — Computing the Span:**
Span is the *width* of the operating window. It is computed as a **positive scalar** using:
$$\text{Span} = \text{URV} - \text{LRV}$$
Note: If URV < LRV (e.g., reverse-acting sensors measuring vacuum), we take the absolute difference. By KTU convention, span is always reported as a **positive magnitude**.

**Step 4 — Establishing the Transfer Function Normalization:**
Many sensor datasheets normalize the input to the span. For any input $x$ within the range, the **normalized input** is:
$$x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}}$$
This yields a dimensionless value between 0 and 1, which is critical for signal conditioning, ADC interfacing, and controller tuning (e.g., PID controllers).

**Step 5 — Connecting to Full Scale Output (FSO):**
The full scale output is the corresponding change in the output signal:
$$\text{FSO} = Y(\text{URV}) - Y(\text{LRV})$$
where $Y(\cdot)$ is the static transfer function of the sensor. For a linear sensor:
$$Y(x) = m \cdot x + c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FSO} = m \cdot \text{Span}$$
where $m$ is the sensitivity (slope) and $c$ is the intercept.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you need for solving KTU 2024 scheme problems on Span. Memorize this before the exam.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{Span} = \text{URV} - \text{LRV}$ | Fundamental definition of span | Same unit as input (e.g., °C, kPa, V) |
| 2 | $x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}}$ | Normalized input (dimensionless) | $0 \le x_{\text{norm}} \le 1$ |
| 3 | $\text{FSO} = Y(\text{URV}) - Y(\text{LRV})$ | Full Scale Output definition | Units of output (e.g., mV, mA) |
| 4 | $\text{FSO} = m \cdot \text{Span}$ | FSO for a linear sensor | $m$ = sensitivity |
| 5 | $\text{Sensitivity } (m) = \dfrac{\text{FSO}}{\text{Span}}$ | Slope of linear transfer function | Output unit per input unit |
| 6 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Span}}{2^n}$ | ADC resolution-limited input resolution | $n$ = number of ADC bits |
| 7 | $\% \text{ of Span} = \dfrac{x - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$ | Often used in process control displays | Dimensionless percentage |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters in the Real World

- **Process Industries (Refineries, Chemical Plants):** Differential Pressure (DP) transmitters are selected based on span. If the expected process pressure varies from 80 kPa to 120 kPa, a transmitter with span 50 kPa (e.g., LRV=75, URV=125) is chosen to give a working range of 10–90% of span (best linearity region).
- **Aerospace Altitude Sensors:** Altitude sensors are sized so the cruise altitude sits near the middle of the span to maximize resolution during both climb and descent.
- **Medical Instrumentation:** ICU blood pressure cuffs have spans matched to human physiological ranges (e.g., 0–250 mmHg) to ensure high resolution in the critical 80–180 mmHg band.
- **IoT and Embedded Systems:** When interfacing an analog sensor to a 12-bit ADC (0–3.3 V), the *span-to-ADC range mismatch* determines whether an op-amp front-end is needed for signal conditioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "select a suitable sensor" in a 14-mark question, the span selection is the **first** step. Always state the operating range first, *then* pick a sensor whose span envelops that range with appropriate headroom (typically 10–25% above and below the expected extremes).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Span, Range, and Normalization for a Generic Sensor

Let us consider a **generic linear sensor** with input $x$ and output $y$, where:
- The input is a physical quantity (e.g., temperature, pressure, displacement).
- The output is an electrical signal (e.g., voltage, current, resistance change).

**Given:**
- The sensor is specified to operate with stated accuracy for $x \in [\text{LRV}, \text{URV}]$.
- The transfer function is **linear** within this range:
$$y = m \cdot x + c$$

We derive the expressions for span, FSO, and normalized transfer function.

**Derivation Step 1 — Expressing the output at LRV:**

When the input is at the lower range value $x = \text{LRV}$, the output is:
$$y(\text{LRV}) = m \cdot \text{LRV} + c$$

**Derivation Step 2 — Expressing the output at URV:**

When the input is at the upper range value $x = \text{URV}$, the output is:
$$y(\text{URV}) = m \cdot \text{URV} + c$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Computing the Full Scale Output (FSO):**

Subtracting Step 1 from Step 2:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSO} &= y(\text{URV}) - y(\text{LRV}) \\
&= \big[m \cdot \text{URV} + c\big] - \big[m \cdot \text{LRV} + c\big] \\
&= m \cdot \text{URV} - m \cdot \text{LRV} \\
&= m \cdot (\text{URV} - \text{LRV}) \\
&= m \cdot \text{Span}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion of Step 3:** This proves the key identity $\text{FSO} = m \cdot \text{Span}$ rigorously.

**Derivation Step 4 — Deriving the Normalized Transfer Function:**

We define the normalized input $x_n$ and normalized output $y_n$ as:
$$x_n = \frac{x - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}}, \quad y_n = \frac{y - y(\text{LRV})}{\text{FSO}}$$

Substituting $y = m \cdot x + c$ into the second expression:
$$
\begin{aligned}
y_n &= \frac{(m \cdot x + c) - (m \cdot \text{LRV} + c)}{m \cdot \text{Span}} \\
&= \frac{m \cdot x - m \cdot \text{LRV}}{m \cdot \text{Span}} \\
&= \frac{m \cdot (x - \text{LRV})}{m \cdot \text{Span}} \\
&= \frac{x - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}} = x_n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion of Step 4:** For a perfectly linear sensor, $y_n = x_n$. This is the **ideal unity-gain normalized transfer function** used universally in control engineering and instrumentation diagrams.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A platinum RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) temperature sensor has a specified range of **–50 °C to +250 °C** and produces a resistance output of **80 Ω at –50 °C** and **200 Ω at +250 °C**. Compute the (a) Span, (b) Sensitivity, (c) Full Scale Output, (d) the resistance at 100 °C.

**Solution:**

**(a) Span:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Span} &= \text{URV} - \text{LRV} \\
&= 250\,°\text{C} - (-50\,°\text{C}) \\
&= 250 + 50 \\
&= 300\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Sensitivity (m):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{y(\text{URV}) - y(\text{LRV})}{\text{Span}} \\
&= \frac{200\,\Omega - 80\,\Omega}{300\,°\text{C}} \\
&= \frac{120\,\Omega}{300\,°\text{C}} \\
&= 0.4\,\Omega/°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Full Scale Output (FSO):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSO} &= m \cdot \text{Span} \\
&= 0.4\,\Omega/°\text{C} \times 300\,°\text{C} \\
&= 120\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Resistance at 100 °C:**

Using the transfer function $y = m \cdot x + c$, where $c = y(\text{LRV}) = 80\,\Omega$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
y(100\,°\text{C}) &= 0.4 \times 100 + 80 \\
&= 40 + 80 \\
&= 120\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer Summary:** Span = **300 °C**, Sensitivity = **0.4 Ω/°C**, FSO = **120 Ω**, Output at 100 °C = **120 Ω**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Span vs Range vs FSO vs FSD

| Term | What it Refers To | Quantities Involved | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | The *interval* of valid inputs | $[\text{LRV}, \text{URV}]$ | Sensor specification sheets |
| **Span (FSI)** | The *width* of valid input window | $\text{URV} - \text{LRV}$ | Calibration, ADC sizing |
| **Full Scale Output (FSO)** | The *width* of the output window | $Y(\text{URV}) - Y(\text{LRV})$ | Instrumentation amplifier gain setting |
| **Full Scale Deflection (FSD)** | Pointer displacement in analog meters | Same as FSO for analog gauges | Galvanometer and meter design |
| **Dynamic Range** | Ratio of max to min measurable input | $\text{URV}/x_{\min}$ | Sensor linearity, signal-to-noise |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Do NOT confuse **Span** with **Range**. In KTU 2024 scheme questions, students frequently lose 2–3 marks by writing "The range of the sensor is 200 °C" when the actual range is 0–200 °C (span = 200 °C, but range is the *pair* of values). Always specify **both** LRV and URV when stating the range.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Span & Range Visualization (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual relationship between **LRV, URV, Span, Range, and the normalized input axis** used in sensor calibration and signal conditioning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Input Stimulus x] --> B[Sensor Transducer Element]
    B --> C[Raw Electrical Signal y]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioner Amplifier and Filter]
    D --> E[ADC and Display]
    F[Lower Range Value LRV] --> G[Span = URV minus LRV]
    H[Upper Range Value URV] --> G
    G --> I[Normalized Input xn = x minus LRV divided by Span]
    I --> J[Controller or Display accepts 0 to 1 normalized value]
    K[Range is the interval LRV to URV] --> G
    L[Full Scale Output FSO = m times Span] --> D
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

Since Span is a *static specification* rather than a *physical mechanism*, the most informative diagram is the **architectural flow** showing how Span is used at each stage of an instrumentation system:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Stage 1 Sensor Sizing"]
        P1[Process Operating Range Identified] --> P2[Apply Safety Margins plus or minus 10 to 25 percent]
        P2 --> P3[Determine Required Span]
    end
    subgraph S2["Stage 2 Sensor Selection"]
        Q1[Match Span to Datasheet] --> Q2[Verify LRV and URV Compatibility]
        Q2 --> Q3[Check Output FSO and Sensitivity m]
    end
    subgraph S3["Stage 3 Signal Conditioning"]
        R1[Compute Normalized Input xn] --> R2[Design Amplifier Gain = FSO divided by ADC Reference]
        R2 --> R3[Map Output to Display or Controller]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**

1. **Stage 1 — Sensor Sizing:** The engineer first identifies the physical operating range of the process (e.g., temperature swings from 80 °C to 140 °C in a chemical reactor), applies safety margins, and computes the required span.
2. **Stage 2 — Sensor Selection:** The datasheet of candidate sensors is consulted; the LRV/URV must envelop the process range, and the FSO/sensitivity must match the downstream electronics.
3. **Stage 3 — Signal Conditioning:** The span is used to (a) compute normalized input for display, (b) calculate amplifier gain to match the ADC reference voltage, and (c) generate the final signal to the controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note:** The diagrams above are *architectural block diagrams*, not physical drawings. They show the *information flow* of how the Span parameter propagates through a measurement chain, which is the key insight KTU examiners test in design-oriented questions.

### 4.3 Span and Range on a Number Line (Conceptual Sketch)

```
Input x →
                                                       
   |--------|------------------|------------------|     
   LRV      25% of Span       75% of Span        URV  
   (Min)                                        (Max)  
   |←──────────── Span = URV − LRV ──────────────→|   
   |←─── Operating Window (Recommended Use) ─────→|   
   ^                                             ^    
   Below this:                              Above this:
   Non-guaranteed                           Sensor saturates
   accuracy, may                            or damages
   drift non-linearly                        permanently
```

**Recommended Practice:** Always operate the sensor in the **middle 50% of its span** (i.e., 25%–75%) for best linearity and accuracy. The extreme 10% near LRV and URV often exhibits non-linear behavior.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Define the term "Span" of a sensor. How is it different from "Range"?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Span (Full Scale Input, FSI)** is the algebraic difference between the upper range value (URV) and the lower range value (LRV) of a sensor. Mathematically, $\text{Span} = \text{URV} - \text{LRV}$.
>
> **Range**, on the other hand, refers to the *inclusive interval* of valid input values, typically expressed as $[\text{LRV}, \text{URV}]$. For example, a thermocouple rated for 0–800 °C has a *Range* of 0–800 °C and a *Span* of 800 °C. The range is a *pair* of numbers, while the span is a *single positive scalar* representing the width of the operating window.
>
> **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the difference, 1 mark for the example.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**A pressure sensor has a range of 20 kPa to 105 kPa and produces an output voltage of 0.5 V at 20 kPa and 4.5 V at 105 kPa. Calculate the span, sensitivity, and full scale output (FSO) of the sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Given:** LRV = 20 kPa, URV = 105 kPa, $y(\text{LRV}) = 0.5$ V, $y(\text{URV}) = 4.5$ V.
>
> **Span:**
> $$\text{Span} = 105 - 20 = 85 \text{ kPa}$$
>
> **Full Scale Output (FSO):**
> $$\text{FSO} = 4.5 - 0.5 = 4.0 \text{ V}$$
>
> **Sensitivity:**
> $$m = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{Span}} = \frac{4.0 \text{ V}}{85 \text{ kPa}} \approx 0.04706 \text{ V/kPa}$$
>
> **(3 marks — 1 mark each for span, FSO, and sensitivity.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with suitable examples: (i) Span, (ii) Range, (iii) Full Scale Output, (iv) Sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear displacement sensor has a specified range of –10 mm to +50 mm. The output voltage varies from 0 V at –10 mm to 5 V at +50 mm. (i) Calculate the span, sensitivity, and full scale output. (ii) If the sensor is interfaced to a 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 5 V, determine the input resolution in mm. (iii) What is the normalized input value when the actual displacement is 20 mm? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions:**

- **(i) Span (FSI):** The algebraic difference between the URV and LRV. For example, a sensor with range –10 to +50 mm has a span of 60 mm.
- **(ii) Range:** The inclusive interval of valid inputs, e.g., –10 mm to +50 mm.
- **(iii) Full Scale Output (FSO):** The change in output corresponding to a full span change in input. For the above sensor, FSO = 5 V – 0 V = 5 V.
- **(iv) Sensitivity (m):** The slope of the transfer function, equal to FSO/Span. Here, $m = 5 \text{ V} / 60 \text{ mm} = 0.0833 \text{ V/mm}$.

**(1.75 marks each = 7 marks total)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Span, Sensitivity, FSO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Span} &= 50 - (-10) = 60 \text{ mm} \\
\text{FSO} &= 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ V} \\
m &= \frac{5 \text{ V}}{60 \text{ mm}} = 0.0833 \text{ V/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Input Resolution with 10-bit ADC:**

The ADC has $2^{10} = 1024$ quantization levels over a 0–5 V range. Each LSB corresponds to:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB size} &= \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{1024} \approx 4.883 \text{ mV} \\
\text{Input Resolution} &= \frac{\text{LSB size}}{m} = \frac{4.883 \text{ mV}}{0.0833 \text{ V/mm}} \\
&= \frac{4.883 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}{0.0833 \text{ V/mm}} \\
&\approx 0.0586 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternatively**, directly:
$$
\text{Input Resolution} = \frac{\text{Span}}{2^n} = \frac{60 \text{ mm}}{1024} \approx 0.0586 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Normalized Input at 20 mm:**

$$
x_n = \frac{x - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}} = \frac{20 - (-10)}{60} = \frac{30}{60} = 0.5
$$

So the normalized input is **0.5** (i.e., 50% of span).

**(7 marks — 2 marks for part (i), 3 marks for part (ii) with step-by-step calculation, 2 marks for part (iii).)**

---

**Question B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the static characteristics of a sensor with reference to Span, Range, Sensitivity, and Full Scale Output. Discuss why operating a sensor in the middle 50% of its span is recommended in industrial practice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermistor-based temperature sensor is specified for a range of –40 °C to +150 °C. Its resistance varies linearly from 1000 Ω at –40 °C to 200 Ω at +150 °C. (i) Find the span and FSO. (ii) Calculate the sensitivity. (iii) If this sensor is connected in a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms having 500 Ω, find the bridge output voltage at 50 °C when excited by a 5 V DC source. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Static Characteristics Discussion:**

The *static characteristics* of a sensor describe its steady-state behavior (as opposed to dynamic characteristics like response time, bandwidth, etc.). The key static characteristics are:

- **Span (FSI):** The width of the input operating window, computed as URV – LRV.
- **Range:** The inclusive interval [LRV, URV] of valid inputs.
- **Sensitivity (m):** The ratio of output change to input change — the slope of the transfer function.
- **Full Scale Output (FSO):** The total change in output as the input sweeps across the entire span.

**Why operate in the middle 50%?**

Most sensors exhibit **non-linear behavior** at the extremes of their span. Near the LRV and URV, the transfer function deviates from the ideal straight line due to physical limitations (e.g., spring saturation in mechanical sensors, magnetic saturation in electromagnetic sensors, hysteresis effects, and thermal effects). Industrial practice recommends operating between **25% and 75% of span** to:
1. Stay within the linear region where calibration is valid.
2. Provide safety margins against process overshoots.
3. Maximize accuracy and minimize measurement uncertainty.
4. Extend sensor lifetime by avoiding mechanical/thermal stress at extremes.

**(7 marks — 4 marks for definitions, 3 marks for the "middle 50%" justification with at least two valid engineering reasons.)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Span and FSO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Span} &= 150 - (-40) = 190\,°\text{C} \\
\text{FSO} &= \vert R(\text{URV}) - R(\text{LRV}) \vert = \vert 200 - 1000 \vert = 800\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Sensitivity:**

$$
m = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{Span}} = \frac{800\,\Omega}{190\,°\text{C}} \approx 4.21\,\Omega/°\text{C}
$$

Note: The sensitivity is *negative* in the physical sense (resistance decreases with temperature for an NTC thermistor) but we report the magnitude for KTU purposes.

**(iii) Bridge Output Voltage at 50 °C:**

First, find the thermistor resistance at 50 °C:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R(50\,°\text{C}) &= R(\text{LRV}) + m \cdot (50 - (-40)) \\
&= 1000 + (-4.21) \cdot 90 \\
&= 1000 - 378.9 \\
&\approx 621.1\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

For a Wheatstone bridge with arms $R_1$ (thermistor), $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 500\,\Omega$, excited by $V_s = 5$ V:

The bridge output voltage is:
$$
V_o = V_s \cdot \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)
$$

For a balanced reference ($R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 500\,\Omega$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= 5 \cdot \left(\frac{621.1}{621.1 + 500} - \frac{500}{500 + 500}\right) \\
&= 5 \cdot \left(\frac{621.1}{1121.1} - 0.5\right) \\
&= 5 \cdot (0.5540 - 0.5) \\
&= 5 \cdot 0.0540 \\
&\approx 0.270 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bridge output at 50 °C ≈ 0.270 V = 270 mV.**

**(7 marks — 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with full bridge equation and substitution.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Span Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Span with Range (–2 marks):** Writing "The range is 200 °C" instead of "The range is 0 °C to 200 °C with a span of 200 °C." Always specify both LRV and URV explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Sensitivity (–1 mark):** The sensitivity must carry units, e.g., **0.4 Ω/°C**, not just **0.4**. KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless numerical answers.
>
> 3. **Sign Errors in FSO (–1 mark):** Always compute FSO as $\vert y(\text{URV}) - y(\text{LRV}) \vert$ or in the direction of *increasing* input. A negative sign in the final FSO answer is usually wrong.
>
> 4. **Incorrect Bridge Equation (–2 marks):** Many students use the wrong Wheatstone bridge formula or forget to convert mV to V. Always double-check the unit consistency.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "middle 50%" Justification (–1 to –2 marks in design questions):** Whenever a question asks you to "select a suitable span," always justify your selection with the linear operating region (25%–75% of span) argument.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Topic: Span (Full Scale Input)**
>
> - **Definition:** Span = URV − LRV (algebraic difference, always a positive scalar).
> - **Unit:** Same as the input quantity (°C, kPa, mm, V, A, etc.).
> - **Range:** Inclusive interval [LRV, URV]; Span is its *width*.
> - **Full Scale Output (FSO):** Output change across the span; for linear sensors, FSO = m × Span.
> - **Sensitivity (m):** Slope of transfer function = FSO / Span (units: output per input).
> - **Normalization:** $x_n = (x - \text{LRV}) / \text{Span}$ — gives a value between 0 and 1.
> - **For an ideal linear sensor:** $y_n = x_n$ (unity-gain normalized transfer function).
> - **Industrial practice:** Operate in the **middle 50% of span** (25%–75%) for best linearity.
> - **ADC resolution:** Input Resolution = Span / $2^n$ for an $n$-bit ADC with matched reference.
> - **Span ≠ Dynamic Range:** Dynamic range is a *ratio* (URV/$x_{\min}$); Span is a *difference*.
> - **FSO ≠ FSD in some contexts:** FSO is generic; FSD specifically refers to pointer deflection in analog meters — they are numerically the same in classical instrumentation.
> - **Sensor Sizing Rule of Thumb:** Pick a sensor whose span is **1.25× to 2× the expected process range** to allow headroom for transients and overshoots.
> - **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Normalization and ADC resolution questions appear almost every semester — practice these until they become second nature.
> - **Common Pitfall:** A vacuum gauge with range –100 kPa to 0 kPa has a span of 100 kPa (not 0 kPa!).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Full-Scale Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full-Scale Output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Full-Scale Output (FSO)** is defined as the algebraic difference between the output signal measured at the **maximum (upper) calibration point** and the output signal measured at the **minimum (lower) calibration point** of a sensor, transducer, or measurement instrument. It represents the complete usable output span that the device is designed to deliver when its input is swept across the entire specified measurement range.

Mathematically, FSO is the difference between the maximum and minimum nominal output values under defined reference conditions (typically at standard temperature of **25°C** and rated supply voltage unless otherwise specified).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Memorize this phrasing):**
> "Full-Scale Output is the output span of a sensor when the input stimulus varies over its entire specified range, from the lower-range value to the upper-range value, measured under reference operating conditions."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **measuring cup** marked from 0 mL to 500 mL. The cup can hold a *total* of 500 mL, but it can also display any value *between* 0 and 500. The **Full-Scale Output is the "500 mL" spread** — the total useful display range from the lowest mark to the highest mark.

Now imagine a **thermometer** that reads from 0°C to 100°C. If the mercury level corresponds to a voltage of 0.2 V at 0°C and 4.8 V at 100°C, then:

- Minimum output = 0.2 V
- Maximum output = 4.8 V
- **Full-Scale Output = 4.8 V − 0.2 V = 4.6 V**

The 0.2 V is called the *zero offset* or *zero-point output*, and the 4.6 V is the FSO. The thermometer can produce *any* voltage within that 4.6 V window depending on temperature.

### 1.3 Distinction from Related Terms

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinctions KTU Examiners Frequently Test:**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |------|---------|
> | **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** | Output *difference* (max − min output) |
> | **Full-Scale Input (FSI)** | The full input range a sensor is designed to measure |
> | **Span** | Numerically equal to FSO in linear sensors |
> | **Dynamic Range** | Ratio of largest to smallest detectable signal (in dB) |
> | **Zero Offset** | Output when input is at its minimum (often non-zero) |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Standard Reference Temperature: **T_ref = 25°C (298.15 K)**
- Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **101.325 kPa**
- FSO is typically expressed in units matching the output (V, mA, kPa, digits, counts, etc.)
- For digital sensors, FSO is often expressed as a count: **FSO = 2^n − 1** (for an *n*-bit unipolar ADC) or **FSO = 2^n** (for bipolar)
- The **resolution** (smallest detectable step) = FSO / 2^n for an *n*-bit system
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Logic

The Full-Scale Output concept is built on three foundational pillars that every KTU student must internalize:

- **Step 1: Lower Calibration Point Identification** — The sensor's output is measured when the input stimulus is at its minimum specified value (e.g., 0°C, 0 bar, 0 lux). This value is often *not* zero due to sensor bias, electronic offsets, or intentional design (e.g., 4 mA "live zero" in industrial transmitters).

- **Step 2: Upper Calibration Point Identification** — The output is then measured when the input is at its maximum specified value (e.g., 100°C, 10 bar, 1000 lux). This defines the upper bound of the usable output range.

- **Step 3: Algebraic Difference Calculation** — FSO is computed strictly as the difference: $\text{FSO} = V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}}$. It is a *delta*, not an absolute value.

- **Step 4: Why It Matters** — FSO determines the calibration slope (sensitivity): the steeper the FSO per unit input, the higher the sensor's sensitivity and the smaller the resolvable input change per unit of output noise.

- **Step 5: Non-Idealities** — In real sensors, the FSO is influenced by temperature (causing thermal drift), aging, supply voltage variation, and loading effects. Manufacturers therefore specify FSO *with* its associated tolerance band.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers 90% of FSO numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{FSO} = V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}}$ | Fundamental FSO definition | V (or output unit) |
| 2 | $\text{Span} = \text{FSO} = \text{FSO}_{\text{output}}$ | Span equals FSO in linear systems | V |
| 3 | $\text{Sensitivity} (S) = \dfrac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}}$ | Slope of the transfer function | V/unit-input |
| 4 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FSO}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable step (n-bit) | V or unit-input |
| 5 | $\text{Accuracy (\%FS)} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{error}}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Error as percent of FSO | % |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{out,min}} + S \times (x - x_{\text{min}})$ | General linear transfer equation | V |
| 7 | $\text{Dynamic Range (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{max,detectable}}}{V_{\text{min,detectable}}}\right)$ | Ratio expressed in decibels | dB |
| 8 | $\text{Non-linearity (\%FS)} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{deviation,max}}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Worst-case deviation from ideal | % |
| 9 | $\text{FSO}_{\text{digital}} = 2^n - 1$ | FSO for unipolar n-bit ADC | counts |
| 10 | $\text{FSO}_{\text{digital}} = 2^n$ | FSO for bipolar n-bit ADC | counts |
| 11 | $\text{Hysteresis (\%FS)} = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{up}} - V_{\text{down}} \vert_{\text{max}}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Output difference for same input | % |
| 12 | $\text{Repeatability (\%FS)} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{repeat,max}}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Output variation under same input | % |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Process Control**: 4–20 mA current-loop transmitters use a live zero of 4 mA, so FSO = 20 mA − 4 mA = **16 mA**, and the 4 mA offset acts as a fault indicator (any value below 3.6 mA signals a broken loop).
- **Strain Gauge Load Cells**: A 100 kg load cell with 2 mV/V FSO at 10 V excitation delivers a 20 mV span, requiring high-gain instrumentation amplifiers.
- **Pressure Transducers**: A 0–100 psi transducer might have FSO = 5 V; the linearity, hysteresis, and thermal effects are all specified as %FSO.
- **Data Acquisition Systems**: ADC FSO determines the LSB size, and over-range inputs must not exceed FSO + allowed headroom to prevent saturation.
- **MEMS Sensors (Accelerometers, Gyroscopes)**: ±2g, ±4g, ±8g selectable FSO ranges trade dynamic range against resolution.

### 2.4 FSO in Different Transducer Classes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Commonly Tested Classes:**
> - **Resistive sensors** (RTD, thermistor, strain gauge): FSO expressed in ΔR or ΔV/V
> - **Voltage-output sensors** (thermocouple, piezoelectric, Hall sensor): FSO in V
> - **Current-output sensors** (photo-diode with TIA, pH probe): FSO in μA or mA
> - **Frequency-output sensors** (quartz crystal, resonant): FSO in Hz
> - **Digital-output sensors** (SPI/I²C smart sensors): FSO in counts or engineering units
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: FSO from a Linear Transfer Characteristic

Consider a general linear sensor with input $x$ and output $V_{\text{out}}$. The ideal transfer function passes through the two calibration endpoints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}}(x) &= V_{\text{out,min}} + S \cdot (x - x_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
\text{where the sensitivity } S &= \frac{V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}}}{x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{min}}} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta x}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means that when $x = x_{\text{max}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}}(x_{\text{max}}) &= V_{\text{out,min}} + \frac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta x} \cdot (x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
&= V_{\text{out,min}} + \Delta V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{out,max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the Full-Scale Output is recovered as the simple algebraic difference:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{FSO} = V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}} = S \cdot (x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{min}}) = S \cdot \text{FSI}\;}
$$

**Conversion logic explanation:** The first equality restates the definition. The second equality substitutes the slope definition and shows that the output change equals the slope times the input range. This compact result is what allows engineers to compute sensitivity simply by dividing the *output* FSO by the *input* FSI.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Industrial 4–20 mA Pressure Transmitter

> **Problem (KTU 2024-Style):** A pressure transmitter has an input range of 0 to 10 bar and produces an output current of 4 mA at 0 bar and 20 mA at 10 bar. Determine (a) the Full-Scale Output, (b) the sensitivity, and (c) the output at 6.5 bar.

**Solution:**

**(a) Full-Scale Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{out,min}} &= 4 \text{ mA} \quad (\text{at } p = 0 \text{ bar}) \\[4pt]
I_{\text{out,max}} &= 20 \text{ mA} \quad (\text{at } p = 10 \text{ bar}) \\[4pt]
\text{FSO} &= I_{\text{out,max}} - I_{\text{out,min}} = 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Sensitivity (Transfer Slope)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}} = \frac{16 \text{ mA}}{10 \text{ bar} - 0 \text{ bar}} = 1.6 \text{ mA/bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Output at 6.5 bar**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{out}}(6.5) &= I_{\text{out,min}} + S \cdot (p - p_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
&= 4 \text{ mA} + 1.6 \text{ mA/bar} \cdot (6.5 - 0) \text{ bar} \\[4pt]
&= 4 + 10.4 = 14.4 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Strain Gauge Load Cell FSO

> **Problem:** A 200 kg load cell has a rated excitation of 10 V and a rated output of 2 mV/V. Find (a) the full-scale output voltage, (b) the output voltage when loaded with 75 kg, and (c) the resolution if a 16-bit ADC is used.

**Solution:**

**(a) Full-Scale Output Voltage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSO} &= \text{Sensitivity (mV/V)} \times V_{\text{excitation}} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \text{ mV/V} \times 10 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV} = 0.020 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Output at 75 kg** (assuming linear behavior)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}}(75) &= V_{\text{out,min}} + \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}} \cdot (m - m_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
&= 0 \text{ V} + \frac{0.020 \text{ V}}{200 \text{ kg}} \cdot 75 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0075 \text{ V} = 7.5 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Resolution with 16-bit ADC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 16 \;\Rightarrow\; 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ counts} \\[4pt]
\text{Resolution (voltage)} &= \frac{\text{FSO}}{2^n} = \frac{0.020 \text{ V}}{65{,}536} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.05 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V} = 0.305 \text{ μV}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolution (mass)} &= \frac{\text{FSI}}{2^n} = \frac{200 \text{ kg}}{65{,}536} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 3.05 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Thermocouple-Type Sensor

> **Problem:** A Type-K thermocouple system with cold-junction compensation has an output of 0.000 V at 0°C and 4.096 V at 500°C. Find (a) FSO, (b) sensitivity, and (c) the temperature corresponding to 2.000 V output.

**Solution:**

**(a) Full-Scale Output**

$$
\text{FSO} = 4.096 \text{ V} - 0.000 \text{ V} = 4.096 \text{ V}
$$

**(b) Sensitivity**

$$
S = \frac{4.096 \text{ V}}{500 °\text{C} - 0 °\text{C}} = 8.192 \text{ mV/°C}
$$

**(c) Temperature at 2.000 V**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= V_{\text{out,min}} + S \cdot (T - T_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
2.000 &= 0.000 + 0.008192 \cdot (T - 0) \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{2.000}{0.008192} \approx 244.14 °\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Percentage-of-FSO Error Analysis

> **Problem:** A sensor with FSO = 10 V shows a maximum non-linearity deviation of 35 mV and a maximum hysteresis of 20 mV. Compute (a) non-linearity as %FSO, (b) hysteresis as %FSO, and (c) the total worst-case error.

**Solution:**

**(a) Non-linearity**

$$
\% \text{Non-linearity} = \frac{35 \text{ mV}}{10{,}000 \text{ mV}} \times 100 = 0.35\,\%\text{FS}
$$

**(b) Hysteresis**

$$
\% \text{Hysteresis} = \frac{20 \text{ mV}}{10{,}000 \text{ mV}} \times 100 = 0.20\,\%\text{FS}
$$

**(c) Total Worst-Case Error (additive for independent errors)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Total}_{\max} &= \%\text{Non-linearity} + \%\text{Hysteresis} \\[4pt]
&= 0.35 + 0.20 = 0.55\,\%\text{FS}
\end{aligned}
$$

In absolute terms, this corresponds to a maximum output uncertainty of $0.55\% \times 10\text{ V} = 0.055 \text{ V} = 55 \text{ mV}$.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Typical FSO Values by Sensor Type

| Sensor Class | Typical FSO | Output Unit | FSI (Input Range) |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| RTD (Pt100) with bridge | 0.4 – 1.0 | V | 0 to 850 °C |
| Thermocouple + amplifier | 4.096 | V | 0 to 500 °C |
| Strain-gauge load cell | 1 – 3 | mV/V | 10 – 10,000 kg |
| Industrial 4–20 mA TX | 16 | mA | Application-specific |
| Piezoelectric accelerometer | 0.5 – 10 | V/g | ±1g to ±10,000g |
| LVDT displacement | 0.1 – 10 | V/mm | ±1 to ±500 mm |
| Capacitive pressure sensor | 1 – 5 | V | 0 to 100 bar |
| Hall-effect current sensor | 0.05 – 4 | V | 1 – 1000 A |
| MEMS gyroscope | ±0.5 to ±10 | V/(°/s) | ±100 to ±2000 °/s |
| Optical encoder (line driver) | 5 | V (RS-422) | Full rotation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — FSO as the Output Span

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Input Stimulus x"] --> node2["Sensor / Transducer Element"]
    node2 --> node3["Signal Conditioning Block"]
    node3 --> node4["Output Signal Vout"]
    node4 --> node5["ADC / Display / Controller"]
    nodeA["xmin, Lower Calibration Point"] -.-> node2
    nodeB["xmax, Upper Calibration Point"] -.-> node2
    nodeC["FSO equals Vout max minus Vout min"] -.-> node4
```

**Reading the diagram:** The input stimulus is converted by the sensor element, conditioned, and presented as an output. The dashed nodes (nodeA, nodeB, nodeC) highlight the calibration endpoints that bracket the FSO window.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Linear Transfer Characteristic Showing FSO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["Start: Define FSI = xmax minus xmin"]
    stepB["Apply minimum input xmin, measure Vout min"]
    stepC["Apply maximum input xmax, measure Vout max"]
    stepD["Compute FSO = Vout max minus Vout min"]
    stepE["Compute Sensitivity S = FSO divided by FSI"]
    stepF["Use S and Vout min in transfer equation"]
    stepG["For any x: Vout = Vout min plus S times x minus xmin"]
    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG
```

**Reading the diagram:** A six-step pipeline that transforms raw calibration data into a fully usable transfer function. The final node (stepG) is the *operating equation* used for all subsequent input-to-output conversions.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Architecture of an FSO-Based Measurement Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    block1["Physical Quantity Source"] --> block2["Sensor Element"]
    block2 --> block3["Signal Conditioner Amplifier Filter"]
    block3 --> block4["ADC n-bit Resolution"]
    block4 --> block5["Digital Processor MCU DSP FPGA"]
    block5 --> block6["Display HMI Output"]
    block7["Excitation Source"] --> block2
    block8["Calibration Constants FSO FSI Voffset"] --> block3
    block8 --> block4
    block8 --> block5
```

**Reading the diagram:** The excitation source powers the sensor, the calibration constants flow into multiple stages (to set amplifier gain, ADC full-scale reference, and processor scaling), and the digital processor applies the inverse transfer function $x = (V_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{offset}})/S$ to recover the physical quantity.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Error Sources Affecting FSO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    errA["Full Scale Output FSO nominal"] --> errB["Thermal Drift"]
    errA --> errC["Aging Effects"]
    errA --> errD["Supply Voltage Variation"]
    errA --> errE["Non-linearity"]
    errA --> errF["Hysteresis"]
    errA --> errG["Repeatability Noise"]
    errB --> errH["Effective FSO under operating conditions"]
    errC --> errH
    errD --> errH
    errE --> errH
    errF --> errH
    errG --> errH
```

**Reading the diagram:** The nominal FSO is degraded in practice by six primary error sources. The resulting "effective FSO" then determines the real-world accuracy of every measurement made with the sensor.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Block — FSO Selection Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fsoA["High FSO range selected"] --> fsoB["Large dynamic range"]
    fsoA --> fsoC["Lower resolution per count"]
    fsoD["Low FSO range selected"] --> fsoE["Limited dynamic range"]
    fsoD --> fsoF["Higher resolution per count"]
    fsoB --> fsoG["Application: high amplitude signals"]
    fsoC --> fsoG
    fsoE --> fsoH["Application: small precision signals"]
    fsoF --> fsoH
```

**Reading the diagram:** Choosing FSO is a fundamental engineering trade-off. A wide FSO captures large signals but sacrifices resolution. A narrow FSO maximises resolution at the cost of headroom.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** of a sensor. How is it different from **Span** and **Full-Scale Input (FSI)**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Full-Scale Output (FSO):** Full-Scale Output is the algebraic difference between the output signal of a sensor measured at its **maximum rated input** and the output measured at its **minimum rated input** under reference conditions (typically 25°C and rated excitation). It is expressed in the units of the output (V, mA, etc.).

- **FSO = V_out,max − V_out,min**

**Distinction:**
- **Span** is numerically equal to FSO for linear sensors — both represent the output change across the input range. Some textbooks use "span" interchangeably with FSO; however, in industrial instrumentation, "span" is sometimes reserved for the *input* range (i.e., FSI).
- **Full-Scale Input (FSI)** refers to the *input* range (x_max − x_min), not the output. FSI is in the units of the measurand (°C, bar, kg, etc.).
- The ratio **FSO/FSI** defines the **sensitivity** (S) of the sensor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for FSO definition, 1 mark for Span distinction, 1 mark for FSI distinction with formula.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
A sensor has a transfer function $V_{\text{out}} = 0.2 + 0.04 \cdot x$, where *x* is in °C and *V_out* is in volts. For an input range of 0 to 100°C, calculate (i) FSO and (ii) sensitivity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At $x_{\text{min}} = 0$ °C:

$$
V_{\text{out,min}} = 0.2 + 0.04 \cdot 0 = 0.2 \text{ V}
$$

At $x_{\text{max}} = 100$ °C:

$$
V_{\text{out,max}} = 0.2 + 0.04 \cdot 100 = 4.2 \text{ V}
$$

**(i) Full-Scale Output:**

$$
\text{FSO} = 4.2 - 0.2 = 4.0 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Sensitivity:**

$$
S = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}} = \frac{4.0 \text{ V}}{100 °\text{C} - 0 °\text{C}} = 0.04 \text{ V/°C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slope of the transfer equation (0.04 V/°C) *is* the sensitivity — this shortcut saves time in the exam.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A**

**Q.A. (a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** *(7 marks)*
Explain the concept of Full-Scale Output (FSO) of a sensor in detail. Discuss how FSO, Span, and Dynamic Range are related, and derive the general linear transfer equation of a sensor in terms of FSO and FSI.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept of FSO (2 marks):**
Full-Scale Output is the *total change* in the output signal of a sensor as the input stimulus is varied across its *entire specified range* — from the lower-range value to the upper-range value — under standard reference conditions. It is computed as:

$$
\text{FSO} = V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}}
$$

For a sensor with a *live zero* (non-zero output at minimum input, e.g., 4 mA industrial transmitters), the FSO is *not* the maximum output, but the *difference* between maximum and minimum outputs. This distinction is critical for interpreting datasheets.

**Relationship between FSO, Span, and Dynamic Range (2 marks):**

- **FSO vs Span:** For a perfectly linear sensor, FSO and Span are numerically equal (both represent the output excursion across the input range). However, "span" is sometimes used in the *input* domain (i.e., FSI). KTU examiners expect students to clarify this ambiguity.
- **Dynamic Range:** Defined as the ratio of the *largest* to the *smallest* signal the sensor can resolve, expressed in decibels:

$$
\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{max,detectable}}}{V_{\text{min,detectable}}}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

Dynamic Range is *not* the same as FSO. A sensor with FSO = 10 V might have a Dynamic Range of 100 dB, meaning it can resolve signals 100,000 times smaller than its full-scale value (down to ~100 μV).

**Derivation of Linear Transfer Equation (3 marks):**

For a linear sensor, let the two calibration points be:
- $(x_1, V_1) = (x_{\text{min}}, V_{\text{out,min}})$
- $(x_2, V_2) = (x_{\text{max}}, V_{\text{out,max}})$

The slope (sensitivity) is:

$$
S = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}}}{x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{min}}} = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}}
$$

Using the point-slope form with $(x_1, V_1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{out,min}} &= S \cdot (x - x_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
V_{\text{out}}(x) &= V_{\text{out,min}} + \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}} \cdot (x - x_{\text{min}})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **general linear transfer equation** in terms of FSO and FSI. It is the master equation from which all FSO-based problems are solved.

---

**Q.A. (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** *(7 marks)*
A load cell with input range 0 to 500 kg produces an output of 0 mV at 0 kg and 30 mV at 500 kg when excited with 12 V. The output is digitised by a 12-bit ADC with input range 0 to 5 V (single-ended). Determine (i) FSO in mV and mV/V, (ii) sensitivity in mV/kg, (iii) the output voltage at 200 kg, (iv) the number of ADC counts at 200 kg, and (v) the mass resolution of the system.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given data:**
- FSI = 0 to 500 kg
- V_out at 0 kg = 0 mV
- V_out at 500 kg = 30 mV
- V_excitation = 12 V
- ADC: 12-bit, 0 to 5 V range

**(i) FSO (1 mark):**

$$
\text{FSO}_{\text{mV}} = 30 - 0 = 30 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\text{FSO}_{\text{mV/V}} = \frac{30 \text{ mV}}{12 \text{ V}} = 2.5 \text{ mV/V}
$$

**(ii) Sensitivity (1 mark):**

$$
S = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}} = \frac{30 \text{ mV}}{500 \text{ kg}} = 0.06 \text{ mV/kg} = 60 \text{ μV/kg}
$$

**(iii) Output voltage at 200 kg (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}}(200) &= V_{\text{out,min}} + S \cdot (m - m_{\text{min}}) \\[4pt]
&= 0 \text{ mV} + 0.06 \text{ mV/kg} \cdot 200 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
&= 12 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) ADC counts at 200 kg (1.5 marks):**

The 12-bit ADC has $2^{12} = 4096$ counts spanning 0 to 5 V, so the LSB size is:

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{4096} = 1.2207 \text{ mV/count}
$$

Counts at 200 kg:

$$
N_{200} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}(200)}{\text{LSB}} = \frac{12 \text{ mV}}{1.2207 \text{ mV/count}} \approx 9.83 \text{ counts}
$$

Since counts must be integer, $N_{200} = 10$ counts (rounded).

**(v) Mass resolution (2 marks):**

Voltage resolution of ADC:

$$
\Delta V = \text{LSB} = 1.2207 \text{ mV}
$$

Mass resolution (the smallest mass change that produces a 1-count change):

$$
\Delta m = \frac{\Delta V}{S} = \frac{1.2207 \text{ mV}}{0.06 \text{ mV/kg}} = 20.345 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently confuse the *ADC input range* (5 V) with the *sensor FSO* (30 mV). The ADC's 5 V full-scale wastes ~99.4% of its dynamic range! Always compute the *effective* number of bits used: $n_{\text{eff}} = \log_2(30/5000 \cdot 4096) \approx 4.6$ bits. A *proper* design would use an instrumentation amplifier with gain ~166 to scale the 30 mV FSO to nearly fill the 5 V ADC range, recovering the full 12-bit resolution. Examiners reward students who identify this design inefficiency.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B. (a)** *(7 marks)*
A pressure transducer has a transfer characteristic $V_{\text{out}} = 1.0 + 0.04 \cdot p$ (V, with *p* in kPa). (i) Find the FSO for an input range of 0 to 100 kPa. (ii) What is the sensitivity? (iii) A non-linearity of ±0.5 mV and a hysteresis of ±0.3 mV are observed. Compute the non-linearity and hysteresis as %FSO and the worst-case total error.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{\text{out}} = 1.0 + 0.04 \cdot p$, with FSI = 0 to 100 kPa.

**(i) Full-Scale Output (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out,min}} &= 1.0 + 0.04 \cdot 0 = 1.0 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{out,max}} &= 1.0 + 0.04 \cdot 100 = 5.0 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
\text{FSO} &= 5.0 - 1.0 = 4.0 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Sensitivity (1 mark):**

$$
S = \frac{4.0 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ kPa}} = 0.04 \text{ V/kPa} = 40 \text{ mV/kPa}
$$

**(iii) Error Analysis (4 marks):**

- **Non-linearity (%FSO):**

$$
\% \text{NL} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mV}}{4000 \text{ mV}} \times 100 = 0.0125\,\%\text{FS}
$$

- **Hysteresis (%FSO):**

$$
\% \text{Hyst} = \frac{0.3 \text{ mV}}{4000 \text{ mV}} \times 100 = 0.0075\,\%\text{FS}
$$

- **Worst-case total error** (additive, since non-linearity and hysteresis are independent):

$$
\% \text{Error}_{\text{total}} = 0.0125 + 0.0075 = 0.020\,\%\text{FS}
$$

In voltage units:

$$
V_{\text{error,max}} = 0.020\% \times 4000 \text{ mV} = 0.8 \text{ mV}
$$

Equivalent pressure error:

$$
\Delta p = \frac{0.8 \text{ mV}}{40 \text{ mV/kPa}} = 0.02 \text{ kPa} = 20 \text{ Pa}
$$

---

**Q.B. (b)** *(7 marks)*
With the aid of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an FSO-based measurement channel. Discuss the role of calibration constants, the ADC full-scale reference, and the digital scaling step.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The measurement channel consists of six sequential blocks:

1. **Physical Quantity Source:** The measurand (temperature, pressure, force, etc.) impinges on the sensor.
2. **Sensor Element:** Transduces the physical quantity into a primary electrical signal (resistance change, voltage, charge, frequency).
3. **Excitation Source:** Powers the sensor with a stable reference (e.g., 5 V or 10 V for strain gauges, constant current for RTDs).
4. **Signal Conditioner:** Comprises an instrumentation amplifier, filter, and offset adjustment. The amplifier gain is selected so that the *sensor's FSO nearly fills the ADC's input range* — this maximises the effective resolution.
5. **ADC (n-bit):** Converts the conditioned analog signal into digital counts. The ADC's *reference voltage* ($V_{\text{ref}}$) defines its own full-scale: $\text{FSO}_{\text{ADC}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)/2^n$.
6. **Digital Processor:** Receives raw counts and applies the inverse transfer function:

$$
x = x_{\text{min}} + \frac{N}{2^n} \cdot \text{FSI} = x_{\text{min}} + \frac{N \cdot \text{FSO}_{\text{analog}}}{2^n \cdot S}
$$

where $N$ is the digital count.

**Role of Calibration Constants (2 marks):**
The three primary calibration constants stored in non-volatile memory are:
- **V_offset** (output at minimum input) — used to subtract the zero-point bias.
- **S** (sensitivity, V per unit input) — used to convert output to input.
- **FSO** and **FSI** — used to scale the display and detect over-range conditions.

**ADC Full-Scale Reference and Digital Scaling (2 marks):**
The ADC's reference voltage is *not* the same as the sensor's FSO. The analog front-end must scale the sensor's FSO to within 80–95% of the ADC's full-scale input range to prevent saturation while maximising resolution. A gain mismatch causes either clipping (if FSO > ADC range) or wasted bits (if FSO << ADC range). The digital scaling step then maps ADC counts back to engineering units (e.g., °C, kPa) using the stored calibration constants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Module 1:**
> - **Conflating FSO with maximum output:** A *live-zero* sensor (4 mA) has maximum output = 20 mA but FSO = 16 mA. Marks are lost for using the wrong value.
> - **Forgetting units in sensitivity:** Sensitivity = FSO/FSI *with consistent units*. Writing "0.04 V/°C" is correct; writing just "0.04" loses a mark.
> - **Confusing span (output) with span (input):** In ISA convention, "span" is the *input* range; in sensor datasheets, it is often the *output* range. Always state which.
> - **Forgetting the offset in transfer equations:** Many students write $V_{\text{out}} = S \cdot x$, omitting $V_{\text{out,min}}$. This is valid *only* when the sensor is zero-based (V_out = 0 at x = 0).
> - **Sign errors in digital conversions:** When the ADC is bipolar, FSO = 2^n, not 2^n − 1. When unipolar, FSO = 2^n − 1.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FSO Definition:** FSO = V_out,max − V_out,min. It is an output *difference*, not an absolute value. Always check whether the sensor has a *live zero* (e.g., 4 mA or 1 V offset) before calculating.
- **Master Formula:** $\text{FSO} = S \cdot \text{FSI}$, where $S$ is the sensitivity. This is the single most-tested relationship.
- **Transfer Equation:** $V_{\text{out}}(x) = V_{\text{out,min}} + \dfrac{\text{FSO}}{\text{FSI}} \cdot (x - x_{\text{min}})$. Memorize this in the form that uses *V_offset*, FSO, and FSI.
- **Resolution for n-bit system:** $R = \text{FSO}/2^n$ in output units, or $\text{FSI}/2^n$ in input units.
- **Digital FSO:** Unipolar ADC → $2^n - 1$ counts; bipolar ADC → $2^n$ counts.
- **All performance specs are %FSO:** Non-linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, thermal drift, and accuracy are *all* expressed as a percentage of FSO — not absolute units.
- **Live Zero Sensors (4–20 mA):** FSO = 16 mA; the 4 mA offset doubles as a fault indicator. Industry standard since the 1960s.
- **ADC Matching Rule:** Design the analog front-end gain so that the *sensor's FSO fills 80–95% of the ADC's full-scale input range*. A poorly matched gain wastes resolution.
- **Dynamic Range vs FSO:** Dynamic Range is the ratio of largest to smallest *detectable* signal, in dB. FSO is the *maximum* output swing. A 100 dB dynamic range sensor with 10 V FSO can resolve signals as small as 100 μV.
- **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Trap:** "The maximum output of a 4–20 mA transmitter is 20 mA. Hence its FSO is 20 mA." → **WRONG.** FSO = 20 − 4 = 16 mA. This single mistake has cost students 2–3 marks per paper for decades.
- **Three-Layer Hierarchy to Memorize:** (1) Definition → (2) Sensitivity = FSO/FSI → (3) Transfer equation using FSO, FSI, V_offset.
- **Units Consistency Rule:** When computing sensitivity, FSO and FSI *must* be expressed in compatible units. A common slip is mixing mV with V — convert first.
- **Thermal Effects:** FSO is specified at 25°C; in operating environments, it drifts. Budget for thermal error by checking the datasheet's "%FSO/°C" specification.
- **Inverting the Transfer Function:** To recover the input from the output, use $x = x_{\text{min}} + \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{out,min}}}{S}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Sensors and Instrumentation**, **Accuracy** is formally defined as the degree of closeness of a measured value (output of a sensor/instrument) to the **true value** (or actual value) of the quantity being measured. It quantifies the *correctness* of a measurement system.

Mathematically, **Accuracy** is often expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{Accuracy (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert x_m - x_t \vert}{x_{FS}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

where:
- $x_m$ = measured value
- $x_t$ = true value (or accepted reference value)
- $x_{FS}$ = full-scale value of the instrument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Accuracy is the **first static characteristic** introduced in Module 1 of PCECT523. It is a *static* (steady-state) characteristic because it is evaluated when the input quantity does not vary with time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Accuracy like Archery:** Imagine you are aiming at a bullseye. If your arrows land very close to the bullseye (center), you have high **accuracy**. If your arrows land scattered all over the board but clustered together, you have high **precision** but **low accuracy**. Accuracy is about hitting the *correct* target, while precision is about hitting the *same* spot repeatedly.

**Real-world example:** Suppose a clinical thermometer reads 98.4 °F when the actual body temperature of a healthy person is 98.6 °F. The thermometer is **accurate** because its reading is *very close* to the true value, even though there is a small error.

### 1.3 The Inverse Relationship: Inaccuracy

**Inaccuracy** is the numerical complement of accuracy. It expresses the deviation from the true value as a percentage.

$$
\text{Inaccuracy (\%)} = \left( \frac{x_m - x_t}{x_t} \right) \times 100\%
$$

A high-accuracy sensor implies **low inaccuracy**. In KTU valuation, the terms *error* and *inaccuracy* are sometimes used interchangeably, but strictly speaking, *inaccuracy* is the normalized (percentage) form of the absolute error.

---

### 1.4 Accuracy in the Wider Sensor Performance Framework

Accuracy does **not** exist in isolation. It is governed by and related to several other static characteristics:

- **Error** (Static Error) → the raw deviation from the true value
- **Precision** → consistency of repeated readings
- **Repeatability** → closeness of readings under *same* conditions
- **Reproducibility** → closeness of readings under *changed* conditions
- **Resolution** → smallest detectable change
- **Sensitivity** → output per unit input change
- **Drift** → slow change in output with time
- **Linearity** → proportionality between input and output
- **Hysteresis** → dependence on past input history

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always remember the hierarchy — *Accuracy depends on minimizing **all** sources of error* (systematic, random, and gross). When asked to "discuss accuracy," examiners expect you to touch upon the **error types** that limit it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of How Accuracy is Determined

A sensor's accuracy is determined by examining the **measured value** versus the **true value** under controlled static conditions. The process is:

- **Step 1 — Apply a Known Reference Input:** A precisely calibrated standard (e.g., a dead-weight tester for pressure, a standard thermocouple for temperature) provides a known *true value* $x_t$ to the sensor under test.

- **Step 2 — Record the Sensor Output:** The output reading $x_m$ from the sensor is noted. This is repeated (typically $n$ times) to assess both accuracy and precision.

- **Step 3 — Compute the Error:** The *absolute error* is obtained:
  $$
  e = x_t - x_m
  $$

- **Step 4 — Normalize the Error:** To make accuracy independent of the magnitude of measurement, the error is normalized. KTU uses two conventions:
  - Normalized with respect to **true value** → gives *inaccuracy*
  - Normalized with respect to **full-scale (FS) value** → gives the more commonly quoted *accuracy rating* (e.g., $\pm$ 0.1 % of FS)

- **Step 5 — Express as Percentage:** Multiply by 100 to obtain the percentage form expected in exam answers.

- **Step 6 — State the Confidence Interval:** In modern instrumentation, accuracy is paired with an uncertainty statement (e.g., $\pm$ 0.05 % at 95 % confidence level, $k = 2$).

### 2.2 Types of Errors Limiting Accuracy

| Error Type | Cause | Effect on Accuracy | Can it be eliminated? |
|------------|-------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Gross Error** | Human mistakes (parallax, misreading) | Large deviations | Yes — by careful reading |
| **Systematic Error** | Instrumental defect, calibration offset, environmental bias | Consistent bias in one direction | Yes — by **calibration** |
| **Random Error** | Noise, fluctuations, environmental turbulence | Small, unpredictable scatter | Reduced (not eliminated) by averaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** Systematic errors reduce **accuracy** but do not affect **precision** (reproducibility). Random errors reduce **precision** but, by the law of large numbers, their effect on accuracy diminishes when many readings are averaged.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every equation you need for accuracy-related problems. **Do NOT use the vertical bar symbol `|` inside any row** — use `\vert` instead to preserve table formatting.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $e = x_t - x_m$ | $x_t$: true, $x_m$: measured |
| 2 | Relative Error | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{x_t - x_m}{x_t}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Percentage Inaccuracy | $\%\text{Inaccuracy} = \varepsilon \times 100$ | — |
| 4 | Percentage Accuracy (FS basis) | $\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert x_t - x_m \vert}{x_{FS}}\right) \times 100$ | $x_{FS}$: full-scale |
| 5 | Percentage Accuracy (True-value basis) | $\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(\dfrac{x_m}{x_t}\right) \times 100$ | Used loosely in textbooks |
| 6 | Mean of $n$ readings | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Average measured value |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Spread / precision indicator |
| 8 | Probable Error | $PE = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | 50 % confidence |
| 9 | Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Accuracy improves as $\sqrt{n}$ |
| 10 | Combined Uncertainty | $u_c = \sqrt{u_1^2 + u_2^2 + \cdots + u_n^2}$ | Root-sum-square method |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Process Control:** A pressure transmitter with $\pm$ 0.075 % FS accuracy is essential in oil refinery reactors to prevent over-pressure incidents.
- **Medical Devices:** Pulse oximeters must have high accuracy at SpO$_2$ levels near 90 % (hypoxemia threshold) — here even 1 % error is clinically significant.
- **Aerospace:** Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) used in aircraft navigation require accuracy budgets specified per axis, with total error budgeted in parts per million.
- **Calibration Laboratories:** National Metrology Institutes (e.g., NABL-accredited labs in India) maintain primary standards whose accuracy is traceable to SI units.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- *Why normalize with full-scale?* → Because instrument accuracy is a property of the *instrument*, not of the magnitude being measured. A voltmeter that is 0.1 % accurate of FS gives the same percentage regardless of whether it reads 5 V or 50 V.
- *Why pair with confidence level?* → Because random errors are statistical; without specifying confidence, the accuracy number is incomplete.
- *Why use root-sum-square for uncertainty?* → Because independent random errors combine in quadrature (statistical independence).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expressing Accuracy from Error

Let us derive the percentage accuracy formula starting from first principles.

**Step 1:** Define the absolute error as the difference between the true value and the measured value.

$$
e = x_t - x_m
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that a single error value is not a meaningful "rating" because it depends on the magnitude of the quantity measured. We therefore normalize.

**Step 3:** Divide the error by a reference quantity. For a *rating-style* accuracy (commonly used in industrial instrumentation), the reference is the full-scale value $x_{FS}$:

$$
\text{Normalized Error} = \frac{e}{x_{FS}} = \frac{x_t - x_m}{x_{FS}}
$$

**Step 4:** To express as a percentage, multiply by 100:

$$
\%\text{Error (of FS)} = \frac{x_t - x_m}{x_{FS}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5:** Since accuracy is the complement of error (higher accuracy = lower error), subtract from unity:

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = 100 - \%\text{Error} = 100 - \frac{x_t - x_m}{x_{FS}} \times 100
$$

**Step 6:** Factor out the 100 to obtain the canonical form taught at KTU:

$$
\boxed{\;\%\text{Accuracy} = \left( 1 - \frac{\vert x_t - x_m \vert}{x_{FS}} \right) \times 100\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The modulus (absolute value) $\vert \cdot \vert$ is included because accuracy is always reported as a *positive* magnitude — it does not matter whether the sensor reads high or low.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A temperature sensor has a full-scale range of **0 °C to 200 °C**. When measuring a calibrated reference temperature of **100 °C**, the sensor reads **99.2 °C**. Calculate: (a) the absolute error, (b) the percentage inaccuracy (true-value basis), and (c) the percentage accuracy (full-scale basis). Comment on the quality of the sensor.

#### Part (a): Absolute Error

**Given:**
- True value, $x_t = 100$ °C
- Measured value, $x_m = 99.2$ °C
- Full-scale value, $x_{FS} = 200$ °C (the span from 0 °C to 200 °C; if FS is interpreted as the maximum range, use $x_{FS} = 200$ °C)

**Apply the error formula:**

$$
e = x_t - x_m
$$

**Substitute the values:**

$$
e = 100 - 99.2
$$

**Evaluate:**

$$
\boxed{e = +0.8 \text{ °C}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The sensor *under-reads* by 0.8 °C (a negative systematic bias).

---

#### Part (b): Percentage Inaccuracy (true-value basis)

**Apply the formula:**

$$
\%\text{Inaccuracy} = \frac{\vert x_t - x_m \vert}{x_t} \times 100
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
\%\text{Inaccuracy} = \frac{\vert 100 - 99.2 \vert}{100} \times 100
$$

**Evaluate the numerator:**

$$
\%\text{Inaccuracy} = \frac{0.8}{100} \times 100
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\%\text{Inaccuracy} = 0.8\,\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The sensor deviates from the true value by only 0.8 % at the 100 °C operating point.

---

#### Part (c): Percentage Accuracy (full-scale basis)

**Apply the canonical formula:**

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert x_t - x_m \vert}{x_{FS}}\right) \times 100
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{0.8}{200}\right) \times 100
$$

**Compute the fraction:**

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = (1 - 0.004) \times 100
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\%\text{Accuracy} = 99.6\,\% \text{ of FS}}
$$

**Comment on Quality:** An accuracy of 99.6 % of full-scale is considered **excellent** for general industrial use. Most commercial industrial sensors are rated between 95 % and 99.9 % accuracy.

---

### 3.3 Advanced Worked Example: Effect of Repeated Readings

> **Problem:** Five repeated measurements of a 50.000 V reference standard yielded: 49.98, 50.01, 49.99, 50.02, 50.00 V. Find (a) mean, (b) standard deviation, (c) standard error of the mean, (d) accuracy of the *mean* reading, (e) probable error.

#### Part (a): Mean

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{5}(49.98 + 50.01 + 49.99 + 50.02 + 50.00)
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{250.00}{5} = 50.000 \text{ V}
$$

#### Part (b): Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{5-1}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(-0.02)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (-0.01)^2 + (0.02)^2 + (0.00)^2}{4}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 + 0.0004 + 0.0000}{4}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.0010}{4}} = \sqrt{0.00025}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.0158 \text{ V}}
$$

#### Part (c): Standard Error of the Mean

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.0158}{\sqrt{5}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{0.0158}{2.236}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\bar{x}} \approx 0.00707 \text{ V}}
$$

#### Part (d): Accuracy of the Mean Reading

True value $x_t = 50.000$ V, mean measured value $\bar{x} = 50.000$ V.

$$
e_{\bar{x}} = x_t - \bar{x} = 0.000 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert 0.000 \vert}{50.000}\right) \times 100 = 100\,\%
$$

The mean is *exact* for this dataset, demonstrating that **averaging eliminates random errors** but does not correct *systematic* bias.

#### Part (e): Probable Error

$$
PE = 0.6745 \times \sigma = 0.6745 \times 0.0158
$$

$$
\boxed{PE \approx 0.0107 \text{ V}}
$$

This means there is a 50 % chance that any single reading lies within $\pm$ 0.0107 V of the mean.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Basis

| Use Case | Preferred Basis | Reason |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| Industrial transducer datasheet (e.g., 4–20 mA loop) | % of Full-Scale (FS) | Independent of operating point |
| Scientific laboratory measurement | % of True Value (Reading) | More meaningful at low magnitudes |
| Worst-case design analysis | Combined (RSS) uncertainty | Captures all independent contributions |
| Compliance / regulatory | FS with stated confidence (e.g., k = 2) | Traceable to SI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sources of Inaccuracy

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional architecture* of how accuracy is degraded by various error sources in a sensor measurement chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Physical Quantity xt] --> B[Sensor Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[ADC and Display Unit]
    D --> E[Measured Reading xm]
    E --> F[Error Calculation e = xt minus xm]

    B --> B1[Instrumental Bias]
    B --> B2[Environmental Drift]
    B --> B3[Hysteresis Loss]
    C --> C1[Amplifier Offset]
    C --> C2[Thermal Noise]
    D --> D1[Quantization Error]
    D --> D2[Display Resolution Limit]

    F --> G[Absolute Error e]
    G --> H[Percentage Inaccuracy]
    H --> I[Accuracy Rating percent of FS]

    B1 --> F
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F

    I --> J{Acceptable Accuracy?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Sensor Approved for Use]
    J -- No --> L[Recalibrate or Reject Sensor]
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **vertical spine** (A → B → C → D → E → F → I) shows the *measurement chain*.
- The **side branches** (B1, B2, B3, C1, C2, D1, D2) are *error injection points* — each one degrades the final accuracy.
- The **decision block** (J) represents the *acceptance test* performed during sensor calibration.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Accuracy vs Precision Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Case A]
        A1[True Value Center] --> A2[Readings Cluster ON Target]
    end
    subgraph S2[Case B]
        B1[True Value Center] --> B2[Readings Scatter Random]
    end
    subgraph S3[Case C]
        C1[True Value Center] --> C2[Readings Cluster OFF Target]
    end
    subgraph S4[Case D]
        D1[True Value Center] --> D2[Readings Scatter Far OFF Target]
    end

    S1 --> L1[High Accuracy AND High Precision]
    S2 --> L2[Low Accuracy AND Low Precision]
    S3 --> L3[Low Accuracy AND High Precision]
    S4 --> L4[Very Low Accuracy AND Very Low Precision]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Sensor Performance Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Perf[Sensor Performance]
    Perf --> Static[Static Characteristics]
    Perf --> Dynamic[Dynamic Characteristics]

    Static --> Acc[Accuracy]
    Static --> Prec[Precision]
    Static --> Rep[Repeatability]
    Static --> Reprod[Reproducibility]
    Static --> Res[Resolution]
    Static --> Sens[Sensitivity]
    Static --> Lin[Linearity]
    Static --> Hys[Hysteresis]
    Static --> Drift[Drift]
    Static --> Span[Span and Range]

    Acc --> AccDef[Closeness to TRUE value]
    Prec --> PrecDef[Closeness to EACH OTHER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram?** KTU frequently asks students to "list the static characteristics of a sensor" and "explain how they differ." This hierarchy helps you remember that *accuracy* and *precision* are *both* static characteristics but answer **different questions** about the measurement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, PCECT523]`
> **Define Accuracy of a sensor. How is it different from Precision?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of a measured value to the **true (actual) value** of the quantity being measured. It quantifies the *correctness* of a sensor's output.

**Difference from Precision:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Definition | Closeness to **true value** | Closeness of **repeated readings to each other** |
| Affected by | Systematic errors, bias | Random errors, noise |
| Improved by | **Calibration** | **Averaging** repeated measurements |
| Example | A voltmeter reading 10.02 V when true value is 10.00 V is accurate but not precise if repeated readings scatter | A voltmeter reading 10.05, 10.06, 10.04 V consistently is precise but **inaccurate** (biased) |

A measurement system can be accurate without being precise, precise without being accurate, both, or neither.

> *(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks if the student provides the definition **and** a clear distinction. A simple "Accuracy = correct reading" without contrast with precision gets only 2 marks.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1, PCECT523]`
> **State and explain the three types of errors that affect the accuracy of a measuring instrument.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three types of errors affecting accuracy are:

1. **Gross Error:** Caused by human mistakes such as parallax, misreading of scale, or incorrect recording. These are large, unpredictable deviations and can be eliminated by careful reading and training.

2. **Systematic Error:** Caused by instrument defects, calibration offset, or environmental bias (e.g., zero drift due to temperature). These errors are *consistent* and *one-directional*, and they reduce accuracy but **not** precision. They can be eliminated by **calibration** against a standard.

3. **Random Error:** Caused by unpredictable fluctuations such as electrical noise, vibration, or air turbulence. These produce *small scatter* in repeated readings and reduce **precision**. They can be reduced (but not eliminated) by averaging many readings, since the standard error decreases as $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU 2024 Scheme Style):** Answer **any ONE** full question from Module 1. Each question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Sub-part (a) typically tests descriptive concepts; sub-part (b) tests calculation or design application.

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1, PCECT523]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of a measuring system. Discuss the static and dynamic characteristics of a measurement system in detail.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A pressure gauge is used to measure a pressure of 50 kPa. The gauge reads 49.6 kPa. If the full-scale range of the gauge is 0 to 100 kPa, calculate (i) the absolute error, (ii) the percentage inaccuracy referred to the true value, and (iii) the percentage accuracy referred to the full-scale value. Comment on the accuracy class of the gauge.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram of a Measuring System:**

```
[Primary Sensing Element] → [Variable Conversion Element] → [Variable Manipulation Element] → [Data Transmission Element] → [Data Presentation Element]
```

**Functional Elements Explained:**

1. **Primary Sensing Element:** Receives the measurand (e.g., a Bourdon tube in a pressure gauge). Performs the *first* energy conversion.
2. **Variable Conversion Element:** Converts the output of the primary element into a more usable form (e.g., strain gauge converts strain to resistance change).
3. **Variable Manipulation Element:** Amplifies, filters, or otherwise conditions the signal (e.g., Wheatstone bridge + instrumentation amplifier).
4. **Data Transmission Element:** Sends the conditioned signal to the display or recording device (e.g., 4–20 mA current loop).
5. **Data Presentation Element:** Displays or records the result (e.g., digital display, chart recorder, or SCADA screen).

**Static Characteristics** (input does not vary with time):
- Accuracy, Precision, Repeatability, Reproducibility, Resolution, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Drift, Threshold, Span, Range.

**Dynamic Characteristics** (input varies with time):
- Speed of response, Rise time, Settling time, Time constant, Dead time, Overshoot, Dynamic error, Bandwidth, Phase lag, Fidelity.

*(7-mark distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for functional elements, 3 marks for static/dynamic characteristic list with examples.)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- True value, $x_t = 50$ kPa
- Measured value, $x_m = 49.6$ kPa
- Full-scale value, $x_{FS} = 100$ kPa

**(i) Absolute Error:**

$$
e = x_t - x_m = 50 - 49.6 = +0.4 \text{ kPa}
$$

The gauge under-reads by 0.4 kPa.

**(ii) Percentage Inaccuracy (referred to true value):**

$$
\%\text{Inaccuracy} = \frac{\vert x_t - x_m \vert}{x_t} \times 100 = \frac{0.4}{50} \times 100 = 0.8\,\%
$$

**(iii) Percentage Accuracy (referred to full-scale):**

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert x_t - x_m \vert}{x_{FS}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{0.4}{100}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.004) \times 100 = 99.6\,\%
$$

**Comment on Accuracy Class:**

An accuracy of $\pm$ 0.4 % of FS (or 99.6 % of FS) classifies this gauge as a **high-accuracy industrial instrument**, suitable for process control applications in petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and power industries. Standard industrial gauges are typically rated at $\pm$ 1.0 % to $\pm$ 2.0 % of FS, so this gauge is **above average** in performance.

*(7-mark distribution: 2 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 2 marks for part (iii), 1 mark for the comment.)*

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2022, Module 1, PCECT523]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following static characteristics of a measurement system with one example each: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Repeatability, (iv) Reproducibility, (v) Resolution, (vi) Sensitivity, (vii) Drift.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The following readings were taken for a voltage measurement: 10.12, 10.15, 10.10, 10.14, 10.11 V. The true voltage is 10.00 V. Calculate (i) the arithmetic mean, (ii) the standard deviation, (iii) the probable error of a single reading, and (iv) the percentage accuracy of the mean reading.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| # | Term | Definition | Example |
|---|------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | Closeness of a measured value to the **true value** | Thermometer reads 99.8 °C when true temp is 100 °C |
| 2 | **Precision** | Degree of agreement among repeated measurements of the same quantity | Five consecutive voltmeter readings: 10.05, 10.06, 10.04, 10.05, 10.05 V |
| 3 | **Repeatability** | Closeness of readings taken under **same conditions, same observer, same instrument, same location** in a short time | Same operator reads pressure gauge 5 times within 1 hour |
| 4 | **Reproducibility** | Closeness of readings taken under **changed conditions** (different operator or instrument or time) | Different lab technician uses a different gauge one week later |
| 5 | **Resolution** | Smallest measurable change in input that produces a detectable change in output | A digital multimeter displaying 3½ digits has resolution of 0.1 mV on the 2 V range |
| 6 | **Sensitivity** | Ratio of change in output to change in input | Thermocouple producing 40 µV per °C has sensitivity of 40 µV/°C |
