**Step 1 — End Points**
$(X_1, Y_1) = (0,\ 0.00)$, $(X_6, Y_6) = (10,\ 5.00)$.

**Step 2 — Slope**

$$
m_{ep} = \frac{5.00 - 0.00}{10 - 0} = 0.50\;\text{V/bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Intercept**

$$
c_{ep} = 0.00 - (0.50)(0) = 0.00\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Reference Line:** $Y_{ref}(X) = 0.50\,X$.

**Step 5 — Deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Y_{1} &= 0.00 - 0.00  &= 0.00 \\
\Delta Y_{2} &= 0.98 - 1.00  &= -0.02 \\
\Delta Y_{3} &= 2.05 - 2.00  &= +0.05 \\
\Delta Y_{4} &= 2.96 - 3.00  &= -0.04 \\
\Delta Y_{5} &= 4.02 - 4.00  &= +0.02 \\
\Delta Y_{6} &= 5.00 - 5.00  &= 0.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Deviation**

$$
\Delta Y_{\max} = 0.05\;\text{V (at 4 bar)}
$$

**Step 7 — Full-Scale Output**

$$
Y_{FS} = 5.00\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 8 — Percentage Nonlinearity**

$$
NL(\%) = \pm\,\frac{0.05}{5.00}\times 100 = \pm\,1.0\,\%\,\text{FSO}
$$

**Final Answer:** Maximum deviation = **0.05 V**, percentage nonlinearity = **±1 % FSO**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Bloom Level: Understand / Apply) — **Internal Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, define and explain **independent nonlinearity (INL)** of a sensor. How is it different from end-point nonlinearity? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor provides the following readings:

| Input $T$ (°C) | 0 | 25 | 50 | 75 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $V$ (mV) | 0.0 | 2.6 | 5.1 | 7.4 | 10.0 |

Determine the **least-squares best-fit straight line** and the **independent nonlinearity** in % FSO. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Independent nonlinearity (INL)** is the maximum deviation of the actual sensor output from the **best-fit straight line** obtained using the **least-squares method**. The least-squares line is positioned such that the sum of the squares of all deviations is minimised.

**Sketch description (text-based):** A curve of output (Y-axis) versus input (X-axis) is drawn, with the actual sensor response (slightly curved) and the best-fit straight line passing through it. Vertical arrows are drawn from each data point to the best-fit line. The longest arrow represents $\Delta Y_{\max,\,INL}$.

**Difference from end-point nonlinearity:**

| Feature | End-Point | Independent (INL) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference line | Joins zero and full-scale points | Least-squares best fit |
| Origin constraint | No | No |
| Computational effort | Low | Higher |
| Numerical value of NL | Larger (or equal) | Smaller (or equal) |
| Statistical validity | Approximate | Rigorous |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Tabulate the Sums**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 5 \\
\sum X_i &= 0+25+50+75+100 &= 250 \\
\sum Y_i &= 0.0+2.6+5.1+7.4+10.0 &= 25.1 \\
\sum X_i^{2} &= 0+625+2500+5625+10000 &= 18\,750 \\
\sum X_i Y_i &= 0+65+255+555+1000 &= 1\,875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Least-Squares Slope**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ls} &= \frac{(5)(1875) - (250)(25.1)}{(5)(18750) - (250)^{2}} \\
       &= \frac{9375 - 6275}{93750 - 62500} \\
       &= \frac{3100}{31250} \\
       &= 0.0992\;\text{mV/°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Least-Squares Intercept**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{ls} &= \frac{25.1 - (0.0992)(250)}{5} \\
       &= \frac{25.1 - 24.80}{5} \\
       &= \frac{0.30}{5} \\
       &= 0.060\;\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best-fit line:** $Y_{ref}(X) = 0.0992\,X + 0.060$.

**Step 4 — Deviations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Y_{1} &= 0.0 - 0.060   &= -0.060\;\text{mV} \\
\Delta Y_{2} &= 2.6 - 2.540   &= +0.060\;\text{mV} \\
\Delta Y_{3} &= 5.1 - 5.020   &= +0.080\;\text{mV} \\
\Delta Y_{4} &= 7.4 - 7.500   &= -0.100\;\text{mV} \\
\Delta Y_{5} &= 10.0 - 9.980  &= +0.020\;\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Absolute Deviation**

$$
\Delta Y_{\max,\,INL} = 0.100\;\text{mV (at 75 °C)}
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage INL**

$$
NL_{INL}(\%) = \pm\,\frac{0.100}{10.00}\times 100 = \pm\,1.0\,\%\,\text{FSO}
$$

**Final Answer:** Best-fit line $Y = 0.0992\,X + 0.060$, INL = **±1.0 % FSO**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign:** Nonlinearity is a magnitude. Always report it as ± or as an absolute value.
> 2. **Mixing numerator and denominator:** Some students divide by the input range (10 bar) instead of the output range (5 V). The denominator is **output full-scale**, not input full-scale.
> 3. **Computing slope incorrectly:** A frequent error is to use $\Delta Y / \Delta X$ over a *single step* rather than between the **end points**. The end-point slope must always be between $(X_1, Y_1)$ and $(X_n, Y_n)$.
> 4. **Losing marks on units:** Always state both **mV (or V)** and **% FSO** in the final answer. Single-unit answers are penalised.
> 5. **Skipping the "max" step:** The maximum absolute deviation $\Delta Y_{\max}$ must be explicitly identified at a specific input value.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Nonlinearity is a **static characteristic** of sensors and actuators.
- It is the **maximum deviation** of the actual output curve from a chosen **reference straight line**.
- Default KTU reference line: **End-Point Method** (unless specified otherwise).
- Standard formula: $NL(\%) = \pm\dfrac{\Delta Y_{\max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$.
- The four reference-line methods are: **End-Point, Zero-Based, Least-Squares (INL), Theoretical**.
- Least-squares (INL) gives the **smallest** and most statistically valid nonlinearity value.
- The denominator $Y_{FS}$ is the **output full-scale**, not the input range.
- Always report both the **absolute deviation (units of output)** and the **percentage (% FSO)**.
- Practical nonlinearity sources: **friction, magnetic saturation, thermal drift, hysteresis, quantization**.
- **End-point method** — quick, simple, KTU default; **Zero-based** — for sensors with true zero at zero input; **Least-squares** — best accuracy, used in lab calibration.
- For an LVDT (a typical KTU example), nonlinearity is typically **≤ ±1 % FSO** at the standard operating range.
- Software linearization (polynomial fit / lookup table) in microcontrollers is the modern engineering solution to nonlinearity.
- **Independence of reference line:** INL uses the best-fit line — the *most "independent"* of the four methods from arbitrary data points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Saturation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Sensors and Actuators (PCECT523)**, **Saturation** is defined as the operational state of a transducer, magnetic core, semiconductor device, or amplifier in which the output signal ceases to respond linearly (or at all) to further increases in the input stimulus. Once the input crosses a critical threshold known as the **Saturation Point** or **Saturation Level**, the output reaches a maximum bounded value and remains essentially constant regardless of any additional increase in the driving input.

Mathematically, saturation occurs when the **incremental sensitivity** (slope of the transfer characteristic) drops to **zero** or near-zero:

$$\frac{dY}{dX} \;\longrightarrow\; 0 \quad \text{as} \quad X \;\geq\; X_{sat}$$

Where:
- $Y$ = Output signal (voltage, current, magnetic flux, etc.)
- $X$ = Input stimulus (field, force, displacement, etc.)
- $X_{sat}$ = Saturation input threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Saturation is the condition in a sensor or actuator where the device has reached its maximum physical, magnetic, electrical, or thermal limit, beyond which the output becomes **non-linear** and eventually **clipped/flattened**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spongy Bucket" Analogy

Imagine a sponge sitting inside a bucket. The sponge represents the sensor's or actuator's ability to absorb and translate an input (water flow).

- **Stage 1 — Dry Sponge (Linear Region):** As water pours in, the sponge absorbs it easily. The weight of the sponge increases **proportionally** with the water poured. This is the **active/linear region** of the sensor.
- **Stage 2 — Saturated Sponge (Saturation Point):** The sponge cannot hold any more water. Even if you pour more water, the weight stays the same. The sponge is **saturated**. The output is now **clipped** at a maximum value.
- **Stage 3 — Overflow (Beyond Saturation):** Water spills over the sides. In a real sensor, this corresponds to **damage, distortion, heating, or permanent loss of calibration** — not just a flat signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A saturated sensor "gives up" — it stops telling you that the input is changing because the input has become too large for the device to physically or electronically represent it.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Magnetic Saturation Flux Density ($B_{sat}$):** Typically **0.3 T to 1.5 T** for electrical steels, **0.4 T to 0.5 T** for silicon steel, up to **2.4 T** for grain-oriented silicon steel.
- **Saturation Magnetization ($M_s$):** Around **4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{6} = 1.26 \times 10^{6} A/m** for pure iron.
- **Saturation Voltage ($V_{sat}$):** For a BJT, typically **0.2 V** ($V_{CE(sat)}$).
- **Output Saturation Current ($I_{sat}$):** The maximum current a current-output sensor can source/sink before clipping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Saturation is fundamentally a **non-linear phenomenon** and is the most common cause of measurement error in sensor systems. Understanding its origin (magnetic, electronic, mechanical, or thermal) is essential for proper sensor selection and signal conditioning.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Saturation in Sensors & Actuators

| Category | Where it Occurs | Example |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| **Magnetic Saturation** | Ferromagnetic cores in inductive sensors, transformers, solenoids, LVDTs | Core of an LVDT cannot carry more flux lines |
| **Semiconductor/Electronic Saturation** | Transistors (BJT, MOSFET), op-amps, Hall sensors | Op-amp output clipped at supply rail |
| **Mechanical Saturation** | Springs, diaphragms, MEMS structures | Diaphragm of a pressure sensor reaching max deflection |
| **Thermal Saturation** | Thermistors, RTDs, thermal actuators | Sensor reaches thermal equilibrium at high input |
| **Optical Saturation** | Photodiodes, phototransistors, CCD/CMOS pixels | All pixels fully exposed; no further contrast possible |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Magnetic Saturation (Most Critical for KTU Module 1)

The **B-H Curve** (Magnetization Curve) of a ferromagnetic material is the locus where saturation is most explicitly visualized.

**Step 1 — Linear Region (Raleigh Region):**  
For low values of magnetizing force $H$, the magnetic flux density $B$ rises **linearly**. The slope is the permeability $\mu$.

$$B = \mu H \quad \text{where} \quad \mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$$

**Step 2 — Knee of the Curve:**  
The slope starts to drop. Small increments in $H$ produce diminishing increments in $B$. The material is approaching its magnetic limit.

**Step 3 — Saturation Region:**  
$B$ becomes nearly constant at $B_{sat}$. Adding more magnetizing current produces almost no additional flux because all magnetic **dipole domains** within the material are fully aligned. The material is "domain-saturated."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this happen?** Inside a ferromagnetic material, microscopic regions called **magnetic domains** (Weiss domains) act like tiny bar magnets. When an external $H$ field is applied, these domains rotate and align with the field. When **all domains are perfectly aligned**, no further magnetization is possible — the material is saturated.

### 2.1.2 Electronic Saturation (BJT Example)

In a BJT used as a sensor amplifier:

- **Active Region:** $I_C = \beta I_B$ (collector current controlled linearly by base current).
- **Saturation Region:** $V_{CE}$ drops to $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V. $I_C$ no longer increases with $I_B$. The transistor is "fully ON."

### 2.1.3 Op-Amp Saturation (Critical for Signal Conditioning)

The output of an op-amp cannot exceed its DC supply rails ($\pm V_{CC}$). When the differential input × open-loop gain $\rightarrow$ a value greater than $V_{CC}$:

$$V_{out} = \pm V_{CC} \quad \text{(clipped)}$$

This is **rail saturation** and is the most common issue in sensor signal chains.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU University Exam. Memorize the **boundary conditions** and **units**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $B = \mu_0(H + M)$ | Fundamental B-H-M relation | Tesla (T), A/m |
| 2 | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Linear (unsaturated) region | Valid only when $H < H_{sat}$ |
| 3 | $\mu_r = \frac{B}{\mu_0 H}$ | Relative permeability definition | Dimensionless; varies with H |
| 4 | $V_{out(sat)} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$ | Op-amp theoretical output | Clipped at $\pm V_{supply}$ |
| 5 | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\,\text{V}$ | BJT saturation voltage drop | Silicon NPN |
| 6 | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | BJT active region | Breaks down in saturation |
| 7 | $L = \frac{N\Phi}{I}$ | Inductance relation | $L$ drops in saturation |
| 8 | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Inductive reactance | Decreases with saturation |
| 9 | $F = kx$ | Linear spring force | Breaks down at elastic limit |
| 10 | $P = k(\Delta T)$ | Thermal sensor (linear) | Breaks down at thermal saturation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** In every formula, the **inequality condition** defines whether the system is in the linear (unsaturated) or saturated regime. Examiner key: *Always state the condition before applying the equation.*

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In LVDTs (Linear Variable Differential Transformers):** Saturation in the core leads to **non-linearity and reduced sensitivity**. Designers use materials with high $B_{sat}$ (e.g., permalloy, nanocrystalline alloys).
- **In Solenoid Actuators:** The pull force plateaus at saturation. This limits the **maximum force output** and is why solenoids are designed with **overflux capacity** buffers.
- **In Current Transformers (CTs):** Saturation causes **ratio error and phase displacement**, critical in power-system metering.
- **In Hall Effect Sensors:** Magnetic saturation of the integrated **magnetic concentrator** determines the upper measurement limit.
- **In MEMS Pressure Sensors:** Diaphragm saturation = mechanical stop = **device failure** if exceeded.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance Drop Due to Magnetic Saturation

**Statement:** Show that as a ferromagnetic core saturates, the effective inductance of a coil wound on it decreases from $L_0$ to a lower value $L_{sat}$.

### Step 1 — Start with the constitutive B-H relation

In the unsaturated linear region, the magnetic flux density $B$ is given by:

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$$

### Step 2 — Define flux through N-turn coil

The total magnetic flux linkage $\lambda$ through a coil of $N$ turns and cross-sectional area $A$ is:

$$\lambda = N \Phi = N \cdot B \cdot A = N \cdot \mu_0 \mu_r H \cdot A$$

### Step 3 — Express H in terms of coil current

The magnetizing field $H$ produced by a current $I$ flowing through $N$ turns over a mean magnetic path length $\ell$ is:

$$H = \frac{N I}{\ell}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute H into the flux linkage equation

$$\lambda = N \cdot \mu_0 \mu_r \left(\frac{N I}{\ell}\right) \cdot A = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell} \cdot I$$

### Step 5 — Apply definition of inductance

By definition, $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{I}$:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell} = L_0$$

This is the constant unsaturated inductance $L_0$.

### Step 6 — Introduce saturation effect

As $H$ increases, $\mu_r$ is **no longer constant** but drops as the core saturates. The effective permeability becomes field-dependent:

$$\mu_r^{eff} = \mu_r(H) = \frac{B(H)}{\mu_0 H}$$

The **incremental permeability** is:

$$\mu_{inc} = \frac{dB}{dH} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu_0}$$

### Step 7 — Express saturated inductance

Replacing $\mu_r$ with $\mu_r^{eff}$:

$$L_{sat} = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r^{eff} N^2 A}{\ell} = \frac{N^2 A}{\ell} \cdot \frac{dB}{dH}$$

### Step 8 — State the final relation

$$\boxed{\;L_{sat} = \frac{N^2 A}{\ell} \cdot \frac{dB}{dH}\;}$$

Because $dB/dH \rightarrow 0$ in deep saturation, the inductance collapses to a small value set only by the **air-core inductance** of the coil.

$$\lim_{H \to H_{sat}} L_{sat} = L_{air} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In a saturated inductive sensor (LVDT, variable reluctance sensor), the inductance — and therefore the output voltage — flattens out. This is the direct cause of the **upper measurement limit** in such devices.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Op-Amp Saturation Output Voltage

**Statement:** Determine the output voltage of an op-amp configured as a non-inverting amplifier when the input exceeds the linear range.

### Step 1 — Write the closed-loop gain equation

For a non-inverting amplifier:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \cdot V_{in}$$

### Step 2 — Define the theoretical maximum output

This equation predicts an *unbounded* output. However, real op-amps have supply rails $\pm V_s$. The actual output is the **smaller of the two**:

$$V_{out}^{actual} = \min\left[\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) V_{in}, \quad +V_s - V_{drop}\right]$$

$$V_{out}^{actual} = \max\left[\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) V_{in}, \quad -V_s + V_{drop}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Define the input saturation threshold

Set the two expressions equal to find the critical input:

$$\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) V_{in}^{sat} = V_s - V_{drop}$$

$$V_{in}^{sat} = \frac{V_s - V_{drop}}{1 + R_f / R_{in}}$$

### Step 4 — Example numerical evaluation

Let $V_s = \pm 12$ V, $V_{drop} \approx 1.5$ V (for a typical LM358), $R_f = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_{in} = 1$ k$\Omega$.

$$1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 1 + \frac{10\,k\Omega}{1\,k\Omega} = 11$$

$$V_{in}^{sat} = \frac{12 - 1.5}{11} = \frac{10.5}{11} = 0.955 \text{ V}$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

Any input voltage above **0.955 V** will produce a **clipped output at +10.5 V**. The sensor signal is no longer proportional to the input. This is the **saturation point** of the signal-conditioning stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The expression $V_s - V_{drop}$ (not just $V_s$) is what a real op-amp's output saturates at. The $V_{drop}$ is the **headroom voltage** lost in the output stage transistors — typically 0.7 to 1.5 V.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Saturation Types

| Aspect | Magnetic Saturation | Electronic Saturation | Mechanical Saturation |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|
| **Physical cause** | All magnetic domains aligned | Transistor fully ON or op-amp rails | Elastic limit reached |
| **Governing equation** | $B = \mu_0(H + M)$ | $V_{CE} \rightarrow V_{CE(sat)}$ | $\sigma \rightarrow \sigma_{yield}$ |
| **Symptom** | L drops, $B$ flat | Output clipped at rail | Permanent deformation |
| **Reversibility** | Fully reversible (B returns when H drops) | Reversible if not damaged | Partially irreversible (plastic region) |
| **KTU example device** | LVDT, current transformer, solenoid | Op-amp, BJT switch | Pressure sensor diaphragm, MEMS cantilever |
| **Design fix** | Use material with higher $B_{sat}$ | Use rail-to-rail op-amp, higher supply | Use stiffer material, add mechanical stop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 B-H Magnetization Curve Showing Saturation

The following Mermaid diagram traces the **input-output transfer characteristic** of a ferromagnetic core, marking all critical regions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT H Low"] --> B["LINEAR REGION B rises proportionally to H"]
    B --> C["KNEE OF CURVE slope begins to decrease"]
    C --> D["SATURATION REGION B nearly constant at Bsat"]
    D --> E["DEEP SATURATION B equals Bsat regardless of H increase"]
    E --> F["REDUCTION OF H B follows hysteresis loop back to origin"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The progression from A to E shows the three classic phases — *linear, knee, and saturation*. Path E to F reminds the student that saturation is reversible, but only via the hysteresis path (energy loss occurs).

---

## 4.2 Sensor Signal Chain Showing Where Saturation Can Occur

This block diagram identifies **every point** in a typical sensor signal-conditioning path where saturation can clip the signal.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["PHYSICAL STIMULUS Field Force Temperature Light"] --> P["PRIMARY SENSOR Element LVDT Hall Diode RTD Photodiode"]
    P --> C1["CONDITIONING STAGE 1 Amplifier Possible Saturation Here"]
    C1 --> C2["CONDITIONING STAGE 2 Filter Rectifier"]
    C2 --> C3["ADC Analog to Digital Converter Clipping at Vref"]
    C3 --> D["DIGITAL OUTPUT Saturated Code Stuck at Maximum"]
    C1 -.-> S1["Saturation 1 Magnetic Core"]
    C2 -.-> S2["Saturation 2 OpAmp Rail"]
    C3 -.-> S3["Saturation 3 ADC Full Scale"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Saturation can occur at *multiple* points in a single signal chain. A sensor might be operating in its linear range, but if the **amplifier saturates first**, the output still goes flat. Proper design requires the **dynamic range of every stage to exceed the worst-case input**.

---

## 4.3 Hysteresis vs. Saturation — Conceptual Diagram

The following diagram contrasts the two phenomena which students often confuse in exams.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HYS["HYSTERESIS LAG OF B BEHIND H"]
        H1["H increasing B follows upper branch"] --> H2["H reaches Hmax B at Bmax"]
        H2 --> H3["H decreasing B follows LOWER branch not same as upper"]
        H3 --> H4["H reaches zero B equals Br Residual Flux"]
    end
    subgraph SAT["SATURATION B AT MAXIMUM Bsat"]
        S1["H increasing"] --> S2["B rises linearly"]
        S2 --> S3["B reaches Bsat Flat Plateau"]
        S3 --> S4["H further increased B unchanged Plateau Continues"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference:** **Hysteresis** = same input produces **two different outputs** depending on history. **Saturation** = input has no further effect on output. They are related (both involve non-linearity) but not identical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define magnetic saturation. What is the significance of the B-H curve knee point in sensor design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Magnetic saturation is the state of a ferromagnetic material in which the magnetic flux density $B$ ceases to increase appreciably with further increase in the applied magnetizing force $H$. All magnetic domains become aligned, and the material cannot carry additional flux.

**Knee Point Significance (2 Marks):** The knee of the B-H curve marks the transition from the linear region to the saturation region. In sensor design, the operating point of a transducer (e.g., LVDT core, current transformer) must be kept **below the knee** to maintain linear proportionality between input and output. Exceeding the knee introduces non-linearity, reduces sensitivity, and degrades measurement accuracy.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between active region, saturation region, and cutoff region of a BJT with reference to its use in a sensor amplifier circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Region | Condition | Sensor Amplifier Use |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Active** | $V_{CE} > V_{CE(sat)}$; $I_C = \beta I_B$ | **Linear amplification** of small sensor signals |
| **Saturation** | $V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V; $I_C$ no longer follows $I_B$ | Acts as a closed switch — used in **switched-mode sensor interfaces** (e.g., PWM output sensors) |
| **Cutoff** | $I_B = 0$; $I_C = 0$ | Transistor OFF — sensor channel disabled |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The active region is preferred for analog sensor signal amplification. Saturation and cutoff are switching states, used only in digital/logic-level sensor outputs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Option A — Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a)** Explain with a neat B-H curve the phenomenon of **magnetic saturation** in ferromagnetic materials. Discuss its effect on the inductance of a coil wound over a saturated core. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** An LVDT is used to measure displacement in the range **0 to 50 mm**. The primary is excited at 5 kHz. Explain the role of core saturation in limiting the LVDT's range and describe two design techniques to extend its linear range. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Curve Explanation (3 Marks):**  
A B-H curve plots magnetic flux density $B$ (y-axis) vs. magnetizing field $H$ (x-axis). In the **initial region** (Raleigh region), $B$ rises linearly with $H$ following $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$. The slope is the permeability $\mu$. At the **knee**, the slope begins to flatten as the magnetic domains progressively align. Beyond the knee, $B$ approaches the saturation value $B_{sat}$, where all domains are aligned and no further increase in $B$ is possible.

**Step 2 — Physical Origin (2 Marks):**  
Ferromagnetic materials consist of **Weiss domains**, each spontaneously magnetized. In the demagnetized state, domains are randomly oriented. An applied $H$ field causes domain wall motion and rotation. When all domains point in the direction of $H$, the material is **saturated**. Any additional $H$ can only add flux in the air (vacuum permeability $\mu_0$), which is negligible compared to the iron path.

**Step 3 — Effect on Inductance (2 Marks):**  
The inductance of an $N$-turn coil on a core is:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell}$$

As the core saturates, the **effective relative permeability** $\mu_r^{eff}$ drops dramatically because $dB/dH \to 0$. The inductance therefore **collapses toward the air-core value**:

$$L_{sat} \approx L_{air} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}$$

This causes a drop in the coil's inductive reactance $X_L = 2\pi f L$, leading to **reduced output voltage and loss of linearity** in inductive sensors.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Role of Core Saturation in Limiting Range (3 Marks):**  
In an LVDT, the secondary voltage is proportional to the differential flux linking the two secondary windings. As the movable core approaches either end of its stroke, the flux in the corresponding secondary leg becomes very high. If the magnetizing force drives that leg's iron **into saturation**, the secondary EMF flattens out, producing a non-linear output. The LVDT's specified linear range is therefore the displacement window within which **no part of the core saturates**.

**Step 2 — Design Technique 1: Use High $B_{sat}$ Core Material (2 Marks):**  
Replace standard silicon steel with **Permalloy (Ni-Fe alloy, $B_{sat} \approx 1.6$ T)** or **nanocrystalline ribbon** which has a much higher saturation flux density and a sharper knee. This delays saturation, allowing the core to remain linear over a longer displacement.

**Step 3 — Design Technique 2: Reduce Excitation Voltage (2 Marks):**  
The primary excitation amplitude can be reduced so that the magnetizing $H$ field at the extreme position remains below the knee. Trade-off: lower output voltage, requiring a **low-noise front-end amplifier**. Alternatively, use a **shorted-turn compensation winding** or a **flux-feedback loop** to linearize the output electronically.

---

### **Option B — Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a)** With the help of a transfer characteristic, explain **electronic saturation in an operational amplifier** used for sensor signal conditioning. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A non-inverting amplifier with gain $A = 20$ is built from an op-amp powered by $\pm 15$ V supplies. The op-amp has an output headroom of 1.5 V. Determine: **(i)** the input saturation voltage, **(ii)** the maximum linear input range, and **(iii)** the consequences if a sensor delivers a 1.2 V input to this stage. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Transfer Characteristic (3 Marks):**  
The op-amp transfer characteristic is a steep line of slope $A_{OL}$ (open-loop gain, typically $10^5$ to $10^6$) passing through the origin. The **linear region** is bounded by the supply rails. The output is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$$

For a closed-loop gain of $A_{CL}$, the linear range is:

$$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in}$$

This linear equation holds only as long as the predicted $V_{out}$ lies **between** $-(V_{CC} - V_{HR})$ and $+(V_{CC} - V_{HR})$, where $V_{HR}$ is the headroom.

**Step 2 — Saturation Clipping (2 Marks):**  
When the predicted $V_{out}$ exceeds the positive rail minus headroom, the output **clips** at $+V_{sat} = +V_{CC} - V_{HR}$. Similarly, on the negative side, $V_{out}$ clips at $-V_{sat} = -V_{CC} + V_{HR}$. The transfer curve becomes **horizontal** in the saturation region — a flat plateau.

**Step 3 — Relevance to Sensors (2 Marks):**  
A sensor's small signal (e.g., thermocouple mV, strain gauge mV) is amplified for ADC. If the sensor input transiently exceeds the linear range (e.g., during a fault, ESD, or over-range event), the op-amp output saturates. The ADC then reads a **stuck-at-maximum value**, losing all information. Recovery requires the input to fall back within the linear window, but the saturation region may also introduce **slow slew recovery**, delaying accurate measurement.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Closed-loop gain: $A_{CL} = 20$
- Supply voltage: $V_{CC} = \pm 15$ V
- Headroom: $V_{HR} = 1.5$ V

**Step 1 — Compute the Saturation Output Voltage (2 Marks):**

$$+V_{sat} = V_{CC} - V_{HR} = 15 - 1.5 = 13.5 \text{ V}$$

$$-V_{sat} = -V_{CC} + V_{HR} = -15 + 1.5 = -13.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Input Saturation Voltage (2 Marks):**

$$V_{in}^{sat} = \frac{V_{sat}}{A_{CL}} = \frac{13.5}{20} = 0.675 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Linear Input Range (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\;V_{in}^{linear} = \pm 0.675 \text{ V}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Consequences for 1.2 V Input (2 Marks):**

Since $1.2$ V $\gg$ $0.675$ V, the amplifier is **deeply saturated**. The output is **clipped at +13.5 V**, not at the expected $20 \times 1.2 = 24$ V (which is impossible because the supply is only 15 V). The ADC reading will be a **constant full-scale value** with no information about the actual input. This constitutes a **measurement error** and could trigger **false alarms** in a safety-critical sensor system. Remedy: either use a **higher supply rail** (e.g., $\pm 18$ V), a **rail-to-rail op-amp**, or a **lower gain setting**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Hysteresis with Saturation (–2 marks typical):** Many students write "saturation = the loop traced when H goes up and down." This is hysteresis. Saturation is the **plateau**, not the loop.
> 2. **Forgetting the Headroom Voltage (–1 mark):** Writing $V_{sat} = V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{sat} = V_{CC} - V_{HR}$. The headroom is what costs you the mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to state boundary conditions in B-H problems (–1 mark):** A formula like $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ is only valid **before** saturation. The examiner expects you to write *"valid for $H < H_{sat}$."*
> 4. **Mixing up BJT saturation and cutoff (–2 marks):** Saturation means transistor is **fully ON**, not OFF. Cutoff is OFF.
> 5. **Skipping units (–0.5 to 1 mark):** Always quote $B$ in Tesla, $H$ in A/m, $V$ in Volts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Saturation (Module 1, PCECT523)**

- **Definition:** Saturation = output flatlines despite further input increase.
- **Magnetic Saturation:** All Weiss domains aligned. $B \to B_{sat}$, $dB/dH \to 0$.
- **B-H Curve Regions:** Linear → Knee → Saturation.
- **Inductance Collapse:** $L \to L_{air} = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell$ when core saturates.
- **Electronic Saturation:** BJT ($V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V) and op-amp ($V_{out} = \pm (V_{CC} - V_{HR})$).
- **Mechanical Saturation:** Diaphragm/Spring hits elastic limit. Often **irreversible**.
- **Saturation vs Hysteresis:** Hysteresis = *two paths* for same input. Saturation = *no further response*.
- **Design Fixes:** High $B_{sat}$ materials, lower excitation, rail-to-rail op-amps, mechanical stops, ADC headroom.
- **Key Formula:** $\mu_r^{eff} = B/(\mu_0 H)$; $L_{sat} = (N^2 A/\ell)(dB/dH)$.
- **Standard $B_{sat}$ values:** Silicon steel ≈ 1.5–2.0 T; pure iron ≈ 2.15 T; ferrite ≈ 0.3–0.5 T.
- **Exam Trick:** Always mention the **condition** (e.g., $H < H_{sat}$) before applying a linear equation.
- **Real Devices Affected:** LVDT, current transformer, solenoid, op-amp, BJT, pressure sensor diaphragm, Hall sensor IC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeatability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repeatability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeatability** is defined as the *ability of a sensor or measurement system to provide the same output (or nearly the same output) when the same input is applied repeatedly, under identical conditions of measurement, using the same instrument, the same observer, the same location, and the same environmental conditions, over a short period of time.*

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon, repeatability is classified as one of the **static characteristics of a sensor**, alongside accuracy, precision, linearity, sensitivity, resolution, drift, and hysteresis. The standard reference for repeatability evaluation is typically documented in **ISO 5725** and **ISA 37.1** standards, which KTU examiners often quote.

Mathematically, repeatability is quantified as the **maximum deviation observed among a set of repeated measurements** of the same input, expressed either as an absolute quantity, as a percentage of the **Full-Scale Output (FSO)**, or as a percentage of the **reading**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCECT523, Module 1):** Repeatability is grouped under the "Static Performance Characteristics of Sensors." The KTU examiner expects students to distinguish repeatability from **reproducibility** — a frequently tested 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are using a **digital weighing scale** in your kitchen. You place a standard 1 kg weight on the scale, remove it, place it again, remove it, and repeat this process **five times in a row**, in the same room, within two minutes, with no one else touching the scale.

- If the scale displays **1.000 kg, 1.001 kg, 1.000 kg, 0.999 kg, 1.000 kg** → The scale has **excellent repeatability**.
- If the scale displays **1.000 kg, 1.050 kg, 0.920 kg, 1.100 kg, 0.880 kg** → The scale has **poor repeatability**, even if on average it shows 0.990 kg (which might be *accurate* or *inaccurate* — that is a separate question).

**Key Takeaway from the Analogy:**
Repeatability cares only about **consistency under identical conditions**, NOT about whether the value is correct (accuracy) or how scattered the readings are in a statistical sense (precision in the broader sense). It is the sensor's promise that *if you don't change anything, I won't either*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the Definition

The following **bolded metrics** are universally referenced when defining repeatability in the KTU 2024 Scheme:

- **Repeatability (R)** — the spread of repeated readings, often the maximum range or a multiple of standard deviation.
- **Repeatability Error (e_R)** — the difference between the maximum and minimum observed outputs divided by the full-scale output, expressed as a percentage.
- **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** — the algebraic difference between the output readings at the upper and lower limits of the measurement range.
- **Number of Trials (N)** — typically 3, 5, 10, or more repeated measurements per KTU numerical problems.
- **Standard Deviation (σ)** — used when repeatability is expressed statistically as ±kσ (usually k = 2 or k = 3, corresponding to 95.4% or 99.7% confidence respectively).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions Required for a "Repeatable" Test

For a measurement to be classified as testing *repeatability* (and NOT reproducibility), the following conditions must ALL hold:

1. **Same Instrument** — The same sensor/transducer is used.
2. **Same Observer** — The same person records the readings.
3. **Same Method** — Identical measurement procedure.
4. **Same Location** — No change in physical setup or mounting.
5. **Same Environmental Conditions** — Temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration unchanged.
6. **Short Time Interval** — Measurements taken close in time, minimizing long-term drift effects.
7. **Same Input** — The exact same input stimulus (same magnitude, polarity, direction).

If **any one** of these conditions changes, the resulting metric is **reproducibility**, NOT repeatability. This distinction is the single most heavily tested KTU 3-mark question on this topic.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Repeatability — Engineering Rationale

- **Why we measure repeatability:** A sensor that produces wildly different outputs for the same input is unusable, regardless of how well-calibrated it claims to be. Repeatability establishes the **lower bound of measurement uncertainty** for a sensor under ideal conditions.
- **How it affects system design:** In feedback control loops (e.g., PID controllers in process industries), poor repeatability directly degrades closed-loop stability because the controller cannot trust the sensor's reading twice in a row.
- **Industry relevance:** Repeatability is the make-or-break metric in **medical instrumentation** (e.g., infusion pumps, blood glucose meters), **aerospace sensors** (e.g., pitot tubes, gyroscopes), and **industrial process control** (e.g., flow meters, pressure transmitters).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **all high-yield formulas** for repeatability problems in KTU ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Repeatability Error (Percentage of FSO) | $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Most common KTU formula. Compares spread to full-scale output. | **% of FSO** |
| 2 | Repeatability Error (Percentage of Reading) | $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{\bar{Y}} \times 100\%$ | Used when FSO is undefined or when the sensor operates over a narrow band. | **% of reading** |
| 3 | Statistical Repeatability (2σ) | $R = \pm 2\sigma$ | Repeatability expressed as 2 times the standard deviation (95.4% confidence). | Same as output unit |
| 4 | Statistical Repeatability (3σ) | $R = \pm 3\sigma$ | 99.7% confidence interval. Used in mission-critical aerospace. | Same as output unit |
| 5 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}$ | Sample standard deviation across N repeated trials. | Same as output unit |
| 6 | Full-Scale Output | $Y_{FS} = Y_{max,range} - Y_{min,range}$ | The calibration range of the sensor. | Same as output unit |
| 7 | Mean Output | $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i$ | Arithmetic mean of repeated readings. | Same as output unit |
| 8 | Repeatability Index (in-band error) | $\epsilon_R = \dfrac{\pm \Delta Y_{max}}{Y_{FS}}$ | Used in calibration certificates as a non-percentage form. | Dimensionless (or unit) |

**Boundary Conditions & Special Notes:**
- Repeatability error is **always non-negative** ($\geq 0$).
- It is expressed as a **percentage** in the majority of KTU numerical problems.
- A smaller repeatability error indicates a better sensor.
- Typical industrial-grade sensors target **$\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ of FSO**; laboratory-grade sensors aim for **$\pm 0.01\%$** or better.

## 2.4 Distinguishing Repeatability from Closely Related Concepts

This distinction is a **guaranteed KTU question** in either Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark). The formula sheet alone is insufficient without clarity on these neighbours:

| Characteristic | Varies Input? | Varies Conditions? | Time Scale | KTU Usual Question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeatability** | No | No | Short | "Sensor X tested 5 times under same conditions" |
| **Reproducibility** | No | **Yes** (one or more) | Long | "Same sensor tested in 3 different labs" |
| **Drift** | Constant | Constant (env.) | **Long** | "Sensor output changes with time at zero input" |
| **Hysteresis** | **Yes (direction)** | No | Short | "Sensor reads differently on ascending vs descending input" |
| **Accuracy** | — | — | — | "Comparison with true value" |
| **Precision** | — | Same | — | "Statistical scatter of measurements" |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Repeatability Error Formula (Percentage of FSO)

We start from the **fundamental concept** that repeatability is a measure of dispersion. In a typical KTU numerical problem, you are given $N$ readings $Y_1, Y_2, Y_3, \ldots, Y_N$ taken at the same input $X_{in}$.

**Step 1:** Identify the maximum and minimum readings from the dataset.

$$
Y_{max} = \max(Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_N)
$$

$$
Y_{min} = \min(Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_N)
$$

> **Logic:** The extreme values represent the worst-case spread that occurred during repeated testing.

**Step 2:** Compute the **range** of the repeated readings.

$$
\Delta Y = Y_{max} - Y_{min}
$$

> **Logic:** The range is the simplest measure of dispersion for a small dataset. KTU uses this approach in most numerical problems.

**Step 3:** Compute the **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** of the sensor. The FSO is determined from the sensor's calibration range, NOT from the repeated measurements at a single input.

$$
Y_{FS} = Y_{upper\ limit} - Y_{lower\ limit}
$$

> **Logic:** FSO is a property of the sensor's design range — independent of the test input chosen.

**Step 4:** Form the **ratio** of the reading spread to the full-scale output. This normalizes the repeatability so that it is independent of the sensor's physical range.

$$
\frac{\Delta Y}{Y_{FS}} = \frac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}}
$$

> **Logic:** Normalization allows comparison of repeatability between, say, a 0–100 °C thermometer and a 0–1000 °C thermometer on a common scale.

**Step 5:** Convert to **percentage** by multiplying by 100.

$$
e_R = \frac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%
$$

> **Logic:** Percentage form is the industry-standard reporting unit, used in sensor datasheets and KTU exam answers.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Standard-Deviation-Based Repeatability

For larger datasets ($N \geq 10$), KTU problems occasionally use the statistical formulation.

**Step 1:** Compute the arithmetic mean of all $N$ repeated readings.

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **deviation** of each reading from the mean.

$$
d_i = Y_i - \bar{Y}
$$

**Step 3:** Square each deviation and sum them.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2
$$

> **Logic:** Squaring removes the sign and emphasizes larger deviations.

**Step 4:** Apply the **Bessel's correction** ($N-1$ in the denominator) to get the sample standard deviation.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}
$$

> **Logic:** Bessel's correction removes the bias introduced when estimating population parameters from a finite sample.

**Step 5:** Express repeatability as a multiple of standard deviation.

$$
R = \pm k \sigma
$$

> **Logic:** The multiplier $k$ corresponds to a confidence interval. Under a normal (Gaussian) distribution, $k = 2$ gives 95.4% and $k = 3$ gives 99.7% confidence that future readings will lie within $\pm k\sigma$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A pressure transducer is calibrated to measure pressures in the range **0 to 10 bar**, with a corresponding output voltage of **0 to 5 V**. The transducer is tested by applying a constant input of **5 bar** five times, and the following output voltages (in V) are recorded:

$$Y_1 = 2.51, \quad Y_2 = 2.48, \quad Y_3 = 2.53, \quad Y_4 = 2.49, \quad Y_5 = 2.50$$

Calculate:
1. The repeatability error as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (FSO).
2. The standard deviation of the readings.
3. The repeatability expressed as $\pm 2\sigma$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify $Y_{max}$ and $Y_{min}$:**

$$
Y_{max} = 2.53 \text{ V}, \qquad Y_{min} = 2.48 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the range $\Delta Y$:**

$$
\Delta Y = 2.53 - 2.48 = 0.05 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the FSO:**

$$
Y_{FS} = 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the repeatability error (Part 1):**

$$
e_R = \frac{0.05}{5} \times 100\% = 1.0\% \text{ of FSO}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mean for Part 2:**

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{2.51 + 2.48 + 2.53 + 2.49 + 2.50}{5} = \frac{12.51}{5} = 2.502 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the squared deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2.51 - 2.502)^2 &= (0.008)^2 = 0.000064 \\
(2.48 - 2.502)^2 &= (-0.022)^2 = 0.000484 \\
(2.53 - 2.502)^2 &= (0.028)^2 = 0.000784 \\
(2.49 - 2.502)^2 &= (-0.012)^2 = 0.000144 \\
(2.50 - 2.502)^2 &= (-0.002)^2 = 0.000004 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Sum the squared deviations:**

$$
\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = 0.000064 + 0.000484 + 0.000784 + 0.000144 + 0.000004 = 0.001480
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the sample standard deviation (Part 2):**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.001480}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.000370} = 0.01924 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute $\pm 2\sigma$ repeatability (Part 3):**

$$
R = \pm 2 \times 0.01924 = \pm 0.03847 \text{ V}
$$

> **Equivalent in % of FSO:** $R = \dfrac{\pm 0.03847}{5} \times 100\% = \pm 0.77\%$ of FSO.

**Comparison and Interpretation:**
- The **range-based** repeatability error ($1.0\%$) is slightly larger than the **statistical** repeatability ($\pm 0.77\%$). This is expected because the range uses only extreme values, while $\pm 2\sigma$ uses all data points.
- For KTU exams, when $N \leq 5$, the **range-based** formula is preferred. When $N \geq 10$, the **standard deviation** approach is preferred.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use?

| Given Data | $N$ (number of readings) | Preferred KTU Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeated readings given, FSO given | $N \leq 5$ | $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Small sample, range is robust |
| Repeated readings given, FSO given | $N \geq 10$ | $R = \pm 2\sigma$ or $\pm 3\sigma$ | Larger sample, statistical methods valid |
| FSO NOT given | Any | $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{\bar{Y}} \times 100\%$ | Normalize by mean instead |
| Sensor in a narrow operating band | Any | $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{\bar{Y}} \times 100\%$ | % of reading more meaningful than % of FSO |
| Confidence interval specified (95%, 99%) | Any | $R = \pm k\sigma$ (with $k = 2$ or $3$) | Statistical interpretation required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Repeatability Testing Protocol

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates the standard procedural flow that KTU examiners expect when a student is asked to "describe the procedure to determine the repeatability of a sensor."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sensor under Test] --> B[Identify Calibration Range and FSO]
    B --> C[Select a Test Input within the Range]
    C --> D[Apply the Same Input Repeatedly N Times]
    D --> E[Record Output Readings Y1 Y2 ... YN]
    E --> F{Is N greater than or equal to 10?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute Mean and Standard Deviation]
    F -->|No| H[Compute Ymax and Ymin]
    G --> I[Express Repeatability as plus or minus k sigma]
    H --> J[Compute Range deltaY equals Ymax minus Ymin]
    J --> K[Compute Repeatability Error as percent of FSO]
    I --> L[Compare with Manufacturer Spec]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Spec Met?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Sensor Passes Repeatability Test]
    M -->|No| O[Sensor Fails: Recalibrate or Reject]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Repeatability in a Measurement System

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows where repeatability fits inside a generalized sensor-based measurement chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Input Quantity X] --> S1[Sensor Transduction Element]
    S1 --> S2[Signal Conditioning Amplification Filtering]
    S2 --> S3[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    S3 --> S4[Digital Display or Data Logger]
    S4 --> O1[Output Reading Y]

    R1[Repeatability Test] -.->|Same X Repeated N Times| S1
    S1 -.->|Multiple Y Readings| R1
    R1 --> R2[Statistical Analysis Range or Sigma]
    R2 --> R3[Repeatability Specification plus or minus percent FSO]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationship of Repeatability to Other Static Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SC[Static Characteristics of a Sensor] --> AC[Accuracy]
    SC --> PR[Precision]
    SC --> RE[Repeatability]
    SC --> RP[Reproducibility]
    SC --> HY[Hysteresis]
    SC --> LN[Linearity]
    SC --> SE[Sensitivity]
    SC --> DR[Drift]
    SC --> RS[Resolution]

    RE --> RE1[Same Conditions]
    RE --> RE2[Short Time Duration]
    RE --> RE3[Same Instrument]
    RE --> RE4[Expressed as plus or minus percent FSO]

    RP --> RP1[Different Conditions]
    RP --> RP2[Long Time Duration]
    RP --> RP3[Possibly Different Instrument]
    RP --> RP4[Expressed as plus or minus percent FSO]

    RE -.->|Both are Dispersion Metrics| PR
    AC -.->|Compared Against True Value| RE
```

## 4.4 Comparative Visualization — Repeatability vs Reproducibility vs Hysteresis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[How do repeated measurements vary?] --> Q1{Conditions Changed?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2[REPEATABILITY]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Time Scale Long?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[REPRODUCIBILITY]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{Input Direction Changed?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[HYSTERESIS]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[DRIFT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define repeatability of a sensor. List any three conditions that must remain unchanged during a repeatability test.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Repeatability** is the ability of a sensor to produce the same output when the same input is applied repeatedly, under identical measurement conditions, using the same instrument, observer, and environment, over a short period of time.

**Three mandatory conditions (any three of the following):**
1. **Same instrument** (sensor, transducer, signal conditioning chain).
2. **Same observer** (the person recording the readings).
3. **Same measurement method** and procedure.
4. **Same location** and physical mounting.
5. **Same environmental conditions** (temperature, humidity, pressure).
6. **Short time interval** between successive measurements.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for conditions)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between repeatability and reproducibility with two key differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Repeatability | Reproducibility |
|---|---|---|
| Condition variation | All conditions kept **identical** | **One or more conditions are varied** (location, instrument, observer, etc.) |
| Time scale | Tests performed in a **short duration** | Tests performed over a **longer duration** with possible environmental changes |

> Repeatability is essentially a *special case* of reproducibility where none of the influencing conditions are changed between trials.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per valid difference + 1 for the special-case statement)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the static characteristic *repeatability* of a sensor. With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure for experimentally determining the repeatability of a pressure transducer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thermocouple is used to measure temperature in the range **0 °C to 500 °C**, producing an output EMF in the range **0 mV to 25 mV**. When the thermocouple junction is held at a constant temperature of **250 °C**, the following five repeated EMF readings are obtained: **12.55 mV, 12.48 mV, 12.62 mV, 12.50 mV, 12.58 mV**. Calculate the repeatability error (a) as a percentage of FSO, and (b) as a percentage of the mean reading.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **repeatability, reproducibility, and hysteresis** of a sensor with suitable examples and formulas. Why is repeatability considered the *lower bound of measurement uncertainty*?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A load cell is rated for **0 to 1000 N** with an output of **0 to 10 mV**. When a constant load of **500 N** is applied 8 times, the readings are: **5.05, 4.98, 5.02, 4.96, 5.04, 5.00, 4.99, 5.03 mV**. Calculate the repeatability error as a percentage of FSO and also express the repeatability as $\pm 2\sigma$ in mV and in % of FSO.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Definition and Procedure

**Definition (3 marks):**
Repeatability of a sensor is defined as the ability of the sensor to produce the **same output** (or outputs lying within a specified tolerance band) when the **same input** is applied **repeatedly**, under the **same conditions of measurement**, using the **same instrument and observer**, within a **short time interval**.

**Procedure with Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Apply Constant Input 250 deg C to Thermocouple] --> B[Record EMF Reading Y1]
    B --> C[Remove Input and Reset]
    C --> D[Reapply Same Input]
    D --> E[Record Y2]
    E --> F{Repeat N Times}
    F -->|Yes| C
    F -->|No| G[Tabulate Y1 to YN]
    G --> H[Find Ymax and Ymin]
    H --> I[Compute eR as percent FSO]
```

**Written Steps (alongside the diagram):**
1. Mount the pressure transducer and complete signal conditioning.
2. Identify the calibration range and corresponding FSO.
3. Apply a chosen constant input (e.g., 50% of full scale).
4. Record the output. Remove the input and let the system settle.
5. Repeat steps 3–4 for $N$ trials ($N \geq 3$ for KTU, $N \geq 5$ for accuracy).
6. Tabulate all $Y_i$ values.
7. Compute $Y_{max}$, $Y_{min}$, and the range.
8. Use the formula $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ to report the repeatability.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**
- Range: 0 to 500 °C, Output: 0 to 25 mV → $Y_{FS} = 25$ mV.
- Five readings at 250 °C: $Y_1 = 12.55$, $Y_2 = 12.48$, $Y_3 = 12.62$, $Y_4 = 12.50$, $Y_5 = 12.58$ mV.

**Step 1 — Identify extremes:**

$$
Y_{max} = 12.62 \text{ mV}, \quad Y_{min} = 12.48 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Range of readings:**

$$
\Delta Y = 12.62 - 12.48 = 0.14 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Repeatability error as % of FSO:**

$$
e_R = \frac{0.14}{25} \times 100\% = 0.56\% \text{ of FSO}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean reading for Part (b):**

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{12.55 + 12.48 + 12.62 + 12.50 + 12.58}{5} = \frac{62.73}{5} = 12.546 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Repeatability error as % of mean:**

$$
e_R = \frac{0.14}{12.546} \times 100\% = 1.116\% \text{ of reading}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Repeatability as **% of FSO = 0.56%**.
- Repeatability as **% of mean reading = 1.12%**.

> The two values differ because FSO (25 mV) is roughly twice the mean (12.55 mV), so the % of FSO is approximately half of the % of reading.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual Comparison

**Repeatability (2 marks):**
- Same input, same conditions, short time.
- Formula: $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$.
- *Example:* Reading a 100 °C thermometer 5 times in the same room, 2 minutes apart.

**Reproducibility (2 marks):**
- Same input, **one or more conditions changed** (different operator, lab, or environment).
- Formula: Same structural form as repeatability, but $\Delta Y$ is computed across conditions.
- *Example:* Same 100 °C thermometer sent to three different labs; each lab takes 5 readings.

**Hysteresis (2 marks):**
- Caused by **input direction reversal** (ascending vs descending input path).
- Formula: $e_H = \dfrac{\vert Y_{asc} - Y_{desc} \vert_{at\ same\ X}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$.
- *Example:* Pressure gauge showing different values at 5 bar when approached from 0 bar vs from 10 bar.

**Lower bound of uncertainty (1 mark):**
Repeatability is the *minimum uncertainty* any sensor can claim, because even with a perfect calibration, no sensor can produce a more consistent output than its own repeatability. All other errors (accuracy, hysteresis, drift) compound on top of this floor.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation for the Load Cell

**Given:**
- Range: 0 to 1000 N, Output: 0 to 10 mV → $Y_{FS} = 10$ mV.
- 8 readings at 500 N: 5.05, 4.98, 5.02, 4.96, 5.04, 5.00, 4.99, 5.03 mV.

**Step 1 — Identify extremes:**

$$
Y_{max} = 5.05 \text{ mV}, \quad Y_{min} = 4.96 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Range:**

$$
\Delta Y = 5.05 - 4.96 = 0.09 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Repeatability error as % of FSO:**

$$
e_R = \frac{0.09}{10} \times 100\% = 0.90\% \text{ of FSO}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mean:**

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{5.05 + 4.98 + 5.02 + 4.96 + 5.04 + 5.00 + 4.99 + 5.03}{8} = \frac{40.07}{8} = 5.00875 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute squared deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5.05 - 5.00875)^2 &= (0.04125)^2 = 0.00170156 \\
(4.98 - 5.00875)^2 &= (-0.02875)^2 = 0.00082656 \\
(5.02 - 5.00875)^2 &= (0.01125)^2 = 0.00012656 \\
(4.96 - 5.00875)^2 &= (-0.04875)^2 = 0.00237656 \\
(5.04 - 5.00875)^2 &= (0.03125)^2 = 0.00097656 \\
(5.00 - 5.00875)^2 &= (-0.00875)^2 = 0.00007656 \\
(4.99 - 5.00875)^2 &= (-0.01875)^2 = 0.00035156 \\
(5.03 - 5.00875)^2 &= (0.02125)^2 = 0.00045156 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = 0.00688748
$$

**Step 7 — Sample standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.00688748}{8 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.000984} = 0.03137 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 8 — $\pm 2\sigma$ repeatability:**

$$
R = \pm 2 \times 0.03137 = \pm 0.0627 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert to % of FSO:**

$$
R = \frac{\pm 0.0627}{10} \times 100\% = \pm 0.627\% \text{ of FSO}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Repeatability (range method) = **0.90% of FSO**.
- Repeatability ($\pm 2\sigma$) = **± 0.0627 mV = ± 0.627% of FSO**.

> The range-based value (0.90%) is **larger** than the $\pm 2\sigma$ value (0.627%), confirming that the range is a more conservative (worst-case) estimator compared to the statistical spread.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Students confuse **repeatability** with **reproducibility**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for every missing condition. Memorize the 6 conditions (instrument, observer, method, location, environment, time).
> 2. **Formula trap:** Always check whether the problem gives **FSO** or only the **test input**. If FSO is given, use % of FSO. If FSO is not given, use % of mean reading. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Statistical formula trap:** KTU problems using standard deviation often omit the multiplier $k$. If the problem says "95% confidence," use $k = 2$; if "99.7% confidence," use $k = 3$. If unspecified, **default to $k = 2$**.
> 4. **Units trap:** Repeatability error is *dimensionless* (a ratio) and is reported in **%**. Do not write units of mV, V, or bar next to it.
> 5. **Bessel's correction trap:** Use $N - 1$ in the denominator for *sample* standard deviation, not $N$. Using $N$ gives a *biased* estimator and KTU will deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Repeatability**

- **Definition:** Repeatability = *same input, same conditions, same instrument, short time → same output*. Memorize verbatim for 3-mark questions.
- **Six Conditions of Repeatability Test:** Same instrument, same observer, same method, same location, same environment, short time interval.
- **Repeatability vs Reproducibility:** Same conditions = Repeatability. Any one condition changed = Reproducibility.
- **Primary Formula:** $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ → use when $N \leq 5$.
- **Statistical Formula:** $R = \pm 2\sigma$ (95.4% confidence) or $R = \pm 3\sigma$ (99.7% confidence) → use when $N \geq 10$.
- **Alternative Normalization:** $e_R = \dfrac{Y_{max} - Y_{min}}{\bar{Y}} \times 100\%$ → use when FSO is not defined.
- **Standard Deviation Formula:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}$ → remember **Bessel's correction** ($N-1$, not $N$).
- **Units:** Repeatability error is dimensionless and expressed in **% of FSO** or **% of reading**.
- **Typical Good Values:** Industrial sensors ± 0.1% to ± 0.5% of FSO; laboratory-grade ± 0.01% or better.
- **Engineering Significance:** Repeatability sets the *floor of measurement uncertainty*; all other errors (accuracy, hysteresis, drift) build on top of it.
- **Never Confuse With:** Hysteresis (different input direction), Drift (long-term change at constant input), Accuracy (closeness to true value).
- **KTU Numerical Defaults:** 3 to 5 readings → use range method; 8 to 10 readings → standard deviation acceptable; always show the formula symbolically before substituting numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dead Band
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dead Band

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of sensors, transducers, and actuators, the **Dead Band** (also referred to as **Dead Zone** or **Dead Space**) is defined as the specific range of input signal values over which the output remains unresponsive, constant, or fails to register any measurable change, despite variations in the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Dead Band is a *static characteristic* of a measurement or actuation system representing a deliberate or inherent insensitivity range. It is the measurable threshold gap between the *minimum input that causes a perceptible output change* and the *zero input reference*.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\text{Dead Band} = \vert I_{\text{lower threshold}} - I_{\text{upper threshold}} \vert
$$

Where the system output $Y(t) = 0$ (or constant) for an input range $I_{\text{lower}} \leq X(t) \leq I_{\text{upper}}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **window with a stiff latch**. You have to push the window with a minimum force of, say, 2 Newtons before it budges. Any force between 0 and 2 N produces *zero visible movement*. That invisible "0 to 2 N" range is the **Dead Band** of the window-latch system.

Now, in a **thermometer**, suppose the mercury level refuses to shift unless the temperature changes by at least 0.5 °C. That 0.5 °C "ignored" interval at the bottom of the scale is the Dead Band. The instrument is technically functional — it is just *unresponsive* within that small input window.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Dead Band From Other Static Errors

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often test whether students can differentiate between **Dead Band**, **Hysteresis**, **Threshold**, and **Resolution**. Memorize the comparison table below.

| Static Characteristic | Definition | Symmetry |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead Band** | A *range* of input values producing *no output* | Usually symmetric about zero |
| **Threshold** | The *minimum* input magnitude to elicit any output | A single value, not a range |
| **Hysteresis** | Output depends on input *direction* (history) | Direction-dependent |
| **Resolution** | Smallest *detectable* change in input | Quantization-step based |
| **Backlash** | Mechanical gap between gear teeth | Mechanical looseness |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Dead Band and Threshold are *not* the same. Threshold is a *point*; Dead Band is a *region* or *range*. A threshold exists at *one* end of the input scale; dead band may straddle zero (e.g., $\pm 0.2$ mA).

## 1.4 Real-World Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Industrial Standard Threshold Limit (ISA-37.1):** Dead band is typically expressed as a **percentage of Full Scale Output (FSO)** — usually **0.1% to 5% FSO** for industrial-grade transducers.
- **Hydraulic Actuator Norm (ISO 10770):** Dead band is bounded to a maximum of **2% of rated stroke**.
- **Piezoelectric Sensor Noise Floor:** Dead band can be as low as **0.05% FSO** in precision MEMS accelerometers.
- **Pneumatic Control Valves (IEC 60534):** Typical inherent dead band ranges from **0.5% to 2%** of valve travel.
- **Stepper Motor Holding Tolerance:** Mechanical dead band (backlash) commonly **0.05° to 0.2°** for NEMA 17/23.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Why Dead Band Exists

A measurement or actuation system exhibits Dead Band due to a combination of physical, electrical, and design-driven reasons:

1. **Static Friction (Stiction):** Internal mechanical friction in pivots, bearings, diaphragms, and gear trains must be overcome before motion initiates. Energy below this friction threshold is dissipated as heat, producing no useful output.
2. **Electrical Threshold Voltages:** Active electronic components (diodes, transistors, op-amps) have intrinsic cut-in voltages (e.g., a silicon diode needs ~**0.7 V** before conduction). Signals below this level are ignored.
3. **Quantization in Digital Systems:** Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs) round input signals to discrete steps. A 12-bit ADC with a 5 V reference has a step size of $\frac{5}{2^{12}} \approx \mathbf{1.22 \text{ mV}}$ — anything below this step is invisible.
4. **Mechanical Backlash:** Loose gear meshing, slack chain drives, and play in lead screws create zones of "free movement" producing no output.
5. **Deliberate Design Dead Band:** Control systems often *intentionally* introduce dead band to suppress noise, prevent chattering, or reduce wear (e.g., thermostat hysteresis buffer).
6. **Magnetic / Dielectric Hysteresis Loss:** In magnetic and ferroelectric materials, residual magnetism/dielectricity resists small reverse signals.
7. **Signal Conditioning Filtering:** Low-pass or notch filters at the input may attenuate small signals below the noise floor.

## 2.2 Transfer Function of a System with Dead Band

For a linear system with input $x(t)$ and output $y(t)$ having a symmetric dead band of width $2d$:

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} K \cdot \big( x(t) - d \big) & \text{if } x(t) > +d \\ 0 & \text{if } -d \leq x(t) \leq +d \\ K \cdot \big( x(t) + d \big) & \text{if } x(t) < -d \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $K$ is the system gain (sensitivity) outside the dead band.
- $d$ is the half-width of the dead band (i.e., dead band total width $= 2d$).
- The flat region from $-d$ to $+d$ is the **insensitive zone**.

## 2.3 Graphical Representation

The dead band appears as a *horizontal flat segment* of width $2d$ centered on the input axis, with the output held at zero. The two flanking linear segments have slope $K$ but are *offset* by the dead band — this is a **non-linearity** even for a system that is otherwise linear.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your one-stop revision for all Dead Band formulas. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dead Band Width | $\text{DB} = x_{\text{upper}} - x_{\text{lower}}$ | Same as input | Total input range producing no output |
| 2 | Symmetric Half-Width | $d = \frac{\text{DB}}{2}$ | Same as input | Distance from zero to either threshold |
| 3 | Dead Band in % FSO | $\%\text{DB} = \frac{\text{DB}}{x_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Industry-standard normalization |
| 4 | Effective Sensitivity | $K_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \quad \text{outside DB}$ | Output / Input | Slope of linear region |
| 5 | Apparent Gain with DB | $K_{\text{apparent}} = \frac{y_{\text{FS}}}{\text{FS} - \text{DB}}$ | Output / Input | Reduced effective gain |
| 6 | SNR Penalty | $\text{SNR}_{\text{loss}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\big(1 - \tfrac{\text{DB}}{x_{\text{FS}}}\big)$ | dB | Signal-to-noise degradation |
| 7 | Dead Time (related) | $t_d = \frac{\text{DB}}{\dot{x}}$ | seconds | Time delay for ramped input |
| 8 | ADC Quantization (dead band lower bound) | $q = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}$ | Volts | Minimum theoretical DB for ideal ADC |
| 9 | Combined Hysteresis + DB | $H_{\text{total}} = \text{DB} + \text{Hysteresis width}$ | Same as input | When loop includes both |
| 10 | Static Error due to DB | $e_{\text{static}} = \frac{\text{DB}}{2}$ (avg) | Same as input | Mean unresponsive offset |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Applications

- **Control Valves:** Dead band prevents valve hunting around setpoint due to noise — a built-in stabilization feature.
- **Robotics Servo Motors:** Dead band compensation routines are coded into the controller to eliminate "slack" at small motion commands.
- **Precision Weighing Scales:** Auto-zeroing algorithms compensate for dead band at the bottom of the load range.
- **Biomedical Pacemakers:** A *small intentional* dead band prevents pacing on noise spikes.
- **Automotive Throttle-by-Wire:** Dead band is tuned to avoid engine response on pedal vibration.
- **Industrial Pressure Transmitters:** HART protocol uses dead band tuning to optimize update rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In control theory, an *intentional* dead band is called a **Threshold Element** or **Relay with Dead Zone**, and it is widely used in non-linear control strategies (sliding mode, bang-bang with hysteresis, etc.).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Linear Gain of a System with Dead Band

**Problem Setup:** A linear measurement system has sensitivity $K = 10$ mV/V over a full-scale input range of $0$ to $x_{\text{FS}} = 50$ V. Due to internal friction, the system exhibits a dead band from $x = 1.5$ V to $x = 2.5$ V (i.e., the output is zero for inputs in this range).

**Goal:** Derive the apparent (effective) gain of the system over its full operating range, accounting for the dead band.

**Step 1 — Identify the dead band boundaries.**

The lower threshold is $x_L = 1.5$ V and the upper threshold is $x_U = 2.5$ V.

Therefore, the dead band width is:

$$
\text{DB} = x_U - x_L = 2.5 - 1.5 = 1.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the half-width (symmetric assumption).**

For a symmetric dead band centered at $x_c$:

$$
x_c = \frac{x_L + x_U}{2} = \frac{1.5 + 2.5}{2} = 2.0 \text{ V}
$$

$$
d = \frac{\text{DB}}{2} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the transfer characteristic.**

For $x < 1.5$ V, the output is zero:

$$
y(x) = 0, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1.5
$$

For $1.5 \leq x \leq 2.5$ V, the output is held at zero (dead band plateau):

$$
y(x) = 0, \quad 1.5 \leq x \leq 2.5
$$

For $x > 2.5$ V, the system resumes its linear response with sensitivity $K = 10$ mV/V:

$$
y(x) = K \cdot (x - x_c) = 10 \cdot (x - 2.0) \text{ mV}, \quad x > 2.5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the full-scale output (FSO).**

At $x = x_{\text{FS}} = 50$ V:

$$
y_{\text{FS}} = 10 \cdot (50 - 2.0) = 10 \cdot 48 = 480 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the apparent (effective) gain.**

$$
K_{\text{apparent}} = \frac{y_{\text{FS}}}{x_{\text{FS}}} = \frac{480 \text{ mV}}{50 \text{ V}} = 9.6 \text{ mV/V}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the gain reduction factor.**

$$
\text{Gain Loss} = K - K_{\text{apparent}} = 10 - 9.6 = 0.4 \text{ mV/V}
$$

$$
\text{Gain Loss \%} = \frac{0.4}{10} \times 100 = 4\%
$$

**Step 7 — Express dead band as a percentage of full scale.**

$$
\%\text{DB} = \frac{\text{DB}}{x_{\text{FS}}} \times 100 = \frac{1.0}{50} \times 100 = 2\% \text{ FSO}
$$

**Conclusion:** Although the nominal sensitivity is 10 mV/V, the effective sensitivity over the full scale is reduced to 9.6 mV/V due to the 2% FSO dead band. This is a significant source of *static error* in low-input measurements.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dead Band Compensation via Inverse Mapping

**Problem Setup:** A pressure transducer has nominal sensitivity $K = 0.5$ V/kPa and exhibits a dead band of $\pm 0.4$ kPa about zero input. The output is sampled as $y_{\text{meas}}$.

**Goal:** Derive the compensation equation that recovers the true input $x_{\text{true}}$ from the measured output $y_{\text{meas}}$.

**Step 1 — Write the raw transfer equation with dead band.**

Without compensation, the inverse (input recovery) is:

$$
x_{\text{raw}} = \frac{y_{\text{meas}}}{K} = \frac{y_{\text{meas}}}{0.5}
$$

This formula fails in the dead band region ($-0.4 \leq x \leq +0.4$ kPa) because $y_{\text{meas}} = 0$ there.

**Step 2 — Apply a piecewise compensation function.**

Define a corrected input estimator:

$$
x_{\text{true}} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{y_{\text{meas}}}{K} + d, & y_{\text{meas}} > 0 \\[8pt] 0, & y_{\text{meas}} = 0 \quad (\text{within DB}) \\[8pt] \dfrac{y_{\text{meas}}}{K} - d, & y_{\text{meas}} < 0 \end{cases}
$$

Where $d = 0.4$ kPa is the half-width of the dead band.

**Step 3 — Verify the compensation at a known input.**

Suppose true input is $x_{\text{true}} = 0.6$ kPa. The output is:

$$
y_{\text{meas}} = K \cdot (x_{\text{true}} - d) = 0.5 \cdot (0.6 - 0.4) = 0.1 \text{ V}
$$

Now invert:

$$
x_{\text{recovered}} = \frac{0.1}{0.5} + 0.4 = 0.2 + 0.4 = 0.6 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Verify inside the dead band.**

Suppose $x_{\text{true}} = 0.2$ kPa (inside the $\pm 0.4$ kPa dead band):

$$
y_{\text{meas}} = 0 \text{ V}
$$

The compensation yields $x_{\text{recovered}} = 0$ kPa, which is a *clamping artifact* — true value cannot be recovered because no information is available inside the dead band. This is a **fundamental information-theoretic limit**.

**Conclusion:** Dead band compensation can correct the *offset* in the linear regions, but cannot recover information lost inside the dead band itself. This is why KTU emphasizes **minimizing** dead band during design rather than compensating for it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dead Band Induced Time Delay for a Ramp Input

**Problem Setup:** A temperature sensor has a dead band of $0.5$ °C. The ambient temperature is rising at $\dot{x} = 0.1$ °C/min.

**Goal:** Find the time delay introduced by the dead band.

**Step 1 — Recognize that the output is delayed by an amount equal to the input change needed to "cross" the dead band.**

$$
\Delta x_{\text{required}} = \text{DB} = 0.5 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time delay.**

$$
t_d = \frac{\Delta x_{\text{required}}}{\dot{x}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ °C}}{0.1 \text{ °C/min}} = 5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

The sensor output will lag the true temperature by 5 minutes. This delay contributes to the system's *transportation lag* and is critical in **process control loop tuning**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Numerical Problems

| Parameter | Symbol | Numerical Value (Sample) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full-scale input | $x_{\text{FS}}$ | 100 | V |
| Lower dead band threshold | $x_L$ | 1.2 | V |
| Upper dead band threshold | $x_U$ | 1.8 | V |
| Dead band width | DB | 0.6 | V |
| Dead band % FSO | %DB | 0.6 | % |
| Nominal sensitivity | $K$ | 0.05 | A/V |
| Output at FS | $y_{\text{FS}}$ | 4.91 | A |
| Effective gain | $K_{\text{eff}}$ | 0.0491 | A/V |
| SNR loss | $\Delta$SNR | −0.052 | dB |
| Ramp rate | $\dot{x}$ | 2 | V/s |
| Dead band time delay | $t_d$ | 0.30 | s |
