- **Term 3 ($\frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$)**: The **piezoresistive intrinsic term**. This is **negligible for metals** but **dominant for semiconductors** (can be 100+).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl (KTU 2024):** For a metal strain gauge, $G \approx 1 + 2\nu$ because the resistivity change is nearly zero. For a silicon piezoresistor, $G \approx \pi_l \cdot E$ where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $E$ is Young's modulus.

## 2.3 Piezoresistive Coefficients in Silicon (Anisotropic Crystal)

Silicon has a **cubic crystal structure** with a non-zero piezoresistive coefficient tensor. The two most important coefficients are:

- **$\pi_{11}$**: Coefficient along the principal cubic axis.
- **$\pi_{12}$**: Coefficient in the transverse direction.
- **$\pi_{44}$**: Shear piezoresistive coefficient (most important for p-type Si).

For a resistor oriented along direction defined by direction cosines $(l_1, l_2, l_3)$ relative to crystal axes, the effective longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient is:

$$\pi_l = \pi_{11} + 2(\pi_{44} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{11})(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)$$

For a resistor on the (100) plane along $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction in p-type Si, the **maximum piezoresistive coefficient** is achieved:

$$\pi_l = \frac{1}{2}(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Values (Memorize for KTU):**
> - p-type Si: $\pi_{44} \approx 138.1 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (largest in the tensor)
> - n-type Si: $\pi_{11} \approx -102.2 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (dominant along $\langle 100 \rangle$)

## 2.4 The Wheatstone Bridge — Signal Conditioning

Piezoresistive sensors produce a **tiny fractional change in resistance** (typically $\Delta R / R \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$). A single resistor cannot easily be measured. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts the small $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential voltage.

### Quarter-Bridge Configuration

A single active piezoresistor $R_1$ is placed in one arm of a bridge with three fixed resistors. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right)$$

For a balanced bridge where initially $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, and $R_1$ changes by $\Delta R$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}$$

### Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge Configurations

| Bridge Type | Active Resistors | Sensitivity Multiplier | Output Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quarter | 1 | 1× | $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ |
| Half | 2 (one in tension, one in compression) | 2× | $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ |
| Full | 4 (two in tension, two in compression) | 4× | $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The full-bridge configuration is the standard in commercial pressure sensors and accelerometers because it **doubles the signal, cancels common-mode temperature errors, and rejects power supply noise**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 EXAM**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law for Resistor | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance in Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | Strain Definition | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Stress Definition | $\sigma = F / A$ | Pressure units (Pa) |
| 4 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | E = Young's Modulus (Pa) |
| 5 | Gauge Factor (General) | $G = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Gauge Factor (Decomposed) | $G = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ | Sum of geometric + intrinsic |
| 7 | Piezoresistor Output (Si) | $G \approx \pi_l \cdot E$ | Approximation for Si |
| 8 | Resistance Change | $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Linearized response |
| 9 | Quarter-Bridge Output | $V_{out} = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Excitation $V_{ex}$ in Volts |
| 10 | Full-Bridge Output | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Maximum sensitivity |
| 11 | Piezoresistive Tensor Relation | $\Delta \rho_i / \rho = \pi_{ij} \sigma_j$ | Tensor form |
| 12 | Longitudinal $\pi_l$ (Si) | $\pi_l = \pi_{11} + 2(\pi_{44} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{11})(l_1^2 l_2^2 + \ldots)$ | Cubic crystal |
| 13 | Transverse $\pi_t$ (Si) | $\pi_t = \pi_{12} - (\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{44})(l_1^2 m_1^2 + \ldots)$ | Perpendicular to current |
| 14 | TC of Gauge Factor | $G(T) = G_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | Temperature compensation |
| 15 | Pressure–Strain Relation (Diaphragm) | $\varepsilon = \frac{3P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{8 E h^2}$ | For circular diaphragm |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, the absolute value symbol has been written as `\vert` style for safety (e.g., $\vert \Delta R \vert / R$). All bracketed quantities are standard.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensors, TPMS (Tire Pressure Monitoring), airbag crash sensors.
- **Medical:** Invasive blood pressure catheters, intracranial pressure sensors, infusion pumps.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone barometers, fitness tracker altimeters, MEMS microphones (with diaphragm).
- **Industrial:** HVAC pressure transmitters, flow meters, level sensors in tanks.
- **Aerospace:** Altimeters, flight control surface strain monitoring, rocket engine chamber pressure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gauge Factor from First Principles

### Goal
Derive the expression $G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ starting from $R = \rho L / A$.

### Step 1 — Initial Resistance

A cylindrical resistor of initial length $L_0$, cross-sectional area $A_0$, and resistivity $\rho_0$ has:

$$R_0 = \rho_0 \frac{L_0}{A_0}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Axial Stress
When a tensile force $F$ is applied along the length, the resistor experiences a longitudinal strain $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_1 = L_0(1 + \varepsilon)$$

### Step 3 — Lateral Contraction (Poisson Effect)
The cross-sectional area reduces. The lateral strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$ in each transverse direction:

$$A_1 = A_0 (1 - \nu \varepsilon)(1 - \nu \varepsilon) = A_0 (1 - \nu \varepsilon)^2$$

### Step 4 — Resistivity Change (Piezoresistive Effect)
The resistivity also changes due to the piezoresistive effect:

$$\rho_1 = \rho_0 (1 + \Delta \rho / \rho)$$

### Step 5 — Final Resistance

$$R_1 = \rho_1 \frac{L_1}{A_1} = \rho_0 \left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right) \cdot \frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon)}{A_0 (1 - \nu \varepsilon)^2}$$

### Step 6 — Form the Ratio

$$\frac{R_1}{R_0} = \left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right) \cdot \frac{(1 + \varepsilon)}{(1 - \nu \varepsilon)^2}$$

### Step 7 — Small-Strain Approximation
For $\varepsilon \ll 1$ (the standard operating regime of piezoresistive sensors, typically $\varepsilon < 10^{-3}$), we expand $(1 - \nu \varepsilon)^{-2} \approx 1 + 2\nu \varepsilon$ using the binomial series $(1 - x)^{-n} \approx 1 + nx$ for small $x$:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_0} = \left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right)(1 + \varepsilon)(1 + 2\nu \varepsilon)$$

### Step 8 — Neglect Higher-Order Terms
Multiplying out and ignoring $\varepsilon^2$ terms (since $\varepsilon^2 \sim 10^{-6}$ is much smaller than $\varepsilon \sim 10^{-3}$):

$$\frac{R_1}{R_0} \approx 1 + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

### Step 9 — Isolate $\Delta R / R$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = \frac{R_1 - R_0}{R_0} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

### Step 10 — Define Gauge Factor

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\Delta R / R_0}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

> **Validity Note:** This derivation is valid for **small strains** ($\varepsilon \ll 1$) and **isotropic materials**. For anisotropic single-crystal silicon, the term $2\nu$ is replaced by the full piezoresistive tensor contribution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheatstone Bridge Quarter-Bridge Output

### Goal
Show that $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4)(\Delta R / R)$ for a quarter-bridge.

### Step 1 — Bridge Definition
Four resistors in a diamond. Excitation $V_{ex}$ across the top-bottom diagonal. Output $V_{out}$ across the left-right diagonal:

$$V_{out} = V_{left} - V_{right}$$

### Step 2 — Voltage Divider (Left Leg)
$R_1$ and $R_2$ form the left voltage divider. The left node voltage (referenced to ground at bottom) is:

$$V_{left} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 3 — Voltage Divider (Right Leg)
$R_3$ and $R_4$ form the right voltage divider:

$$V_{right} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

### Step 4 — Differential Output

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Balanced Condition
Initially $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, so $V_{out} = 0$. Now only $R_1$ changes to $R_1 = R + \Delta R$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R}{R + \Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{2R}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Common Denominator

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2R - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

### Step 7 — Small-Signal Approximation
Since $\Delta R \ll R$, we have $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$:

$$V_{out} \approx -V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

### Step 8 — Magnitude of Output

$$\boxed{\vert V_{out} \vert = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

> **Note:** The negative sign simply indicates the polarity (which side of the bridge is at higher potential). For full-bridge, the same procedure with 4 active resistors gives $\vert V_{out} \vert = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piezoresistor Sensitivity to Pressure (Diaphragm Sensor)

### Goal
Derive the strain at the edge of a circular diaphragm under uniform pressure $P$, and from that, the resistance change of a piezoresistor placed at the diaphragm edge.

### Step 1 — Maximum Strain Formula (From Plate Theory)
For a thin circular diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $h$, clamped at the edge, under uniform pressure $P$, the **maximum radial strain** at the diaphragm center is:

$$\varepsilon_r^{max} = \frac{3P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{8 E h^2}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum Tangential Strain (At the Edge)
The maximum **tangential strain** at the clamped edge (where piezoresistors are typically placed) is:

$$\varepsilon_t^{max} = -\frac{3P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{4 E h^2}$$

### Step 3 — Piezoresistor Change
Place a piezoresistor aligned with the tangential direction at the edge:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon_t^{max} = -G \cdot \frac{3P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{4 E h^2}$$

### Step 4 — Bridge Output (Full-Bridge)
With full-bridge configuration, the output is:

$$\boxed{\vert V_{out} \vert = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \frac{3P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{4 E h^2}}$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity
Sensitivity (output per unit pressure):

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dP} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \frac{3 a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{4 E h^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sensitivity scales as $a^2 / h^2$. To increase sensitivity, designers either **increase the diaphragm radius** or — more commonly — **decrease the diaphragm thickness** (which is why pressure sensor dies are often back-etched to leave a thin silicon diaphragm).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Metal vs. Semiconductor Piezoresistors

| Parameter | Metal Strain Gauge (Constantan) | Semiconductor (Silicon) |
|---|---|---|
| **Dominant Mechanism** | Geometric ($1 + 2\nu$) | Piezoresistive ($\pi_l E$) |
| **Gauge Factor $G$** | 2.0 – 5.0 | 50 – 200 |
| **Sensitivity to Strain** | Low | Very High |
| **Temperature Coefficient of $G$** | Low, well-compensated (e.g., constantan, Karma alloy) | High, requires compensation |
| **Resistance Value** | Low (120 $\Omega$, 350 $\Omega$, 1 k$\Omega$ standard) | High (1 – 10 k$\Omega$ typical) |
| **Linearity** | Excellent (≤ 0.1% error) | Moderate (non-linearity up to 1%) |
| **Frequency Response** | Excellent (DC to MHz) | Excellent (DC to MHz) |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | Small but present |
| **Integration with ICs** | Difficult (discrete) | Excellent (monolithic with CMOS) |
| **Cost** | Low | Low at high volume (batch fabricated) |
| **Typical Use** | Load cells, structural health monitoring | MEMS pressure sensors, accelerometers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Piezoresistive Sensor Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Input Force F or Pressure P] --> B[Elastic Diaphragm or Cantilever Beam]
    B --> C[Strain Field epsilon x and epsilon y at Strategic Locations]
    C --> D[Piezoresistor R1 placed at Max Strain Region]
    C --> E[Piezoresistor R2 placed at Max Strain Region]
    C --> F[Piezoresistor R3 placed at Min Strain Region]
    C --> G[Piezoresistor R4 placed at Min Strain Region]
    D --> H[Wheatstone Bridge Network]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Differential Output Voltage Vout]
    I --> J[Instrumentation Amplifier with Gain A]
    J --> K[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    K --> L[Digital Microcontroller or Signal Processor]
    L --> M[Display Calibration and Output in Engineering Units]
    M --> N[Final Reading e.g. kPa or Newton]
    H --> O[Temperature Compensation Network]
    O --> J
    P[Excitation Voltage Source Vex] --> H
    P --> O
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **mechanical input** deflects the diaphragm, creating a **strain field**.
- Piezoresistors strategically placed at high-strain regions change their resistance.
- A **Wheatstone bridge** converts resistance imbalance into a differential voltage.
- The signal is **amplified**, **digitized**, and **calibrated** into a final engineering reading.
- A **temperature compensation network** corrects for thermal drift in the piezoresistors.

---

## 4.2 Piezoresistor Placement on a Square Diaphragm (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Diaphragm [Square Silicon Diaphragm Top View]
        direction TB
        R1["R1 Longitudinal at Edge East West"] --- R2["R2 Transverse at Edge North South"]
        R3["R3 Longitudinal at Edge West East"] --- R4["R4 Transverse at Edge South North"]
    end
    Diaphragm --> Bond[Wire Bonds to Package]
    Bond --> ASIC[CMOS Readout ASIC]
    ASIC --> Output[Differential Analog Output Vout plus minus]
```

**Explanation:** Piezoresistors R1 and R3 are oriented **longitudinally** (parallel to the principal stress direction), while R2 and R4 are **transversely** oriented. The bridge is wired such that as the diaphragm deflects under pressure, R1 and R3 increase in resistance while R2 and R4 decrease (or vice versa), maximizing the differential output.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Piezoresistive Pressure Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Process Pressure P] --> S1[Isolation Diaphragm and Silicone Oil Fill]
    S1 --> S2[Sensing Diaphragm with Four Piezoresistors]
    S2 --> S3[Bridge Excitation Vex 5V or 10V]
    S3 --> S4[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    S4 --> S5[ADC 16 bit or 24 bit Sigma Delta]
    S5 --> S6[Microcontroller with Linearization and Temperature Compensation]
    S6 --> S7[Current Loop Output 4 to 20 mA or Digital Protocol HART]
    S7 --> P2[Control Room DCS or PLC]
    S8[RTD or On Chip Temperature Sensor] --> S6
    S9[EEPROM Calibration Coefficients] --> S6
```

**Engineering Use Case:** This is the standard architecture of an **industrial pressure transmitter** used in oil refineries, chemical plants, and power stations. The piezoresistive die is mechanically isolated from the process fluid by a stainless steel isolation diaphragm, and the signal is digitized and linearized before transmission over a 4–20 mA current loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Gauge Factor. Why is it high in semiconductors? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The Gauge Factor ($G$) of a piezoresistive sensor is defined as the ratio of the **fractional change in electrical resistance** ($\Delta R / R$) to the **applied mechanical strain** ($\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$):

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity** and is the most important figure of merit for a strain-sensitive resistor.

**Physical Origin (2 Marks):** The gauge factor is expressed as:

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

- In **metals**, the first two geometric terms dominate ($1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6$ to $2.0$), and $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is negligible because the free electron gas is largely unaffected by small lattice distortions.
- In **semiconductors** like silicon, the term $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is very large because applied stress **deforms the conduction and valence band structure**, redistributing carriers among valleys and changing mobility. For p-type Si along $\langle 110 \rangle$, the effective gauge factor reaches **100 to 200**, which is **50–100× higher** than metallic gauges. This is why silicon piezoresistors are preferred in high-sensitivity MEMS sensors.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between the piezoresistive effect and the piezoelectric effect. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Piezoresistive Effect | Piezoelectric Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Quantity** (1.5 Marks) | Change in **electrical resistance** $R$ | Generation of **voltage / charge** $V$ or $Q$ |
| **Material Type** (1.5 Marks) | Conductors and semiconductors (Si, Ge, polysilicon, metals) | Non-centrosymmetric dielectric crystals (Quartz, PZT, BaTiO₃, PVDF) |
| **DC / Static Measurement** | Possible (DC strain → DC $\Delta R$) | Not possible (requires time-varying input) |
| **Active / Passive** | Passive — needs external excitation | Active — self-generating |
| **Typical Application** | Pressure sensors, accelerometers, strain gauges | Microphones, ultrasonic transducers, spark igniters |

> **One-line examiner's key:** *Piezoresistive = change in R; Piezoelectric = generation of V.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### CHOICE A: Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Derive an expression for the gauge factor of a piezoresistive sensor. Show that $G = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial Resistance (1 Mark):** A resistor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ has:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Strain (1 Mark):** Under axial tensile force, the length increases by $\Delta L$:

$$L' = L(1 + \varepsilon), \quad \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Area Reduction by Poisson Effect (1 Mark):** Lateral strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$:

$$A' = A(1 - \nu \varepsilon)^2 \approx A(1 - 2\nu \varepsilon)$$

**Step 4 — Resistivity Change (1 Mark):** Intrinsic piezoresistive effect changes the resistivity:

$$\rho' = \rho\left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final Resistance (1 Mark):**

$$R' = \rho' \frac{L'}{A'} = \rho\left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right) \cdot \frac{L(1+\varepsilon)}{A(1-2\nu\varepsilon)}$$

**Step 6 — Form Ratio and Apply Small-Strain Approximation (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{R'}{R} = \left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right)(1 + \varepsilon)(1 + 2\nu \varepsilon) \approx 1 + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 7 — Gauge Factor Definition (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

---

#### Part (b): With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Wheatstone bridge with one active piezoresistor. Derive the expression for output voltage. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bridge Description (2 Marks):**

```
              Vex (+)
                |
       +-------+-------+
       |               |
      R1              R3
       |               |
   Vleft ----- Vout -+----- Vright
       |               |
      R2              R4
       |               |
       +-------+-------+
                |
              Vex (-)
```

The bridge has four arms: $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$. $R_1$ is the **active piezoresistor**; the rest are fixed. Excitation $V_{ex}$ is applied across the top-bottom diagonal.

**Step 2 — Balanced Condition (1 Mark):** Initially $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, the bridge is balanced, $V_{out} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Apply Strain → $R_1$ Changes to $R + \Delta R$ (1 Mark):** Using voltage divider on each leg:

$$V_{left} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_{right} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Output Voltage (1 Mark):**

$$V_{out} = V_{left} - V_{right} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 5 — Small-Signal Approximation ($\Delta R \ll R$) (1 Mark):** $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

**Step 6 — In Terms of Strain (1 Mark):** Substituting $\Delta R / R = G \varepsilon$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

> **Conclusion:** A quarter-bridge converts a small strain into a measurable differential voltage linearly proportional to strain. By using a full-bridge (4 active resistors), the sensitivity is **quadrupled** to $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$.

---

### CHOICE B: Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain the working principle of a piezoresistive pressure sensor with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of Wheatstone bridge configuration in improving sensitivity. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sensor Construction (2 Marks):**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of:
- A **thin silicon diaphragm** (typically 10–100 µm thick) fabricated by anisotropic etching of a silicon wafer.
- **Four piezoresistors** diffused or implanted into the diaphragm surface at locations of maximum strain (typically at the diaphragm edges).
- The piezoresistors are **interconnected in a Wheatstone bridge**.
- The assembly is mounted in a package with a pressure port.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- When external pressure $P$ is applied to the diaphragm, it deflects.
- The deflection creates **tensile and compressive strains** at strategic locations on the diaphragm.
- Piezoresistors placed at the **edge of the diaphragm** (where strain is maximum) experience large strain.
- Their resistance changes according to $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$.
- The bridge converts this resistance imbalance into a differential output voltage.

**Step 3 — Strain in Diaphragm (1 Mark):** From plate theory, the tangential strain at the clamped edge of a circular diaphragm is:

$$\varepsilon_t = \frac{3 P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{4 E h^2}$$

**Step 4 — Output Voltage with Full-Bridge (1 Mark):** With all four resistors active (two in tension, two in compression):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon_t = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \frac{3 P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{4 E h^2}$$

**Step 5 — Bridge Advantages (1 Mark):**
- **Sensitivity:** Full-bridge gives 4× the output of a quarter-bridge.
- **Temperature Compensation:** Common-mode resistance changes (e.g., from temperature) are rejected.
- **Linearity:** Differential architecture cancels even-order nonlinearities.
- **Noise Rejection:** Power supply noise and EMI common to both legs are rejected.

---

#### Part (b): A silicon piezoresistor has a gauge factor of 120 and a nominal resistance of 1 kΩ. It is used in a quarter-bridge with an excitation of 5 V. If the applied strain is 500 µε, calculate (i) the change in resistance, and (ii) the output voltage. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Gauge Factor, $G = 120$
- Nominal resistance, $R = 1 \, \text{k}\Omega = 1000 \, \Omega$
- Excitation voltage, $V_{ex} = 5 \, \text{V}$
- Applied strain, $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$

**Part (i) — Change in Resistance (3 Marks):**

Using the gauge factor definition:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 120 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.06 = 6\%$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta R = 0.06 \times 1000 = \boxed{60 \, \Omega}$$

**Part (ii) — Output Voltage (3 Marks):**

For a quarter-bridge configuration:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{5}{4} \times 120 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times 0.06 = \boxed{0.075 \, \text{V} = 75 \, \text{mV}}$$

**Alternative — Direct Substitution:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{5}{4} \times 0.06 = 0.075 \, \text{V} = 75 \, \text{mV}$$

> **Note:** 75 mV is a small but easily measurable signal using a precision instrumentation amplifier. If a full-bridge were used, the output would quadruple to **300 mV**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing the two effects:** Students frequently write "piezoelectric" when they mean "piezoresistive" in long answers — this is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the small-strain approximation:** The gauge factor formula $G = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ is valid **only for small strains** ($\varepsilon \ll 1$). Stating it without this caveat costs a mark.
> 3. **Sign errors in the bridge:** $V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ — the negative sign only indicates polarity, but students often write a wrong magnitude.
> 4. **Mixing up units:** Strain is dimensionless but is sometimes given in µε. Always convert: $500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$.
> 5. **Confusing geometric vs. piezoresistive contribution:** In a numerical problem, the examiner expects you to identify whether the material is metallic or semiconducting, and apply the correct dominant term.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels:** The bridge diagram in Part (b) must be neatly drawn with all four resistors labeled, the excitation source shown with polarity, and the output terminals marked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (KTU 2024)**

- **Piezoresistive Effect:** Change in resistivity of a material under mechanical stress. **Not to be confused with piezoelectric effect** (which generates voltage).
- **Gauge Factor Formula:** $G = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$.
- **Metals:** $G \approx 2$ (geometric term dominates, $\Delta \rho \approx 0$).
- **Semiconductors (Silicon):** $G \approx 100$ to $200$ (piezoresistive term dominates).
- **Silicon Anisotropy:** P-type Si has maximum piezoresistive coefficient along $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction; n-type along $\langle 100 \rangle$.
- **Piezoresistive Coefficient Units:** $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$. Standard values: $\pi_{44}$ (p-Si) $\approx 138 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹.
- **Wheatstone Bridge Output:**
  - Quarter: $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4)(G \varepsilon)$
  - Half: $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/2)(G \varepsilon)$
  - Full: $V_{out} = V_{ex}(G \varepsilon)$
- **Diaphragm Strain (edge):** $\varepsilon_t = \frac{3Pa^2(1-\nu^2)}{4Eh^2}$. Sensitivity scales with $a^2 / h^2$.
- **Temperature Compensation:** Piezoresistors have a large Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR ~ 10⁻³ /°C) and Temperature Coefficient of Gauge Factor (TCGF). Compensation is done using on-chip resistors, Wheatstone bridge symmetry, and digital calibration.
- **MEMS Integration:** Piezoresistors are **diffused or ion-implanted** into the silicon diaphragm and monolithically integrated with CMOS readout.
- **Static vs. Dynamic:** Unlike piezoelectric sensors, piezoresistive sensors can measure **static (DC) signals** (e.g., constant pressure, weight).
- **Top Applications (KTU-favorite):** Blood pressure transducers, MAP sensors, accelerometers, load cells, altimeters.
- **Formula to Always Memorize:** $G \cdot \varepsilon$ appears in every bridge output equation — this is the single most-tested expression.
- **Numerical Problem Pattern:** Given $G$, $R$, $V_{ex}$, $\varepsilon$ → find $\Delta R$ and $V_{out}$. Practice this combination at least 3 times before the exam.
- **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level:** Part A tests *Remembering* and *Understanding*; Part B tests *Applying* and *Analyzing* — expect derivations, numericals, and circuit analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitive Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capacitive Sensors # CAPACITIVE SENSORS – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Capacitive Sensor** is a non-contact or contact-type electromechanical transducer that operates on the fundamental principle of detecting a change in electrical capacitance caused by a variation in one or more of the following physical parameters: - The **distance (d)** between the conductive plates - The **effective overlapping area (A)** of the plates - The **dielectric permittivity (εᵣ)** of the medium separating the plates > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A capacitive sensor is a device that converts a physical measurand (displacement, pressure, level, humidity, force, etc.) into a measurable change in capacitance, which is then conditioned by an electronic circuit to produce a proportional electrical output. The underlying physical law governing the sensor is the **parallel-plate capacitor equation**: $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$ Where: - $C$ = Capacitance in **Farads (F)** - $\varepsilon_0$ = Permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (universal constant) - $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the medium (**dimensionless**) - $A$ = Effective overlapping plate area in **m²** - $d$ = Distance (gap) between plates in **m** --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The "Water Bucket" of Electricity** Imagine a **plastic bucket (capacitor)** that can hold a certain amount of water (electric charge). The amount of water it can hold depends on: 1. **How wide the bucket is** → Equivalent to **Area (A)** of the plate 2. **How shallow or deep the bucket is** → Equivalent to **Gap distance (d)** 3. **The material of the bucket** (thin plastic holds less, thick rubber holds more) → Equivalent to **Dielectric constant (εᵣ)** If you **squeeze** the plates closer (reduce $d$), the bucket becomes "shallower" and can hold MORE charge → capacitance **INCREASES**. If you insert a finger (your body has $\varepsilon_r \approx 50$, much higher than air's $\varepsilon_r = 1$) between the plates of a **touchscreen**, the capacitance changes — and the phone detects the exact point where you touched. **This is exactly how capacitive touchscreens work!** --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $\varepsilon_0$ | Vacuum Permittivity | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** | | $\varepsilon_r$ (air) | Relative Permittivity of Air | **≈ 1.0006** | | $\varepsilon_r$ (water) | Relative Permittivity of Water | **≈ 80** | | $\varepsilon_r$ (human body) | Relative Permittivity of Skin | **≈ 40–50** | | $C$ | Capacitance | Farads (F) | | Sensitivity | $\frac{dC}{dx}$ | Farads per unit measurand | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize $\varepsilon_0$ and the relative permittivity of air, water, and a few common dielectrics (glass, mica, paper, oil). These constants appear repeatedly in numerical problems. --- ## 1.4 Classification Overview Capacitive sensors are classified into **three fundamental types** based on which parameter in $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ is varied: | Type | Parameter Varied | Common Measurand | |---|---|---| | **Variable Distance (Gap)** Type | $d$ | Micro-displacement, vibration, pressure | | **Variable Area (Overlapping)** Type | $A$ | Linear/angular displacement, position | | **Variable Dielectric** Type | $\varepsilon_r$ | Liquid level, humidity, composition | # CAPACITIVE SENSORS – Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle – The "Why" and "How" A capacitive sensor functions in **three sequential stages**: 1. **Sensing Stage:** An external physical stimulus (force, displacement, humidity, etc.) changes one of the capacitor's geometric/dielectric parameters. 2. **Transduction Stage:** This geometric change produces a proportional change in capacitance $\Delta C$. 3. **Signal Conditioning Stage:** A **bridge circuit**, **oscillator**, or **charge amplifier** converts the small $\Delta C$ into a measurable voltage, current, or frequency output. > [!NOTE] > **Why capacitive sensors are preferred:** > - **High sensitivity** (especially to small displacements, down to nanometers) > - **Non-contact operation** possible (low wear, long life) > - **Low power consumption** > - **Good frequency response** (suitable for dynamic measurements) > - **Insensitive to temperature** (when differential design is used) --- ## 2.2 The Three Operating Modes ### 2.2.1 Variable Distance (Gap) Type The two plates are arranged parallel to each other, and one plate moves axially when subjected to a measurand (e.g., diaphragm deflection due to pressure). **Key relationship:** $$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d \pm \Delta d}$$ **Sensitivity:** $$S_d = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C_0}{d}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that an *increase* in gap *decreases* the capacitance (inverse relationship). The **non-linear** nature is the chief drawback. ### 2.2.2 Variable Area (Overlapping) Type One plate slides laterally over the other, changing the effective overlap area while keeping the gap constant. **Key relationship:** $$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A_0 \pm \Delta A)}{d}$$ **Sensitivity:** $$S_A = \frac{dC}{dA} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d} = \text{constant}$$ The sensitivity is **constant** → gives a **linear** output. This is the **most preferred mode** in KTU problems. ### 2.2.3 Variable Dielectric Type A dielectric material (liquid, gas, or solid) enters the space between the plates, changing $\varepsilon_r$. **Key relationship (for partial dielectric insertion):** $$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[\varepsilon_{r1} + (\varepsilon_{r2} - \varepsilon_{r1})\frac{x}{L}\right]$$ Where $x$ is the length of dielectric inserted and $L$ is the total plate length. --- ## 2.3 Differential Capacitive Sensor To **eliminate non-linearity** and **double the sensitivity**, two capacitors are connected in a **differential configuration** (e.g., part of an AC bridge). **Differential output:** $$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = 2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}\left(\frac{\Delta d}{d}\right)\bigg/(1 - (\Delta d/d)^2)$$ For small $\Delta d$, the output becomes **linear**: $$\Delta C \approx 2C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A differential capacitive sensor **doubles** the sensitivity and **cancels** common-mode errors (temperature drift, supply noise). Examiner almost always asks: "Why use a differential configuration?" – answer: linearity and 2× sensitivity. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **master reference** for solving any numerical problem on capacitive sensors in KTU 2024 exams. | # | Sensor Type | Capacitance Expression | Sensitivity $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Linearity | Typical Measurand | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Variable Gap (d)** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $-\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$ | **Non-linear** | Micro-displacement, vibration | | 2 | **Variable Area (A)** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$ | **Linear** | Position, length | | 3 | **Variable Dielectric** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | **Linear** | Level, humidity | | 4 | **Differential (gap)** | $\Delta C \approx 2C_0 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | $\dfrac{2C_0}{d}$ | **Linear** (for small $\Delta d$) | Pressure, force | | 5 | **Series Combination** | $C_s = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | — | — | Multi-dielectric setups | | 6 | **Parallel Combination** | $C_p = C_1 + C_2$ | — | — | Multi-dielectric setups | | 7 | **Frequency Output (Oscillator)** | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | — | — | All capacitive sensors | | 8 | **Charge Amplifier Output** | $V_o = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C_f}$ | — | — | Signal conditioning | **Crucial Conversion (often used in KTU numericals):** $$\boxed{\Delta C \;\text{(in pF)} \;=\; 8.854 \times \frac{\varepsilon_r \cdot A\,(\text{m}^2)}{d\,(\text{m})} \;\text{pF}}$$ (Use the conversion $1 \text{ F} = 10^{12} \text{ pF}$.) --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Mode Used | Industry | |---|---|---| | Smartphone Touchscreen | Variable dielectric (finger) | Consumer electronics | | MEMS Accelerometer | Variable gap | Automotive, mobile | | Fuel Level Gauge | Variable dielectric (fuel vs air) | Automotive | | Paper Thickness Measurement | Variable gap | Manufacturing | | Proximity Switch | Variable dielectric (object) | Industrial automation | | Humidity Sensor | Variable dielectric (hygroscopic polymer) | HVAC, meteorology | | Hydraulic Pressure Sensor | Variable gap (diaphragm) | Process control | | Capacitive Mic (studio) | Variable gap (diaphragm) | Audio engineering | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Placement Insight:** Capacitive MEMS accelerometers (used in airbags, gaming controllers, and smartphones) are one of the highest-selling sensors globally, with a market size of several billion USD. # CAPACITIVE SENSORS – Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Skip Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No step is omitted. Read each numbered line carefully. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor **Starting from Gauss's Law:** Consider two parallel plates of area $A$, separated by a distance $d$, with surface charge density $\sigma = Q/A$ on the inner surfaces. Apply Gauss's law to a rectangular pillbox enclosing one plate: $$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ The flux is non-zero only through the area $A$ (the sides of the pillbox are parallel to the field): $$E \cdot A = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ **Solving for the electric field:** $$E = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ **Voltage across the plates (definition of potential difference):** $$V = E \cdot d = \frac{Q \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ **Capacitance (definition: $C = Q/V$):** $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{Q \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ $$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity of Variable-Gap Capacitive Sensor **Initial capacitance** (gap = $d$): $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ **New capacitance** (gap = $d + \Delta d$): $$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$ **Change in capacitance:** $$\Delta C = C_0 - C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} - \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$ **Factor out common terms:** $$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d + \Delta d}\right]$$ **Combine the fractions (LCD = $d(d+\Delta d)$):** $$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[\frac{(d + \Delta d) - d}{d(d + \Delta d)}\right]$$ **Simplify the numerator:** $$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[\frac{\Delta d}{d(d + \Delta d)}\right]$$ **For small $\Delta d$ (i.e., $\Delta d \ll d$):** $$\Delta C \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2}$$ **Therefore, sensitivity:** $$\boxed{S_d = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = \frac{C_0}{d}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key observation:** Sensitivity is **inversely proportional to $d^2$**. So, *smaller* initial gap gives *much higher* sensitivity. This is why capacitive micro-sensors are made with very small initial gaps. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity of Variable-Area Capacitive Sensor **Initial capacitance** (overlap area = $A$): $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ **New capacitance** (overlap area = $A - \Delta A$): $$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A - \Delta A)}{d}$$ **Change in capacitance:** $$\Delta C = C_0 - C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} - \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A - \Delta A)}{d}$$ **Distribute and cancel:** $$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}\left[A - A + \Delta A\right] = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot \Delta A}{d}$$ **Sensitivity:** $$\boxed{S_A = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta A} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d} = \text{constant}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** Since $S_A$ is **independent** of $A$, the output is **perfectly linear** with displacement. This makes the variable-area type the **most preferred** in instrumentation systems. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Variable Dielectric Sensor (Partial Insertion) A dielectric slab of length $x$, permittivity $\varepsilon_{r2}$, is inserted into a capacitor of plate length $L$, originally filled with $\varepsilon_{r1}$. The system can be modeled as **two capacitors in parallel**: - $C_1$ (region with air): $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} (L-x) W}{d}$ - $C_2$ (region with dielectric): $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} \cdot x \cdot W}{d}$ Where $W$ is the plate width. **Total capacitance (parallel combination):** $$C_x = C_1 + C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W}{d}\left[\varepsilon_{r1}(L-x) + \varepsilon_{r2} \cdot x\right]$$ **Regroup terms:** $$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W L \varepsilon_{r1}}{d} + \frac{\varepsilon_0 W x}{d}(\varepsilon_{r2} - \varepsilon_{r1})$$ **Recognize the first term as $C_0$ (empty capacitor):** $$C_x = C_0 + \frac{\varepsilon_0 W}{d}(\varepsilon_{r2} - \varepsilon_{r1}) \cdot x$$ **Sensitivity (per unit length of insertion):** $$\boxed{S_x = \frac{\partial C_x}{\partial x} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W (\varepsilon_{r2} - \varepsilon_{r1})}{d} = \text{constant}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Application:** This is the principle behind **capacitive fuel-level sensors** in vehicles. As the fuel level rises, the length $x$ of the immersed (high $\varepsilon_r$) section increases, raising $C_x$ proportionally. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Differential Capacitive Sensor (Two-Plate Configuration) **Setup:** A central movable plate is flanked by two fixed plates. - $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_1}$ (upper gap) - $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_2}$ (lower gap) - Mechanical constraint: $d_1 + d_2 = 2d$ (constant) - If central plate moves up by $\Delta d$: $d_1 = d - \Delta d$, $d_2 = d + \Delta d$ **Expressions for both capacitances:** $$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - \Delta d} \;\;\text{and}\;\; C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + \Delta d}$$ **Differential output:** $$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{1}{d - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d + \Delta d}\right]$$ **Common denominator:** $$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{(d + \Delta d) - (d - \Delta d)}{(d - \Delta d)(d + \Delta d)}\right]$$ **Simplify:** $$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{2 \Delta d}{d^2 - (\Delta d)^2}\right]$$ **For small displacement ($\Delta d \ll d$):** $$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2} = 2C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$ **Differential sensitivity:** $$\boxed{S_{\text{diff}} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d} \approx \frac{2C_0}{d} = 2 \times \text{(single plate sensitivity)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why KTU loves this derivation:** It is a **direct proof** that a differential capacitive sensor has **twice the sensitivity** of a single-plate sensor AND has a **linear** response for small displacements. Examiners often set this as a full 7-mark question. --- ## 3.6 Comprehensive Numerical Pattern (KTU-Style) **Problem Pattern:** *A parallel-plate capacitive sensor has plate area 25 cm² and initial separation 0.5 mm, with air as dielectric. Find: (i) the initial capacitance, (ii) the change in capacitance for a 10 µm increase in gap, and (iii) the sensitivity.* **Given:** - $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - $d = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$ - $\Delta d = 10 \,\mu\text{m} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ - $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air), $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m **(i) Initial Capacitance:** $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 25 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$C_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 = 44.27 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$$ $$\boxed{C_0 = 44.27 \text{ pF}}$$ **(ii) Change in Capacitance (for variable gap, small displacement):** $$\Delta C = \frac{C_0 \cdot \Delta d}{d} = \frac{44.27 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$\Delta C = 44.27 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.02 = 0.8854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta C \approx 0.885 \text{ pF}}$$ **(iii) Sensitivity:** $$S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d} = \frac{0.8854 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.854 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m}$$ $$\boxed{S = 88.54 \text{ pF/m} \; (\text{or } 0.0885 \text{ pF/mm})}$$ # CAPACITIVE SENSORS – Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Notice:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid any conflict with reserved Mermaid keywords. All labels use clean uppercase text without markdown formatting. --- ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – Capacitive Sensor System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[PHYSICAL STIMULUS
DISPLACEMENT OR PRESSURE
OR HUMIDITY OR LEVEL] B1[SENSING ELEMENT
VARIABLE CAPACITOR CX] C1[BRIDGE OR OSCILLATOR
CIRCUIT STAGE] D1[SIGNAL CONDITIONER
AMPLIFIER AND FILTER] E1[OUTPUT STAGE
VOLTAGE OR FREQUENCY] F1[MICROCONTROLLER
OR DISPLAY UNIT] A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1 C1 --> D1 D1 --> E1 E1 --> F1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Classification of Capacitive Sensors ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[CAPACITIVE SENSORS] A1 --> B1[VARIABLE GAP TYPE] A1 --> C1[VARIABLE AREA TYPE] A1 --> D1[VARIABLE DIELECTRIC TYPE] B1 --> B2[PARALLEL PLATE
GAP CHANGES] B1 --> B3[DIAPHRAGM TYPE
USED IN PRESSURE] B1 --> B4[NON LINEAR OUTPUT
SENSITIVITY INVERSE TO D SQUARE] C1 --> C2[SLIDING PLATE
LINEAR DISPLACEMENT] C1 --> C3[ROTARY TYPE
ANGULAR DISPLACEMENT] C1 --> C4[LINEAR OUTPUT
CONSTANT SENSITIVITY] D1 --> D2[LIQUID LEVEL
FUEL GAUGE] D1 --> D3[HUMIDITY SENSOR
HYGROSCOPIC LAYER] D1 --> D4[PROXIMITY SENSOR
OBJECT DETECTION] ``` --- ## 4.3 Working Principle of Variable-Gap Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[FIXED PLATE AT TOP] B1[AIR GAP D PLUS DELTA D] C1[MOVABLE PLATE AT BOTTOM] D1[EXTERNAL FORCE APPLIED] A1 --- B1 B1 --- C1 D1 --> C1 C1 --> E1[GAP DECREASES] E1 --> F1[CAPACITANCE CX INCREASES] F1 --> G1[OUTPUT VOLTAGE OR FREQUENCY CHANGES] ``` --- ## 4.4 Differential Capacitive Sensor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[AC EXCITATION
SOURCE VEX] A1 --> B1[FIXED PLATE 1] A1 --> C1[FIXED PLATE 2] D1[MOVABLE CENTRAL PLATE] --> B1 D1 --> C1 B1 --> E1[CAPACITANCE C1
UPPER GAP D MINUS DELTA D] C1 --> E2[CAPACITANCE C2
LOWER GAP D PLUS DELTA D] E1 --> F1[DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER] E2 --> F1 F1 --> G1[DOUBLED SENSITIVITY
AND LINEAR OUTPUT] ``` --- ## 4.5 Signal Conditioning Flow (Oscillator-Based) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[VARIABLE CAPACITOR CX] B1[OSCILLATOR CIRCUIT] A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1[FREQUENCY TO VOLTAGE
CONVERTER STAGE] C1 --> D1[LOW PASS FILTER] D1 --> E1[LINEAR VOLTAGE OUTPUT
PROPORTIONAL TO CX] E1 --> F1[ADC AND DISPLAY] ``` # CAPACITIVE SENSORS – KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question is worth 3 marks. Answers should be concise (typically 80–120 words) with a clear definition and a short example or formula. ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a capacitive sensor. Mention any two of its advantages over an inductive sensor.** **Model Answer:** A capacitive sensor is a transducer that converts a physical measurand (displacement, pressure, level, humidity) into a measurable change in electrical capacitance, based on the equation $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$. **Two advantages over inductive sensors:** 1. **Higher sensitivity** to small displacements (down to nanometre range) because capacitance can be measured with sub-pF resolution. 2. **Non-contact operation** is possible, leading to zero mechanical wear and a much longer operational life. *(Acceptable alternatives: better frequency response, lower power consumption, less affected by magnetic interference, smaller size, suitable for non-metallic targets.)* *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for each advantage)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between the variable-gap and variable-area type capacitive sensors with respect to linearity and sensitivity.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Variable-Gap Type | Variable-Area Type | |---|---|---| | **Output** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | | **Linearity** | **Non-linear** (capacitance is inversely proportional to $d$) | **Linear** (capacitance is directly proportional to $A$) | | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{C_0}{d}$ (inversely proportional to $d^2$) | $S = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$ (constant, independent of $A$) | | **Range** | Small (limited by non-linearity) | Larger, linear range | | **Preferred use** | Pressure & vibration sensors | Position & length measurement | *(3 marks: tabular comparison with at least 3 valid contrast points)* --- ## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an OR-based internal choice between two questions. Each choice has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Solve ANY ONE full question from the choice.** --- ### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the sensitivity of a **parallel-plate variable-gap capacitive sensor**. Show that the output is non-linear, and explain how a **differential configuration** can make it linear with double the sensitivity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Initial Capacitance (1 mark):** $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ **Step 2 – New Capacitance after gap change $\Delta d$ (1 mark):** $$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$ **Step 3 – Change in capacitance (2 marks):** $$\Delta C = C_0 - C_1 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d + \Delta d}\right] = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d(d + \Delta d)}$$ **Step 4 – Sensitivity for small $\Delta d$ (1 mark):** $$S_d = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = \frac{C_0}{d}$$ **Step 5 – Non-linearity explanation (1 mark):** The relationship between $C$ and $d$ is hyperbolic ($C \propto 1/d$), hence inherently non-linear. The sensitivity itself varies with $d$, making calibration complex. **Step 6 – Differential configuration (1 mark):** Two capacitors are placed symmetrically about a central movable plate. When the plate moves by $\Delta d$, one gap becomes $d - \Delta d$ and the other $d + \Delta d$. The differential output is: $$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2 - (\Delta d)^2} \approx 2C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$ **Conclusion:** For small $\Delta d$, this becomes **linear** and the sensitivity is **exactly double** the single-plate value $2C_0/d$. --- **(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive sensor has **plate area = 100 cm²**, **initial gap = 1.5 mm**, and uses air as dielectric. If the gap is reduced by **30 µm**, calculate **(i)** the initial capacitance, **(ii)** the change in capacitance, and **(iii)** the sensitivity in pF/mm. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data (0.5 marks):** - $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$ - $d = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $\Delta d = 30 \,\mu\text{m} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ **(i) Initial Capacitance (2 marks):** $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.903 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 59.03 \text{ pF}$$ **(ii) Change in Capacitance (3 marks):** $$\Delta C = \frac{C_0 \cdot \Delta d}{d} = \frac{59.03 \times 10^{-12} \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\Delta C = 59.03 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.02 = 1.1806 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 1.18 \text{ pF}$$ **(iii) Sensitivity in pF/mm (1.5 marks):** $$S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d} = \frac{1.18 \text{ pF}}{0.03 \text{ mm}} = 39.35 \text{ pF/mm}$$ --- ### 🔷 QUESTION B (Alternative Choice – 14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **variable-area capacitive displacement sensor**. Derive its sensitivity and state why it gives a **linear output**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Construction (2 marks):** A variable-area sensor consists of two sets of fixed interleaved plates (stator) and a movable plate (rotor) sandwiched between them. As the rotor moves, the effective overlap area $A$ changes, while the gap $d$ and dielectric remain constant. **Step 2 – Capacitance Expression (1.5 marks):** $$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_x}{d}$$ **Step 3 – Sensitivity Derivation (2 marks):** If the overlap area changes by $\Delta A$: $$\Delta C = C_0 - C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (A - \Delta A)}{d} \text{ (decrease)} \;\text{or}\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \Delta A}{d} \text{ (change)}$$ $$S_A = \frac{dC}{dA} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d} = \text{constant}$$ **Step 4 – Linearity Explanation (1.5 marks):** Since $C \propto A$ and $S_A$ is **constant** (independent of $A$), the relationship between capacitance and displacement is **perfectly linear**. This is the key advantage of the variable-area configuration. --- **(b)** A capacitive sensor operates in the **variable-dielectric mode** to measure the level of a liquid in a tank. The plates have width $W = 5$ cm and length $L = 30$ cm, separated by $d = 4$ mm. The relative permittivity of the liquid is $\varepsilon_{r2} = 4$, and the space above the liquid is filled with air ($\varepsilon_{r1} = 1$). Calculate the capacitance when the liquid level is at $x = 10$ cm. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data (1 mark):** - $W = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - $L = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$ - $d = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $\varepsilon_{r1} = 1$ (air), $\varepsilon_{r2} = 4$ (liquid) - $x = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m **Step 1 – Identify the two regions (1 mark):** - $C_1$ (air region, length $L - x = 20$ cm = 0.20 m) - $C_2$ (liquid region, length $x = 10$ cm = 0.10 m) **Step 2 – Compute each capacitance (3 marks):** $$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} (L-x) W}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 0.20 \times 0.05}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.01}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 22.14 \text{ pF}$$ $$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} \cdot x \cdot W}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 0.10 \times 0.05}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$C_2 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.02}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.7708 \times 10^{-13}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 44.27 \text{ pF}$$ **Step 3 – Total Capacitance (Parallel combination) (2 marks):** $$C_x = C_1 + C_2 = 22.14 + 44.27 = 66.41 \text{ pF}$$ $$\boxed{C_x \approx 66.41 \text{ pF}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams** > > 1. **Unit conversion errors (–1 to –2 marks):** The most common error. $1\,\mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}$ m, NOT $10^{-3}$ m. Always convert mm, cm, µm, and nm to SI metres before substituting. > > 2. **Forgetting the permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0$ (–1 mark):** Many students write $C = \varepsilon_r A / d$ forgetting $\varepsilon_0$. The final answer comes out wrong by 11 orders of magnitude. > > 3. **Wrong sign in variable-gap derivation (–1 mark):** A gap **increase** causes a capacitance **decrease**. Sensitivity is $-(C_0/d^2)$, but the magnitude is what matters. Examiners expect you to state the inverse nature explicitly. > > 4. **Mixing up variable-gap and variable-area formulae (–2 marks):** Use the correct expression. Quick check: $C$ vs $d$ is **inverse** (gap type), $C$ vs $A$ is **direct** (area type). > > 5. **Not using the parallel combination $C_1 + C_2$ for variable dielectric (–2 marks):** Students often multiply $C_1$ and $C_2$ (using series formula) instead of adding. Remember: the two regions are *side by side*, so they are in **parallel**. > > 6. **Skipping the assumption of small $\Delta d$ in non-linear derivations (–1 mark):** Always state: *"For small displacement $\Delta d \ll d$, we approximate..."* Examiners reward the clarity of assumptions. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this and stick it on your wall before the exam!):** ### ⚡ Core Equations (Memorize These) - **Parallel Plate Capacitance:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ - **Sensitivity (Gap type):** $S_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = \dfrac{C_0}{d}$ - **Sensitivity (Area type):** $S_A = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$ (constant) - **Sensitivity (Dielectric type):** $S_x = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 W (\varepsilon_{r2} - \varepsilon_{r1})}{d}$ - **Differential Output:** $\Delta C \approx \dfrac{2C_0 \Delta d}{d}$ (linear, 2× sensitivity) ### ⚡ Key Constants to Remember - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (vacuum permittivity) - $\varepsilon_r$ (air) ≈ 1; $\varepsilon_r$ (water) ≈ 80; $\varepsilon_r$ (skin) ≈ 50 ### ⚡ The 3 Types at a Glance | Type | Parameter Varied | Linearity | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | **Variable Gap** | $d$ | ❌ Non-linear | Pressure, vibration | | **Variable Area** | $A$ | ✅ Linear | Position, length | | **Variable Dielectric** | $\varepsilon_r$ | ✅ Linear | Level, humidity | ### ⚡ Top 5 Application Examples (Memorize for Viva) 1. **Touchscreen** → variable dielectric (finger) 2. **MEMS Accelerometer** → variable gap (micro-machined plates) 3. **Fuel Level Gauge** → variable dielectric (petrol vs air) 4. **Capacitive Microphone** → variable gap (acoustic diaphragm) 5. **Humidity Sensor** → variable dielectric (hygroscopic polymer absorbs water) ### ⚡ Three High-Yield Lines for Any Theory Answer 1. *"A capacitive sensor converts a physical measurand into a proportional change in capacitance based on the equation $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$."* 2. *"A differential configuration provides twice the sensitivity and a linear output for small displacements, making it the preferred topology in precision instrumentation."* 3. *"The signal conditioning is typically done using an AC bridge, an oscillator (to convert $\Delta C$ to $\Delta f$), or a charge amplifier (to convert $\Delta Q$ to $\Delta V$)."*

topics.title: Force, Strain and Tactile Sensors - Strain Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Force, Strain and Tactile Sensors - Strain Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Strain Gauge** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a corresponding change in electrical resistance. It operates on the principle of **piezoresistivity** — the phenomenon whereby the electrical resistance of a material changes when it is subjected to mechanical stress or strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT523 / Module 2):**
> A strain gauge is fundamentally a *force/strain sensing element* whose output is an *analog change in resistance*. It is the most widely used sensor in experimental stress analysis and forms the foundational building block for **load cells, pressure sensors, torque sensors, and accelerometers.**

Mathematically, the operating principle of a strain gauge is governed by the **Gauge Factor (GF)**, defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied strain:

$$
GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

where:
- $R$ is the nominal (unstrained) resistance of the gauge
- $\Delta R$ is the change in resistance due to strain
- $\varepsilon$ is the mechanical strain (dimensionless, often expressed in microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thin rubber band with a wire wrapped tightly around it. Now pull the rubber band — the wire stretches, becomes longer and thinner, and its electrical resistance increases. The more you pull, the higher the resistance becomes. A **strain gauge** works on exactly the same physical principle, except the "rubber band" is a carefully engineered thin metallic foil (typically **Constantan, Karma, or Nichrome**) bonded to a flexible backing, and the "wire" is a precisely etched serpentine (back-and-forth) pattern that maximizes the sensitive length within a tiny area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of a strain gauge as a **"resistance ruler"** — it doesn't measure length, it measures *how much* something is stretching or compressing. Just as a thermometer translates temperature into a height of mercury, a strain gauge translates mechanical deformation into a tiny but measurable change in electrical resistance.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Values to Remember:**
> - **Nominal Gauge Resistance:** Standard values are **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 500 Ω, 1000 Ω, and 5000 Ω**. The 350 Ω gauge is most commonly used in precision transducers.
> - **Typical Gauge Factor (metallic foil):** $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$
> - **Typical Gauge Factor (semiconductor):** $GF \approx \pm 100$ to $\pm 200$ (much higher sensitivity)
> - **Standard Gauge Length:** $0.4 \text{ mm}$ to $150 \text{ mm}$
> - **Poisson's Ratio (for Constantan):** $\nu \approx 0.3$
> - **Young's Modulus (for steel test specimen):** $E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$

### 1.4 Classification of Strain Gauges (KTU Taxonomy)

For the KTU 2024 scheme, strain gauges are classified as follows:

| Classification Type | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Bonded Metallic Foil** | Etched Constantan foil bonded to test surface | Most common; GF ≈ 2.0 |
| **Unbonded Metal Wire** | Wires stretched between posts on a flexible frame | Used in load cells |
| **Semiconductor (Piezoresistive)** | Doped silicon strip bonded to substrate | Very high GF, temperature-sensitive |
| **Thin-Film / Sputtered** | Vacuum-deposited thin metallic film | Used at high temperatures |
| **Capacitive / Optical** | Not strictly resistive; measure strain via other means | EMI-immune, used in harsh environments |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Origin of Piezoresistivity — Why Resistance Changes

When a metallic conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is subjected to mechanical strain, its resistance changes due to **two simultaneous effects**:

1. **Geometric Effect:** The conductor becomes longer ($L \to L + \Delta L$) and thinner ($A \to A - \Delta A$), directly altering the $L/A$ ratio in the resistance equation.
2. **Piezoresistive Effect:** The resistivity $\rho$ itself changes because the applied strain alters the inter-atomic spacing in the conductor's crystal lattice, modifying the mean free path of conducting electrons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** In *metallic* strain gauges, the geometric effect dominates. In *semiconductor* strain gauges, the piezoresistive effect dominates — this is why semiconductor gauges have a Gauge Factor that is **50–100× higher** than metallic gauges.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Gauge Factor Equation (Conceptual Walkthrough)

Starting from the fundamental resistance equation:

$$
R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

Differentiating both sides (using the product rule with three variables $\rho, L, A$):

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

For a cylindrical conductor of diameter $d$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \implies \frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dd}{d}
$$

By the definition of **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$:

$$
\nu = -\frac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{longitudinal strain}} = -\frac{(dd/d)}{(dL/L)}
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon
$$

The **Gauge Factor** is therefore:

$$
GF = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

> The first term $(1 + 2\nu)$ is the **geometric contribution** (≈ 1.6 for Constantan with $\nu = 0.3$).
> The second term is the **piezoresistive contribution**, which is small in metals but enormous in semiconductors.

### 2.3 Wheatstone Bridge Configuration for Strain Measurement

A single strain gauge produces a *tiny* resistance change (typically $\Delta R / R \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$). To accurately detect this, the strain gauge is almost always connected in a **Wheatstone Bridge** circuit.

For a quarter-bridge configuration with one active gauge ($R_g = R + \Delta R$) and three fixed resistors of value $R$:

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}
$$

Since $\Delta R \ll R$, this simplifies to:

$$
V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** The bridge converts a tiny *resistance* variation into a *voltage* signal that is linearly proportional to strain. The excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ (typically **5 V or 10 V**) directly scales the output sensitivity.

### 2.4 Temperature Compensation and Apparent Strain

Strain gauges are sensitive to **temperature** for two reasons:
1. The base metal's resistivity changes with temperature (resistance temperature coefficient).
2. The test specimen and the gauge material have **different coefficients of thermal expansion**, producing *apparent strain* even with no load.

The compensated output in a **half-bridge** (two gauges, one active in tension, one active dummy in compression) or **full-bridge** (four active gauges) configuration inherently cancels these temperature effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apparent Strain Formula (Board Exam Favorite):**
> $$\varepsilon_{\text{apparent}} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$$
> where $\alpha_g$ and $\alpha_s$ are the thermal expansion coefficients of the gauge and the specimen respectively, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Fundamental resistance | Ω |
| 2 | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + (1/\varepsilon)(d\rho/\rho)$ | Theoretical Gauge Factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change vs strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (stress-strain) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 6 | $F = \sigma \cdot A$ | Force from stress | N |
| 7 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Quarter-bridge output voltage | V |
| 8 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/2) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Half-bridge output (active + dummy) | V |
| 9 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge output (all four active) | V |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{\text{apparent}} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal apparent strain | dimensionless |
| 11 | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha_R \Delta T)$ | Resistance vs temperature | Ω |
| 12 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | Poisson's ratio definition | dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Strain Gauges Are Used in Industry:**
> - **Load Cells:** Weighing scales, force platforms, material testing machines (full-bridge configuration)
> - **Pressure Transducers:** Diaphragm deflection measured by bonded gauges
> - **Aerospace:** Wing and fuselage stress monitoring (often semiconductor gauges)
> - **Civil Engineering:** Bridge and dam structural health monitoring
> - **Automotive:** Crash-test dummy instrumentation, suspension load sensing
> - **Robotics / Tactile Sensors:** Flexible skin with embedded gauge arrays
> - **Torque Measurement:** Shaft surface strain → torque via $T = \sigma \cdot J / r$ relationship

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Resistance Equation to Gauge Factor

**Starting Point:**

$$
R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides to separate the variables cleanly:**

$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to strain. Since $R$, $\rho$, $L$, and $A$ are all functions of strain, and because we are dealing with *infinitesimal* changes, we use $d/d\varepsilon$:**

$$
\frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + \frac{1}{L}\frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{A}\frac{dA}{d\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify each term using physical definitions.**

The longitudinal strain is defined as:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{dL}{L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{L}\frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the area change. For a circular cross-section conductor of diameter $d$:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \implies dA = \frac{\pi}{2} d \cdot dd
$$

Dividing both sides by $A$:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dd}{d}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Poisson's ratio definition. By definition:**

$$
\nu = -\frac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{longitudinal strain}} = -\frac{(dd/d)}{(dL/L)}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dd}{d} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Substituting back into the area-change equation:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = 2(-\nu \cdot \varepsilon) = -2\nu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting all the parts into the logarithmic derivative:**

$$
\frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + 1 - (-2\nu)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + 1 + 2\nu
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by strain $\varepsilon$ and rearrange. By definition, the Gauge Factor is $(dR/R)/\varepsilon$:**

$$
\frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\cdot\frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

$$
\boxed{GF = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\cdot\frac{d\rho}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical evaluation for Constantan (typical metallic gauge):**

For Constantan: $\nu \approx 0.30$ and the piezoresistive term is negligible.

$$
GF \approx 1 + 2(0.30) = 1 + 0.60 = 1.60 \;\;\text{(theoretical)}
$$

Measured values are typically $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.1$, with the small excess attributed to the small but nonzero piezoresistive term.

**Step 9 — Numerical evaluation for silicon semiconductor (e.g., p-type):**

For doped silicon, the geometric term is still $\approx 1.6$, but the piezoresistive term is enormous ($\approx 100$):

$$
GF_{\text{Si}} \approx 1.6 + 100 = 101.6 \;\;\text{(typical value, can exceed 200)}
$$

This explains the dramatically higher sensitivity of semiconductor strain gauges.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

**Circuit Setup:** A Wheatstone bridge with four arms: $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$. The excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied between the top and bottom nodes. The output voltage $V_o$ is measured between the left and right nodes.

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply this to a quarter-bridge: $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:**

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{R + \Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{R + R} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify each fraction:**

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Common denominator manipulation:**

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator expansion and cancellation:**

$$
2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R = \Delta R
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply small-signal approximation $\Delta R \ll R$:**

$$
V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Gauge Factor relation $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$
V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This equation is the *single most important formula* in strain gauge instrumentation. It directly converts a *mechanical strain* $\varepsilon$ into a *measurable voltage* $V_o$ through two designer-controlled parameters — the excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ and the Gauge Factor $GF$ of the chosen gauge.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Bridge Configurations

| Configuration | Active Gauges | Sensitivity Multiplier | Temperature Compensation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quarter-Bridge (1 active)** | 1 | $V_{ex}/4$ | Requires external dummy gauge | General-purpose lab |
| **Half-Bridge (2 active)** | 2 | $V_{ex}/2$ | Built-in (adjacent arms) | Bending-beam load cells |
| **Full-Bridge (4 active)** | 4 | $V_{ex}$ | Excellent | High-precision load cells |
| **Quarter-Bridge with Dummy** | 1 + 1 dummy | $V_{ex}/4$ | Built-in (dummy on same material) | Field measurements |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A 350 Ω strain gauge with $GF = 2.10$ is bonded to a steel beam. The beam is subjected to a stress of $\sigma = 140 \text{ MPa}$. Young's modulus of steel is $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$. The gauge is connected in a quarter-bridge with $V_{ex} = 10 \text{ V}$. Calculate:
1. The strain on the beam
2. The change in gauge resistance
3. The output voltage

**Solution:**

**(1) Strain using Hooke's Law:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{140 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 7.0 \times 10^{-4} = 700 \; \mu\varepsilon
$$

**(2) Change in resistance:**

$$
\Delta R = GF \cdot R \cdot \varepsilon = 2.10 \times 350 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\Delta R = 0.5145 \; \Omega
$$

**(3) Output voltage (quarter-bridge):**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{10 \times 2.10 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4}
$$

$$
V_o = 3.675 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 3.675 \text{ mV}
$$

This small but measurable voltage is the **raw signal** that an instrumentation amplifier conditions for further processing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Strain Gauge Measurement System

The diagram below maps the complete signal flow from mechanical input to electronic output in a strain gauge measurement system, following the standard KTU block-schematic convention.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECH[MECHANICAL DOMAIN]
        A[APPLIED FORCE F] --> B[STRESS SIGMA ON BEAM]
        B --> C[STRAIN EPSILON ON SURFACE]
    end

    subgraph TRANSD[TRANSDUCER DOMAIN]
        C --> D[BONDED STRAIN GAUGE]
        D --> E[CHANGE IN RESISTANCE DELTA R]
    end

    subgraph COND[CONDITIONING DOMAIN]
        E --> F[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE]
        F --> G[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
        G --> H[FILTER AND ADC]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT DOMAIN]
        H --> I[DISPLAY OR PROCESSOR]
        I --> J[STRAIN VALUE EPSILON]
    end

    subgraph COMP[COMPENSATION LOOP]
        K[TEMPERATURE SENSOR] --> F
    end
```

### 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Topology (Quarter-Bridge with One Active Gauge)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VEX["V_EX (EXCITATION)"] --> N1
    N1 --> R1["R1 = R + DELTA R (ACTIVE GAUGE)"]
    N1 --> R2["R2 = R"]
    R1 --> N2
    R2 --> N2
    N2 --> GND["GROUND"]
    N2 --> VO["V_OUT"]
    N2 --> R3["R3 = R"]
    N2 --> R4["R4 = R"]
    R3 --> N3
    R4 --> N3
    N3 --> GND
    VO --> AMP["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    AMP --> DISPLAY["DISPLAY OR DAQ"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Derivation of Gauge Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: R EQUALS RHO TIMES L DIVIDED BY A] --> B[TAKE LOGARITHM BOTH SIDES]
    B --> C[DIFFERENTIATE WITH RESPECT TO STRAIN]
    C --> D[IDENTIFY GEOMETRIC TERMS]
    D --> E[APPLY POISSON RATIO RELATION]
    E --> F[COMBINE ALL TERMS]
    F --> G[GF EQUALS 1 PLUS 2 NU PLUS PIEZOELECTRIC TERM]
    G --> H[NUMERICAL EVAL FOR CONSTANTAN GF APPROX 2.0]
    G --> I[NUMERICAL EVAL FOR SILICON GF APPROX 100 PLUS]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Force-to-Voltage Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FORCE INPUT F NEWTONS] --> S1[STRESS SIGMA EQUALS F DIVIDED BY A]
    S1 --> S2[STRAIN EPSILON EQUALS SIGMA DIVIDED BY E]
    S2 --> G1[STRAIN GAUGE DEFORMATION]
    G1 --> G2[RESISTANCE CHANGE DELTA R]
    G2 --> W1[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE]
    W1 --> A1[AMPLIFIER STAGE]
    A1 --> F2[LOW PASS FILTER]
    F2 --> C1[ADC CONVERSION]
    C1 --> D1[DIGITAL DISPLAY]
```

### 4.5 Decision Matrix: Choosing a Strain Gauge Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[CHOOSE STRAIN GAUGE TYPE] --> Q1{APPLICATION ENVIRONMENT}
    Q1 -->|ROOM TEMP, GENERAL USE| M1[METALLIC FOIL GAUGE]
    Q1 -->|HIGH SENSITIVITY NEEDED| M2[SEMICONDUCTOR GAUGE]
    Q1 -->|HIGH TEMPERATURE| M3[THIN FILM GAUGE]
    Q1 -->|EMI HAZARD| M4[OPTICAL FIBER GAUGE]

    M1 --> Q2{PRECISION REQUIRED}
    Q2 -->|HIGH| F1[FULL BRIDGE 4 ACTIVE GAUGES]
    Q2 -->|MODERATE| F2[QUARTER BRIDGE WITH DUMMY]

    M2 --> Q3{TEMPERATURE STABLE}
    Q3 -->|NO| T1[USE TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION DUMMY]
    Q3 -->|YES| T2[DIRECT BRIDGE CONNECTION]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Define the term **Gauge Factor** of a strain gauge. Mention its typical value for a metallic foil gauge and a semiconductor gauge.

**Model Answer:**

The **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in resistance to the applied mechanical strain:

$$
GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$
