It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the sensitivity of the strain gauge.

> Typical values:
> - **Metallic foil strain gauge (Constantan):** $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$
> - **Semiconductor (silicon) strain gauge:** $GF \approx 100$ to $200$ (significantly higher due to the dominant piezoresistive effect)

The Gauge Factor is the fundamental specification used to relate mechanical strain to the resulting resistance change in the gauge.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
Why is a **Wheatstone bridge** used with a strain gauge for measurement? Explain the principle of **quarter-bridge** configuration.

**Model Answer:**

A single strain gauge produces a very small fractional change in resistance (typically $\Delta R / R \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$), which is impractical to measure directly. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts this small resistance change into a measurable differential voltage signal, with excellent common-mode rejection and inherent temperature compensation capability.

> **Quarter-Bridge Configuration:**
> - Only **one arm** contains the active strain gauge ($R_g = R + \Delta R$)
> - The other three arms are fixed precision resistors of value $R$
> - The output voltage is given by:
> $$V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$$
> - Sensitivity is the lowest among bridge configurations, but it is the simplest and most economical to implement.

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to use *small-signal approximation* ($\Delta R \ll R$) in bridge derivations — examiners specifically look for this step in the working.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *quarter-bridge* ($V_{ex}/4$ factor) with *half-bridge* ($V_{ex}/2$ factor) — a single wrong factor means **−1 to −2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Omitting the explanation of *piezoresistive effect* when describing why semiconductor gauges have higher GF — at least one full mark is reserved for this in a 7-mark sub-part.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not drawing the bridge circuit or omitting the formula derivation step-by-step. Always show the substitution of $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Mixing up the units of strain (dimensionless, but typically reported in $\mu\varepsilon$ or $\text{mm/m}$).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q. (a) Derive the expression for the Gauge Factor of a strain gauge starting from the fundamental resistance equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the fundamental resistance equation for a uniform conductor:

$$
R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to strain $\varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dA}{d\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the geometric strain term. Since $\varepsilon = dL / L$, we have $dL / (L \cdot d\varepsilon) = 1$.

**Step 4:** For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$, $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, so $dA/A = 2 \cdot dd/d$. Using Poisson's ratio $\nu = -(dd/d)/(dL/L)$, we get $dd/d = -\nu\varepsilon$, hence $dA/A = -2\nu\varepsilon$.

**Step 5:** Substitute all terms back:

$$
\frac{dR/R}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{d\rho/\rho}{d\varepsilon} + 1 + 2\nu
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $\varepsilon$ and apply the definition $GF = (dR/R)/\varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{GF = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Interpretation:**
- For metallic (Constantan) gauges, $d\rho/\rho \approx 0$ and $\nu \approx 0.30$, giving $GF \approx 1.6$. Measured values are slightly higher ($\approx 2.0$).
- For semiconductor gauges, the piezoresistive term dominates, giving $GF$ values of 100 to 200.

---

**Q. (b) A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit uses a 350 Ω gauge with $GF = 2.05$. The gauge is bonded to a steel cantilever beam subjected to a force. The bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 10 \text{ V}$. If the output voltage is measured as $V_o = 2.05 \text{ mV}$, determine: (i) the strain on the beam, (ii) the change in gauge resistance, and (iii) the corresponding stress if Young's modulus of steel is $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Strain calculation:**

Using the quarter-bridge output formula:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{4 V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot GF} = \frac{4 \times 2.05 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 2.05}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 400 \; \mu\varepsilon
$$

**(ii) Change in resistance:**

$$
\Delta R = GF \cdot R \cdot \varepsilon = 2.05 \times 350 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\Delta R = 0.287 \; \Omega
$$

**(iii) Stress from Hooke's Law:**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 200 \times 10^9 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\sigma = 8.0 \times 10^7 \; \text{Pa} = 80 \; \text{MPa}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q. (a) Explain the working of a Wheatstone bridge with a strain gauge. Derive the output voltage for a quarter-bridge, half-bridge, and full-bridge configuration. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Wheatstone bridge** is a null-type measurement circuit used to detect small resistance changes. It consists of four resistive arms arranged in a diamond configuration, with an excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ applied across opposite vertices and the output voltage $V_o$ measured across the other two.

> **General Bridge Equation:**
> $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Quarter-Bridge (1 active gauge):** $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$.

After simplification using $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}
$$

**Half-Bridge (2 active gauges, one in tension, one in compression):** $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R - \Delta R$, $R_3 = R_4 = R$.

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{2}}
$$

**Full-Bridge (4 active gauges, alternating tension and compression):**

$$
\boxed{V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}
$$

> **Summary:** Sensitivity scales by a factor of 1, 2, or 4 depending on the number of active gauges. The full-bridge has 4× the sensitivity of the quarter-bridge and provides automatic temperature compensation.

---

**Q. (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the complete measurement chain of a strain-gauge-based force/load measurement system. Discuss the role of temperature compensation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of the measurement chain:**

> Applied Force → Test Specimen (Beam) → Strain → Bonded Strain Gauge → Wheatstone Bridge → Instrumentation Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Microprocessor/Display

**Stage-by-stage explanation:**

1. **Applied Force → Strain:** The mechanical force $F$ applied to a cantilever beam or load cell body produces a stress $\sigma = F/A$, which generates a strain $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$ on the surface.

2. **Bonded Strain Gauge:** The strain gauge, bonded to the surface, deforms with the specimen. Its resistance changes according to $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$.

3. **Wheatstone Bridge:** Converts the small resistance change into a differential voltage signal $V_o$, while providing first-order temperature compensation.

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Amplifies the small bridge output (typically millivolts) to a usable level (typically 0–10 V). It has high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR > 100 dB).

5. **Filter:** Low-pass filter removes high-frequency noise and aliasing effects.

6. **ADC and Processor:** Digital conversion and final computation of force using $F = \varepsilon \cdot E \cdot A$.

**Role of Temperature Compensation:**

Strain gauges are inherently sensitive to temperature through two mechanisms:
- **Resistance Temperature Coefficient (RTC):** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha_R \Delta T)$, causing resistance drift with temperature.
- **Differential Thermal Expansion:** The mismatch between the gauge's expansion coefficient $\alpha_g$ and the specimen's coefficient $\alpha_s$ produces an **apparent strain** $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$ even in the absence of mechanical load.

**Compensation techniques:**
- **Dummy Gauge Method:** A second identical gauge is mounted on an unstrained piece of the same material, placed in an adjacent arm of the bridge. Both gauges experience the same temperature, so their temperature-induced changes cancel in the bridge output.
- **Self-Temperature-Compensated Gauges:** Specially manufactured gauges where the alloy is chosen so that $\alpha_g \approx \alpha_s$ for a specific substrate material (e.g., Constantan for steel).
- **Full-Bridge Configuration:** Using four active gauges in opposing arms of the bridge provides excellent temperature stability because the temperature effect is common-mode and is rejected by the bridge.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Strain Gauge** converts mechanical strain into a resistance change via piezoresistivity.
- **Gauge Factor** $GF = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$ — dimensionless, equals **~2.0 for metals**, **~100 for semiconductors**.
- Theoretical $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + (1/\varepsilon)(d\rho/\rho)$ — geometric term + piezoresistive term.
- **Standard resistances:** 120 Ω, 350 Ω, 500 Ω, 1000 Ω. **350 Ω is industry standard.**
- **Wheatstone Bridge** is *always* used for practical measurement — it converts $\Delta R$ into $V_o$.
- **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ (sensitivity = $V_{ex}/4$)
- **Half-bridge output:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ (sensitivity = $V_{ex}/2$, with built-in temp compensation)
- **Full-bridge output:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (sensitivity = $V_{ex}$, best compensation)
- **Apparent strain** due to thermal expansion mismatch: $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$
- **Poisson's ratio of Constantan:** $\nu \approx 0.30$
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ — used to back-calculate stress or force from measured strain.
- **Semiconductor strain gauges** have $\approx 50\times$ higher sensitivity than metallic gauges but worse temperature stability and are more brittle.
- **Temperature compensation techniques:** dummy gauge, self-compensated alloy selection, full-bridge configuration.
- **Always use small-signal approximation** $\Delta R \ll R$ in bridge derivations unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tactile Sensors - Switch Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tactile Sensors - Switch Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tactile Sensors — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **tactile sensor** is a device that detects and measures physical contact parameters—such as touch, force, pressure, vibration, or texture—arising from physical interaction with an object or surface. It converts these mechanical stimuli into measurable electrical signals.

Tactile sensors are broadly classified into three categories based on the type of output they produce:

| Type | Output Nature | Typical Application |
|------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Binary / Switch Sensors** | ON–OFF (Digital) | Touch detection, object presence |
| **Analog Tactile Sensors** | Continuous voltage/current | Force/pressure profiling |
| **Hybrid Tactile Arrays** | Spatially distributed matrix | Robotic skin, slip detection |

The focus of this study unit is the **Binary / Switch Sensor** sub-family — the simplest, cheapest, and most widely deployed tactile sensor category.

---

## 1.2 Switch Sensor — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switch Sensor (KTU Standard Definition)**
> A **switch sensor** is a form of tactile sensor that operates on the principle of *contact closure*. It produces a **discrete two-state electrical output** — typically interpreted as logic HIGH (actuated) or logic LOW (rest) — whenever the applied mechanical input (force, displacement, or pressure) crosses a predefined threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Distinction**
> Unlike analog tactile sensors, switch sensors do not measure *how much* force is applied — they only answer the binary question: **"Is something touching me, yes or no?"**

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Imagine a doorbell button:**
- When no one touches the button → the circuit is open, no current flows, the bell is silent.
- When a person presses the button → the contacts close, current flows, the bell rings.

A switch sensor works on exactly this principle. It is the *electrical equivalent of your fingertip* — it tells a robot, a machine, or a microcontroller that **"contact has occurred"** so the next action can be triggered.

**Practical everyday examples:**
- A laptop's lid-closure sensor (magnetic reed switch).
- The micro-switch inside a washing machine door lock.
- The dome-switch under your TV remote's power button.
- The bump sensor on a robotic vacuum cleaner (IR/contact switch).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation**
> - **Actuation Force ($F_a$):** The minimum force required to change state (typically **0.1 N to 5 N**).
> - **Release Force ($F_r$):** The force at which the switch returns to its rest state.
> - **Travel Distance ($d$):** The mechanical displacement before actuation (typically **0.1 mm to 2 mm**).
> - **Contact Bounce Time ($t_b$):** Unwanted mechanical oscillation duration (**≤ 5 ms**).
> - **Mechanical Life Cycles:** Rated switching operations (typically **$10^5$ to $10^7$ cycles**).
> - **Operating Temperature:** Standard industrial range **$-25°C$ to $+85°C$**.
> - **Contact Resistance ($R_c$):** Closed-state resistance (typically **< 50 mΩ** for metallic contacts).

---

## 1.5 Block Diagram Summary

> [!NOTE]
> A switch sensor signal chain in any KTU block diagram typically has the form:
> **Mechanical Force → Sensing Element → Threshold Comparator → Binary Output (0 / 1)**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Switch Sensors

Switch sensors are engineered in several physical forms. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights the following five major families:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical / Electromechanical Switches

These rely on the physical movement of conductive contacts. The most common sub-types are:

**a) Limit Switches**
- A spring-loaded lever arm physically pushes two metal contacts together.
- Widely used in industrial automation (CNC machines, elevators, conveyor belts).
- Very high current handling capability (up to **10 A**).

**b) Micro-switches (Snap-Action Switches)**
- Use a pre-stressed spring that *snaps* the contacts closed the moment the actuation point is reached.
- The snap mechanism prevents slow contact transitions → minimises arcing.
- Used in microwave oven doors, vending machines, safety interlocks.

**c) Push-Button / Dome Switches**
- A flexible metal dome inverts under finger pressure, touching a lower contact pad.
- Found in keypads, remote controls, and appliance panels.

**d) Membrane Switches**
- A sandwich of flexible PET/PC layers with printed silver-ink traces separated by a spacer.
- Finger pressure bridges the upper and lower conductive traces.
- Used in microwaves, medical devices, industrial control panels — fully sealed against dust/moisture.

---

### 2.1.2 Conductive Elastomer Switches

- The sensing element is **carbon-loaded silicone or PDMS rubber**.
- In its rest state, the elastomer is an insulator.
- Under pressure, conductive carbon particles come into contact, lowering resistance dramatically.
- Resistance model:

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A_{eff}(F)}$$

where $A_{eff}(F)$ is the force-dependent effective cross-sectional contact area.

- **Key advantage:** Flexibility and conformability to curved surfaces.
- **Limitation:** Hysteresis due to viscoelastic creep of the rubber.

---

### 2.1.3 Capacitive Switch Sensors

- A capacitor is formed between two electrodes separated by a dielectric (air + cover layer).
- Touching the surface **changes the dielectric constant** and/or **brings a conductive body (finger) closer**, altering capacitance.
- Capacitance model:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

- For a fingertip at distance $d$ from the electrode:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + d_{cover}}$$

- Touching increases $\varepsilon_r$ (skin ≈ 40× air) and reduces effective $d$ → measurable $\Delta C$ → threshold comparator produces a binary output.
- Used in **smartphone touchscreens, smart home panels, proximity-sensing automotive dashboards**.

---

### 2.1.4 Piezoelectric Switch Sensors

- Materials like **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), PVDF (Polyvinylidene fluoride), or BaTiO₃** generate a charge $Q$ when mechanically stressed.
- The fundamental piezoelectric equation (direct effect, mode 33):

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

where:
- $Q$ = generated charge (Coulombs)
- $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N)
- $F$ = applied mechanical force (N)

- The generated charge is integrated by a charge amplifier to give a voltage, then compared to a threshold.
- **Zero standby power** — ideal for battery-less wireless push buttons (EnOcean standard).

---

### 2.1.5 Magnetic Switch Sensors (Reed & Hall Effect)

**a) Reed Switches**
- Two ferromagnetic reed blades sealed in a glass capsule.
- An external magnetic field causes the reeds to attract and close.
- Pure passive device — no power needed to sense.

**b) Hall-Effect Switches**
- A semiconductor Hall plate outputs a voltage proportional to perpendicular magnetic flux density $B$.
- When $B > B_{threshold}$, the internal comparator flips the output to logic LOW.
- Solid-state → no mechanical wear, billions of switching cycles.

The Hall voltage relation:

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{q \cdot n \cdot t}$$

where $q$ is electron charge, $n$ is carrier density, $t$ is sensor thickness.

---

## 2.2 Why & How — Engineering Logic Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters | How it works |
|------|----------------|--------------|
| **Force thresholding** | Prevents false triggers from vibration/noise | Comparator with hysteresis (Schmitt trigger) |
| **Debounce circuitry** | Mechanical contacts bounce for ~5 ms | RC low-pass + Schmitt trigger, or software delay |
| **Pull-up / pull-down** | Defines logic level when open | $10\text{ k}\Omega$ resistor to $V_{CC}$ or GND |
| **Hysteresis design** | Prevents chattering at threshold | $V_{TH+} = V_{ref} + \Delta V$ and $V_{TH-} = V_{ref} - \Delta V$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F = k \cdot x$ | Hooke's law for switch actuation spring | N, N/m, m |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of a parallel-plate tactile switch | F |
| 3 | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge generation | C, C/N, N |
| 4 | $V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{q \cdot n \cdot t}$ | Hall-effect voltage | V |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A_{eff}(F)}$ | Conductive elastomer resistance | Ω |
| 6 | $f_{LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Debounce low-pass cutoff | Hz |
| 7 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{sensor}}{R_{sensor} + R_{pull}}$ | Voltage-divider binary output | V |
| 8 | $t_{response} = t_{mech} + t_{elec}$ | Total switch response time | s |
| 9 | $\Delta V = V_{TH+} - V_{TH-}$ | Hysteresis band | V |
| 10 | $B_{op}, B_{rel}$ | Magnetic operate / release flux density | mT |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU expects you to study switch sensors:**
> 1. **Robotics:** Bump detection, gripper object-presence feedback, tactile skins on prosthetic hands.
> 2. **Automotive:** Brake-pedal sensors, seat-occupancy detection, gear-position interlocks.
> 3. **Industrial Automation:** Conveyor object counting, safety light curtains (with reed interlocks), CNC end-of-travel limits.
> 4. **Medical Devices:** Infusion pump touch panels, surgical instrument contact detection.
> 5. **IoT & Smart Home:** Wireless self-powered switches (EnOcean), capacitive smart switches, elevator call buttons.
> 6. **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone touchscreens, gaming controllers, laptop lid-closure sensors.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Debounce Time-Constant of a Mechanical Switch

When a mechanical switch closes, the contacts physically bounce for a few milliseconds. We need an RC low-pass filter to mask this bounce.

**Given:**
- Pull-up resistor: $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- Capacitor (filter): $C = 100 \text{ nF}$

**Step 1 — State the RC time constant.**

$$\tau = R \cdot C$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\tau = (10 \times 10^3 \, \Omega) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-9} \, F)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$\tau = 10^4 \cdot 10^{-7} \, s = 10^{-3} \, s$$

**Step 4 — Result.**

$$\tau = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Comment.** Since mechanical bounce duration $t_b$ is typically ≤ 5 ms, the time constant $\tau = 1$ ms ensures the capacitor cannot charge/discharge fast enough to register a bounce. The output settles cleanly within $\approx 5\tau = 5$ ms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitance Change in a Capacitive Switch

A capacitive switch is built with:
- Top electrode area: $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- Cover dielectric (glass): $\varepsilon_r = 7$, thickness $d_{cover} = 1 \text{ mm}$
- Air gap (no touch): $d_{air} = 2 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Rest-state capacitance (no finger).**

$$C_{rest} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_{r,glass} \cdot A}{d_{glass} + d_{air}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$C_{rest} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 7 \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6})}{(1 + 2) \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 7 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5495 \times 10^{-15} \, F\cdot m$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $3 \times 10^{-3}$ m.**

$$C_{rest} = 5.165 \times 10^{-13} \, F = 0.516 \, pF$$

**Step 5 — Touch-state (finger introduces $\varepsilon_{r,skin} \approx 40$ in the air gap).**

$$C_{touch} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{\dfrac{d_{glass}}{\varepsilon_{r,glass}} + \dfrac{d_{air,eff}}{\varepsilon_{r,skin}}}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting values.**

$$C_{touch} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6})}{\dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{7} + \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{40}}$$

**Step 7 — Compute denominator.**

$$\frac{10^{-3}}{7} = 1.4286 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\frac{10^{-3}}{40} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\text{Sum} = 1.6786 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8 — Final calculation.**

$$C_{touch} = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-16}}{1.6786 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.319 \times 10^{-12} \, F = 1.319 \, pF$$

**Step 9 — Compute the delta.**

$$\Delta C = C_{touch} - C_{rest} = 1.319 - 0.516 = 0.803 \, pF$$

**Step 10 — Interpretation.** A change of 0.8 pF is easily detectable by a modern capacitance-to-digital converter (CDC) such as the TI FDC1004, allowing reliable touch detection.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Piezoelectric Voltage Output of a Switch

**Given:**
- PVDF film, $d_{33} = 33 \times 10^{-12} \, C/N$
- Applied fingertip force: $F = 5 \text{ N}$
- Sensor capacitance: $C_p = 1 \text{ nF}$

**Step 1 — Generated charge.**

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$Q = (33 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 5 = 165 \times 10^{-12} \, C = 165 \, pC$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across sensor (open-circuit).**

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{165 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.165 \, V = 165 \, mV$$

**Step 4 — Practical note.** A charge amplifier with gain of $\approx 30$ raises this to **~5 V**, comfortably above a digital logic threshold.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hall-Effect Switch Threshold Detection

**Given Hall plate parameters:**
- Drive current $I = 5 \text{ mA}$
- Carrier density $n = 1 \times 10^{21} \text{ m}^{-3}$
- Thickness $t = 100 \,\mu\text{m} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Threshold flux density $B_{th} = 30 \text{ mT}$

**Step 1 — Hall voltage at threshold.**

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{q \cdot n \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$V_H = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (30 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{21}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-6})}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-3} = 150 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, V\cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**

$$1.6 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{21} \cdot 10^{-4} = 1.6 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute.**

$$V_H = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-2}} = 9.375 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 9.375 \, mV$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Only ~9 mV is generated at threshold, so an integrated low-noise differential amplifier inside the Hall switch IC is essential to provide a clean logic-level output.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | Mechanical | Conductive Elastomer | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Magnetic (Reed/Hall) |
|-----------|------------|----------------------|------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Output** | Binary | Binary (variable R) | Binary | Pulse (charge) | Binary |
| **Active Power** | None (passive) | None | Required (for sense) | None | None / Low |
| **Wear** | High ($10^5$–$10^7$) | Medium | None | None | None / High |
| **Sensitivity** | Low–Med | Med | High | Very High | Med |
| **Sealing** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Response Time** | 1–10 ms | 5–50 ms | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | < 1 ms |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | Med–High | Low–Med |
| **Hysteresis** | High | High | Low | Low | Low |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | Low | Low | Med | Low | Med |
| **Typical Use** | Industrial limits | Robotics skin | Touchscreens | Wireless switches | Door sensors, BLDC |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a tactile sensor and a switch sensor. Give two real-world examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **tactile sensor** is any device that detects physical contact phenomena (force, pressure, vibration, texture) and converts them into an electrical signal. Its output may be *analog* (continuous) or *digital* (binary).
>
> A **switch sensor** is a *sub-class* of tactile sensor that produces a **binary two-state output** — ON or OFF — based purely on whether an actuation threshold has been crossed. It does not quantify the magnitude of the contact force.

| Category | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Tactile sensor (analog) | Robotic fingertip force sensor | Pressure-mapping mattress sensor |
| Switch sensor (binary) | Refrigerator door reed switch | TV remote dome switch |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for examples)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List any three types of switch sensors and state the working principle of a piezoelectric switch sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

Three types of switch sensors:
1. **Mechanical / micro-switch**
2. **Capacitive switch sensor**
3. **Magnetic / reed switch**

**Working principle of piezoelectric switch sensor:**
When mechanical force is applied to a piezoelectric material (e.g., PVDF or PZT), the crystal lattice deforms, displacing charge centres and producing a net electric charge on its surface. This charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ is integrated by a charge amplifier and compared to a threshold to produce a binary output.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for list, 1.5 for principle)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** *Answer any one full question. Each full question carries 14 marks (sub-parts 7 + 7).*

---

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected / Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a **micro-switch** with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss its snap-action mechanism and state any two industrial applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of a **capacitive touch switch sensor** with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for capacitance change when a finger approaches the sensor electrode and explain how the change is converted to a binary output.

---

#### Solution to Q-A(a):

**Construction of a Micro-switch:**

A micro-switch consists of:
- **Actuator plunger** — receives external mechanical input.
- **Pre-stressed leaf spring** — provides snap-action energy storage.
- **Movable contact blade** — common terminal.
- **Fixed NC contact** — closed at rest.
- **Fixed NO contact** — closes after snap.
- **External terminal block** — for circuit connection.

**Working principle:**
1. In the rest position, the pre-stressed leaf spring holds the movable blade against the NC contact.
2. As the plunger is depressed, the spring stores elastic energy.
3. The instant the stored energy overcomes the restoring force, the spring **snaps** the blade across the air gap.
4. The blade now touches the NO contact, opening the NC path and closing the NO path.
5. On plunger release, the cycle reverses.

**Why snap-action matters:**
- The sudden contact transition minimises **arcing** (since the transition time is < 1 ms).
- It also makes the switch largely **insensitive to the speed of actuation**, ensuring consistent output.

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Door interlock** in washing machines and microwave ovens (safety cut-off).
2. **Limit detection** in CNC machines and 3D printers (end-of-travel signalling).

**Diagram (text representation):**
- Plunger (top) → Spring → Blade pivoting between NC and NO contacts.

*(7 marks: 2 construction + 3 working + 1 snap-action rationale + 1 applications)*

---

#### Solution to Q-A(b):

**Construction:**
A capacitive touch switch has:
- Top protective layer (glass/polycarbonate, $\varepsilon_r \approx 7$, thickness ~1 mm).
- ITO or copper sensor electrode patterned on a PCB.
- Dielectric spacer / air gap.
- Reference ground plane.
- Front-end capacitance-to-digital converter (CDC).

**Working Principle:**

The sensor and the environment form a parasitic capacitor. When a finger approaches, it introduces a second capacitive path through the body, which changes the total measured capacitance.

**Derivation of capacitance change:**

Rest-state capacitance (no finger):

$$C_{rest} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_{r,cover} \cdot A}{d_{cover} + d_{air}}$$

Touch-state capacitance (finger introduces $\varepsilon_{r,skin} \approx 40$ across the air gap):

$$C_{touch} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{\dfrac{d_{cover}}{\varepsilon_{r,cover}} + \dfrac{d_{air,eff}}{\varepsilon_{r,skin}}}$$

Capacitance change:

$$\Delta C = C_{touch} - C_{rest}$$

**Conversion to binary output:**
1. The CDC IC (e.g., TI FDC1004) measures $\Delta C$ in femtofarads.
2. A threshold comparator (often with hysteresis built into the IC) checks if $\Delta C > C_{threshold}$.
3. If yes → logic HIGH output (touch detected). If no → logic LOW.
4. Software debounce or auto-calibration routines suppress false triggers from temperature drift.

**Diagram (functional blocks):**
- Finger → Capacitive electrode → CDC → Comparator → Digital output → MCU.

*(7 marks: 2 construction + 2 derivation + 3 working + conversion-to-binary logic)*

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Expected]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of a **piezoelectric switch sensor** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the charge generated and the resulting output voltage for a typical PVDF film sensor under a 5 N load.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Hall-effect switch** and **reed switch**. Compare their construction, power requirements, lifetime, and typical applications in a tabulated form.

---

#### Solution to Q-B(a):

**Block Diagram (verbal):**
Mechanical Force → PVDF Piezo Film → Generated Charge $Q$ → Charge Amplifier (op-amp with feedback capacitor) → Comparator with Hysteresis → Binary Output (0 / 1)

**Working Principle:**
When a mechanical force $F$ is applied to the PVDF film, the polymer chains align, producing a net dipole moment per unit volume. Charge accumulates on the electrodes, and:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

For a typical PVDF film:
- $d_{33} = 33 \times 10^{-12} \, C/N$ (≈ 33 pC/N)

For $F = 5 \, N$:

$$Q = 33 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 5 = 165 \times 10^{-12} \, C = 165 \, pC$$

The output voltage across the film capacitance $C_p \approx 1 \, nF$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{165 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.165 \, V = 165 \, mV$$

The charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f = 33 \, pF$ and feedback resistor $R_f = 10 \, M\Omega$ gives:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{165 \, pC}{33 \, pF} = -5 \, V$$

This 5 V output is then fed to a comparator to generate a clean TTL/CMOS logic level.

**Special feature — self-powered wireless switch:**
The harvested energy per press (~50 µJ) is sufficient to power a single 868 MHz RF transmission, making battery-less wall switches possible (EnOcean technology).

*(7 marks: 1.5 diagram + 2 working + 1.5 charge derivation + 2 voltage derivation)*

---

#### Solution to Q-B(b):

**Hall-Effect Switch:**

A Hall-effect switch is a solid-state sensor consisting of:
- A thin semiconductor Hall plate.
- A constant-current source.
- A differential amplifier.
- A Schmitt-trigger comparator.
- An open-collector/drain output stage.

When a magnetic field perpendicular to the plate exceeds a set threshold $B_{op}$, the generated Hall voltage $V_H = \frac{IB}{qnt}$ is amplified, and the comparator flips the output to logic LOW (or HIGH, depending on the device).

**Reed Switch:**

A reed switch is a pair of ferromagnetic reed blades hermetically sealed in a glass tube filled with inert gas. When an external magnet is brought close, the blades magnetise, attract each other, and close the contact. When the magnet moves away, the blades spring apart.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Reed Switch | Hall-Effect Switch |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Construction** | Two ferromagnetic reeds in glass | Semiconductor IC |
| **Operating Principle** | Magnetic attraction of reeds | Hall voltage generation |
| **Power Required (sensing)** | Zero (passive) | Low (mA range) |
| **Output Type** | Mechanical contact closure | Open-collector logic output |
| **Switching Speed** | ~1 ms | < 1 µs |
| **Mechanical Life** | $10^6$–$10^7$ cycles | Unlimited (solid state) |
| **Bounce** | Yes (debounce required) | None |
| **Sensitivity Range** | 10–60 mT | 1–100 mT (programmable) |
| **Vibration Resistance** | Poor (false triggers) | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Low–Medium |
| **Typical Applications** | Door/window sensors, washing-machine lid interlock | BLDC commutation, rotary encoders, automotive gear position |

*(7 marks: 2 Hall + 2 Reed + 3 comparison table)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Switch Sensor questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "tactile" and "switch" sensors** — always explicitly state that switch sensors are a *sub-class* with *binary* output.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in derivations — $C$ in Farads, $F$ in Newtons, $Q$ in Coulombs, $V_H$ in Volts. Marks deducted for unit errors.
> 3. **Snap-action explanation skipped** in micro-switch answers — this is a 1- to 2-mark item and frequently asked.
> 4. **Not mentioning hysteresis / Schmitt trigger** when discussing debounce — examiner expects a comparator with hysteresis.
> 5. **Confusing "magnetic" with "Hall-effect"** — reed switch is *mechanical + magnetic*; Hall switch is *solid-state + magnetic*.
> 6. **No block diagram or labelled figure** in 7-mark sub-parts = loss of 1–2 marks even if theory is correct.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **switch sensor** is a **binary** tactile sensor — outputs only ON or OFF.
- [x] Five major families: **Mechanical, Conductive Elastomer, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Magnetic**.
- [x] **Mechanical / micro-switch** uses *snap-action* to minimise arcing and bounce.
- [x] **Membrane switch** = printed silver-ink traces on flexible PET/PC layers — sealed and waterproof.
- [x] **Conductive elastomer** resistance: $R = \rho L / A_{eff}(F)$ — decreases as force increases.
- [x] **Capacitive switch** working formula: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — finger increases $\varepsilon_r$ and reduces effective $d$.
- [x] **Piezoelectric charge:** $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ — for PVDF, $d_{33} \approx 33 \, pC/N$.
- [x] **Hall voltage:** $V_H = IB/(qnt)$ — only a few mV, so internal amplification is essential.
- [x] **Reed switch** is *passive* (no power needed); **Hall switch** needs supply but is *solid-state*.
- [x] **Debounce** is mandatory for mechanical switches — use RC filter + Schmitt trigger.
- [x] **Pull-up resistor** (typically **10 kΩ**) defines the open-state logic level.
- [x] **Hysteresis** in comparator prevents chatter at threshold: $V_{TH+} \ne V_{TH-}$.
- [x] **Applications** to remember: bump sensors, robotic skin, door interlocks, wireless light switches, touchscreens, seat-occupancy detection, CNC end-of-travel limits.
- [x] **EnOcean** wireless switches harvest energy from the press itself — zero battery.
- [x] **Capacitive touch** dominates modern UI (smartphones, kiosks) due to zero mechanical wear.
- [x] **Standard metrics to memorise:** $F_a = 0.1$–$5$ N, $t_b \le 5$ ms, life = $10^5$–$10^7$ cycles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric Effect (KTU Syllabus Definition):** The *piezoelectric effect* is the ability of certain crystalline materials to generate an internal electric charge (**D**, electric displacement) on their surfaces when subjected to a mechanical stress (**T**). The reverse phenomenon — deformation (**S**, strain) produced in the crystal under an applied electric field (**E**) — is called the *converse (or inverse) piezoelectric effect*.

The word *piezo* is derived from the Greek word **"piezein"**, meaning *to press or squeeze*. The phenomenon was first discovered in 1880 by the brothers **Pierre and Jacques Curie** in natural crystals like **quartz (SiO₂)**, **Rochelle salt**, and **tourmaline**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Crystallographic Requirement:** A crystal exhibits piezoelectricity **only if it lacks a centre of symmetry** (non-centrosymmetric). Of the 32 crystal classes, **20 classes** are non-centrosymmetric, and only these can show the piezoelectric effect. Materials with a centre of symmetry (e.g., NaCl) are piezoelectrically **inactive**.

## 1.2 Two Reciprocal Phenomena

| Effect | Stimulus | Response | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** (Sensor Mode) | Mechanical stress **T** | Electric charge / voltage | $D = d \cdot T$ |
| **Converse Piezoelectric Effect** (Actuator Mode) | Applied electric field **E** | Mechanical strain **S** | $S = d \cdot E$ |

The constant **d** is the same in both equations — this reciprocity is known as the **Maxwell reciprocity relation** in electromechanical systems.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a "mechanical battery" with no chemicals:**
> - When you **press** a piezoelectric gas-lighter button, you apply force → the crystal squeezes → positive and negative charges separate on opposite faces → a spark jumps across the gap. No battery is needed!
> - The *converse* is also true: feed a voltage to a buzzer element, and it physically bends, producing sound.
> - In a **piezoelectric microphone (sensor)**, sound waves vibrate a diaphragm coupled to the crystal → tiny charges are produced proportional to the acoustic pressure → these charges are amplified to reproduce the sound.
> - In a **piezoelectric speaker/buzzer (actuator)**, an audio voltage deforms the crystal → vibrations create sound waves.

This dual capability makes piezoelectric materials unique — they are simultaneously **sensors and actuators**, which is why they form the heart of MEMS devices, ultrasonic transducers, and precision positioning stages.

## 1.4 Common Piezoelectric Materials

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - **Curie Temperature (T_c)**: the temperature above which the spontaneous polarization disappears and piezoelectricity is permanently lost. For **PZT ~ 200–350 °C**; for **Quartz ~ 573 °C**; for **PVDF ~ 80–100 °C**.
> - **Relative permittivity (ε_r)** of PZT ≈ **1200–3000**; Quartz ≈ **4.5**; PVDF ≈ **10–12**.
> - **Density (ρ)** of PZT ≈ **7500–7800 kg/m³**; Quartz ≈ **2650 kg/m³**.

| Material | Type | $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | $g_{33}$ (mV·m/N) | Curie T (°C) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quartz (SiO₂)** | Natural crystal | 2.3 | 58 | 573 | Precision force/pressure standards |
| **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** | Ferroelectric ceramic | 250–600 | 20–40 | 200–350 | Accelerometers, ultrasonic Tx/Rx |
| **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** | Polymer film | 20–33 | 200–300 | 80–100 | Flexible impact/pressure sensors |
| **BaTiO₃ (Barium Titanate)** | Ferroelectric ceramic | 190 | 14 | 120 | Capacitors, transducers |
| **Rochelle Salt** | Natural crystal | 480 | 90 | 24 | Low-frequency microphones (historical) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

When a mechanical force **F** is applied across a piezoelectric element (area **A**, thickness **t**):

1. **Mechanical Stress Generation:** $\;T = F / A$ (Pa, N/m²).
2. **Direct Piezoelectric Conversion:** The stress polarizes the crystal, producing an electric displacement $D = d \cdot T$ (C/m²).
3. **Surface Charge Accumulation:** The charge appearing on the electrodes of area **A** is
   $$Q = D \cdot A = d \cdot T \cdot A = d \cdot F$$
4. **Open-Circuit Voltage:** With the crystal itself behaving as a parallel-plate capacitor $C_p = \varepsilon A / t$, the voltage is
   $$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon \cdot A}$$
5. **Signal Conditioning:** The high-impedance charge is converted to a usable voltage by a **charge amplifier** (gain $1/C_f$).

> [!TIP]
> **Why the High Output Impedance Matters:** A piezoelectric sensor is essentially a charge source in parallel with a small capacitor (tens to hundreds of pF). Any loading resistance $R_L$ forms an RC high-pass filter with time constant $\tau = R_L C_p$, which is why piezoelectric sensors **cannot measure true DC/static quantities** — they have a low-frequency roll-off at $f_{-3dB} = 1 / (2\pi R_L C_p)$.

## 2.2 Piezoelectric Constitutive Equations (Tensor Form)

The full behaviour is governed by two coupled linear constitutive equations relating the four field variables: **Stress T, Strain S, Electric Field E, Electric Displacement D**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarization Convention (IEEE Std 176):**
> - The polarization axis is labelled **3 (Z-axis)**.
> - The axes **1 (X)** and **2 (Y)** lie in the plane perpendicular to it.
> - Subscript **33** means "field along 3 produced by stimulus along 3".
> - Subscript **31** means "field along 3 produced by stimulus along 1".

**Direct Effect (Sensor mode):**
$$D_i = d_{ij} \cdot T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij} \cdot E_i$$

**Converse Effect (Actuator mode):**
$$S_i = s^{E}_{ij} \cdot T_j + d_{ij} \cdot E_i$$

Where:
- $d_{ij}$ — **Piezoelectric charge coefficients** (C/N or m/V), indexed (i = 1,2,3; j = 1,…,6).
- $s^{E}_{ij}$ — **Elastic compliance** at constant E (m²/N).
- $\varepsilon^{T}_{ij}$ — **Permittivity** at constant stress (F/m).
- $T_j$ — 6-component stress tensor; $S_i$ — 3-component strain vector.

The key **d-coefficients** are $d_{33}$, $d_{31}$, $d_{15}$, with the standard relationship for most ceramics: $d_{33} \approx -2 \, d_{31}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your KTU exam-ready cheat sheet. Memorize the column "Unit" and the order of magnitude.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol | Unit | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | d, F | C, N | Linear for small F |
| 2 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon A}$ | d, F, t, ε, A | V | Depends on geometry |
| 3 | Charge Sensitivity | $k_q = d$ | d | C/N | Material property |
| 4 | Voltage Sensitivity | $k_v = g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ | d, ε, g | V·m/N or mV·m/N | High-g materials have low ε |
| 5 | Crystal Capacitance | $C_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ | ε, A, t | F | Few pF to nF |
| 6 | Electromechanical Coupling | $k^2 = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot Y_s}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{d^2}{s \cdot \varepsilon}$ | d, Y, s, ε | dimensionless | $0 \le k \le 1$ |
| 7 | Charge Amplifier Output | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f} = -\dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_f}$ | Q, C_f | V | Independent of $C_p$ |
| 8 | Piezo Accelerometer Output | $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$ | d, m, a | C | Seismic mass m |
| 9 | Resonance Frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\dfrac{Y_s}{\rho}}$ | Y, ρ, t | Hz | Thickness mode |
| 10 | Lower Cut-off Frequency | $f_{low} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_L C_p}$ | R, C | Hz | Set by $R_L$ |
| 11 | Relative Permittivity | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{C_p \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}$ | C, t, A | — | — |
| 12 | Curie Temperature Limit | $T_{op} < T_c$ (typically $T_c / 2$) | T | K or °C | Above T_c → depoling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusions in KTU exams:**
> 1. $d$ has units of **C/N** when used as a *sensor* (charge per force) and **m/V** when used as an *actuator* (strain per field) — **numerically identical, different physical meaning**.
> 2. **$g$ (voltage coefficient)** is NOT the same as **$d$ (charge coefficient)**. $g = d / \varepsilon$, so high-$g$ materials (e.g., PVDF, quartz) are preferred for **voltage-mode readout**, while high-$d$ materials (e.g., PZT) are preferred for **actuators and charge-mode sensors**.

## 2.4 Equivalent Circuit of a Piezoelectric Sensor

A piezoelectric element can be modelled in **two equivalent forms**:

**Charge-Source Model (preferred for analysis):**

> A current source $i = dQ/dt$ in parallel with the crystal capacitance $C_p$, with parallel leakage resistance $R_p$ (representing dielectric loss and cable resistance $R_L$).

**Voltage-Source Model (Thévenin equivalent):**

> An ideal voltage source $V = Q/C_p$ in series with $C_p$.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Quartz** is the *standard* in precision manometers and gravimeters due to its near-zero hysteresis and thermal stability up to **573 °C**.
> - **PZT** dominates in industrial accelerometers, ultrasonic NDT probes, inkjet print heads, and sonar because of its enormous $d_{33}$ (≈ 500 pC/N).
> - **PVDF** is the *only* flexible piezoelectric and is used in wearable health monitors, textile pressure mats, and acoustic emission sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Charge-to-Voltage Relationship for a Piezoelectric Element

**Given:** A piezoelectric disc of area **A**, thickness **t**, permittivity **ε = ε₀ε_r**, subjected to a normal force **F** on its major face along the polarization (3) direction.

**Step 1 — Mechanical stress on the crystal face.**

The applied force distributed over the area A produces a stress:

$$T_3 = \frac{F}{A}$$

> *Logic: Pressure = Force / Area by definition of stress in the 3-direction.*

**Step 2 — Direct piezoelectric effect generates electric displacement.**

The direct piezoelectric constitutive relation along the polarization axis (i = 3, j = 3) gives:

$$D_3 = d_{33} \cdot T_3$$

> *Logic: A normal stress along 3 produces a co-linear electric displacement along 3. The d₃₃ coefficient is the slope.*

**Step 3 — Total free charge on the electrode surface.**

Charge density $D_3$ (C/m²) integrated over the electrode area A gives the total free charge Q:

$$Q = D_3 \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot T_3 \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot \frac{F}{A} \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot F$$

> *Logic: The two A's cancel — a clean result showing the *force-charge* relation is geometry-independent.*

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,Q = d_{33} \cdot F\,}$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** for a piezoelectric force/pressure sensor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage Output

**Step 1 — Model the crystal as a parallel-plate capacitor.**

A piezoelectric disc with electrode area A and thickness t behaves as:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

> *Logic: Standard parallel-plate capacitance, with the crystal dielectric as the insulator.*

**Step 2 — Apply V = Q / C.**

The voltage that appears across the electrodes when the generated charge Q sits on C_p is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$V = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot A}$$

> *Logic: Rearranging gives the geometry-dependent voltage-mode output. Notice that **thicker** crystals → higher voltage (good for low-input-impedance readout).*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer

**Setup:** A seismic mass *m* is attached to a piezoelectric element of stiffness *k*. The whole assembly is mounted on a vibrating base with acceleration *a(t)*.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion of the seismic mass.**

In the base-excited frame, the inertial (pseudo) force on the mass is:

$$F_{inertial} = m \cdot a(t)$$

This force is transmitted to the piezoelectric element, compressing it by $\Delta x$.

**Step 2 — Force on the piezoelectric element.**

For frequencies well below the natural frequency $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$, the displacement tracks the base displacement, and the force on the crystal is purely inertial:

$$F = m \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Charge generated.**

Substituting into the fundamental equation $Q = d \cdot F$:

$$Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

**Step 4 — Charge sensitivity (charge per unit acceleration).**

$$S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d \cdot m \quad \left[\frac{C \cdot s^2}{m}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Voltage sensitivity (with built-in amplifier of capacitance C_a).**

$$S_v = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_a} \quad \left[\frac{V \cdot s^2}{m}\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The accelerometer sensitivity is increased by (a) choosing high-$d$ material (PZT > quartz), and (b) increasing seismic mass. But both are limited by the desired size and the upper frequency limit, since $f_n$ decreases with larger *m* (for fixed *k*).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient k

The coupling coefficient **k** is defined as the square-root of the ratio of *mutual (converted) energy* to *input (geometric mean) energy*:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{U_{mutual}^2}{U_{mech} \cdot U_{elec}}}$$

For a piezoelectric bar under stress $T_3$ and field $E_3$:

- Stored mechanical energy: $U_{mech} = \tfrac{1}{2} s \cdot T_3^2$ per unit volume.
- Stored electrical energy: $U_{elec} = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon \cdot E_3^2$ per unit volume.
- Mutually converted energy: $U_{mutual} = d \cdot T_3 \cdot E_3$ per unit volume.

Substituting into the definition:

$$k^2 = \frac{(d \cdot T_3 \cdot E_3)^2}{(s T_3^2)(\varepsilon E_3^2)} = \frac{d^2}{s \cdot \varepsilon}$$

Using the relation $Y = 1/s$ (Young's modulus = inverse compliance):

$$\boxed{\,k^2 = \frac{d^2 \cdot Y}{\varepsilon}\,}$$

> *Logic: The T₃ and E₃ terms cancel exactly because k is a *material figure-of-merit*, independent of the applied stimulus — a hallmark of any physical coupling coefficient.*

For **PZT** $k_{33} \approx 0.7$; for **Quartz** $k_{11} \approx 0.1$. Higher k means more efficient energy conversion, which is why PZT is preferred over quartz in ultrasonic transducers.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Lower Cut-off Frequency of Piezoelectric Sensor

When a piezoelectric sensor (capacitance $C_p$) is loaded by an external resistance $R_L$ (cable + amplifier input), the circuit forms a high-pass filter.

The transfer function from generated voltage $V_0$ to output voltage $V_{out}$ across $R_L$ in series with $C_p$ is:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_0} = \frac{j \omega R_L C_p}{1 + j \omega R_L C_p}$$

The −3 dB corner frequency occurs when $\omega R_L C_p = 1$:

$$f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_L C_p}$$

> *Logic: At low frequencies, the charge leaks through $R_L$ faster than the measurement can be made. At high frequencies ($\omega R_L C_p \gg 1$), the output follows the input faithfully.*

For a typical accelerometer: $C_p = 1000$ pF, $R_L = 10^{10} \;\Omega$:

$$f_{low} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{10} \times 10^{-9}} \approx 0.016 \text{ Hz}$$

This is why piezoelectric sensors are inherently **AC-coupled** and cannot measure *true* DC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Piezoelectric Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input\nForce, Pressure, Acceleration] --> B[Piezoelectric\nCrystal Element\nQ = d x F]
    B --> C[Equivalent Circuit\nCharge Source Q in parallel with Cp]
    C --> D[Shielded Cable\nwith capacitance Cc]
    D --> E[Charge Amplifier\nOp-amp with feedback Cf]
    E --> F[Voltage Output\nVout = Q divided by Cf]
    F --> G[Signal Processing\nFilter and ADC]
    G --> H[Microcontroller\nor Display Unit]
    
    I[High-Impedance\nBias Resistor Rb] -.-> E
    J[Power Supply\nplus and minus 15 V] -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Accelerometer Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASE[Vibrating Base]
        B1[Base Acceleration a of t]
    end
    
    subgraph SENSOR[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
        B1 --> MASS[Seismic Mass m]
        MASS --> SPRING[Spring of stiffness k]
        SPRING --> PZT[PZT Crystal Element]
        PZT --> CHG[Charge Q = d x m x a]
    end
    
    CHG --> CABLE[Coaxial Cable\nCc and R insulation]
    CABLE --> AMP[Charge Amplifier\nVout = minus Q divided by Cf]
    AMP --> GAIN[Voltage Gain Stage]
    GAIN --> OUT[Analog Output 0 to 5 V]
    OUT --> MCU[Microcontroller ADC]
    MCU --> DSP[Digital Filtering FFT]
    DSP --> DISP[Display\nAcceleration g m/s squared]
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Models (Two Forms)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NORTON[Norton Charge Source Model]
        N1[Current Source i = dQ/dt] --- N2[Capacitance Cp]
        N2 --- N3[Leakage Resistance Rp]
        N3 --- N1
    end
    
    subgraph THEV[Thévenin Voltage Source Model]
        T1[Voltage Source V = Q / Cp] --- T2[Series Capacitor Cp]
        T2 --- T3[Series Inductance Lm for mass]
        T3 --- T4[Series Capacitor Cm for compliance]
        T4 --- T1
    end
```

## 4.4 Charge Amplifier Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CA[Charge Amplifier Circuit]
        IN[Input: Charge Q from sensor\nplus Cable Capacitance Cc] --> INNODE((Inverting Node))
        INNODE --- CF[Feedback Capacitor Cf]
        CF --> AMP[Op-Amp]
        AMP --- OUT[Output Vout]
        INNODE --- RF[Feedback Resistor Rf\nSets DC bias]
        RF --- AMP
        VPLUS[plus 15 V Supply] -.-> AMP
        VMINUS[minus 15 V Supply] -.-> AMP
    end
    
    OUT -.-> EQUATION[Transfer Function:\nVout = minus Q divided by Cf]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a charge amplifier and not a voltage amplifier?**
> - **Cable noise immunity:** Cable capacitance $C_c$ appears in parallel with $C_p$ and *does not* affect the output of a charge amplifier (its transfer function depends only on $C_f$).
> - **High input impedance:** No loading of the high-impedance piezoelectric source.
> - **Inverting output:** Polarity inversion is convenient for many signal-processing chains.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the piezoelectric effect. Distinguish between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoelectric effect** is the phenomenon by which certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress.

| Aspect | Direct Piezoelectric Effect | Converse Piezoelectric Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Stimulus | Mechanical stress (T) | Electric field (E) |
| Response | Electric charge / voltage | Mechanical strain / deformation |
| Governing eq. | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $S = d \cdot E$ |
| Application | Sensor (microphone, accelerometer) | Actuator (buzzer, ultrasonic Tx) |
| Example | Sound pressure → voltage in a piezo microphone | Audio voltage → diaphragm vibration in a piezo buzzer |

The two effects are governed by the same piezoelectric coefficient **d** (Maxwell reciprocity).

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction table + 1 mark example]**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four piezoelectric materials with their typical $d_{33}$ values and state two important applications of piezoelectric sensors.**

**Model Answer:**

| Material | $d_{33}$ (pC/N) |
|---|---|
| Quartz (SiO₂) | 2.3 |
| PZT (ceramic) | 250 – 600 |
| PVDF (polymer) | 20 – 33 |
| Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃) | 190 |

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Piezoelectric accelerometer** — vibration measurement in machines and aerospace.
2. **Ultrasonic transducer** — non-destructive testing (NDT), medical sonography, sonar.
3. **Piezoelectric microphone** — acoustic pickups, hearing aids.
4. **Piezoelectric pressure sensor** — dynamic pressure in engines, blast-wave measurement.
5. **Inkjet print-head actuator** — droplet ejection in printers.
6. **Quartz crystal microbalance** — thin-film mass sensing in chemistry.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for table + 1 mark for applications]**

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the constitutive equations of the piezoelectric effect and explain the significance of the $d_{33}$ and $d_{31}$ coefficients.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The four field variables in piezoelectricity are the **mechanical stress T**, **mechanical strain S**, **electric field E**, and **electric displacement D**. The two constitutive equations couple them linearly:

**Direct effect (Sensor mode):**
$$D_i = d_{ij} \cdot T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij} \cdot E_i \quad (i = 1,2,3;\; j = 1,\ldots,6)$$

**Converse effect (Actuator mode):**
$$S_i = s^{E}_{ij} \cdot T_j + d_{ij} \cdot E_i$$

**Significance of $d_{33}$:** It represents the charge (or strain) generated per unit stress (or field) applied **along the polarization axis (3-direction)**. For a disc-shaped crystal under axial load, $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$. It is the *most used* coefficient in axial-mode sensors.

**Significance of $d_{31}$:** It represents the charge (or strain) generated in the 3-direction when stress is applied along the perpendicular 1-direction (lateral compression). For a rod compressed sideways, a voltage appears across the 3-faces. It governs **transverse-mode** sensors and is typically negative (≈ $-d_{33}/2$ for most ceramics).

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks each constitutive equation + 2 marks d33 explanation + 1 mark d31 explanation + 2 marks full derivation]**

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Derive the expression for its charge sensitivity.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Working (block diagram description):**

A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a *seismic mass m* mounted on a piezoelectric crystal (PZT), the whole enclosed in a rigid housing. The base of the housing is bolted to the vibrating structure.

1. Base vibrates with acceleration $a(t)$.
2. Inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ acts on the seismic mass.
3. This force compresses the piezoelectric element.
4. The crystal generates a charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$.
5. The charge is fed via a shielded cable to a charge amplifier that converts it to a proportional voltage.
6. Output voltage $V_{out} = -Q / C_f = -d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a / C_f$.

**Derivation of Charge Sensitivity:**

The charge generated is:
$$Q = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$$

Therefore, the **charge sensitivity** is:

$$S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d_{33} \cdot m \quad \left[\frac{C \cdot s^2}{m}\right]$$

And the **voltage sensitivity** (with charge amplifier of feedback capacitance $C_f$):

$$S_v = \frac{V_{out}}{a} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_f} \quad \left[\frac{V \cdot s^2}{m}\right]$$

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 3 marks working + block diagram + 4 marks derivation steps]**

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Explain the equivalent circuit of a piezoelectric sensor. Discuss why a charge amplifier is preferred over a voltage amplifier for signal conditioning.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Equivalent Circuit:**

A piezoelectric element can be represented by two equivalent models:

**1. Norton Model (Charge-Source Form):**
A current source $i = dQ/dt$ in parallel with the crystal capacitance $C_p$ and the leakage/dielectric resistance $R_p$.

**2. Thévenin Model (Voltage-Source Form):**
A voltage source $V = Q/C_p$ in series with $C_p$ (and a series resistance $R_s$ for finite insulation).

**Why a Charge Amplifier is Preferred:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Cable capacitance immunity** | The cable adds $C_c$ in parallel with $C_p$, which *changes* the voltage-mode output but *not* the charge. A charge amplifier's output $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$ depends only on $C_f$, hence cable length does not affect calibration. |
| **High input impedance** | Piezoelectric sources are high-impedance; a charge amplifier presents very high input impedance, preventing charge leakage. |
| **Stable gain** | The transfer function $V_{out}/Q = -1/C_f$ is determined by a single, stable, low-value capacitor $C_f$ (often a few pF to nF). |
| **Low-frequency response** | The feedback resistor $R_f$ across $C_f$ sets a low cut-off $f_{low} = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f)$, which can be made very low without being affected by cable capacitance. |

A voltage amplifier, in contrast, has gain proportional to $1/C_{total}$, which depends on $C_p + C_c$, so the gain **drifts with cable length and temperature**.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks equivalent circuit + 3 marks charge amp advantages + 2 marks comparison table]**

---

**(b) A piezoelectric pressure sensor uses a PZT crystal of area $5 \text{ cm}^2$ and thickness 2 mm. The $d_{33}$ of PZT is 400 pC/N and $\varepsilon_r = 1200$. Calculate (i) the capacitance of the crystal, (ii) the charge generated when a force of 50 N is applied, and (iii) the open-circuit voltage output.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- $t = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $d_{33} = 400 \text{ pC/N} = 400 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N}$
- $\varepsilon_r = 1200$
- $F = 50 \text{ N}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$

**(i) Capacitance of the crystal:**

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1200 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_p = \frac{5.3124 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.656 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 2.656 \text{ nF}$$

**(ii) Charge generated:**

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C} = 20 \text{ nC}$$

**(iii) Open-circuit voltage:**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-8}}{2.656 \times 10^{-9}} = 7.53 \text{ V}$$

**Cross-check using $V = dFt/(\varepsilon A)$:**

$$V = \frac{400 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1200 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-11}}{5.312 \times 10^{-12}} = 7.53 \text{ V} \;\checkmark$$

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks (i) + 2 marks (ii) + 3 marks (iii) with cross-check]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors (most frequent −1 to −2 marks loss):** Always convert cm² to m², mm to m, pC to C, *before* substituting. A wrong unit on A or t propagates and gives a wrong answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide Q by $C_p$ for voltage:** Many students correctly compute $Q$ but write the answer in coulombs instead of volts. State the formula $V = Q / C_p$ *before* substituting.
> 3. **Confusing $d_{33}$ with $d_{31}$:** For axial (thickness-mode) sensors, the correct coefficient is **$d_{33}$**. Using $d_{31}$ gives an answer off by a factor of ~2 (since $d_{33} \approx -2 d_{31}$ for PZT).
> 4. **Neglecting to state the assumption "open-circuit":** In part (iii) the voltage is the *open-circuit* voltage; loaded voltage is smaller and depends on load impedance.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize for KTU Module 2 (Piezoelectric Sensors):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Piezoelectric effect = mechanical stress → electric charge (and converse: electric field → strain) in non-centrosymmetric crystals.
- [ ] **Direction convention:** Polarization axis = **3**; perpendicular axes = **1, 2**. $d_{33}$ = axial, $d_{31}$ = transverse.
- [ ] **Fundamental equation:** $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ — geometry-independent force-to-charge.
- [ ] **Voltage equation:** $V = d \cdot F \cdot t / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)$ — depends on geometry.
- [ ] **Charge amplifier output:** $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$ — cable-length independent.
- [ ] **Capacitance of crystal:** $C_p = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t$.
- [ ] **Coupling coefficient:** $k^2 = d^2 Y / \varepsilon$; range 0 to 1.
- [ ] **Curie temperature:** Above $T_c$, piezoelectricity is **lost permanently** (depoling).
- [ ] **Lower cut-off frequency:** $f_{low} = 1 / (2\pi R_L C_p)$ — sets the minimum measurable frequency.
- [ ] **Static (DC) measurement is impossible** with bare piezoelectric sensors — they are inherently AC-coupled.
- [ ] **Material choice:**
  - *High d, charge-mode* → **PZT** (large $d_{33}$, high ε).
  - *High g, voltage-mode* → **Quartz, PVDF** (low ε).
  - *High temperature* → **Quartz** ($T_c = 573$ °C).
  - *Flexibility / large area* → **PVDF** film.
- [ ] **Accelerometer output:** $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$; charge sensitivity $S_q = d \cdot m$.
- [ ] **Equivalent circuit:** Charge source $i = dQ/dt$ in parallel with $C_p$ and $R_p$.
- [ ] **Applications to remember:** accelerometer, ultrasonic Tx/Rx, microphone, gas lighter, inkjet print head, buzzer, force/pressure sensor, NDT probes, sonography, sonar.
- [ ] **Constitutive equations** — both forms, with index conventions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoresistive Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoresistive Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoresistive Effect (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> The *piezoresistive effect* is the phenomenon in which the **electrical resistivity** of a material changes when the material is subjected to an applied **mechanical stress** (tensile or compressive). A sensor that exploits this effect to convert mechanical input quantities (force, pressure, acceleration, strain) into a measurable electrical output (change in resistance) is termed a **Piezoresistive Sensor**.

Mathematically, the relative change in resistivity $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$ of a material is directly proportional to the applied mechanical stress $\sigma$ through the **piezoresistive coefficient tensor** $\pi_{ij}$:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_i}{\rho} = \sum_{j} \pi_{ij} \sigma_j$$

The macroscopic measurable quantity is the change in resistance $\Delta R$, which is related to both the **geometric change** (change in length and cross-sectional area) and the **intrinsic resistivity change** of the material.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Sponge"

Imagine a **kitchen sponge soaked in salt water** (representing a conductive medium). When you gently squeeze the sponge between your fingers:
- The water (electrons/current carriers) is forced to flow through a **narrower and longer path**.
- This increases the **electrical resistance** of the sponge-salt water system.
- Release the squeeze, and the resistance drops back.

A piezoresistive sensor behaves in a very similar way, except the "sponge" is a **crystalline material** (silicon, germanium, polysilicon) and the squeeze is **precision mechanical stress** (e.g., from a diaphragm under pressure). The key twist with semiconductors is that squeezing also **alters the internal band structure**, dramatically changing the mobility and density of charge carriers — this intrinsic effect is what makes semiconductor piezoresistors **100x more sensitive** than metallic strain gauges.

## 1.3 Why Piezoresistive Sensors? (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic is a KTU High-Priority Module:**
> - Piezoresistive sensors are the **most widely manufactured MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) devices** in the world.
> - They form the core of **automotive airbag systems, blood pressure monitors, industrial pressure transmitters, and MEMS accelerometers**.
> - They are easily **monolithically integrated with CMOS readout circuits** on a single silicon die.
> - The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes their **principle, gauge factor, and Wheatstone bridge signal conditioning**.

## 1.4 Piezoresistive Effect vs. Piezoelectric Effect — A Critical Distinction

| Property | Piezoresistive Effect | Piezoelectric Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Quantity** | Change in **resistance** (R) | Change in **voltage / charge** (V or Q) |
| **Material Class** | Semiconductors (Si, Ge, Poly-Si), some metals | Non-centrosymmetric crystals (Quartz, PZT, BaTiO₃) |
| **DC Response** | **Yes** (static strain is measurable) | **No** (only dynamic / time-varying signals) |
| **Active / Passive** | Passive (needs external excitation) | Active (self-generating voltage) |
| **Sensitivity (typical)** | Gauge Factor: 100–200 (Si) | Charge coefficient: 100–500 pC/N |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | High (must be compensated) | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Piezoresistors are **resistive** sensors — they require an external excitation voltage/current and measure a resistance change. They are not self-generating like piezoelectric sensors.

---

## 1.5 Engineering Units & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Stress** $\sigma$: measured in **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**. Typical MEMS diaphragm stresses: **$10^5$ – $10^8$ Pa**.
- **Strain** $\varepsilon$: dimensionless, often expressed in **microstrain (µε)** where **$1 \, \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$**.
- **Gauge Factor** $G$: dimensionless. For metals: **2.0 – 5.0**; For silicon: **50 – 200**.
- **Young's Modulus** $E$: **Pa**. For silicon along $\langle 111 \rangle$: **$1.87 \times 10^{11}$ Pa**; For polysilicon: **$1.6 \times 10^{11}$ Pa**.
- **Poisson's Ratio** $\nu$: dimensionless. For silicon: **0.28**; For steel: **0.30**.
- **Piezoresistive Coefficient** $\pi$: units of **Pa⁻¹** or **m²/N**. For p-type Si: **$\pi_{44} \approx 138.1 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of the Piezoresistive Effect

The piezoresistive effect arises from **two distinct physical contributions**:

### A. Geometric (Shape) Effect
When a resistor of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is stretched, its resistance changes purely due to geometry:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

- $L$ increases by $\Delta L$
- $A$ decreases due to the **Poisson contraction** $\nu$ (lateral strain)
- $A_{new} = A(1 - \nu \varepsilon)$ where $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$

### B. Piezoresistive (Intrinsic) Effect
The resistivity $\rho$ itself changes under stress. This is the **dominant mechanism** in semiconductors. In silicon, applied stress **deforms the band structure**, lifting the degeneracy of conduction band valleys (in n-type) or shifting the valence band (in p-type), which **changes carrier mobility** $\mu$ and therefore resistivity.

The total relative change in resistance is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

## 2.2 The Gauge Factor — The Heart of the Piezoresistive Sensor

The **Gauge Factor (G)** is defined as the ratio of **fractional change in resistance** to the **applied strain**:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}}$$

where $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the longitudinal strain.

### 2.2.1 Decomposition of the Gauge Factor

Substituting the geometric and intrinsic contributions:

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

- **Term 1 ($+1$)**: Direct length contribution.
- **Term 2 ($+2\nu$)**: Poisson's area-reduction contribution. For metals, this dominates; e.g., for constantan $\nu \approx 0.33$, giving $G \approx 1 + 0.66 = 1.66$.
- **Term 3 ($\frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$)**: The **piezoresistive intrinsic term**. This is **negligible for metals** but **dominant for semiconductors** (can be 100+).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl (KTU 2024):** For a metal strain gauge, $G \approx 1 + 2\nu$ because the resistivity change is nearly zero. For a silicon piezoresistor, $G \approx \pi_l \cdot E$ where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient and $E$ is Young's modulus.

## 2.3 Piezoresistive Coefficients in Silicon (Anisotropic Crystal)

Silicon has a **cubic crystal structure** with a non-zero piezoresistive coefficient tensor. The two most important coefficients are:

- **$\pi_{11}$**: Coefficient along the principal cubic axis.
- **$\pi_{12}$**: Coefficient in the transverse direction.
- **$\pi_{44}$**: Shear piezoresistive coefficient (most important for p-type Si).

For a resistor oriented along direction defined by direction cosines $(l_1, l_2, l_3)$ relative to crystal axes, the effective longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient is:

$$\pi_l = \pi_{11} + 2(\pi_{44} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{11})(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)$$

For a resistor on the (100) plane along $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction in p-type Si, the **maximum piezoresistive coefficient** is achieved:

$$\pi_l = \frac{1}{2}(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Values (Memorize for KTU):**
> - p-type Si: $\pi_{44} \approx 138.1 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (largest in the tensor)
> - n-type Si: $\pi_{11} \approx -102.2 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (dominant along $\langle 100 \rangle$)

## 2.4 The Wheatstone Bridge — Signal Conditioning

Piezoresistive sensors produce a **tiny fractional change in resistance** (typically $\Delta R / R \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$). A single resistor cannot easily be measured. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts the small $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential voltage.

### Quarter-Bridge Configuration

A single active piezoresistor $R_1$ is placed in one arm of a bridge with three fixed resistors. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\right)$$

For a balanced bridge where initially $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, and $R_1$ changes by $\Delta R$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}$$

### Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge Configurations

| Bridge Type | Active Resistors | Sensitivity Multiplier | Output Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quarter | 1 | 1× | $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ |
| Half | 2 (one in tension, one in compression) | 2× | $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ |
| Full | 4 (two in tension, two in compression) | 4× | $V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The full-bridge configuration is the standard in commercial pressure sensors and accelerometers because it **doubles the signal, cancels common-mode temperature errors, and rejects power supply noise**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 EXAM**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law for Resistor | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance in Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | Strain Definition | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Stress Definition | $\sigma = F / A$ | Pressure units (Pa) |
| 4 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | E = Young's Modulus (Pa) |
| 5 | Gauge Factor (General) | $G = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Gauge Factor (Decomposed) | $G = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ | Sum of geometric + intrinsic |
| 7 | Piezoresistor Output (Si) | $G \approx \pi_l \cdot E$ | Approximation for Si |
| 8 | Resistance Change | $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Linearized response |
| 9 | Quarter-Bridge Output | $V_{out} = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Excitation $V_{ex}$ in Volts |
| 10 | Full-Bridge Output | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Maximum sensitivity |
| 11 | Piezoresistive Tensor Relation | $\Delta \rho_i / \rho = \pi_{ij} \sigma_j$ | Tensor form |
| 12 | Longitudinal $\pi_l$ (Si) | $\pi_l = \pi_{11} + 2(\pi_{44} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{11})(l_1^2 l_2^2 + \ldots)$ | Cubic crystal |
| 13 | Transverse $\pi_t$ (Si) | $\pi_t = \pi_{12} - (\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{44})(l_1^2 m_1^2 + \ldots)$ | Perpendicular to current |
| 14 | TC of Gauge Factor | $G(T) = G_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | Temperature compensation |
| 15 | Pressure–Strain Relation (Diaphragm) | $\varepsilon = \frac{3P a^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{8 E h^2}$ | For circular diaphragm |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, the absolute value symbol has been written as `\vert` style for safety (e.g., $\vert \Delta R \vert / R$). All bracketed quantities are standard.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensors, TPMS (Tire Pressure Monitoring), airbag crash sensors.
- **Medical:** Invasive blood pressure catheters, intracranial pressure sensors, infusion pumps.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone barometers, fitness tracker altimeters, MEMS microphones (with diaphragm).
- **Industrial:** HVAC pressure transmitters, flow meters, level sensors in tanks.
- **Aerospace:** Altimeters, flight control surface strain monitoring, rocket engine chamber pressure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
