# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are framed per **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment norms: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice. Mark split for Part B is 7 + 7.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

*"List any three disadvantages of using an Ion-Selective Electrode (ISE) for continuous monitoring of $\text{Na}^+$ in blood."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Slope deviation from Nernstian value** — Real ISEs show $50\text{–}57\,\text{mV/decade}$ instead of the ideal $59.16\,\text{mV/decade}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$ due to non-ideal activity coefficients in the ionophore-doped PVC membrane.

2. **Reference electrode drift** — The liquid-junction potential at the salt bridge varies by $0.1\text{–}2\,\text{mV/day}$, causing baseline drift in long-term continuous monitoring. Solid-state reference electrodes mitigate this.

3. **Interference from other ions (selectivity loss)** — The Nicolsky-Eisenman equation accounts for this; e.g., $\text{K}^+$ interferes with $\text{Na}^+$ ISE with selectivity coefficient $K_{\text{Na,K}}^{\text{pot}} \sim 10^{-2}$.

*(Award 1 mark per correctly stated disadvantage with brief quantitative justification.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

*"Give two applications and one disadvantage of SPR-based biosensors."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Applications:**
1. **Real-time, label-free kinetics** in drug discovery (Biacore) — measures $k_{on}$, $k_{off}$, and $K_D$ without fluorescent or radioactive labels.
2. **Foodborne pathogen detection** (e.g., *Salmonella* in milk) — antibody-functionalized gold surface captures target, resonance shift is proportional to bound mass.

**Disadvantage:**
- **High instrument cost ( $\sim \$50\text{k–}200\text{k}$ )** and **sensitivity to bulk refractive index changes** (temperature fluctuations cause $50\,\text{nm/°C}$ resonance shift), limiting field deployment. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern:** Each Part B question is internally divided into (a) and (b), typically 7 + 7. Always write sub-parts in the same numerical order as the question. Do not mix them.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Clark-type dissolved oxygen sensor**. List its **three main disadvantages** and explain the mitigation strategy for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **glucose oxidase (GOx) biosensor** is to be designed for subcutaneous continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). The enzyme has $K_M = 10\,\text{mM}$ and $V_{max} = 60\,\mu\text{A}$ at $37^\circ\text{C}$. (i) Calculate the current response at blood glucose concentrations of $3\,\text{mM}$ (hypoglycemia), $5\,\text{mM}$ (normal fasting), and $15\,\text{mM}$ (postprandial peak). (ii) Comment on the suitability of this sensor's linear range for the entire physiological range $2\text{–}20\,\text{mM}$. (iii) Suggest **two engineering modifications** to extend the linear range. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Construction & Working (4 marks):**

A Clark-type dissolved oxygen sensor consists of:
- A **cathode** (Pt or Au) and an **anode** (Ag/AgCl) immersed in an **electrolyte** (typically KCl).
- The electrodes are separated from the sample by a **gas-permeable membrane** (PTFE or polypropylene, thickness $\sim 10\text{–}25\,\mu\text{m}$).
- A polarization voltage of $\approx -0.7\,\text{V}$ is applied between the cathode and anode (with respect to Ag/AgCl).

**Working principle:** $\text{O}_2$ from the sample diffuses through the gas-permeable membrane and is reduced at the Pt cathode:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

The resulting **reduction current** is proportional to the dissolved $\text{O}_2$ concentration (Fick's law gives a linear relationship in the steady state). The Ag anode is oxidized to balance the charge:

$$\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl} + e^-$$

**Disadvantages & Mitigations (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Disadvantage: Slow response time ($\tau_{90} \sim 30\text{–}60\,\text{s}$)** due to diffusion through the gas-permeable membrane.
   - *Mitigation:* Reduce membrane thickness to $5\,\mu\text{m}$ and use a micro-cathode ($< 25\,\mu\text{m}$ diameter) to minimize the diffusion layer.

2. **Disadvantage: Consumes $\text{O}_2$ during measurement**, perturbing the local concentration in low-DO samples (e.g., anaerobic bioreactors).
   - *Mitigation:* Use a pulsed-amperometric mode with low duty cycle (e.g., $0.1\%$ on-time) so that the net consumption is negligible.

3. **Disadvantage: Flow-dependence** of the diffusion-limited current.
   - *Mitigation:* Use a stirred-cell configuration or incorporate a flow-through housing with defined hydrodynamics.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Current Calculations (3 marks):**

Using Michaelis-Menten:

$$v = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

At $[S] = 3\,\text{mM}$:

$$v(3) = \frac{60 \times 3}{10 + 3} = \frac{180}{13} = 13.85\,\mu\text{A}$$

At $[S] = 5\,\text{mM}$:

$$v(5) = \frac{60 \times 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{300}{15} = 20.00\,\mu\text{A}$$

At $[S] = 15\,\text{mM}$:

$$v(15) = \frac{60 \times 15}{10 + 15} = \frac{900}{25} = 36.00\,\mu\text{A}$$

**(ii) Suitability Comment (2 marks):**

The Michaelis-Menten response is **linear only for $[S] \ll K_M$**, i.e., $[S] \le 1\,\text{mM}$ (using the $[S]/K_M \le 0.1$ criterion). For the physiological range $2\text{–}20\,\text{mM}$, the sensor is operating in the **transition-to-saturation regime** ($0.2\,K_M \le [S] \le 2\,K_M$). This means:
- **At 2 mM:** $v = 10\,\mu\text{A}$ — small signal, more susceptible to noise.
- **At 20 mM:** $v \approx 40\,\mu\text{A}$ — saturation begins.
- The **calibration is non-linear** and requires a look-up table or polynomial fit in firmware.

**(iii) Two Engineering Modifications (2 marks):**

1. **Use a mass-transport limiting membrane (outer diffusion layer)** with controlled permeability, so that $[S]$ reaching the enzyme layer is much less than the bulk $[S]$. This artificially lowers the effective $[S]/K_M$ ratio and **linearizes** the response over a wider range.

2. **Use a low-$K_M$ enzyme variant or a co-immobilized catalase** to scavenge $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ and shift the apparent $K_M$ upward, extending the linear range. Alternatively, employ **redox mediation (second-generation biosensor)** to decouple the rate from oxygen concentration entirely.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a)** Compare **electrochemical, optical, and acoustic (QCM) biosensors** in terms of: (i) transducer principle, (ii) one key advantage each, and (iii) one key disadvantage each. Identify the most suitable class for a **field-deployable, low-cost glucose monitor for rural diabetic screening**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **potentiometric $\text{NH}_3$ gas sensor** (Severinghaus type) is used to measure the partial pressure of $\text{NH}_3$ in industrial stack emissions. The sensor has a Nernstian slope of $59.16\,\text{mV/decade}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$. The stack temperature is $45^\circ\text{C}$ and the calibration reports a slope of $61.5\,\text{mV/decade}$. (i) Calculate the **percent deviation** from Nernstian behavior at $45^\circ\text{C}$. (ii) State **two environmental factors** that would degrade the sensor's accuracy in the field and how they can be mitigated. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Electrochemical | Optical (SPR/Fluorescence) | Acoustic (QCM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transducer principle** | Analyte participates in redox reaction → current/voltage change | Analyte binding changes refractive index or fluorescence on surface | Analyte mass loading shifts quartz resonance frequency (Sauerbrey) |
| **Key advantage** | Low cost, low power ($\mu\text{W}$), portable | Label-free, real-time, high selectivity | Picogram-level mass sensitivity, simple electronics |
| **Key disadvantage** | Interference from co-existing electroactive species | High cost, ambient light interference, photobleaching | Sensitive to temperature and viscosity of liquid medium |
| **Typical LoD** | $\mu\text{M}$ range | $\text{nM}$ to $\text{pM}$ | $\text{ng/cm}^2$ mass sensitivity |

**Field-Deployable Selection (2 marks):**

For **rural diabetic screening**, the **electrochemical amperometric biosensor** is most suitable because:
- **Lowest cost** (strip + meter $\sim \$0.50$ per test vs. $\$50$ for optical).
- **Battery-powered** with simple electronics (no laser, no PMT, no RF oscillator).
- **Robust to field conditions** (vibration, dust, light) compared to optical sensors.

The QCM requires careful temperature stabilization (oven to $\pm 0.1^\circ\text{C}$) and the SPR requires a laser source — both impractical in rural clinics.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Percent Deviation (4 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate ideal Nernstian slope at $T = 45^\circ\text{C} = 318\,\text{K}$.**

Convert to Kelvin and substitute:

$$S_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{0.19843 \times T}{z} = \frac{0.19843 \times 318}{1}$$

$$S_{\text{ideal}} = 63.10\,\text{mV/decade}$$

**Step 2: Compute deviation:**

$$\% \text{Deviation} = \frac{S_{\text{meas}} - S_{\text{ideal}}}{S_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Deviation} = \frac{61.5 - 63.10}{63.10} \times 100 = \frac{-1.60}{63.10} \times 100 \approx -2.54\%$$

**Step 3: Interpretation (1 mark):**

A deviation of $-2.54\%$ is **within the IUPAC-acceptable tolerance of $\pm 5\%$** for a clinical-grade ISE. The sub-Nernstian behavior is attributable to the temperature dependence of the internal electrolyte activity and to the gas-permeable membrane's variable permeability with temperature.

**(ii) Two Environmental Factors & Mitigations (2 marks):**

1. **Temperature fluctuations in the stack** — Slow response due to thermal mass of electrolyte; mitigation: integrate a thermistor and apply on-board Nernst-slope temperature compensation:
$$S(T) = S(298) \times \frac{T}{298}$$
2. **Interference from acidic gases ($\text{SO}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{CO}_2$)** — These dissolve in the internal electrolyte and alter pH; mitigation: install an **ascorbate/acid-scrubber pre-filter** (e.g., $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$ impregnated membrane) that selectively removes acidic interferents while passing $\text{NH}_3$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Module 3 — Disadvantages & Applications:**
>
> 1. **"Vague disadvantage" trap:** Writing "the sensor is not accurate" loses 1–2 marks. Always quantify (e.g., "drift of 2 mV/day", "LoD of 50 $\mu$M, insufficient for sub-$\mu$M trace analysis").
> 2. **"Application without context" trap:** Writing "used in medical field" is incomplete. Always specify the analyte, the matrix, and the *reason* this technology is chosen.
> 3. **"Formula without units" trap:** All Nernst, LoD, and Michaelis-Menten answers **must include units** in the final numerical result. A unitless number typically loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 4. **"No trade-off analysis" trap:** Applications questions often have a "compare and recommend" sub-part. Always end with a one-sentence *trade-off statement*: *"Chosen over X because Y, despite Z."*
> 5. **"Mixing sub-parts" trap:** For a 7+7 question, students often write a continuous essay. Use clear headings **(a)** and **(b)** to fetch full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High Density):**

- **Disadvantages of Electrochemical Sensors:** Electrode fouling, reference drift, ionic-strength dependence, oxygen dependence (first-gen biosensors), pH sensitivity, slow response at low ionic strength.
- **Disadvantages of Optical Sensors:** Photobleaching, ambient light interference, indicator leaching, high cost, bulk RI sensitivity (SPR), temperature sensitivity.
- **Disadvantages of Biosensors:** Short shelf/operational life, thermal fragility, pH sensitivity, fouling, batch-to-batch variability, sterilization impossible, irreversible binding (immunosensors), long incubation time.
- **Nernstian slope at 298 K for $z=1$:** $S = 59.16\,\text{mV/decade}$. Universal: $S = 0.19843\,T/z$ in mV/decade.
- **LoD formula:** $\text{LoD} = 3\sigma_{\text{blank}} / S_{\text{cal}}$, reported in molarity.
- **Michaelis-Menten:** Linear only for $[S] \le 0.1\,K_M$.
- **Cottrell:** $i \propto t^{-1/2}$ for planar diffusion; useful for sensor characterization.
- **Stern-Volmer:** $I_0/I = 1 + K_{SV}[Q]$ — basis of optical $\text{O}_2$ sensors.
- **SPR:** $\Delta\theta = S_n \cdot \Delta n$; gold film 50 nm at 633 nm; sensitivity $\sim 100^\circ/\text{RIU}$.
- **Applications Mapping (must memorize):** pH-ISE → biopharma; Clark $\text{O}_2$ → wastewater/BOD; GOx amperometric → CGM; SPR → drug kinetics; Taguchi $\text{SnO}_2$ → LPG detector; $\text{pCO}_2$ optode → blood gas; DNA biosensor → food pathogen.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** *No sensor is universally good — match the disadvantage to the deployment environment.*
- **Mitigation Toolkit:** Membrane protection (Nafion, cellulose acetate), reference stabilization (solid-state), enzyme stabilization (PEG, trehalose, lyophilization), temperature compensation (on-board thermistor + Nernst correction), pulsed measurement modes, multi-channel referencing.
- **Numerical constants to memorize:** $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$, $F = 96485\,\text{C/mol}$, $RT/F$ at $298\,\text{K} = 25.693\,\text{mV}$, $2.303\,RT/F = 59.16\,\text{mV}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators - Definition and Classification (Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Actuators - Definition and Classification (Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Actuator (KTU 2024 Official Definition):** An **actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts an input control signal — typically electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, or chemical energy — into a **physical output in the form of motion, force, torque, or displacement**. It is the *physical execution* counterpart of a sensor in any measurement-and-control loop.

In any closed-loop **Mechatronic / Automation system**, sensors *perceive* the physical world, controllers *decide* the corrective action, and **actuators *execute*** that decision by producing the required mechanical output.

### The Energy Transformation Chain

$$
\text{Input Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Actuator}} \text{Mechanical Output (Force / Motion / Torque)}
$$

| Energy Domain | Input Signal Form | Output Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | Voltage / Current | Rotary / Linear Motion |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized Fluid Flow | Linear Force / Torque |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed Air | Linear / Rotary Motion |
| **Thermal** | Heat | Expansion / Contraction |
| **Chemical** | Reaction | Gas Generation / Volume Change |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Human Body Analogy:** Think of a sensor as your **eyes, ears, and skin** (they detect stimuli). Think of the brain/CPU as the **decision-making unit**. Now think of an actuator as your **muscles and hands** — they receive the electrical nerve signal from the brain and physically *act* upon the world. Without muscles, your brain's decision to "pick up a pen" would be useless.

A more engineering-friendly analogy is the **steering system of a car**:
- The driver turns the steering wheel (analogous to a control signal).
- Hydraulic fluid is pressurized (energy domain conversion).
- The steering rack moves left or right (mechanical actuation).
- The wheels turn — this physical motion is the actuator's output.

## 1.3 Role of Actuators in a Mechatronic System

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A complete KTU answer should *always* position the actuator as the **final control element** in the chain: **Sensor → Signal Conditioning → Controller → Amplifier/Driver → Actuator → Mechanical Load**.

A typical block sequence is:
1. **Input Energy Source** (battery, pump, compressor).
2. **Signal Source** (controller, PLC, microcontroller).
3. **Power Conversion Stage** (amplifier, valve, relay).
4. **Actuator** (motor, cylinder, solenoid).
5. **Mechanical Load** (the object being moved).

## 1.4 Performance Metrics (Highlighted Constants & Standards)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Force** | $F$ | Newton (**N**) | 1 N – 10 MN |
| **Torque** | $T$ | N·m | 0.01 – 10⁶ N·m |
| **Stroke Length** | $s$ | mm | 1 mm – 5 m |
| **Operating Pressure** | $P$ | bar | **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** |
| **Response Time** | $t_r$ | ms | 1 ms – 1 s |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | dimensionless | 0.3 – 0.95 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Pressure:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 100 \text{ kPa}$. Hydraulic systems typically operate at **100–350 bar**, whereas pneumatic systems are limited to **4–8 bar** for safety reasons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Operating Principle of an Actuator

Regardless of energy domain, every actuator follows the same three-step internal logic:

- **Step 1 — Energy Reception:** The actuator receives energy in a usable form (voltage, pressure, heat).
- **Step 2 — Energy Conversion:** Internal mechanisms (magnetic field, fluid pressure, thermal expansion) convert this energy into mechanical force.
- **Step 3 — Mechanical Output Delivery:** The mechanical force is delivered to a load via a shaft, rod, piston, or armature, producing **linear motion**, **rotary motion**, or **oscillatory motion**.

## 2.2 Why Classify Actuators?

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Criteria Used by KTU 2024:** Actuators are classified by **(i) energy source**, **(ii) motion type**, and **(iii) control method**. For Module 4, the syllabus emphasizes **energy-source-based classification**, which yields three primary families: **Electric, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic**.

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Each Class

### 2.3.1 Electric Actuators

- Convert **electrical energy** into **mechanical motion** using electromagnetic or electrostatic principles.
- **Sub-classes:** DC motors, AC motors, stepper motors, servo motors, solenoids, piezoelectric actuators.
- *How it works:* Current flowing through a coil creates a magnetic field that interacts with a permanent magnet or ferromagnetic core, producing **Lorentz force** $F = B I L \sin(\theta)$.
- *Advantages:* High precision, clean operation, easy computer control, fast response.
- *Disadvantages:* Limited force/torque density compared to fluid power.

### 2.3.2 Hydraulic Actuators

- Use a **pressurized incompressible liquid** (typically mineral oil) as the working medium.
- **Sub-classes:** Hydraulic cylinders (linear), hydraulic motors (rotary).
- *How it works:* A pump delivers oil at high pressure to a cylinder; pressure acts on the piston area to generate force $F = P \cdot A$.
- *Advantages:* Highest force-to-size ratio, smooth motion under heavy load, self-lubricating.
- *Disadvantages:* Leakage risk, requires pump and reservoir, noisy, expensive.

### 2.3.3 Pneumatic Actuators

- Use a **compressible gas** (typically air) at moderate pressure (4–8 bar).
- **Sub-classes:** Pneumatic cylinders, pneumatic motors, air muscles (McKibben).
- *How it works:* Compressed air enters the chamber and pushes the piston/vanes; force $F = P \cdot A$ is again the governing equation, but because air is compressible, motion is **softer and less precise**.
- *Advantages:* Cheap, lightweight, fast, safe in explosive environments (no sparks).
- *Disadvantages:* Low force capability, requires compressor, air compressibility causes position drift.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas** — memorize the units and boundary conditions; examiners frequently test conversions between **bar ↔ Pa** and **hp ↔ kW**.

| # | Formula | Description | Domain | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F / A$ | Pressure = Force / Piston area | Hydraulic / Pneumatic | Pa, N, m² |
| 2 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Output force from cylinder | Hydraulic / Pneumatic | N, Pa, m² |
| 3 | $P_{out} = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical power (linear) | All | W, N, m/s |
| 4 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power (rotary) | Electric / Hydraulic motor | W, N·m, rad/s |
| 5 | $V = E - I \cdot R_a$ | DC motor armature equation | Electric | V, A, Ω |
| 6 | $T = K_t \cdot I$ | Motor torque from current | Electric | N·m, A |
| 7 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF from speed | Electric | V, rad/s |
| 8 | $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$ | Efficiency | All | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | Hydraulic / Pneumatic | m³/s, m², m/s |
| 10 | $\omega = 2 \pi N / 60$ | Angular velocity | Rotary | rad/s, RPM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** When calculating **cylinder force**, students often forget to subtract the rod-side area. The full formula is:
> $$
> F_{retract} = P \cdot (A_p - A_r)
> $$
> where $A_p$ is the piston area and $A_r$ is the rod area. The **extend force** uses $A_p$ alone.

## 2.5 Comparative Engineering Utility

| Field of Application | Preferred Actuator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **CNC Machine Tool Feed** | Electric (servo) | High precision, position feedback |
| **Earth-mover / Excavator** | Hydraulic | Very high force requirement |
| **Food Packaging Line** | Pneumatic | Clean, fast, intrinsically safe |
| **Robotic Arm Joint** | Electric (BLDC servo) | Compact, accurate, programmable |
| **Aircraft Flight Control** | Hydraulic (redundant electric) | High power density, reliability |
| **Punch Press** | Hydraulic | Sustained full force at dead-center |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Generated by a Hydraulic/Pneumatic Cylinder

We will derive the governing force equation for a double-acting cylinder, starting from Newton's laws applied to the piston.

### Step 1 — Define the piston geometry
Let the **bore diameter** be $D$ and the **rod diameter** be $d$.

The full piston area is:
$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

The annular (rod-side) area is:
$$
A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Pascal's pressure principle
The pressurized fluid exerts a force normal to every surface it contacts. On the piston cap, the **extending force** is:

$$
F_{ext} = P_{sup} \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_r
$$

where $P_{sup}$ is the supply-line pressure (acting on the cap side) and $P_{ret}$ is the return-line pressure (acting on the rod side).

### Step 3 — Simplify for the standard KTU assumption
Assume the return line discharges to a near-zero-pressure tank (atmospheric for pneumatic, reservoir for hydraulic). Then $P_{ret} \approx 0$, giving:

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

This is the form the KTU exam expects.

### Step 4 — Derive the retracting force
For the retracting stroke, supply pressure acts on the rod-side annular area:

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_r = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

### Step 5 — Subtract the form to get the force ratio
Divide the two expressions:

$$
\frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2} > 1
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This proves that the **extending force is always greater** than the retracting force for a double-acting cylinder, an exam-favorite result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mechanical Power of a Linear Actuator

### Step 1 — Start from the definition of work
For a constant force $F$ moving through a stroke $s$:

$$
W = F \cdot s
$$

### Step 2 — Convert work to power using time
Power is work per unit time:

$$
P_{mech} = \frac{W}{t} = F \cdot \frac{s}{t}
$$

### Step 3 — Recognize velocity
Since $v = s / t$:

$$
P_{mech} = F \cdot v
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the force from a hydraulic cylinder
Using $F = P \cdot A$ and $v = Q / A$ (where $Q$ is the flow rate):

$$
P_{mech} = (P \cdot A) \cdot \left( \frac{Q}{A} \right) = P \cdot Q
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This yields the elegant result that **fluid-power mechanical output** equals **pressure × volumetric flow rate**, paralleling the electrical result $P = V \cdot I$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DC Motor Speed-Torque Relationship

### Step 1 — Armature circuit equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)
$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

where $E_b$ is the back-EMF, $I_a$ is armature current, and $R_a$ is armature resistance.

### Step 2 — Back-EMF proportional to speed
$$
E_b = K_e \cdot \omega
$$

### Step 3 — Torque proportional to current
$$
T = K_t \cdot I_a
$$

where $K_t = K_e$ in SI units (motor constant, in N·m/A).

### Step 4 — Solve for current
$$
I_a = \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a}
$$

### Step 5 — Express torque as a function of speed
$$
T = K_t \cdot \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a}
$$

This is the **linear torque–speed curve** every DC motor obeys.

### Step 6 — Identify the two key operating extremes

**No-load speed** ($\omega_{nl}$, where $T = 0$): set $V - K_e \omega = 0$:

$$
\omega_{nl} = \frac{V}{K_e}
$$

**Stall torque** ($T_{st}$, where $\omega = 0$): set $\omega = 0$ in the torque equation:

$$
T_{st} = \frac{K_t \cdot V}{R_a}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** These two points define the motor's linear operating envelope. KTU frequently plots this graph and asks students to identify the **safe operating region**, **rated torque**, and **rated speed** at e.g. 25% of stall torque.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Electricity | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| **Operating Pressure** | n/a | 100–350 bar | 4–8 bar |
| **Force Density (N/kg)** | 50–200 | 1000–5000 | 200–800 |
| **Speed Range** | 0.1–10000 RPM | 0.01–3000 RPM | 0.1–10000 RPM |
| **Position Accuracy** | High ($\mu$m) | Medium (mm) | Low (cm) |
| **Compressibility of Medium** | n/a | Negligible | High |
| **Response Time** | 1–10 ms | 10–100 ms | 5–50 ms |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Noise** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Cleanliness** | High (no leak) | Poor (oil leak) | High (air leak OK) |
| **Typical Efficiency** | 70–95% | 40–70% | 30–50% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree (Strictly Alphanumeric Node IDs)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[ACTUATORS]
    rootA --> classB[Classification by Energy Source]
    classB --> elecC[Electric Actuators]
    classB --> hydrD[Hydraulic Actuators]
    classB --> pneuE[Pneumatic Actuators]

    elecC --> elecF[Linear Type]
    elecC --> elecG[Rotary Type]

    elecF --> elecF1[Solenoid]
    elecF --> elecF2[Linear Stepper]
    elecF --> elecF3[Piezoelectric Stack]

    elecG --> elecG1[DC Motor]
    elecG --> elecG2[AC Induction Motor]
    elecG --> elecG3[Brushless DC Motor]
    elecG --> elecG4[Servo Motor]

    hydrD --> hydrF[Linear Cylinder]
    hydrD --> hydrG[Rotary Motor]
    hydrD --> hydrH[Rotary Vane]

    pneuE --> pneuF[Single Acting Cylinder]
    pneuE --> pneuG[Double Acting Cylinder]
    pneuE --> pneuH[Pneumatic Motor]
    pneuE --> pneuI[Air Muscle McKibben]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Input Energy Source]
    ctlB[Controller PLC MCU]
    drvC[Driver Amplifier]
    actD[Actuator]
    loadE[Mechanical Load]
    fbF[Feedback Sensor]

    srcA --> drvC
    ctlB --> drvC
    drvC --> actD
    actD --> loadE
    loadE -.-> fbF
    fbF -.-> ctlB
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows = forward power/signal path. Dashed arrows = feedback path. The **closed loop** is formed by the feedback sensor reporting actual load position back to the controller for error correction.

## 4.3 Force-Pressure-Velocity Functional Block for a Hydraulic Cylinder

```mermaid
graph TD
    pumpA[Variable Displacement Pump]
    valvB[Direction Control Valve 4 by 3]
    cylC[Double Acting Cylinder]
    tankD[Reservoir]

    pumpA -- High Pressure Oil --> valvB
    valvB -- Port A Extend Stroke --> cylC
    cylC -- Port B Return Oil --> valvB
    valvB -- Exhaust --> tankD
    tankD -- Suction Line --> pumpA
```

> **Functional Logic:** When the directional valve shifts to the left position, pressurized oil enters **Port A** of the cylinder and extends the rod; oil from **Port B** returns to the tank. The four-port, three-position design allows hold, extend, and retract modes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an actuator. Mention the three primary classifications of actuators based on energy source.**

**Model Answer:**
An **actuator** is a device that converts an input control signal — typically in the form of electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy — into a physical output such as **force, torque, displacement, or rotary motion**. It is the *final control element* in any automated system.

Based on the **source of input energy**, actuators are classified into three primary types:

1. **Electric Actuators** — use electrical energy (e.g., DC motor, stepper motor, solenoid).
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — use pressurized incompressible liquid (e.g., hydraulic cylinder).
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — use compressed gas / air (e.g., pneumatic cylinder, air motor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Word-Count Strategy for 3 marks:** Definition = 1.5 marks, Classification list with one example each = 1.5 marks. Aim for 60–80 words.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between hydraulic and pneumatic actuators in terms of working fluid, operating pressure, force capability, and precision.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Working fluid** | Incompressible oil | Compressible air |
| **Operating pressure** | High (100–350 bar) | Low (4–8 bar) |
| **Force capability** | Very high (suitable for heavy load) | Low to medium |
| **Precision** | Moderate (mm level) | Low (cm level due to air compressibility) |
| **Cost** | High (pump + reservoir) | Low (only compressor needed) |

**Conclusion:** Hydraulic actuators are chosen where **high force and stiffness** are required, whereas pneumatic actuators are chosen for **lightweight, low-cost, and intrinsically safe** applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Question – 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **force generated by a double-acting hydraulic cylinder** in both the extending and retracting strokes. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a **bore diameter of 100 mm** and a **rod diameter of 60 mm**. It is supplied with oil at a **pressure of 120 bar**. Calculate:
- (i) The extending force.
- (ii) The retracting force.
- (iii) The force ratio $F_{ext} / F_{ret}$.
- (iv) The mechanical power output when the piston extends at a velocity of **0.25 m/s**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry setup.**
Let the bore diameter = $D$, rod diameter = $d$.

Full piston (cap-side) area:
$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Annular (rod-side) area:
$$
A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's law.**
The pressure $P$ acts uniformly on the piston face, generating force:
$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

**Step 3 — Extending stroke (assumptions: $P_{ret} = 0$, frictionless seal):**
$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = \frac{P \pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Retracting stroke:**
$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_r = \frac{P \pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Force ratio:**
$$
\frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}
$$

**Assumptions stated:** incompressible fluid, frictionless piston, no leakage, return line at atmospheric pressure.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m, $d = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m, $P = 120$ bar $= 120 \times 10^5$ Pa, $v = 0.25$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute areas.**
$$
A_p = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$
$$
A_r = \frac{\pi (0.1^2 - 0.06^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Extending force.**
$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = (120 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3})
$$
$$
F_{ext} = 9.425 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Retracting force.**
$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_r = (120 \times 10^5) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3})
$$
$$
F_{ret} = 6.032 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Force ratio.**
$$
\frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{9.425 \times 10^4}{6.032 \times 10^4} = 1.563
$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical power.**
$$
P_{mech} = F_{ext} \cdot v = (9.425 \times 10^4) \times 0.25
$$
$$
P_{mech} = 2.356 \times 10^4 \text{ W} = 23.56 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $F_{ext} = 94.25$ kN
- $F_{ret} = 60.32$ kN
- $F_{ext} / F_{ret} = 1.563$
- $P_{mech} = 23.56$ kW

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU Model Question – Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of actuators** with a neat block diagram, highlighting electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic types along with two examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **torque–speed characteristic curve**, derive the **no-load speed** and **stall torque** for a separately excited DC motor. A 220 V DC motor has armature resistance 0.5 Ω and motor constant $K_e = 1.5$ V·s/rad. Calculate the no-load speed and stall torque. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Classification

Actuators are devices that **convert input energy into mechanical output**. Classification by energy source:

1. **Electric Actuators** — input is electrical energy; output is mechanical motion.
   - Examples: **DC motor**, **stepper motor**, **solenoid**, **piezoelectric stack**.

2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — input is pressurized incompressible oil; output is high-force motion.
   - Examples: **hydraulic cylinder**, **hydraulic motor**, **vane actuator**.

3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — input is compressed air; output is fast, low-force motion.
   - Examples: **pneumatic cylinder**, **pneumatic motor**, **McKibben air muscle**.

(Include the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 here in the answer script.)

#### Part (b) — DC Motor Derivation

**Step 1 — Armature circuit equation:**
$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF relation:**
$$
E_b = K_e \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Torque–current relation:**
$$
T = K_t I_a
$$

**Step 4 — Torque–speed equation by substitution:**
$$
I_a = \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a} \Rightarrow T = \frac{K_t(V - K_e \omega)}{R_a}
$$

**Step 5 — No-load speed** (set $T = 0$, hence $I_a = 0$):
$$
\omega_{nl} = \frac{V}{K_e}
$$

**Step 6 — Stall torque** (set $\omega = 0$):
$$
T_{st} = \frac{K_t V}{R_a}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical calculation** ($K_e = K_t = 1.5$, $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω):
$$
\omega_{nl} = \frac{220}{1.5} = 146.67 \text{ rad/s} \approx 1400 \text{ RPM}
$$
$$
T_{st} = \frac{1.5 \times 220}{0.5} = 660 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\omega_{nl} = 146.67$ rad/s (≈ 1400 RPM), $T_{st} = 660$ N·m.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert **bar to Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa). Always show the multiplication step.
> 2. **Mixing up rod-side and cap-side areas:** The retracting force uses $A_r$, NOT $A_p$.
> 3. **No-load speed vs rated speed:** No-load speed is theoretical maximum at zero torque. Rated speed is at the design operating point (typically 70–80% of $\omega_{nl}$).
> 4. **Forgetting assumptions:** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** in derivation questions if the student does not explicitly state assumptions like *frictionless piston, no leakage, incompressible fluid*.
> 5. **Missing block diagram:** For 7-mark theory questions, a labelled diagram carries at least **2 marks** — never write a long classification without a sketch.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- An **actuator** converts energy into mechanical output — it is the *executive element* in any mechatronic chain.
- Three primary classifications: **Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic**.
- **Electric actuators** = high precision, low to medium force, clean.
- **Hydraulic actuators** = incompressible oil, very high force, **100–350 bar**, used in heavy machinery.
- **Pneumatic actuators** = compressible air, low force, **4–8 bar**, used in packaging and light automation.
- **Core force equation** (applies to both hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders): $F = P \cdot A$.
- **Standard pressure unit** in actuator problems: **1 bar = $10^5$ Pa**.
- **Cylinder area formulas:** $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$, $A_r = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$.
- **Mechanical power (linear):** $P = F \cdot v$.
- **Mechanical power (rotary):** $P = T \cdot \omega$.
- **DC motor key equations:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, $T = K_t I_a$, $E_b = K_e \omega$.
- **No-load speed:** $\omega_{nl} = V / K_e$.
- **Stall torque:** $T_{st} = K_t V / R_a$.
- **Force ratio of double-acting cylinder:** $F_{ext} / F_{ret} = D^2 / (D^2 - d^2) > 1$.
- **Compressibility** is the single biggest reason pneumatic systems are *less precise* than hydraulic.
- **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in classification questions — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic System - Physical Components and Typical Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic System - Physical Components and Typical Circuit ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Hydraulic System** is a power transmission and control system that uses a pressurized incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) as the working medium to transmit energy from one location to another, performing mechanical work through actuators such as hydraulic cylinders and hydraulic motors. The pressure energy generated by a prime mover-driven pump is converted into linear or rotary mechanical energy at the actuator, with magnitude and direction regulated by a network of control valves. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCECT523 – Sensors and Actuators), hydraulic systems are studied as one of the two principal **fluid power actuator technologies**, the other being pneumatic systems. The subject matter encompasses the **physical components** (hardware elements) and a **typical circuit** (their functional interconnection) that constitute a closed-loop fluid power system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)** > The expected outcomes for this topic are: > - *CO4*: Illustrate the constructional features and working of hydraulic and pneumatic circuits and components. > - Identify the physical components of a hydraulic system, describe their function, and trace the fluid flow in a typical hydraulic circuit. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a hydraulic system as the **"circulatory system of a heavy machine."** Just as the human heart (pump) pushes blood (hydraulic fluid) through arteries (pipes) to reach muscles (actuators) which then move the body, a hydraulic pump pressurizes oil that travels through valves (control valves) and pipelines (hoses) to actuators (cylinders/motors) that perform the actual mechanical work. A relatable real-world analogy is a **water dispenser with a foot pump**: stepping on the pump (pump) pressurizes the water (hydraulic fluid), the water travels through a tube (hose) to a piston (actuator) that pushes the cup up. The key engineering principle is **Pascal's Law** — pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions, so a small force on a small piston can generate a huge force on a large piston. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metric** > - Standard working pressure range: **35 bar to 350 bar** (typical industrial hydraulics) > - Standard hydraulic fluid: **ISO VG 32 / VG 46 mineral oil** (kinematic viscosity 32–46 cSt at 40°C) > - Power transmission efficiency: **85–95 %** (compared to ~50 % for pneumatics) ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot Hydraulic systems are classified based on the type of circuit configuration: | Classification Basis | Type 1 | Type 2 | |---|---|---| | Circuit topology | Open-center (tandem / series) | Closed-center (parallel / load-sensing) | | Pressure regulation | Constant pressure | Variable pressure | | Control philosophy | Throttle control | Meter-out / Meter-in | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Components of a Hydraulic System — Structured Breakdown A complete industrial hydraulic system contains **eight essential categories** of components. Each category is described below in KTU-exam-ready bullet form. #### A. Prime Mover (Energy Source) - **Definition**: An electric motor or an internal combustion (IC) engine that supplies mechanical input power to the pump. - **Function**: Converts electrical (or chemical) energy into mechanical rotational energy. - **Typical Rating**: **0.5 kW to 250 kW** for industrial applications; up to several MW for aerospace/marine hydraulics. - **KTU Expected Note**: The prime mover is *not* a hydraulic component — it is the *driver* of the hydraulic system. #### B. Hydraulic Pump (Heart of the System) - **Definition**: A positive-displacement device that converts mechanical energy from the prime mover into hydraulic (pressure) energy. - **Function**: Draws low-pressure oil from the reservoir and delivers it to the system at high pressure. - **Operating Principle**: Uses **positive displacement** — a fixed volume of fluid is mechanically trapped and forced into the discharge port per cycle. - **Common Types** (exam-favorite): 1. **Gear Pump** (External / Internal) — fixed displacement, low cost, noisy above 150 bar. 2. **Vane Pump** (Fixed / Variable) — quieter than gear, used in machine tools. 3. **Piston Pump** (Axial / Radial) — highest efficiency, used in high-pressure (>250 bar) circuits. > [!NOTE] > **Pump Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU)** > Fixed-displacement pumps are paired with throttle/flow-control valves. Variable-displacement pumps are used in load-sensing and energy-saving circuits. #### C. Reservoir (Tank) - **Function**: Stores hydraulic fluid, dissipates heat, allows air/particle separation, and supports the pump inlet. - **Design Features**: Baffled to prevent oil swirl; mounted below the pump to provide positive suction head; sized for **3 to 5 times** the pump flow rate per minute. - **Accessories**: Breather (air filter), level gauge, thermometer, drain plug, and cleaning cover. #### D. Control Valves (Brain of the System) Control valves regulate the **direction, pressure, and flow** of the fluid. They are sub-classified as: 1. **Directional Control Valves (DCV)** — control fluid path direction. - Number of ports: **2-way, 3-way, 4-way** - Number of positions: **2-position, 3-position** (e.g., 4/3 DCV = 4-port, 3-position) - Actuation methods: Solenoid, manual, pilot-operated, hydraulic, mechanical, electro-hydraulic. 2. **Pressure Control Valves** — limit or regulate system pressure. - **Pressure Relief Valve** (safety valve) — opens at set pressure to protect the system. - **Pressure Reducing Valve** — maintains reduced downstream pressure. - **Sequence Valve** — opens when upstream pressure reaches a set value (for sequenced operations). - **Counterbalance valve** — holds load in place; opens only when pilot pressure is applied. 3. **Flow Control Valves** — regulate flow rate (and hence actuator speed). - **Needle valve** (simple throttling). - **Pressure-compensated flow control valve** — maintains constant flow regardless of pressure variations. - **Meter-in / Meter-out** — controls flow entering or leaving the actuator respectively. #### E. Hydraulic Actuators (Muscles of the System) Two main types: 1. **Hydraulic Cylinder (Linear Actuator)** - Single-acting cylinder: fluid pressure acts on one side only; return by gravity/spring. - **Double-acting cylinder**: fluid pressure drives piston in both directions; provides force in extension and retraction strokes. - Force equation: $F = P \times A$ - Where $P$ = pressure (N/m²) and $A$ = effective piston area (m²). - Cylinder speed: $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$, where $Q$ is the flow rate. 2. **Hydraulic Motor (Rotary Actuator)** - Converts hydraulic pressure energy into rotary mechanical energy. - Types: Gear motor, vane motor, piston motor. - **Torque**: $T = \dfrac{P \times D_m}{2\pi}$, where $D_m$ is the motor displacement (m³/rev). - **Speed**: $N = \dfrac{Q}{D_m}$ (in rev/s). #### F. Filters and Strainers - **Function**: Remove contaminants to protect pumps, valves, and actuators. - **Location**: Suction-line filter (at reservoir outlet), pressure-line filter (downstream of pump), return-line filter (before reservoir). - **Standard**: **ISO 4406** cleanliness code. #### G. Accumulators - **Function**: Store pressurized fluid and release it on demand; absorb pressure surges; provide emergency power. - **Types**: Bladder, piston, diaphragm. - **Pre-charge**: Nitrogen at **60–70 %** of working pressure. #### H. Piping, Hoses, Seals, and Fittings - **Pipes**: Rigid steel tubes (seamless, cold-drawn) for fixed installations. - **Hoses**: Flexible rubber-reinforced hoses for moving/rotating components. - **Seals**: O-rings, U-cups, wipers — prevent internal/external leakage. - **Fittings**: Threaded, flange, or quick-disconnect couplings. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are must-know for KTU ESE Part B (14-mark) questions.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cylinder Force (extension) | $F_{ext} = P \times A_p$ | $P$: Pa, $A_p$: m² | | 2 | Cylinder Force (retraction) | $F_{ret} = P \times (A_p - A_r)$ | $A_r$: rod-side area | | 3 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | $Q$: m³/s | | 4 | Pump Flow Rate | $Q_p = V_d \times N$ | $V_d$: displacement, $N$: rpm | | 5 | Pump Power | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{P \times Q}{60}$ (kW if P in bar, Q in L/min) | | | 6 | Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{P \times D_m}{2\pi}$ | $D_m$: m³/rev | | 7 | Motor Speed | $N = \dfrac{Q}{D_m}$ | rev/s | | 8 | Motor Power | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | watts | | 9 | Pressure (Pascal) | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | | | 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | flow classification in pipes | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Construction Equipment**: Excavators, bulldozers, cranes — heavy lifting and digging. - **Manufacturing**: Presses, injection molding machines, machine tool feed drives. - **Aerospace**: Aircraft flight control surfaces, landing gear actuation. - **Automotive**: Power steering, brake systems (in conjunction with pneumatics). - **Marine**: Deck cranes, hatch covers, steering gear. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force and Velocity Relationship in a Double-Acting Cylinder **Problem Statement**: A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter $D_p = 80$ mm and a rod diameter $D_r = 45$ mm. The pump delivers a flow rate of $Q = 30$ L/min at a pressure of $P = 140$ bar. Determine: - (a) The extension force. - (b) The extension velocity. - (c) The retraction force. - (d) The retraction velocity. **Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI units** $$ \begin{aligned} D_p &= 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m} \\ D_r &= 45 \text{ mm} = 0.045 \text{ m} \\ Q &= 30 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ P &= 140 \text{ bar} = 140 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the piston (cap-end) and rod-end effective areas** $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_p^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^{2} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\ A_r &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D_p^{2} - D_r^{2}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \bigl[(0.080)^{2} - (0.045)^{2}\bigr] \\ A_r &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.006400 - 0.002025) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.004375) \\ A_r &= 3.4359 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the extension force $F_{ext}$** (full area acts on cap end) $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P \times A_p \\ &= (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 70{,}371 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 70.37 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the extension velocity $v_{ext}$** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{ext} &= \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.09947 \text{ m/s} \\ &\approx 5.97 \text{ m/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the retraction force $F_{ret}$** (only annular area acts on rod end) $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ret} &= P \times A_r \\ &= (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \times (3.4359 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 48{,}103 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 48.10 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the retraction velocity $v_{ret}$** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{ret} &= \dfrac{Q}{A_r} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4359 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.14552 \text{ m/s} \\ &\approx 8.73 \text{ m/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Verify the fundamental relationship** In a cylinder, the product of force and velocity equals hydraulic power: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{hyd,ext} &= F_{ext} \times v_{ext} = 70{,}371 \times 0.09947 \approx 7{,}000 \text{ W} = 7.0 \text{ kW} \\ P_{hyd,ret} &= F_{ret} \times v_{ret} = 48{,}103 \times 0.14552 \approx 7{,}000 \text{ W} = 7.0 \text{ kW} \\ P \times Q &= (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 7{,}000 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight**: Power is conserved in an ideal cylinder. Hence, the retraction velocity is *higher* than the extension velocity because the retraction area is *smaller*. This is a frequent conceptual question in Part A (3 marks). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pump and Motor Sizing for a Given Load **Problem Statement**: A hydraulic motor must deliver a torque of $T = 250$ N·m at a speed of $N = 1450$ rpm. The system pressure is $P = 175$ bar. Determine: - (a) The motor displacement $D_m$. - (b) The required pump flow rate $Q$. - (c) The motor power output. **Step 1 — Compute motor displacement $D_m$ from the torque equation** $$ \begin{aligned} T &= \dfrac{P \times D_m}{2\pi} \\ D_m &= \dfrac{2\pi T}{P} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 250}{175 \times 10^{5}} \\ D_m &= \dfrac{1570.80}{1.75 \times 10^{7}} \\ D_m &= 8.976 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} \\ D_m &= 89.76 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute required flow rate $Q$ from the speed equation** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{Q}{D_m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q = N \times D_m \\ \text{Converting } N = 1450 \text{ rpm} &= \dfrac{1450}{60} \text{ rev/s} = 24.167 \text{ rev/s} \\ Q &= 24.167 \times (8.976 \times 10^{-5}) \\ Q &= 2.169 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ Q &= 2.169 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 10^{3} = 130.16 \text{ L/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute motor output power** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{motor} &= \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(with N in rpm)} \\ &= \dfrac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 250}{60} \\ &= 37{,}958 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 37.96 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-verify using $P \times Q$** $$ \begin{aligned} P \times Q &= (175 \times 10^{5}) \times (2.169 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 37{,}958 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight**: Both the torque method and the $P \times Q$ method must yield the same power, confirming energy conservation. Any mismatch indicates an arithmetic or unit-conversion error — a common KTU valuation pitfall. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Function Reference Table | Component | Energy Domain In | Energy Domain Out | Key Parameter | |---|---|---|---| | Prime Mover | Electrical / Chemical | Mechanical (rotational) | Power (kW), Speed (rpm) | | Hydraulic Pump | Mechanical (rotational) | Hydraulic (pressure × flow) | $V_d$ (cc/rev), $P$ (bar), $\eta_{vol}$ | | DCV | Hydraulic | Hydraulic (routed) | Port count, Position count | | Pressure Relief Valve | Hydraulic (variable P) | Hydraulic (clamped P) | Cracking pressure | | Flow Control Valve | Hydraulic (Q variable) | Hydraulic (Q constant) | Orifice size, $C_v$ | | Hydraulic Cylinder | Hydraulic (P×Q) | Mechanical (linear) | Bore, Stroke, Force, Speed | | Hydraulic Motor | Hydraulic (P×Q) | Mechanical (rotational) | $D_m$, Torque, Speed | | Accumulator | Hydraulic (stored) | Hydraulic (released) | Pre-charge, Volume | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Hydraulic System The following block diagram shows the functional flow of energy and fluid in a standard industrial hydraulic system. Each block represents one of the eight component categories introduced in Section 2. ```mermaid flowchart LR PM[PRIME MOVER
Electric Motor or IC Engine] --> PUMP[HYDRAULIC PUMP
Fixed or Variable Displacement] RSVR[RESERVOIR
Oil Storage and Cooling] -->|Suction Line| PUMP PUMP -->|High-Pressure Line| FILT[FILTER
Pressure Line Filter] FILT --> DCV[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE
4/3 Solenoid Operated] DCV -->|Port A| CYL[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER
Linear Actuator] CYL -->|Return Line Port B| DCV DCV -->|Exhaust to Tank| RV[RETURN LINE FILTER] --> RSVR RV --> RSVR PUMP -.Pressure Tapping.-> PCV[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE
Safety Valve] PCV --> RSVR DCV -.Control Signal.-> FCV[FLOW CONTROL VALVE
Throttle Valve] FCV --> DCV ACC[ACCUMULATOR
Bladder Type] -.Backup Supply.-> DCV ``` **How to read this diagram (KTU-exam language):** 1. The **prime mover** drives the pump. 2. The pump **draws oil from the reservoir** through a suction strainer. 3. Oil is delivered to the **directional control valve** through a pressure-line filter. 4. The DCV routes the fluid to the appropriate port (A or B) of the cylinder. 5. Return oil passes through a return-line filter back to the reservoir. 6. The **pressure relief valve** limits the maximum system pressure. 7. The **flow control valve** regulates actuator speed. 8. The **accumulator** provides supplementary flow and absorbs pressure spikes. ### 4.2 Functional State Diagram of a 4/3 DCV (Three Positions) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 s1: Position 1
Neutral Center
All Ports Blocked s2: Position 2
Left Envelope
P to A, B to T s3: Position 3
Right Envelope
P to B, A to T s1 --> s2: Solenoid A Energized s2 --> s1: Solenoid A De-energized s1 --> s3: Solenoid B Energized s3 --> s1: Solenoid B De-energized ``` **KTU Tip**: A *4/3 DCV* has **4 ports** (P, T, A, B) and **3 positions** (center, left, right). The notations P (Pump), T (Tank), A and B (cylinder ports) are standardized as per **ISO 1219**. ### 4.3 Symbolic Representation of Hydraulic Components (ISO 1219) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SYMBOLS[ISO 1219 SYMBOLS] PUMP_SYM["Fixed-Displacement Pump: Circle with Solid Arrow"] MOT_SYM["Hydraulic Motor: Circle with Hollow Arrow"] DCV_SYM["4/3 DCV: Two Adjacent Squares with Arrows"] CYL_SYM["Double-Acting Cylinder: Rectangle with Two Triangles"] FCV_SYM["Throttle Valve: Bow-Tie Shape"] RV_SYM["Pressure Relief Valve: Square with Spring Arrow"] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Tip**: In KTU exam answer sheets, you may use simplified block diagrams as above. For full marks on a "typical hydraulic circuit" question, **label every component, every flow line, and indicate all ports**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **Define a hydraulic system. List any four physical components used in it.** **Model Answer** (60–80 words): > A hydraulic system is a power transmission system that uses a pressurized incompressible fluid to transmit energy and perform mechanical work, based on **Pascal's Law**. The four physical components are: > 1. **Hydraulic Pump** – converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy. > 2. **Directional Control Valve** – controls direction of fluid flow. > 3. **Hydraulic Actuator** (cylinder/motor) – converts hydraulic energy into mechanical work. > 4. **Reservoir** – stores the hydraulic fluid and dissipates heat. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + Pascal's Law mention; 2 marks for any 4 correctly listed components with brief function.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]** **Differentiate between a hydraulic pump and a hydraulic motor. (Any 3 points)** **Model Answer**: | Parameter | Hydraulic Pump | Hydraulic Motor | |---|---|---| | Energy conversion | Mechanical → Hydraulic | Hydraulic → Mechanical | | Direction of fluid | Suction + Discharge | Single inlet + outlet | | Prime mover | Requires external input | Self-actuating from oil | | Pressure direction | Generates pressure | Driven by pressure | | Typical unit | cc/rev displacement | cc/rev displacement | *(3 marks — 1 mark per valid, distinct point.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — From KTU 2024 Model Paper, Module 4** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **physical components and typical hydraulic circuit** used in an industrial machine tool. State the function of each component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm** and a rod diameter of **60 mm**. The pump supplies oil at a flow rate of **40 L/min** and a pressure of **160 bar**. - (i) Calculate the extension force and extension velocity. - (ii) Calculate the retraction force and retraction velocity. - (iii) Determine the hydraulic power delivered to the cylinder. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks** The eight key physical components and their functions are: 1. **Prime Mover (1 mark)** — Electric motor providing mechanical input to the pump. 2. **Reservoir (0.5 mark)** — Stores oil, dissipates heat, supports pump suction. 3. **Hydraulic Pump (1 mark)** — Converts mechanical energy to hydraulic energy; supplies pressurized oil. 4. **Filter (0.5 mark)** — Removes contaminants to protect sensitive components. 5. **Pressure Relief Valve (1 mark)** — Limits maximum system pressure (safety). 6. **Directional Control Valve (1 mark)** — Routes oil to extend or retract the cylinder. 7. **Flow Control Valve (0.5 mark)** — Regulates cylinder speed by throttling flow. 8. **Double-Acting Cylinder (1 mark)** — Linear actuator that performs the actual work. 9. **Accumulator + Piping (0.5 mark)** — Stores energy, smoothens pressure pulses; provides connection. *Students must include a labeled block diagram for full marks (1 mark reserved for diagram).* **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — SI unit conversions (0.5 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} D_p &= 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m} \\ D_r &= 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m} \\ Q &= 40 \text{ L/min} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ P &= 160 \text{ bar} = 1.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute areas (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\ A_r &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} [(0.100)^2 - (0.060)^2] = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.0064) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Extension force and velocity (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P \times A_p = (1.6 \times 10^{7}) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 125{,}664 \text{ N} \approx 125.66 \text{ kN} \\ v_{ext} &= \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.08488 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Retraction force and velocity (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ret} &= P \times A_r = (1.6 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 80{,}424 \text{ N} \approx 80.42 \text{ kN} \\ v_{ret} &= \dfrac{Q}{A_r} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Hydraulic power (1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{hyd} &= P \times Q = (1.6 \times 10^{7}) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-4}) = 10{,}667 \text{ W} \approx 10.67 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification (0 mark — additional credit)**: $$ F_{ext} \times v_{ext} = 125{,}664 \times 0.08488 = 10{,}666 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Alternate]** **(a)** Explain the working of a **hydraulic pump** and **hydraulic motor** with neat sketches. State the difference between a fixed-displacement and variable-displacement pump. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic motor has a displacement of **100 cc/rev** and operates at a pressure of **200 bar** with a flow rate of **90 L/min**. - (i) Find the motor speed in rpm. - (ii) Find the torque developed. - (iii) Find the hydraulic power input and the mechanical power output (assume 90% volumetric and 88% mechanical efficiency). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks** **Hydraulic Pump (3 marks)**: A hydraulic pump is a **positive-displacement device** that traps a fixed volume of fluid and forces (discharges) it into the hydraulic system per cycle. The pump inlet is connected to the reservoir (low pressure), and the outlet to the high-pressure side. On each shaft rotation, a known displacement $V_d$ of oil is delivered. The pump flow rate is $Q = V_d \times N$, where $N$ is the pump speed in rev/s. **Hydraulic Motor (3 marks)**: A hydraulic motor performs the **reverse** function: pressurized oil from the pump enters the motor, pushes against the vanes/pistons/rotor, and creates a turning moment (torque) on the output shaft. The motor speed is $N = Q / D_m$ and the torque is $T = (P \times D_m) / (2\pi)$. **Fixed vs. Variable Displacement Pump (1 mark)**: | Parameter | Fixed-Displacement | Variable-Displacement | |---|---|---| | Displacement $V_d$ | Constant | Adjustable | | Flow control | Achieved by flow/relief valve | Achieved by changing $V_d$ | | Energy efficiency | Lower (throttling losses) | Higher (load-sensing possible) | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Application | Simple presses, jacks | Mobile hydraulics, servo systems | *(Students must include labeled diagrams of a gear pump and a vane/piston motor for full marks.)* **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — SI conversions (0.5 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} D_m &= 100 \text{ cc/rev} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} \\ Q &= 90 \text{ L/min} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ P &= 200 \text{ bar} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Motor speed (1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{Q}{D_m} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 15 \text{ rev/s} \\ N_{rpm} &= 15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ rpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Torque developed (1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} T &= \dfrac{P \times D_m}{2\pi} = \dfrac{(2.0 \times 10^{7}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4})}{2\pi} \\ T &= \dfrac{2000}{6.2832} = 318.31 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Hydraulic power input (1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{hyd,input} &= P \times Q = (2.0 \times 10^{7}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 30{,}000 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Mechanical power output (2 marks)** Overall efficiency $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.90 \times 0.88 = 0.792$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{mech,output} &= \eta_{overall} \times P_{hyd,input} \\ &= 0.792 \times 30{,}000 = 23{,}760 \text{ W} = 23.76 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification (0 mark — bonus)**: $$ P_{mech} = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times 15 \times 318.31 = 30{,}000 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ kW} \text{ (theoretical)} $$ Applied mechanical power (after efficiency): $30 \times 0.792 = 23.76$ kW ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (lose 1–2 marks)**: Students often forget to convert bar → Pa ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa) and L/min → m³/s. Always show the conversion step. > 2. **Confusing Areas (lose 1 mark)**: In double-acting cylinders, students mistakenly use the full bore area for *retraction* force. Use the **annular area** $A_r = A_p - A_{rod}$. > 3. **Missing Block Diagram (lose 2–3 marks)**: In Part (a) of a 14-mark question, *the diagram is mandatory*. A textual description without a labeled block diagram will cap marks at 60–70 %. > 4. **Forgetting Pascal's Law**: Always open your answer by stating the governing principle: *"A hydraulic system works on Pascal's Law, which states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions."* (1 mark reserved for this.) > 5. **Mixing Pump and Motor Equations**: The same $D_m$ appears in *both* $T$ and $N$ equations — students sometimes apply them inconsistently. Memorize: $T \propto P \times D_m$ and $N \propto Q / D_m$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] A hydraulic system transmits power using an **incompressible fluid** under pressure, governed by **Pascal's Law**. - [x] The **eight essential components** are: Prime Mover, Reservoir, Pump, Filter, Control Valves, Actuator, Accumulator, and Piping/Seals. - [x] **Cylinder force** is $F = P \times A$; **cylinder velocity** is $v = Q / A$. - [x] For a **double-acting cylinder**, retraction force uses the **annular area**, not the full bore area. - [x] **Pump flow** $Q = V_d \times N$; **Motor torque** $T = (P \times D_m) / (2\pi)$. - [x] **Hydraulic power** is always $P \times Q$ (with units converted to SI). - [x] **Retraction velocity is always greater than extension velocity** in a double-acting cylinder (smaller area → higher speed). - [x] **Directional Control Valves (DCVs)** are described as **ports/positions** (e.g., 4/3 = 4 ports, 3 positions). - [x] **Pressure Relief Valve** is a *must-have* safety device; **Flow Control Valve** regulates speed; **Counterbalance Valve** holds vertical loads. - [x] Standard **working pressure range** is **35–350 bar**; standard fluid is **ISO VG 32/46 mineral oil**. - [x] **ISO 1219** governs all hydraulic symbols used in circuit diagrams. - [x] Always draw a **neat, labeled block diagram** of the typical circuit in 14-mark answers. - [x] **Energy conservation check**: $F \times v = P \times Q$ — use this to verify any numerical answer. - [x] For KTU Part A (3 marks): definition + 4 components in 60–80 words is the standard format. - [x] For KTU Part B (14 marks): 7 marks for theory + labeled diagram; 7 marks for numerical problem with full SI conversions. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Day Strategy** > - Start with the definition + Pascal's Law (1 line). > - Sketch the block diagram *first*, then describe components. > - In numericals, **convert units to SI in the first step** and write it explicitly. > - Always end a numerical with a **verification** using $P \times Q = F \times v$ or $T \times 2\pi N = P \times Q$. > - Leave **5 minutes** at the end to verify block diagram labels and units.

topics.title: Hydraulic Actuators - Linear Actuators, Rotary Actuators (Gear Motor, Vane Motor)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Actuators - Linear Actuators, Rotary Actuators (Gear Motor, Vane Motor)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydraulic Actuators – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Actuator:** A mechanical device that converts the **hydraulic energy** (pressure energy of a working fluid, typically mineral oil) supplied by a hydraulic pump into **mechanical energy** in the form of linear motion, rotary motion, or oscillatory motion to perform useful work.

Hydraulic actuators are categorized based on the type of mechanical output they produce:

| Category | Output Motion | Typical Devices |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Linear** | Straight-line reciprocating | Hydraulic Cylinders (single-acting, double-acting, telescopic) |
| **Rotary** | Continuous rotary shaft motion | Hydraulic Motors (Gear, Vane, Piston) |
| **Semi-rotary** | Limited angle rotation (≤ 360°) | Rack-and-pinion actuators, Rotary vane actuators |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope (Module 4):** The course PCECT523 specifically requires a focused study of **Linear Hydraulic Actuators** and the two principal classes of **Rotary Hydraulic Actuators** — namely the **External/Internal Gear Motor** and the **Vane Motor**.

### 1.2 Working Principle – The Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Pump Principle:**
> Think of a bicycle pump. When you push the handle, air (the fluid) is compressed inside a narrow cylinder and forced out through a small nozzle. The energy stored in the pressurized air does work against the atmosphere. A hydraulic actuator works on the **exact opposite principle**: instead of *creating* pressure, it *receives* pressurized fluid from a pump (the "heart" of the hydraulic system) and uses that pressure to move a piston or shaft against a load.

In formal terms, a hydraulic system follows **Pascal's Law**:
> *Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid.*

This single principle is what allows a small pump delivering fluid at high pressure to generate enormous mechanical forces when that fluid is channeled into a larger cylinder area.

### 1.3 System Block Diagram — A Hydraulic Actuation Loop

The complete hydraulic actuation chain has four essential blocks:

| Block | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **1. Prime Mover** | Electric motor / Engine | Provides mechanical input power |
| **2. Energy Converter** | Hydraulic Pump | Converts mechanical → hydraulic energy (raises fluid pressure) |
| **3. Control & Distribution** | Valves (DCV, PRV, FCV) | Directs fluid flow and regulates pressure/flow |
| **4. Actuator (Output)** | Cylinder / Motor | Converts hydraulic → mechanical energy (the load) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Hydraulic Metrics to Memorize (KTU High-Weightage):**
> - **System Pressure (P):** 70 – 350 bar (typical industrial), up to 700 bar (heavy-duty)
> - **Operating Temperature:** 30 °C – 80 °C
> - **Hydraulic Fluid:** ISO VG 32, 46, or 68 mineral oil
> - **Density of Oil:** ≈ **860 kg/m³**
> - **Bulk Modulus of Oil:** ≈ **1.4 – 2.0 GPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Hydraulic Actuators — Hydraulic Cylinders

A hydraulic cylinder is a mechanical actuator that produces force and motion in a straight line by using a pressurized hydraulic fluid acting on a piston fitted inside a cylindrical bore.

#### 2.1.1 Classification of Hydraulic Cylinders

**A. Based on the Method of Force Transmission:**

1. **Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC):**
   - Hydraulic fluid acts on **only one side** of the piston.
   - The return stroke is achieved by an external force (gravity, spring, or load weight).
   - Used in: lifting jacks, clamping devices, dump trucks, ejection systems.

2. **Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC):**
   - Hydraulic fluid is supplied alternately to **both sides** of the piston.
   - Both the extension and retraction strokes are powered — providing controlled force in both directions.
   - Used in: machine tools, press machines, injection moulding, robotic arms, excavator booms.

**B. Based on Construction:**

1. **Tie-Rod Cylinder:** Uses external tie-rods to clamp end caps; easily serviceable.
2. **Welded (Mill-Type) Cylinder:** End caps are welded to the barrel; high-pressure, compact, used in heavy industry.
3. **Telescopic Cylinder:** Has multiple nested stages; produces a very long stroke from a short retracted length (used in tipper trucks and cranes).

#### 2.1.2 Working of a Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)

The DAC is the workhorse of industrial hydraulics. It has two ports:
- **Port A (Cap end / Head end):** Fluid enters to push the piston outward → **Extension Stroke**.
- **Port B (Rod end):** Fluid enters to push the piston inward → **Retraction Stroke**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymmetry Effect:** Because the piston rod occupies space on the rod-end side, the effective area on the rod side is **less** than the cap side. This creates an inherent asymmetry in force and velocity between the two strokes — a critical concept in KTU problems.

- **Piston (Bore) Diameter = D**
- **Piston Rod Diameter = d**

**Effective Areas:**
- **Cap-side (Piston-side) Effective Area:**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

- **Rod-side (Annular) Effective Area:**

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

#### 2.1.3 Performance Equations for Linear Actuators

**A. Force Generated (Theoretical):**

The theoretical force on any side of the piston is simply the product of the supply pressure and the effective area on that side.

- **Extension (Cap-side) Force:**

$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

- **Retraction (Rod-side) Force:**

$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

- **Effective Force (after friction losses):**

$$F_{eff} = F_{th} - F_{friction} = F_{th} - \mu \cdot P \cdot A$$

**B. Velocity of Piston:**

Velocity equals the volumetric flow rate divided by the area on the receiving side (conservation of mass / continuity).

- **Extension Velocity:**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_p} = \frac{4 Q_{in}}{\pi D^2}$$

- **Retraction Velocity:**

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_a} = \frac{4 Q_{in}}{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}$$

**C. Power Developed:**

Hydraulic power is the product of pressure and flow rate; mechanical power is force times velocity. Both are equal under ideal (lossless) conditions.

$$P_{hydraulic} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v = P_{mechanical}$$

**D. Cylinder Time (Stroke Time):**

The time required to complete one stroke is the stroke length divided by the average velocity.

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \cdot A}{Q}$$

where **L** = stroke length, **A** = effective area on the receiving side.

**E. Cushioning Pressure (Deceleration in end-position):**

To prevent impact damage at the ends of the stroke, cushioning is provided. The peak cushioning pressure is given by:

$$P_{cushion} = P_s + \frac{m \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot V_c} \cdot \eta$$

where $P_s$ = supply pressure, $m$ = mass of moving load, $V_c$ = cushion volume, $\eta$ = energy transfer efficiency.

#### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility

Linear hydraulic actuators are used wherever high forces and precise linear positioning are required:
- **Automobile assembly lines** (welding, stamping)
- **Aerospace** (flap actuators, landing gear)
- **Steel and rolling mills** (massive press forces)
- **Agricultural machinery** (ploughs, harvesters, tippers)
- **Plastic injection moulding machines** (clamping tonnage)
- **Construction equipment** (excavators, loaders, cranes)

---

### 2.2 Rotary Hydraulic Actuators — Hydraulic Motors

A hydraulic motor is a mechanical actuator that converts hydraulic pressure and flow into continuous rotary torque and angular motion. It is essentially the **reverse** of a hydraulic pump.

#### 2.2.1 Gear Motors

A gear motor converts hydraulic energy into mechanical rotary energy using the meshing action of gears. There are two main types:

**A. External Gear Motor:**
- Two identical external spur gears (one drive, one idler) mesh inside a closely toleranced housing.
- Pressurized fluid enters and fills the spaces between the gear teeth on the inlet side.
- The gears are forced to rotate by the pressure acting on the teeth, carrying fluid from inlet to outlet around the housing periphery.
- Fluid is forced out of the meshing point (where the teeth mesh and the volume shrinks to nearly zero).

**B. Internal Gear Motor:**
- A **rotor** (external gear with fewer teeth) rotates inside an **idler gear** (internal ring gear with more teeth).
- A **crescent-shaped partition** (stationary filler block) separates the inlet and outlet ports.
- Used in lower-pressure, high-flow applications (e.g., steering systems, lubrication).

**Key Characteristics of Gear Motors:**

| Feature | External Gear | Internal Gear |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| **Pressure Range** | Up to 250 bar | Up to 175 bar |
| **Speed Range** | 1000 – 3000 rpm | 100 – 2000 rpm |
| **Efficiency** | High (≈ 90%) | Moderate (≈ 80%) |
| **Noise Level** | Higher | Lower |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Typical Use** | General industrial | Steering, lubrication |

#### 2.2.2 Vane Motors

A vane motor uses **sliding vanes** mounted in a slotted rotor. The rotor is mounted **off-center (eccentrically)** inside a circular stator ring. This eccentricity creates crescent-shaped chambers of varying volume.

**Working Principle:**
- Pressurized fluid enters through the inlet port and pushes on each vane.
- The pressure acting on the projected vane area creates a tangential force that rotates the rotor.
- As the rotor turns, the chamber volume **expands** near the inlet (suction) and **contracts** near the outlet (discharge).
- Fluid is expelled at low pressure.

**Types of Vane Motors:**

1. **Unbalanced Vane Motor:**
   - Single chamber design with offset rotor.
   - High starting torque.
   - Limited to lower pressures (≈ 175 bar).

2. **Balanced Vane Motor:**
   - Two opposed chambers (180° apart), so hydraulic forces on the rotor are balanced.
   - Higher pressures (up to 250 bar).
   - Longer bearing life.

3. **Variable Displacement Vane Motor:**
   - Eccentricity (e) can be varied by an adjustment ring → variable displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Eccentricity" Matters:** The stator-to-rotor offset distance **e** is the single most important geometric parameter. As e increases, chamber volume per revolution increases, raising both torque and displacement per revolution.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following consolidated table is the **MASTER REFERENCE** for all KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems on Hydraulic Actuators. Print this mentally.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Symbol Key |

| 4 | Retraction force | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a$ | N | — |

| 6 | Retraction velocity | $v_{ret} = Q/A_a$ | m/s | — |

| 9 | Stroke time | $t = L \cdot A / Q$ | s | L = stroke length |
| 10 | Gear motor displacement | $V_d = 2 \pi \cdot m \cdot B$ | m³/rev | m = module, B = face width |
| 11 | Gear motor theoretical torque | $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_d / (2 \pi)$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop |
| 12 | Gear motor speed | $N = Q / V_d$ | rev/s | — |
| 13 | Vane motor displacement | $V_d = 2 \pi \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2)$ | m³/rev | R = stator radius, r = rotor radius |
| 14 | Vane motor torque | $T = \Delta P \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2)$ | N·m | — |
| 15 | Vane motor power | $P = T \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W | — |
| 16 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = Q_{th}/Q_{act}$ | — | (ratio, dimensionless) |
| 17 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = T_{act}/T_{th}$ | — | — |
| 18 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | — |

> - 1 **bar** = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa = 14.5 psi
