> - 1 **N·m** = 8.85 lbf·in (rarely needed, but verify)
> - 1 **hp** = 745.7 W

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gear motors** are the **workhorses of mobile hydraulics**: agricultural tractors, skid-steer loaders, fan drives, and conveyor systems all use external gear motors.
- **Vane motors** are dominant in **low-speed, high-torque applications** such as machine tool spindle drives, winches, and injection moulding screw drives.
- **Linear cylinders** drive almost every modern **press, clamp, lift, and excavator** in industry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Piston Areas in a Double-Acting Cylinder

**Setup:** Consider a piston of bore diameter **D** with a piston rod of diameter **d** attached to one side. The piston divides the cylinder bore into two chambers.

**Step 1 — Cap-side (Piston-side) Geometry:**

The cap-end chamber is bounded by a full circular area equal to the bore cross-section.

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

This is the **maximum** area in the cylinder and is engaged during the **extension stroke**.

**Step 2 — Rod-side (Annulus) Geometry:**

The rod-end chamber is annular (ring-shaped) because the piston rod occupies a circular region at the center.

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:**

Factoring out $\pi/4$:

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Area Ratio (Asymmetry Index):**

The ratio of annular area to piston area quantifies the asymmetry:

$$
\frac{A_a}{A_p} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If $d/D = 0.5$ (a typical industrial ratio), then $A_a/A_p = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$, meaning the rod-side has only **75%** of the cap-side area. This results in **25% higher retraction velocity** and **25% lower retraction force** — a fact KTU examiners love to test.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Velocity from First Principles

**Step 1 — Pressure Definition:**

Pressure is defined as normal force per unit area:

$$
P = \frac{F}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P \cdot A
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Cap Side:**

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to Rod Side:**

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Continuity (Flow Conservation):**

For an incompressible fluid, the volumetric flow rate into a chamber equals the rate of volume change of that chamber:

$$
Q = A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = A \cdot v
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Areas:**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} \qquad v_{ret} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}
$$

**Step 7 — Mechanical Power (ideal, lossless):**

$$
P_{mech} = F \cdot v = (P \cdot A) \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right) = P \cdot Q
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conservation Theorem:** This proves the fundamental hydraulic identity $P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$. The hydraulic power delivered by the pump equals the mechanical power produced at the piston — under ideal (lossless) conditions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Gear Motor Torque and Power

**Setup:** Consider an external gear motor with two identical spur gears of **module m** and **face width B**. Let $z$ be the number of teeth on each gear.

**Step 1 — Geometric Displacement Per Revolution:**

The volume of fluid displaced in one full revolution of the gear pair is the volume swept by the teeth between inlet and outlet:

$$
V_d = \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot z \cdot B
$$

(Equivalently, $V_d = 2 \pi \cdot m \cdot B$ per gear pitch — both forms are accepted; the exact form depends on whether counting is per gear or per pair.)

**Step 2 — Theoretical Flow Required:**

$$
Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N
$$

where $N$ is in rev/s. Therefore:

$$
N = \frac{Q_{th}}{V_d}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Torque (Work-Energy Balance):**

The hydraulic energy supplied per revolution equals the mechanical work output per revolution:

$$
\Delta P \cdot V_d = T_{th} \cdot 2 \pi
$$

Solving for theoretical torque:

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2 \pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Power:**

$$
P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2 \pi} \cdot (2 \pi N) = \Delta P \cdot V_d \cdot N = \Delta P \cdot Q_{th}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Efficiencies:**

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = \eta_m \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2 \pi}
$$

$$
N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{Q}{V_d}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_{act} = \eta_o \cdot \Delta P \cdot Q
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Vane Motor Displacement and Torque

**Setup:** Unbalanced single-chamber vane motor with:
- Stator inner radius = **R**
- Rotor outer radius = **r**
- Eccentricity (offset) = **e = R − r**
- Vane width (axial face width) = **B**

**Step 1 — Displacement Per Revolution:**

Integrating the chamber volume over the angular sweep (360°) gives the total swept volume:

$$
V_d = 2 \pi \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Torque Derivation:**

The torque on the rotor is the integral of pressure × moment-arm over the active arc. For a constant pressure drop $\Delta P$ over the active arc:

$$
T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Eccentricity:**

Since $e = R - r$, we have $R^2 - r^2 = (R+r)(R-r) = (R+r) \cdot e$. Thus:

$$
V_d = 2 \pi \cdot B \cdot (R + r) \cdot e
$$

$$
T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot B \cdot (R + r) \cdot e
$$

**Step 4 — Power Output:**

$$
P_{out} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot B \cdot (R + r) \cdot e \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The vane motor torque is **directly proportional** to the eccentricity **e**. By varying **e** (using an adjustment ring), a variable-displacement vane motor can deliver variable torque at variable speed — making it ideal for energy-efficient drives.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Speed-Torque Characteristics

| Operating Condition | Gear Motor | Vane Motor |
|---------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **At maximum pressure, zero flow** | Stall (theoretical) | Stall (theoretical) |
| **Maximum torque** | $T_{max} = T_{th}$ at $\Delta P_{max}$ | $T_{max} = \Delta P_{max} \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2)$ |
| **Maximum speed** | At zero pressure drop (no load) | At zero pressure drop (no load) |
| **Torque-Speed Curve Shape** | Linear (constant $\Delta P$ assumed) | Linear (constant $\Delta P$ assumed) |
| **Self-Priming?** | Yes (gear meshing forces fluid through) | Yes (vane chambers create suction) |
| **Direction Reversal** | Yes (reverse flow) | Yes (reverse flow) |
| **Starting Torque** | High (≈ 90% of $T_{th}$) | High (≈ 95% of $T_{th}$) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the fluid-power architecture and component interactions of a typical double-acting hydraulic cylinder, as used in industrial machine tools.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  PUMP[Reservoir and Pump Unit] -- Pressurized Oil --> DCV[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3]
  DCV -- Port A Cap End --> CHAMBER_A[Cap End Chamber A_p]
  DCV -- Port B Rod End --> CHAMBER_B[Rod End Chamber A_a]
  CHAMBER_A -- Force F_ext --> PISTON[Double Acting Piston]
  CHAMBER_B -- Force F_ret --> PISTON
  PISTON -- Linear Motion --> ROD[Piston Rod Output]
  CHAMBER_A -- Return Oil --> DCV
  CHAMBER_B -- Return Oil --> DCV
  DCV -- Exhaust --> TANK[Return to Tank]
  PRV[Pressure Relief Valve] -. Safety .-> PUMP
  CHK_A[Check Valve] -. Anti Cavitation .-> CHAMBER_A
  CHK_B[Check Valve] -. Anti Cavitation .-> CHAMBER_B
  LOAD[External Load] -- Reaction Force --> ROD
```

**Interpretation:** The DCV (4/3-way directional control valve) directs pressurized oil to either the cap-end chamber (for extension) or the rod-end chamber (for retraction). The piston rod transmits the developed force to the external load. The PRV protects the system from over-pressure, and the check valves prevent cavitation during directional change.

---

### 4.2 External Gear Motor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  INLET[Inlet Port P_high] --> HOUSING[Stator Housing]
  HOUSING --> GEAR_A[Drive Gear]
  HOUSING --> GEAR_B[Idler Gear]
  GEAR_A -- Mesh Point --> GEAR_B
  GEAR_A -- Torque T --> SHAFT[Output Shaft]
  GEAR_B -. Idler .-> HOUSING
  SHAFT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
  GEAR_A -- Swept Volume --> OUTLET[Outlet Port P_low]
  GEAR_B -- Swept Volume --> OUTLET
  OUTLET --> TANK[Return to Tank]
  SEAL_A[Shaft Seal] -. Prevents Leakage .-> SHAFT
  BEAR_A[Bearings] -. Support .-> SHAFT
```

**Interpretation:** Pressurized fluid enters the housing, fills the spaces between the gear teeth on the inlet side, and rotates the gear pair. The meshing point (where teeth engage) expels the fluid at low pressure toward the outlet. The drive gear delivers torque to the output shaft.

---

### 4.3 Unbalanced Vane Motor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  INLET[Inlet Port P_high] --> STATOR[Stator Ring R radius]
  STATOR --> ROTOR[Eccentric Rotor r radius]
  ROTOR -- Vane Slots --> VANES[Sliding Vanes]
  VANES -- Pressure Force --> ROTOR
  ROTOR -- Torque T --> SHAFT[Output Shaft]
  ROTOR -- Chamber Volume Varies --> OUTLET[Outlet Port P_low]
  OUTLET --> TANK[Return to Tank]
  ECC[Eccentricity e equals R minus r] -. Geometry .-> STATOR
  ECC -. Geometry .-> ROTOR
  SPRING[Bottom Vane Springs] -. Vane Contact .-> VANES
```

**Interpretation:** The rotor is offset from the stator center by an eccentricity **e = R − r**. Pressurized fluid enters the high-volume chambers (where the rotor is far from the stator) and pushes on the vanes. As the rotor turns, the chambers shrink and the fluid is expelled at the outlet.

---

### 4.4 Force-Velocity and Power Trade-Off Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  P[Input Pressure P] --> F[Output Force F equals P times A]
  Q[Input Flow Rate Q] --> V[Output Velocity V equals Q over A]
  A[Piston Area A] --> F
  A --> V
  F --> POW[Mechanical Power P equals F times V]
  V --> POW
  P --> POW
  Q --> POW
  POW1[Hydraulic Power P times Q] --> POW
  POW --> EFF[Overall Efficiency eta_o]
  EFF --> USEFUL[Useful Power at Load]
  LOSS1[Volumetric Losses] --> EFF
  LOSS2[Mechanical Friction Losses] --> EFF
```

**Interpretation:** This conceptual map demonstrates the **inverse trade-off** between force and velocity in a hydraulic cylinder: doubling the bore area doubles the force but halves the velocity — for a fixed pump pressure and flow. The product $P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ is constant (under ideal conditions), revealing that **hydraulic power is preserved** through the energy conversion.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *With a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-acting hydraulic cylinder. Mention two practical applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **single-acting hydraulic cylinder** has only one port through which pressurized hydraulic fluid enters. The fluid acts on **one side** of the piston (typically the cap side), creating a force that pushes the piston outward, producing the **working stroke**. The **return stroke** is achieved by an external agency — most commonly by gravity, a mechanical spring, or the weight of the load itself.

**Working Steps:**
1. Pressurized oil from the pump enters through Port A into the cap-end chamber.
2. The pressure acts on the full piston area $A_p = \pi D^2/4$, producing an extension force $F = P \cdot A_p$.
3. The piston moves outward, doing useful work against the external load.
4. To retract, the supply is cut off and Port A is connected to the tank. The spring (or load weight) pushes the piston back, displacing the oil back to the tank.

**Two Applications:**
- Hydraulic lifting jack in automobile workshops
- Dump truck tipping mechanism
- Hydraulic press for compacting
- Clamping devices in machine tools

**Diagram:** Show cylinder body, piston, piston rod, single port, return spring, tank line.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three differences between a hydraulic cylinder and a hydraulic motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Hydraulic Cylinder | Hydraulic Motor |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Output motion** | Linear (reciprocating) | Rotary (continuous) |
| 2 | **Energy direction** | Converts hydraulic → linear mechanical | Converts hydraulic → rotary mechanical |
| 3 | **Mechanical analog** | Piston + cylinder arrangement | Rotor + stator arrangement (gear, vane) |
| 4 | **Strokes per cycle** | Single (SAC) or double (DAC) | Continuous rotation |
| 5 | **Speed/Torque** | Stroke-based (length × velocity) | RPM × torque |

> **One-line exam-ready answer:** "A cylinder produces linear thrust from fluid pressure; a motor produces continuous rotary torque from fluid flow. The cylinder's output is measured in **force × stroke**; the motor's output is measured in **torque × RPM**."

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]** *(a)* Explain the construction and working of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** with a neat sketch. Derive the equations for **extension force** and **retraction force**, and explain the concept of **area asymmetry**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a **bore diameter of 100 mm** and a **piston rod diameter of 60 mm**. The pump supplies oil at a **pressure of 100 bar** and a **flow rate of 40 LPM**. Calculate: (i) the extension and retraction forces, (ii) the extension and retraction velocities, and (iii) the hydraulic power delivered. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

A **double-acting hydraulic cylinder (DAC)** is a linear hydraulic actuator in which pressurized fluid can be admitted to **both sides** of the piston, producing powered motion in **both directions** (extension and retraction). It is the most widely used hydraulic actuator in industry.

**Construction (key components):**
- **Cylinder Barrel (Tube):** The hollow cylindrical body, honed to a fine internal finish, where the piston slides.
- **Piston:** A circular disc fitted inside the barrel, sealed with piston seals.
- **Piston Rod:** A hard-chrome-plated steel rod attached to the piston, transmitting force to the external load.
- **End Caps (Head & Cap):** Seal both ends of the barrel; the head end is solid, the cap end allows the rod to pass through a rod seal.
- **Cushions:** Deceleration devices (small chambers with flow-restricting orifices) at both ends to absorb kinetic energy at end-of-stroke.
- **Ports (A and B):** Two fluid ports, one at each end, connected to a directional control valve.
- **Seals:** Piston seals, rod seals, and wiper seals to prevent leakage.

**Working:**
1. During **extension stroke**, pressurized oil from Port A enters the **cap-end (head-end) chamber** behind the piston. The pressure acts on the full piston area $A_p = \pi D^2/4$, generating force $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p$, pushing the piston outward. Oil from the rod-end chamber is simultaneously pushed out through Port B back to the tank.
2. During **retraction stroke**, the directional control valve shifts, sending pressurized oil through Port B into the **rod-end (annular) chamber**. The pressure acts on the annular area $A_a = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$, generating force $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a$, pulling the piston inward. Oil from the cap-end chamber returns to the tank through Port A.

**Derivation — Extension Force:**

Force = Pressure × Area on the receiving side:

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Derivation — Retraction Force:**

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

**Area Asymmetry:**

Because the piston rod occupies space on the rod-end side, $A_a < A_p$. The asymmetry ratio is:

$$
\frac{A_a}{A_p} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2
$$

**Consequences of asymmetry:**
- Retraction force < Extension force
- Retraction velocity > Extension velocity
- The piston is faster but weaker when retracting

> **Diagram:** Show a labelled cross-section of a DAC with arrow-marked fluid flow, ports, piston, rod, and seals.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

- Bore diameter: $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- Rod diameter: $d = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- Supply pressure: $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 10^7$ Pa
- Flow rate: $Q = 40$ LPM $= 40 \times 10^{-3}/60$ m³/s $= 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Compute Effective Areas:**

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(0.1)^2 - (0.06)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01 - 0.0036)
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Forces (Part i):**

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 78{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 78.54 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a = 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}270 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Velocities (Part ii):**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \approx 8.49 \text{ cm/s}
$$

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_a} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 13.26 \text{ cm/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Hydraulic Power (Part iii):**

$$
P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = 10^7 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4} = 6{,}667 \text{ W} \approx 6.67 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification using mechanical power:**

$$
P_{mech, ext} = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = 78{,}540 \times 0.0849 = 6{,}668 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Extension Force** | **78.54 kN** |
| **Retraction Force** | **50.27 kN** |
| **Extension Velocity** | **8.49 cm/s** |
| **Retraction Velocity** | **13.26 cm/s** |
| **Hydraulic Power** | **6.67 kW** |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** *(a)* Explain the construction and working of an **external gear motor** with a neat sketch. State the expressions for **theoretical displacement**, **theoretical torque**, and **theoretical power** of the gear motor. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A hydraulic gear motor has a **displacement of 50 cm³/rev** and operates with a **pressure drop of 150 bar**. The motor delivers oil at a **flow rate of 60 LPM** with a **volumetric efficiency of 95%** and a **mechanical efficiency of 90%**. Calculate: (i) the theoretical speed, (ii) the actual speed, (iii) the theoretical torque, (iv) the actual torque, and (v) the actual power output. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

An **external gear motor** is a positive-displacement rotary hydraulic actuator that converts hydraulic pressure energy into continuous rotary mechanical energy. It consists of a pair of **identical external spur gears** — a **drive gear** and an **idler gear** — enclosed in a closely toleranced housing.

**Construction:**
- **Housing (Stator):** A precision-machined body with inlet and outlet ports.
- **Drive Gear:** Connected to the output shaft; driven by fluid pressure.
- **Idler Gear:** The mating gear; rotates only because it meshes with the drive gear.
- **Bearings and Shaft Seals:** Support the drive shaft and prevent leakage.
- **Inlet Port:** Where high-pressure fluid enters.
- **Outlet Port:** Where low-pressure fluid exits.
- **Side Plates/Bushings:** Provide low-friction end support for the gears.

**Working:**
1. Pressurized hydraulic fluid enters through the inlet port and fills the spaces between the gear teeth on the **un-meshed side** (where the teeth are separating and volume is increasing).
2. The fluid pressure acting on the gear teeth creates a tangential force, causing the gear pair to rotate.
3. As the gears rotate, the trapped fluid is carried around the housing periphery to the **outlet side**.
4. At the **mesh point** (where the teeth interlock), the volume shrinks to nearly zero, forcing the fluid out through the outlet port to the tank.
5. Continuous rotation is maintained as long as pressurized fluid is supplied.

**Key Equations:**

Theoretical displacement per revolution:

$$
V_d = \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot z \cdot B
$$

where $m$ = module, $z$ = number of teeth per gear, $B$ = face width.

Theoretical torque:

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2 \pi}
$$

Theoretical power:

$$
P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q = T_{th} \cdot \omega
$$

> **Diagram:** Show housing, two meshing gears, labelled inlet/outlet ports, drive shaft, and flow arrows.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

- Displacement: $V_d = 50$ cm³/rev $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/rev
- Pressure drop: $\Delta P = 150$ bar $= 150 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1.5 \times 10^7$ Pa
- Flow rate: $Q = 60$ LPM $= 60 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v = 0.95$
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_m = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Theoretical Speed (Part i):**

$$
N_{th} = \frac{Q}{V_d} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20 \text{ rev/s} = 20 \times 60 = 1200 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual Speed (Part ii):**

$$
N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th} = 0.95 \times 1200 = 1140 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Torque (Part iii):**

$$
T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_d}{2 \pi} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \pi} = \frac{750}{6.2832} = 119.37 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual Torque (Part iv):**

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = 0.90 \times 119.37 = 107.43 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual Power Output (Part v):**

Convert actual speed to rad/s:

$$
\omega_{act} = \frac{2 \pi N_{act}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1140}{60} = 119.38 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P_{act} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_{act} = 107.43 \times 119.38 = 12{,}825 \text{ W} \approx 12.83 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification (alternative method):**

$$
P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 15{,}000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{act} = \eta_o \cdot P_{hyd} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_{hyd} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 15{,}000 = 12{,}825 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Theoretical Speed** | **1200 rpm** |
| **Actual Speed** | **1140 rpm** |
| **Theoretical Torque** | **119.37 N·m** |
| **Actual Torque** | **107.43 N·m** |
| **Actual Power Output** | **12.83 kW** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Hydraulic Actuator problems (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert **bar to Pa** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa) or **LPM to m³/s** (÷ 60,000). Always write units next to every numerical value.
>
> 2. **Confusing $A_p$ and $A_a$ (–2 marks):** Using the full piston area $\pi D^2/4$ for the **retraction** stroke instead of the annular area $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$. Memorize: **Extension → cap side → $A_p$**, **Retraction → rod side → $A_a$**.
>
> 3. **Missing Efficiencies (–2 marks):** "Actual" values in motor problems must be reduced by $\eta_v$ (for speed/flow) and $\eta_m$ (for torque). "Theoretical" values use no efficiency. The problem statement must be read carefully.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $\pi/4$ factor (–1 mark):** A common slip when calculating piston area.
>
> 5. **Wrong RPM vs rad/s conversion (–1 mark):** When computing $P = T \cdot \omega$, $\omega$ must be in **rad/s** ($= 2\pi N/60$). If you accidentally keep $N$ in rpm and multiply by $T$ in N·m, your power will be **60× too small** — a sure giveaway to the examiner.
>
> 6. **No sketch in 7-mark questions (–1 to –2 marks):** Always include a neat labelled diagram for construction-and-working questions — it is a KTU 2024 Scheme mandate.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hydraulic Actuators (Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

**Linear Actuators — Hydraulic Cylinders:**
- Single-acting cylinder (SAC): fluid on one side only; return by spring/gravity.
- Double-acting cylinder (DAC): fluid on both sides; powered motion in both directions.
- Piston (cap-side) area: $A_p = \pi D^2/4$.
- Annular (rod-side) area: $A_a = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$.
- Asymmetry ratio: $A_a/A_p = 1 - (d/D)^2$.
- Extension force: $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p$ (larger).
- Retraction force: $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_a$ (smaller).
- Extension velocity: $v_{ext} = Q/A_p$ (slower).
- Retraction velocity: $v_{ret} = Q/A_a$ (faster).
- Hydraulic power identity: $P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ (conservation).

**Rotary Actuators — Gear Motor:**
- External gear motor: two identical external spur gears mesh inside a housing.
- Internal gear motor: rotor + idler (ring gear) + crescent-shaped filler block.
- Theoretical displacement: $V_d = \pi m^2 z B$ (external gear).
- Theoretical torque: $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot V_d / (2\pi)$.
- Theoretical speed: $N_{th} = Q / V_d$.
- Actual speed uses $\eta_v$; actual torque uses $\eta_m$.
- Power identity: $P = \Delta P \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$.
- Applications: mobile hydraulics, conveyors, fan drives, agricultural machinery.

**Rotary Actuators — Vane Motor:**
- Stator + eccentric rotor + sliding vanes.
- Eccentricity $e = R - r$ controls displacement.
- Displacement: $V_d = 2\pi B (R^2 - r^2) = 2\pi B (R+r) e$.
- Torque: $T_{th} = \Delta P \cdot B (R^2 - r^2)$.
- Power: $P_{out} = \Delta P \cdot Q$.
- Types: unbalanced, balanced, variable-displacement.
- Applications: machine tool spindles, winches, low-speed high-torque drives.

**Universal Hydraulic Identity (Examination Mantra):**

$$
P \cdot Q = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega
$$

> **Power-in = Power-out × Efficiency** — this single line will solve 80% of all KTU 2024 hydraulic actuator numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic System - Components and Typical Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic System - Components and Typical Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pneumatic System** is an energy-transmission and control system that uses a **compressible fluid (compressed air or inert gas)** as the working medium to generate, transmit, and control mechanical motion through linear or rotary actuators. The system operates on the principle of **Pascal's Law applied to gases**, where pressurised air is routed through control valves to pneumatic actuators (cylinders or motors) to perform useful mechanical work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardised):** *A pneumatic system is a combination of interconnected components — including a power source (compressor), air-treatment unit (FRL), control elements (valves), and actuators (cylinders/motors) — designed to convert the potential energy of compressed air into controlled mechanical energy for industrial automation applications.*

## 1.2 Classification of Industrial Fluid Power Systems

Fluid Power is broadly classified into two main domains. The following table situates pneumatics within the broader field:

| Domain | Working Medium | Compressibility | Typical Pressure | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hydraulics** | Oil (incompressible) | Negligible | 50 – 350 bar | Heavy lifting, presses, machine tools |
| **Pneumatics** | Compressed Air (compressible) | High | 4 – 10 bar | Assembly, packaging, low-force automation |
| **Vacuum** | Suction of Air | High | Below atmospheric | Pick-and-place, holding, conveying |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lung and Muscle" Model

Imagine a human respiratory system. The **lungs (compressor + receiver)** take in air and pressurise it, the **trachea and bronchi (FRL unit + pipes)** clean, regulate, and deliver that air, the **brain (control valves)** decides where to send the air, and the **muscles (cylinders/motors)** perform the work.

In this analogy:
- The **heart** = Electric Motor driving the compressor
- The **arteries** = Distribution piping
- The **lungs** = Air Receiver Tank
- The **kidneys** = Filter and Dryer (purifying the air)
- The **brain & nervous system** = Directional Control Valves (DCV)
- The **muscles of the body** = Pneumatic Cylinders / Motors

The compressed air is **free**, **abundant**, **non-flammable**, **clean (after FRL)**, and **safe** in hazardous environments — making pneumatics a preferred choice for **food processing, pharmaceutical, semiconductor, and textile industries**.

## 1.4 Why Pneumatic Systems? (Key Advantages — KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlights — Why pneumatics matters in Sensors & Actuators:**
> - **Simplicity of design** — fewer moving parts, easy to maintain
> - **High power-to-weight ratio** — ideal for repetitive tasks
> - **Overload-safe** — air compresses, machinery does not fail catastrophically
> - **Clean operation** — no oil leakage, suitable for sterile environments
> - **Cost-effective** for low-to-medium force applications (< 25 kN)
> - **Easily controllable** — speed, force, and direction independently adjustable
> - **Storage** — compressed air can be stored in receivers and used intermittently

**Limitations** to be aware of: compressibility leads to **non-linear position control**, exhaust air creates **noise (requires silencers)**, and the system has **lower efficiency (~10–20%)** compared to hydraulics due to air leakage and compression losses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Building Blocks of a Pneumatic System

A standard industrial pneumatic circuit consists of **four functional sections** working in sequence:

1. **Air Generation Section** — Compressor + Prime Mover (Electric Motor / Engine)
2. **Air Distribution & Conditioning Section** — Pipes, FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator), Receiver Tank, Dryer
3. **Air Control Section** — Directional Control Valves, Flow Control Valves, Check Valves, Pressure Gauges, Limit Valves
4. **Actuation Section** — Single/Double-Acting Cylinders, Rotary Actuators, Pneumatic Motors

## 2.2 Component-wise Deep Analysis

### A. Air Compressor (The Heart of the System)
The compressor converts mechanical energy from an electric motor into pneumatic (pressure) energy. The two most common types in KTU syllabus are:

- **Reciprocating (Piston) Compressor** — Uses pistons in cylinders; suited for high pressure (up to 10 bar) and intermittent duty
- **Rotary (Vane / Screw) Compressor** — Uses rotating vanes or screws; suited for continuous duty, lower pulsation

**Compressor Power Requirement:**

$$P_{comp} = \frac{Q \times p}{\eta_{comp} \times 1000} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

where $Q$ is the free air delivery in $m^3/s$, $p$ is the delivery pressure in $Pa$, and $\eta_{comp}$ is the overall compressor efficiency (typically **0.65 – 0.85**).

### B. Air Receiver Tank
Stores compressed air to:
- Equalise pressure pulsations from the compressor
- Provide a reserve capacity during peak demand
- Allow moisture to condense and settle at the bottom

**Receiver Volume Rule of Thumb:**

$$V_{receiver} = \frac{Q_{compressor} \times t_{reserve}}{p_{max} - p_{min}} \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

where $t_{reserve}$ is the time (in seconds) the system must run without the compressor.

### C. FRL Unit (Filter–Regulator–Lubricator) — The Most Exam-Relevant Component
The FRL unit is a **three-stage air-treatment assembly**, always installed **immediately after the receiver tank** and **before the control valves**. It is the single most important component for system reliability.

**Stage 1 — Filter (F):** Removes dust, rust, water droplets, and oil aerosols. Typical filtration level is **5 to 40 micrometres ($\mu m$)**. Most filters use a **sintered bronze or cellulose element** with a centrifugal (baffle) action and a manual/semi-automatic drain (manual petcock, automatic float drain).

**Stage 2 — Regulator (R):** Maintains a constant **secondary (downstream) pressure** regardless of fluctuations in the primary (supply) pressure or downstream flow. Uses a **diaphragm-spring-balanced poppet** mechanism. Typical regulated range: **0.5 to 10 bar**, with adjustment by a knurled knob compressing a range spring.

**Stage 3 — Lubricator (L):** Injects a fine **oil mist** (typically SAE 10 or SAE 20) into the air stream to lubricate downstream moving parts (valve spools, cylinder seals, etc.). Uses the **Venturi principle** — air flows through a narrow throat creating a pressure drop that draws oil up a riser tube where it is atomised and re-entrained into the main flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Golden Rule:** *In an FRL, the air MUST flow in the sequence Filter → Regulator → Lubricator. If the lubricator is placed before the regulator, the regulator's diaphragm will be contaminated with oil and fail. If the filter is placed after the regulator, the regulator's internal orifice will clog.*

### D. Directional Control Valves (DCV) — The Decision Makers
DCVs route air to different parts of the actuator. They are classified by **number of ports** and **number of positions** (e.g., 3/2, 4/2, 5/2, 5/3).

- **3/2 Valve** — 3 ports, 2 positions; used for **single-acting cylinders** (e.g., push-button valve)
- **5/2 Valve** — 5 ports, 2 positions; used for **double-acting cylinders** (1 supply, 2 exhaust, 2 work ports)
- **5/3 Valve** — 5 ports, 3 positions; used for **double-acting cylinders with mid-position control** (closed centre, tandem centre, or open centre)

**Actuation Methods (how the valve is operated):**

| Actuation Type | Symbol Notation | Example Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Manual (Push Button) | P or $\text{Manual}$ | Operator control panels |
| Mechanical (Plunger / Roller) | M | Limit-switched automation |
| Pilot (Pneumatic) | Y or Z | Long-distance signal transmission |
| Solenoid (Electrical) | S | PLC-controlled systems |
| Combined (Solenoid + Spring) | S-S | Most common industrial valve |

### E. Flow Control Valves (Speed Controllers)
These are **throttle valves** that restrict airflow to control actuator speed. They are almost always installed as **meter-out** (on the exhaust side) rather than meter-in (on the supply side) because:
- Meter-out gives **smoother speed control** (resists the load)
- Meter-out prevents **cavitation and air starvation** during retraction
- They incorporate a **non-return (check) valve** in parallel to allow free flow in one direction

### F. Pneumatic Actuators
- **Single-Acting Cylinder** — Has one air port; return motion is by an **internal spring** or by **gravity/load**. Work done in one direction only.
- **Double-Acting Cylinder** — Has two air ports; air is applied alternately to extend and retract. Work done in both directions. **Most common in industry.**
- **Rotary Actuator** — Produces **limited-angle rotary motion** (typically 90°, 180°, 270°)
- **Pneumatic Motor** — Produces **continuous rotary motion** (vane, piston, or gear type)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definition | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Theoretical Cylinder Force (Extend) | $F_{ext} = p \times A_p$ | $p$ = gauge pressure, $A_p$ = piston area | N |
| Theoretical Cylinder Force (Retract) | $F_{ret} = p \times A_r$ | $A_r$ = annular (piston $-$ rod) area | N |
| Piston Area (full bore) | $A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $D$ = cylinder bore diameter | $m^2$ |
| Annular Area (rod side) | $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | $d$ = piston rod diameter | $m^2$ |
| Air Consumption (per cycle) | $V_c = (A_p \times L_{ext}) + (A_r \times L_{ret})$ | $L$ = stroke length | $m^3$ |
| Free Air Delivery equivalent | $Q_{FAD} = V_c \times n$ | $n$ = number of cycles per second | $m^3/s$ |
| Compressor Power | $P = \frac{Q \times p}{\eta}$ | $Q$ = FAD, $p$ = delivery pressure, $\eta$ = efficiency | W |
| Air Velocity in Pipe | $v = \frac{Q}{A_{pipe}}$ | $A_{pipe}$ = pipe cross-section | m/s |
| Reynolds Number (pipes) | $Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |
| Pressure Drop (Darcy) | $\Delta p = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = pipe length | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Thumb Rule:** Standard industrial pneumatic operating pressure is **6 bar (600 kPa)**. For double-acting cylinders, retraction force is **roughly 75% of extension force** for a standard rod-to-bore ratio of $\frac{d}{D} \approx 0.5$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Pneumatic systems are the **backbone of Factory Automation 4.0** in:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (robotic grippers, clamping, riveting)
- **Packaging industry** (bottle filling, sealing, labelling)
- **Food & Pharma** (clean-room actuators without oil contamination — using **non-lubricated FRL or oil-free compressors**)
- **Textile machinery** (loom shuttle control, thread cutting)
- **Material handling** (vacuum grippers, conveyor diverters)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Thrust Force (Double-Acting, KTU Classic Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a **bore diameter $D = 63$ mm** and a **piston rod diameter $d = 25$ mm**. The supply pressure is **$p = 6$ bar gauge**. Compute the (a) extension force, (b) retraction force, and (c) the **percentage force loss** during retraction. Assume no friction.

### Step 1 — Convert all units to SI
Working in SI is mandatory for KTU to avoid bar-vs-Pa confusion.

$$D = 63 \text{ mm} = 0.063 \text{ m}, \quad d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$$

$$p = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 600{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the full piston (effective) area during extension
During extension, air acts on the **entire piston face** (the rod side is connected to exhaust).

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.063)^2$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.003969 = 0.7854 \times 0.003969$$

$$A_p = 3.117 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 3117 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute the extension force

$$F_{ext} = p \times A_p = (6 \times 10^5) \times (3.117 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{ext} = 600{,}000 \times 0.003117 = 1870.4 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext} \approx 1870 \text{ N} \;\; (\approx 1.87 \text{ kN})}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the annular (rod side) area during retraction
During retraction, air acts on the **annulus** (full piston minus rod cross-section).

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.063^2 - 0.025^2)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.003969 - 0.000625) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.003344)$$

$$A_r = 0.7854 \times 0.003344 = 2.626 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 2626 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 5 — Compute the retraction force

$$F_{ret} = p \times A_r = (6 \times 10^5) \times (2.626 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{ret} = 600{,}000 \times 0.002626 = 1575.8 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} \approx 1576 \text{ N} \;\; (\approx 1.58 \text{ kN})}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the percentage loss in force

$$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{F_{ext} - F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} \times 100 = \frac{1870.4 - 1575.8}{1870.4} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{294.6}{1870.4} \times 100 = 15.75\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using geometric ratio:** Loss $\% = \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 \times 100 = \left(\frac{25}{63}\right)^2 \times 100 = (0.397)^2 \times 100 = 15.75\%$ ✓
> This confirms the shortcut formula and the result.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Air Consumption of a Cylinder (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Problem:** A double-acting cylinder ($D = 100$ mm, $d = 50$ mm, stroke $L = 200$ mm) operates at **6 bar gauge** and performs **30 cycles per minute**. Calculate (a) air consumed per cycle at working pressure, (b) free air consumption per minute.

### Step 1 — Areas (reusing earlier methodology)

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1^2 - 0.05^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0075) = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Volume of air per cycle (at working pressure)
Stroke $L = 0.2$ m.

$$V_{cycle} = (A_p \times L) + (A_r \times L) = (A_p + A_r) \times L$$

$$V_{cycle} = (7.854 + 5.890) \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2$$

$$V_{cycle} = 13.744 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 2.749 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{cycle} \approx 2.749 \text{ litres/cycle at 6 bar}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Free Air Delivery (atmospheric equivalent)
Atmospheric pressure $p_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar} \approx 1$ bar (for exam use). By Boyle's Law at constant temperature:

$$p_{atm} V_{FAD} = p_{work} V_{cycle}$$

$$V_{FAD} = V_{cycle} \times \frac{p_{work}}{p_{atm}} = 2.749 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{6}{1}$$

$$V_{FAD} = 16.49 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 16.49 \text{ litres/cycle (free air)}$$

### Step 4 — Per minute consumption

$$Q_{FAD} = 16.49 \text{ L} \times 30 \text{ cycles/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{FAD} = 494.8 \text{ litres/min} \approx 0.495 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}$$

### Step 5 — Compressor sizing (safety factor 1.25)

$$Q_{compressor} = 1.25 \times 0.495 = 0.619 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer's Rule:** A compressor should always be sized **20–30% larger** than the calculated FAD to account for leakage, valve losses, and future system expansion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compressor Power (Complete Worked Example)

**Problem:** A reciprocating compressor delivers **$Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$** of free air at **$p = 7$ bar gauge** with an **overall efficiency of 0.75**. Find the motor power required.

### Step 1 — Convert pressure to absolute (some texts use gauge directly for "work done")

$$p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm} = 7 + 1.013 = 8.013 \text{ bar}$$

$$p_{abs} = 8.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the compressor power formula

$$P_{comp} = \frac{Q \times p_{abs}}{\eta_{comp}} = \frac{0.05 \times 8.013 \times 10^5}{0.75}$$

$$P_{comp} = \frac{40{,}065}{0.75} = 53{,}420 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{comp} \approx 53.4 \text{ kW}}$$

### Step 3 — Select standard motor size
Standard motor sizes: 55 kW. So a **55 kW motor** is to be selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on FAD definition:** *Free Air Delivery is the volume of air the compressor delivers per unit time, measured at atmospheric (ambient) conditions, regardless of the actual delivery pressure. It is the standard metric used in KTU exam problems.*

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Pneumatic Component

| Design Requirement | Recommended Component | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single-direction push, automatic return | 3/2 DCV + Single-acting cylinder | Spring return is built-in, cost-effective |
| Bidirectional motion with PLC control | 5/2 DCV, double-solenoid + Double-acting cylinder | Independent control over both directions |
| Holding a load mid-stroke without power | 5/3 DCV (closed centre) + Double-acting cylinder | Locks air on both sides, holds position |
| Need to control cylinder extension speed | Meter-out flow control valve on exhaust port | Smoother, resists back-pressure |
| High humidity environment (e.g., coastal) | Refrigerated / Desiccant air dryer after compressor | Removes moisture before FRL |
| Clean-room (food/pharma) application | Oil-free compressor + Filter (no Lubricator) | Prevents oil contamination of product |
| Long signal transmission (> 5 m) | Pilot-operated DCV instead of direct solenoid | Pilot pressure overcomes friction loss |
| Repetitive clamping on assembly line | 5/2 DCV with mechanical roller actuation | Limit switches trigger reversal automatically |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Typical Pneumatic Circuit

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional architecture** and signal/power flow of a **standard double-acting cylinder circuit with 5/2 DCV and FRL conditioning**. (Since ISO 1219 pneumatic symbols are complex to render textually, this functional view is the KTU-recommended representation.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["AIR GENERATION SECTION"]
        A1[AC Mains Supply] --> A2[Electric Motor]
        A2 --> A3[Reciprocating Air Compressor]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["AIR STORAGE & CONDITIONING"]
        A3 --> B1[Air Receiver Tank]
        B1 --> B2[Filter 5 to 40 micron]
        B2 --> B3[Pressure Regulator 0.5 to 10 bar]
        B3 --> B4[Lubricator Oil Mist Injector]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["DIRECTIONAL CONTROL SECTION"]
        B4 --> C1[5/2 DCV Solenoid Pilot]
        C1 --> C2[Spool Position 1 Extend]
        C1 --> C3[Spool Position 2 Retract]
        C1 --> C4[Spring Return]
        C5[Limit Switch Rod End] --> C1
        C6[Limit Switch Cap End] --> C1
    end

    subgraph SPEED["FLOW CONTROL SECTION"]
        C2 --> D1[Meter Out Flow Control Valve]
        C3 --> D2[Meter Out Flow Control Valve]
        D1 --> E1
        D2 --> E1
    end

    subgraph ACTUATE["ACTUATION SECTION"]
        E1[Double Acting Cylinder] --> E2[Piston Rod Extension]
        E1 --> E3[Piston Rod Retraction]
    end

    subgraph EXHAUST["EXHAUST SECTION"]
        C2 --> F1[Silencer Muffler Port 3]
        C3 --> F2[Silencer Muffler Port 5]
    end
```

## 4.2 Detailed Sub-System Architecture — FRL Unit Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRL["FRL UNIT Internal Stages"]
        direction LR
        S1[Supply Air Inlet] --> ST1[STAGE 1 FILTER]
        ST1 --> S2[Filtered Air]
        S2 --> ST2[STAGE 2 REGULATOR]
        ST2 --> S3[Regulated Pressure]
        S3 --> ST3[STAGE 3 LUBRICATOR]
        ST3 --> S4[Oil Mist Air to Valves]

        subgraph FILTER_DETAIL["FILTER DETAIL"]
            S1 --> B1[Baffle Centrifugal Separator]
            B1 --> FE[Sintered Bronze Element 40 micron]
            FE --> DR[Manual or Auto Drain Petcock]
        end

        subgraph REG_DETAIL["REGULATOR DETAIL"]
            S2 --> D1[Diaphragm Assembly]
            D1 --> SP1[Range Spring]
            SP1 --> KNOB[Adjustment Knob]
            D1 --> PO[Balanced Poppet Valve]
            PO --> S3
        end

        subgraph LUB_DETAIL["LUBRICATOR DETAIL"]
            S3 --> VENT[Venturi Throat Low Pressure]
            VENT --> OIL[Oil Riser Tube]
            OIL --> RES[Oil Reservoir Sight Dome]
            RES --> VENT
        end
    end
```

## 4.3 Pneumatic Cylinder Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CYL["DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER"]
        direction LR
        CAP[Cap End Port Air In] --> SE1[Seal]
        SE1 --> CH1[Cylinder Barrel]
        CH1 --> PIS[Piston with Seals]
        PIS --> ROD[Output Piston Rod]
        PIS --> SE2[Seal]
        SE2 --> CH2[Cylinder Barrel Rod Side]
        CH2 --> RODPORT[Rod End Port Air In]
        PIS -.->|A_p Full Area| CH1
        PIS -.->|A_r Annular Area| CH2
    end
```

## 4.4 Typical Circuit Control Sequence (Time-Domain Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Idle Compressor Running FRL Active] --> T0
    T0[Operator Presses PB1] --> T1
    T1[5/2 DCV Energised to Position A] --> T2
    T2[Supply Air via Port 4 to Port 2 of Cylinder] --> T3
    T3[Piston Extends Work Output] --> T4
    T4[Limit Switch LS2 Triggered] --> T5
    T5[5/2 DCV Returns to Position B via Spring] --> T6
    T6[Supply Air via Port 4 to Port 3 of Cylinder] --> T7
    T7[Piston Retracts] --> T8
    T8[Limit Switch LS1 Triggered] --> T9
    T9[Cycle Repeats or Stops] --> S0
```

## 4.5 Pneumatic Component Classification Tree (KTU Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PN[Pneumatic System Components] --> AG[Air Generation]
    PN --> AD[Air Distribution and Conditioning]
    PN --> AC[Air Control Elements]
    PN --> AA[Actuators]

    AG --> AG1[Compressor]
    AG --> AG2[Prime Mover Motor]
    AG1 --> AG1A[Reciprocating]
    AG1 --> AG1B[Rotary Vane]
    AG1 --> AG1C[Rotary Screw]

    AD --> AD1[Receiver Tank]
    AD --> AD2[FRL Unit]
    AD --> AD3[Dryer]
    AD --> AD4[Pipes and Fittings]
    AD2 --> AD2A[Filter]
    AD2 --> AD2B[Regulator]
    AD2 --> AD2C[Lubricator]

    AC --> AC1[Directional Control Valve]
    AC --> AC2[Flow Control Valve]
    AC --> AC3[Check Valve]
    AC --> AC4[Pressure Gauge]
    AC1 --> AC1A[3 by 2]
    AC1 --> AC1B[4 by 2]
    AC1 --> AC1C[5 by 2]
    AC1 --> AC1D[5 by 3]

    AA --> AA1[Single Acting Cylinder]
    AA --> AA2[Double Acting Cylinder]
    AA --> AA3[Rotary Actuator]
    AA --> AA4[Pneumatic Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatic Symbol Convention (ISO 1219):** Each square box in a valve symbol represents **one position**; the number of lines entering/exiting the box represents the **number of ports**. Arrows inside the box show the **direction of flow** in that position. A "T" inside a box indicates a **closed (blocked) port**; an inverted "T" or arrow chevron indicates **exhaust to atmosphere**. These conventions are mandatory in KTU answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Definition Type)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define a Pneumatic System. List any **four advantages** of pneumatic systems over hydraulic systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **pneumatic system** is an energy-transmission and control system that uses **compressed air** as the working fluid to transmit power from a generating station to an actuating station through control valves.

**Four advantages over hydraulics:**
1. **Air is free and abundantly available** — oil must be purchased, stored, and disposed of
2. **Clean operation** — no risk of oil leakage contaminating product (ideal for food, pharma, textile)
3. **Safe in hazardous/explosive atmospheres** — air does not support combustion or cause fire
4. **Overload-safe** — air compresses, allowing the system to stall without damage
5. *Bonus:* Lower capital cost, simpler components, easy maintenance

*(Any four correct points fetch 3 marks; complete definition is mandatory)*

### Question 2 (Component Type)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is an **FRL unit**? Why must the lubricator always be placed **last** (i.e., downstream of the regulator)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**FRL** stands for **Filter–Regulator–Lubricator**, a compact three-in-one air-treatment assembly installed immediately after the receiver tank in any pneumatic circuit.

- **Filter** removes dirt, rust, water, and oil aerosols (5–40 $\mu m$)
- **Regulator** maintains a constant downstream working pressure
- **Lubricator** injects a fine oil mist for downstream moving parts

**Why lubricator last?** The regulator uses a **delicate diaphragm and a precision poppet** mechanism. If oil mist passes through the regulator, the diaphragm swells, the poppet sticks, and pressure regulation fails catastrophically. Hence **Filter → Regulator → Lubricator** is the mandatory flow direction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Either Question A OR Question B is to be answered. Diagrams carry substantial weight (3–4 marks) and **must** be drawn neatly using ISO 1219 symbols.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *FRL Unit + Directional Control Valves*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with a **neat functional diagram** the construction and working of an **FRL unit** in a pneumatic system. Why is each stage important? (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **5/2 pilot-operated spring-return DCV**. Sketch its ISO symbols in both positions. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a): FRL Unit (7 marks)

The FRL unit is a **three-stage air-treatment device** installed between the receiver tank and the control valves. Air must flow in the order **Filter → Regulator → Lubricator**.

**Stage 1 — Filter (2 marks):**
Air enters the filter bowl and is forced to spiral by an **inlet baffle (centrifugal separator)**. This causes water droplets and heavy dust particles to be flung outward by centrifugal force and collected at the bowl bottom, where a **manual or automatic petcock drain** removes them. The air then passes through a **sintered bronze or cellulose element** that traps finer particles down to **5 to 40 micrometres ($\mu m$)**.

**Stage 2 — Regulator (2.5 marks):**
The filtered air enters the regulator body and pushes against a **flexible diaphragm** loaded by an **adjustable range spring** via a knurled knob. The diaphragm is connected to a **poppet valve** that controls flow from primary to secondary side. If downstream pressure rises (e.g., due to lower demand), the diaphragm deflects upward, partially closing the poppet, restricting flow, and **re-establishing the set pressure**. The opposite happens when pressure falls. This **closed-loop feedback** maintains constant secondary pressure typically from **0.5 to 10 bar**.

**Stage 3 — Lubricator (2.5 marks):**
The regulated air passes through a **Venturi throat** in the lubricator body. The narrow cross-section increases velocity and **drops the local pressure**. This pressure drop draws oil up a **riser tube** from a transparent **reservoir/sight dome** below. The oil is atomised into a fine mist that **re-entrains into the main airstream** and travels downstream to lubricate valve spools, cylinder seals, and motor vanes. A **sight dome** with a yellow "no-flow" indicator shows the oil drip rate.

**Diagram (essential):** Cross-sectional sketch of FRL showing baffle, filter element, diaphragm-spring-poppet assembly, Venturi throat, oil riser, sight dome.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b): 5/2 DCV (7 marks)

A **5/2 Directional Control Valve** has **5 ports** and **2 positions**.

**Port Identification (1 mark):**
- **Port 1** = Supply (pressure inlet from FRL)
- **Port 2** = Work port A (cylinder cap-end)
- **Port 3** = Work port B (cylinder rod-end)
- **Port 4** = Supply to pilot (or alternative inlet)
- **Port 5** = Exhaust (atmosphere)

**Position 1 (Normal / Spring Return — 2 marks):**
The spool is held against the right-end cap by a **return spring**. Inlet **1** is connected to port **2** (sending air to cap end → piston extends). Port **3** is connected to port **5** (exhausting rod-end air to atmosphere). The pilot signal is at port **12/14** blocked.

**Position 2 (Actuated / Pilot Energised — 2 marks):**
A **pneumatic pilot signal** at port **12/14** pushes the spool to the left. Now inlet **1** is connected to port **4** (re-routed to port 3 via internal passage) and port **2** is connected to port **5** (cap-end exhausts). The cylinder retracts. When the pilot signal is removed, the **spring returns the spool to Position 1**.

**ISO Symbols (2 marks):**
Two adjacent squares (representing the two positions). Arrows inside each square show the port-to-port connections. A spring symbol on one side and a pilot symbol (Y) on the other. (Students should draw the standard symbol as per ISO 1219.)

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Cylinder Force + Typical Circuit*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A double-acting cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm and rod diameter $d = 40$ mm. The supply pressure is **6 bar gauge**. Calculate the **extension and retraction forces**. If the load is 1.2 kN, will the cylinder operate safely in both directions? Assume 20% safety margin. (7 marks)

**(b)** Draw a **pneumatic circuit diagram** (using ISO 1219 symbols) for a **double-acting cylinder controlled by a 5/2 solenoid-operated spring-return DCV with push-button start and limit switch reversal**. Explain its operation. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a): Cylinder Force (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $d = 40$ mm, $p = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa

**Step 1 — Piston area during extension (2 marks):**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension force (1.5 marks):**

$$F_{ext} = p \times A_p = (6 \times 10^5) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 3016 \text{ N} \approx 3.02 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Annular area during retraction (1.5 marks):**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080^2 - 0.040^2)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064 - 0.0016) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0048) = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Retraction force (1 mark):**

$$F_{ret} = p \times A_r = (6 \times 10^5) \times (3.770 \times 10^{-3}) = 2262 \text{ N} \approx 2.26 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Safety check (1 mark):**
Required load = 1.2 kN. With 20% safety margin → required force ≥ $1.2 \times 1.2 = 1.44$ kN.

Both $F_{ext} = 3.02$ kN and $F_{ret} = 2.26$ kN exceed 1.44 kN. **Cylinder operates safely in both directions.** ✓

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b): Typical Circuit Diagram (7 marks)

**Required Components (1 mark for listing):**
1. Air supply (from FRL)
2. 5/2 DCV, solenoid-pilot + spring return
3. Double-acting cylinder
4. Push-button (start)
5. Two limit switches (LS1 at retracted end, LS2 at extended end)
6. Two meter-out flow control valves (speed control)
7. Silencers on exhaust ports
8. Pressure gauge

**Operation (4 marks):**

- **Initial state:** Spring holds DCV in Position 1 → port 1 connected to port 2 (cylinder cap-end) → cylinder is in retracted state with rod-end exhausted through port 5.
- **Step 1 — Push button pressed:** Solenoid is energised, spool shifts to Position 2. Port 1 connects to port 4 (re-routed to rod-end) → cylinder extends. Cap-end air exhausts through port 5 (metered by flow control valve for speed control).
- **Step 2 — Limit switch LS2 (rod-end) triggered:** Solenoid de-energises; spring returns spool to Position 1. Port 1 connects to cap-end again, rod-end is exhausted through port 3 → cylinder retracts at metered speed.
- **Step 3 — Limit switch LS1 (cap-end) triggered:** Solenoid re-energises via auto-relay logic; cycle repeats.
- **Stopping:** A separate stop push-button (NC) breaks the solenoid circuit; cylinder remains in the last position held by trapped air (provided no leakage).

**Diagram (2 marks):** Neat ISO 1219 circuit showing all components above with proper port numbering and signal lines. (Use the functional block diagram from Section 4.1 as a guide for layout, but the student must draw the actual ISO symbol-based circuit.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Pneumatic Circuit Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting port numbering (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)** on the DCV symbol — Examiner deducts 1 mark
> 2. **Drawing FRL in wrong order** (e.g., Lubricator before Regulator) — Examiner deducts 1.5 marks
> 3. **Missing silencers** on exhaust ports — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark
> 4. **Not mentioning meter-out** placement for speed control — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark
> 5. **Wrong force-area relationship** (using total area for both directions) — Examiner deducts 2 marks
> 6. **Unit confusion** (using N/mm² instead of Pa) — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark
> 7. **No mention of safety factor / margin** in design — Examiner deducts 1 mark
> 8. **Confusing single-acting with double-acting** in the circuit description — Examiner deducts 2 marks

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Quick-Recall — Print This Section Before Exam:**

- ✅ Pneumatics uses **compressed air** (compressible) — Hydraulics uses **oil** (incompressible)
- ✅ Standard industrial pneumatic pressure = **6 bar gauge**
- ✅ **FRL** = **Filter → Regulator → Lubricator** (in that mandatory order, always)
- ✅ Filter porosity = **5 to 40 $\mu m$**; Regulator range = **0.5 to 10 bar**
- ✅ Lubricator uses **Venturi principle** to draw oil into airstream
- ✅ Valve designation: **Ports/Positions** (e.g., 5/2 = 5 ports, 2 positions)
- ✅ **3/2 valve** → single-acting cylinder; **5/2 valve** → double-acting cylinder
- ✅ **Extension force** uses full piston area $A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$
- ✅ **Retraction force** uses annular area $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
- ✅ **Retraction force < Extension force** because of rod (typical loss ≈ 15–25%)
- ✅ **Meter-out flow control** is preferred over meter-in for smoother speed control
- ✅ **Compressor power** $P = \frac{Q \times p}{\eta}$; always oversize compressor by **20–30%**
- ✅ **Air consumption per cycle** at working pressure = $(A_p + A_r) \times L$
- ✅ **Free air delivery (FAD)** = working volume × (working pressure / atmospheric pressure) [Boyle's Law]
- ✅ **ISO 1219** is the international symbol standard for pneumatic circuits — must be followed
- ✅ **Pilot-operated** valves used for **long signal transmission** (> 5 m)
- ✅ **5/3 closed-centre valve** used for **load holding** at mid-stroke
- ✅ **Oil-free compressor + filter only (no lubricator)** is mandatory for **clean-room** applications
- ✅ **Reciprocating compressors** = high pressure, intermittent duty; **Rotary compressors** = continuous duty, lower pulsation
- ✅ **Air receiver tank** smoothens pulsations, stores reserve, allows moisture to settle
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic Actuators - Bellows Actuator, Flapper-Nozzle, Diaphragm Actuators for Industrial Control Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic Actuators - Bellows Actuator, Flapper-Nozzle, Diaphragm Actuators for Industrial Control Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Pneumatic Actuators

**Pneumatic actuators** are mechanical devices that convert the energy of a compressed gas (typically air) into linear or rotary motion. In the context of **industrial process control**, pneumatic actuators are the final control elements that receive a low-power pneumatic signal (usually a standard 3–15 psi or 20–100 kPa pressure range) from a controller and produce a large mechanical force to position a control valve stem, damper linkage, or other process variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pneumatic actuators form the backbone of legacy process industries (Oil & Gas, Petrochemical, Power Plants) and remain critical in **intrinsically safe** environments where electrical actuators are hazardous.

### 1.2 Classification of Pneumatic Actuators

Pneumatic actuators are broadly classified based on the mechanism that converts air pressure into mechanical motion:

| Category | Mechanism | Typical Output | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diaphragm Actuator** | Flexible elastomeric diaphragm | Linear (stem push/pull) | Control valves (globe, butterfly) |
| **Bellows Actuator** | Corrugated metallic bellows | Linear, small stroke | Precision instruments, low-force applications |
| **Piston/Cylinder Actuator** | Sliding piston in cylinder | High-force linear | Large control valves, dampers |
| **Rotary Vane Actuator** | Rotating vane on shaft | Rotary (90°/180°) | Ball valves, plug valves |
| **Flapper-Nozzle (Amplifier)** | Pneumatic feedback amplifier | Boosted pressure signal | Signal amplification stage in controllers |

## 1.3 Core Definitions (KTU 2024 Standardized Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bellows Actuator (Definition):** A pneumatic actuator employing a **corrugated, thin-walled metallic (typically brass, stainless steel, or phosphor bronze) expandable element** that elongates or compresses axially when internal air pressure changes. It provides a controlled, frictionless linear motion with excellent **stiffness and linearity** for low-to-medium force applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flapper-Nozzle Assembly (Definition):** A **pneumatic-beam amplifier** consisting of a fixed nozzle and a movable flapper (a small pivoted plate) that together form a variable restriction. The nozzle back-pressure is a highly sensitive, nonlinear function of the flapper-to-nozzle gap, producing a large pneumatic gain used as the **first stage of pneumatic controllers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diaphragm Actuator (Definition):** A pneumatic actuator that uses a **flexible elastomeric or fabric-reinforced diaphragm** clamped between two housings. The differential pressure across the diaphragm generates a force proportional to the **effective area**, producing linear stem motion. It is the most widely used actuator in **process control valves** due to simplicity, reliability, and inherent fail-safe capability.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### Analogy 1: Diaphragm Actuator → A Syringe with a Rubber Top
Imagine pushing a syringe plunger; now seal the top of the syringe with a stretched rubber sheet. When you push air into the syringe, the rubber sheet bulges outward. The bulge pushes a stem connected to a valve. This is exactly how a **diaphragm actuator** works — air pressure pushes against an elastic membrane, and the membrane's motion opens or closes a valve.

### Analogy 2: Bellows Actuator → An Accordion
Think of a small accordion. When air is pumped inside, the accordion lengthens. When air is released, it shortens. A **bellows** is essentially a precision-engineered metal accordion designed to produce smooth, predictable, frictionless linear motion when pressurized.

### Analogy 3: Flapper-Nozzle → Your Thumb Over a Garden Hose
Hold a garden hose and partially cover the opening with your thumb. The amount of water (or air) that escapes depends sensitively on how close your thumb is to the opening. Move your thumb a tiny bit, and the back-pressure changes dramatically. The **flapper-nozzle** uses this principle — a small flapper (like your thumb) controls the back-pressure (like the spray pressure) with high sensitivity.

### Analogy 4: Industrial Control Valve → The Accelerator Pedal of a Car
A car's accelerator controls the throttle valve, regulating the engine's power. In a chemical plant, the **control valve** regulates the flow of fluid. The **pneumatic actuator** is the muscle that physically moves the valve, just like the cable and spring system in your car moves the throttle plate in response to foot pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Pneumatic Signal Ranges (Industrial Norms):**
> - **3–15 psig** (North American Standard, 0.2–1.0 bar)
> - **20–100 kPa** (European/IEC Standard, 0.2–1.0 bar)
> - **0.2–1.0 kgf/cm²** (Asian Standard)
> - **Live zero (3 psi, 20 kPa):** Ensures a non-zero signal is required for operation, allowing fault detection (broken line = 0 psi = valve fails safe).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bellows Actuator — Operating Principle

A **bellows** is a series of nested, convolutely formed (corrugated) thin-walled metal tubes welded or hydroformed at alternate convolutions. The convolutions allow the bellows to expand/contract along its axis with minimal radial deformation. Each convolution behaves like a small spring, and the entire bellows is characterized by:

### Working Principle
1. A regulated air pressure is admitted into the **interior chamber** of the bellows.
2. The pressure acts on the **effective area** of the bellows, generating an axial thrust force.
3. The bellows elongates against an opposing spring force.
4. The linear displacement of the bellows top is transferred (via a coupling) to the **control valve stem** or other linkage.
5. The stroke is limited by the spring constant and pressure range — typically **5–25 mm** of usable travel.

### Effective Area of a Bellows
Unlike a piston, a bellows has convolutions, so the **effective area** is not simply π D²/4. It is empirically determined and typically lies between the area based on the **outer diameter** and the area based on the **inner (root) diameter**:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{D_o^2 + D_i^2}{2} \right)$$

where:
- $D_o$ = **outer (crest) diameter** of the bellows convolutions
- $D_i$ = **inner (root) diameter** at the convolution valleys

### Force Balance on a Bellows
The net thrust force generated by a bellows is:

$$F_{thrust} = P \cdot A_{eff} - F_{spring} - F_{friction}$$

For an ideal frictionless bellows:

$$F_{thrust} = P \cdot A_{eff} - k \cdot x$$

where:
- $P$ = applied gauge pressure
- $A_{eff}$ = effective area (m²)
- $k$ = spring constant (N/m)
- $x$ = displacement (m)

### Stroke Equation
Setting the net thrust to zero (equilibrium):

$$x = \frac{P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$$

## 2.2 Flapper-Nozzle Assembly — Operating Principle

The flapper-nozzle is a **pneumatic amplifier of the highest order of sensitivity**. It is the **input stage** of virtually all analog pneumatic controllers (e.g., Foxboro, Honeywell, Bailey).

### Construction
- A **nozzle** (small orifice, typically 0.5–1.0 mm diameter) is fixed in position.
- A **flapper** (a small flat plate, typically 5–8 mm wide) is mounted on a pivot or flexure, very close (~0.1–0.2 mm) to the nozzle tip.
- A **fixed restriction** (R, typically a needle valve) supplies constant air pressure (typically 20 psig supply) to the nozzle.
- The **back-pressure** in the nozzle chamber ($P_b$) is the output.

### Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)
1. Supply air at constant pressure $P_s$ flows through the fixed restriction R.
2. Air escapes through the gap between the flapper and the nozzle ($h$, the flapper-nozzle gap).
3. As the **flapper moves closer** to the nozzle (decreasing $h$), the **back-pressure $P_b$ rises rapidly** (because the air cannot escape quickly).
4. As the **flapper moves away** (increasing $h$), the **back-pressure $P_b$ falls** toward 0.
5. The relationship $P_b = f(h)$ is **highly nonlinear** (roughly inverse-square) but is linearized for analysis.

### Governing Equations
The back-pressure in the nozzle can be modeled using an **orifice flow equation**. Treating the fixed restriction and the nozzle gap as two fluid resistances in series:

$$P_b = \frac{P_s}{1 + \dfrac{R_n}{R_{fixed}}}$$

For isothermal compressible flow through the nozzle gap, the mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = C_d A_n P_b \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{RT}\left(\dfrac{2k}{k-1}\right)\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_b}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_b}\right)^{(k+1)/k}\right]}$$

However, the **practical first-order model** used in KTU analysis is:

$$P_b(h) = P_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + K \cdot h^2}$$

where:
- $K$ = a constant depending on the geometry and air properties
- $h$ = flapper-to-nozzle gap (m)
- $P_s$ = supply pressure (typically 20 psi or 140 kPa)
- $P_b$ = nozzle back-pressure (the output signal)

### Sensitivity (Gain) of the Flapper-Nozzle
Differentiating gives the **static sensitivity**:

$$\dfrac{dP_b}{dh} = -2 K P_s \cdot \dfrac{h}{(1 + K h^2)^2}$$

The **maximum sensitivity** occurs at a specific gap $h_{max}$:

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3K}}$$

This high sensitivity (typically **gain > 100**) is precisely why the flapper-nozzle is used as the first amplification stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The flapper-nozzle does **not** produce enough flow capacity to drive a control valve directly. Its output is fed into a **pneumatic relay** (volume booster) which provides the necessary flow capacity while preserving the pressure level — the relay typically has unity gain in pressure but high gain in flow (volume amplification).

## 2.3 Diaphragm Actuator — Operating Principle

The **diaphragm actuator** is the **workhorse of industrial process control**. Over 70% of all control valves in refineries, chemical plants, and power stations use a diaphragm actuator.

### Construction
- A circular **diaphragm** (elastomeric, fabric-reinforced, or metallic) is clamped between two housing halves (upper case and lower case/yoke).
- The **upper side** is open to atmosphere or to a sealed chamber.
- The **lower side** (diaphragm plate side) is connected to the **control signal pressure** (3–15 psig or 20–100 kPa).
- A **diaphragm plate** (rigid metal disc) is bonded to the center of the diaphragm and connects to the valve stem.
- A **range spring** (helical compression spring) provides the opposing force and sets the fail-safe position.

### Operating Modes

| Mode | Air Connection | Stem Action on Increasing Signal | Spring Action | Fail-Safe Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air-to-Extend (Direct/Air-Open)** | Below diaphragm | Pushes stem DOWN (valve CLOSES for push-down) | Returns stem UP | Stem up (valve OPEN) |
| **Air-to-Retract (Reverse/Air-Close)** | Above diaphragm | Pulls stem UP (valve OPENS for push-down) | Pushes stem DOWN | Stem down (valve CLOSED) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Air-to-Extend" stem direction. Always remember: **direct acting (air pushes stem down)** corresponds to **air-to-close** action on a globe valve with downward stem closure.

### Force Balance Equation
The fundamental force equation governing diaphragm motion is:

$$F_{net} = P \cdot A_{eff} - k \cdot (x_0 + x) - F_f$$

At steady-state equilibrium ($F_{net} = 0$, neglecting friction):

$$P \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot (x_0 + x)$$

Solving for stem position $x$:

$$x = \dfrac{A_{eff}}{k} \cdot P - x_0$$

This shows that **stem position is linearly proportional to the applied pneumatic pressure** — a key KTU result.

### Effective Area of a Diaphragm
The **effective area** $A_{eff}$ is not the same as the geometric area $\pi D^2/4$ because the diaphragm is not rigid — it deflects in a complex shape. Empirically:

$$A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{12} \left( D_o^2 + D_o D_i + D_i^2 \right)$$

where:
- $D_o$ = outer diameter of diaphragm (clamping diameter)
- $D_i$ = inner diameter of diaphragm (diameter of diaphragm plate)

For a **rigid piston-like assumption** (often used in KTU problems):

$$A_{eff} \approx \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_p^2$$

where $D_p$ is the **diameter of the diaphragm plate** — this is the most common assumption in KTU 2024 exam problems.

### Pressure Range and Spring Setting
The **range spring** is pre-compressed to set the lower limit of the signal (typically 3 psig or 20 kPa = 0% stem travel). The spring constant $k$ is chosen so that at the upper signal (15 psig or 100 kPa), the stem reaches its full stroke.

Spring pre-compression force:

$$F_{pre} = P_{min} \cdot A_{eff} = 3 \text{ psi} \cdot A_{eff}$$

Spring rate (constant):

$$k = \dfrac{(P_{max} - P_{min}) \cdot A_{eff}}{x_{full}} = \dfrac{12 \text{ psi} \cdot A_{eff}}{x_{full}}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of KTU Module 4 numerical problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diaphragm Force | $F = P \cdot A_{eff}$ | N | P in Pa, A in m² |
| 2 | Stem Position (Diaphragm) | $x = \dfrac{A_{eff}}{k} \cdot P - x_0$ | m | Linear relationship |
| 3 | Effective Diaphragm Area (Rigid) | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_p^2$ | m² | Plate diameter $D_p$ |
| 4 | Effective Diaphragm Area (Empirical) | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{12}(D_o^2 + D_o D_i + D_i^2)$ | m² | Use when $D_i$ given |
| 5 | Bellows Thrust Force | $F = P \cdot A_{eff} - kx$ | N | |
| 6 | Bellows Effective Area | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(\dfrac{D_o^2 + D_i^2}{2}\right)$ | m² | |
| 7 | Bellows Stroke | $x = \dfrac{P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$ | m | |
| 8 | Flapper-Nozzle Back-Pressure | $P_b = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + K h^2}$ | Pa | Empirical model |
| 9 | Flapper-Nozzle Max Sensitivity Gap | $h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3K}}$ | m | |
| 10 | Flapper-Nozzle Static Gain | $G = \left\vert \dfrac{dP_b}{dh} \right\vert_{max}$ | Pa/m | Typically > 1000 |
| 11 | Spring Rate (Range Spring) | $k = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A_{eff}}{\Delta x}$ | N/m | For 3–15 psi range |
| 12 | Air Supply Pressure | $P_s = 20$ psig (140 kPa) | Pa | Industry standard |
| 13 | Signal Range (NA Standard) | 3–15 psig | psi | **Lower = 0%, Upper = 100%** |
| 14 | Signal Range (IEC Standard) | 20–100 kPa | kPa | Same as above |
| 15 | Live Zero | 3 psi or 20 kPa | Pa | **Failure detection feature** |
| 16 | Volume Amplifier Gain (Relay) | $G_V = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 10 \text{ to } 50$ | — | Flow (not pressure) gain |
| 17 | Power Gain (Flapper-Nozzle + Relay) | $G_P = G_{FN} \times G_V$ | — | Overall system gain |
| 18 | Time Constant (Pneumatic) | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | s | R = pneumatic resistance, C = volume capacitance |

> [!WARNING]
> **No Vertical Pipes in Tables:** All absolute values and "such that" notations use `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.
