# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Diaphragm Stem Position vs. Applied Pressure

**Problem Statement:** A diaphragm actuator has a diaphragm plate of diameter 150 mm, a range spring of stiffness 8 kN/m, and a pre-compression of 8 mm. The lower signal pressure is 20 kPa. Determine: (a) The signal pressure at which the stem begins to move, and (b) The signal pressure for a 15 mm stem travel.

### Given Data
- Diaphragm plate diameter, $D_p = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Spring stiffness, $k = 8 \text{ kN/m} = 8000 \text{ N/m}$
- Pre-compression, $x_0 = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$
- Minimum signal pressure, $P_{min} = 20 \text{ kPa} = 20{,}000 \text{ Pa}$

### Step 1: Calculate the Effective Area
Assuming the diaphragm behaves as a rigid plate (standard KTU assumption):

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Force Balance at Pre-Compression
At $P_{min}$, the pneumatic force just overcomes the pre-compression. Therefore:

$$P_{min} \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot x_0$$

**Verification:**

$$P_{min} \cdot A_{eff} = 20{,}000 \times 0.01767 = 353.4 \text{ N}$$

$$k \cdot x_0 = 8000 \times 0.008 = 64 \text{ N}$$

**Discrepancy observed!** This means at $P_{min} = 20$ kPa, the pneumatic force (353.4 N) already exceeds the spring pre-compression force (64 N). This implies the stem has already started moving well before the "minimum" signal. In practice, this is resolved by designing the spring rate and pre-compression so that at $P_{min}$, the stem is at the **start of its travel** (0% position). 

**Re-interpreting the problem:** The pre-compression is the spring force at the lower end of the stem's travel. Thus the force balance at the lower end is:

$$P_{min} \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot x_0$$

$$20{,}000 \times 0.01767 = 8000 \times x_0$$

$$353.4 = 8000 \times x_0$$

$$x_0 = 0.0442 \text{ m} = 44.2 \text{ mm}$$

So the **actual pre-compression corresponding to 20 kPa is 44.2 mm**, not 8 mm. The "8 mm" stated in the problem may be the additional compression from signal change, or the problem is using different conventions. Let me redo with consistent interpretation:

**Re-interpretation:** The spring pre-compression $x_0$ is the deflection of the spring at $P_{min} = 20$ kPa. The given 8 mm is the **additional travel from $P_{min}$ to $P_{max}$**. Therefore:

### Step 3: Calculate Full-Scale Spring Compression
At $P_{max}$, the spring compresses an additional 8 mm beyond pre-compression:

$$k \cdot (x_0 + 0.008) = P_{max} \cdot A_{eff}$$

At $P_{min}$:

$$k \cdot x_0 = P_{min} \cdot A_{eff}$$

Subtracting:

$$k \cdot 0.008 = (P_{max} - P_{min}) \cdot A_{eff}$$

$$8000 \times 0.008 = (P_{max} - 20{,}000) \times 0.01767$$

$$64 = (P_{max} - 20{,}000) \times 0.01767$$

$$P_{max} - 20{,}000 = \frac{64}{0.01767} = 3622 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{max} = 23{,}622 \text{ Pa} \approx 23.6 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 4: Answer to Question (a) — Pressure at Start of Stem Motion
The stem starts to move when the pneumatic force just exceeds the pre-compression. Using $P_{min} \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot x_0$:

$$x_0 = \frac{P_{min} \cdot A_{eff}}{k} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 0.01767}{8000} = 0.0442 \text{ m} = 44.2 \text{ mm}$$

The signal pressure at which the stem starts to move is $P_{min} = 20$ kPa (by design, this corresponds to 0% stem position).

### Step 5: Answer to Question (b) — Pressure for 15 mm Stem Travel
For an additional 15 mm travel beyond $P_{min}$:

$$x_0 + 0.015 = \frac{P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$$

$$P = \frac{k \cdot (x_0 + 0.015)}{A_{eff}}$$

$$P = \frac{8000 \times (0.0442 + 0.015)}{0.01767}$$

$$P = \frac{8000 \times 0.0592}{0.01767}$$

$$P = \frac{473.6}{0.01767} = 26{,}802 \text{ Pa} \approx 26.8 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 6: Final Answers
- **(a)** Stem begins to move at $P = 20$ kPa (lower range limit).
- **(b)** For 15 mm travel from the lower limit: $P \approx 26.8$ kPa.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Bellows Actuator Force Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A brass bellows has 5 active convolutions, an outer (crest) diameter of 30 mm, and an inner (root) diameter of 18 mm. The spring constant of the bellows is 150 N/m. If the supply pressure is 100 kPa, determine: (a) The effective area, (b) The thrust force produced, and (c) The equilibrium stroke.

### Step 1: Calculate Effective Area
$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{D_o^2 + D_i^2}{2} \right)$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{(0.030)^2 + (0.018)^2}{2} \right)$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{0.0009 + 0.000324}{2} \right)$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{0.001224}{2} \right)$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.000612$$

$$A_{eff} = 4.806 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \approx 480.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2: Calculate Thrust Force
At equilibrium (steady state, frictionless):

$$F_{thrust} = P \cdot A_{eff} - k \cdot x$$

At the moment of pressure application (before motion, $x = 0$):

$$F_{thrust,initial} = P \cdot A_{eff}$$

$$F_{thrust,initial} = 100{,}000 \times 4.806 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_{thrust,initial} = 48.06 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Equilibrium Stroke
At equilibrium, $F_{thrust} = 0$:

$$P \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot x_{eq}$$

$$x_{eq} = \frac{P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$$

$$x_{eq} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 4.806 \times 10^{-4}}{150}$$

$$x_{eq} = \frac{48.06}{150}$$

$$x_{eq} = 0.3204 \text{ m} = 320.4 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Final Answers
- **(a)** $A_{eff} = 4.806 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 480.6 \text{ mm}^2$
- **(b)** $F_{thrust,initial} = 48.06 \text{ N}$
- **(c)** $x_{eq} = 320.4 \text{ mm}$ (note: this exceeds practical bellows stroke, indicating design needs larger $k$)

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Flapper-Nozzle Sensitivity

**Problem Statement:** A flapper-nozzle has supply pressure $P_s = 140$ kPa and the constant $K = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^{-2}$. Determine: (a) The back-pressure when the gap is 0.5 mm, (b) The back-pressure when the gap is 0.2 mm, and (c) The gain in the range $h = 0.1$ to 0.3 mm.

### Step 1: Calculate Back-Pressure at h = 0.5 mm
$$P_b = \frac{P_s}{1 + K h^2}$$

$$P_b(0.5 \text{ mm}) = \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 1.2 \times 10^6 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 0.30}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1.30} = 107{,}692 \text{ Pa} \approx 107.7 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Back-Pressure at h = 0.2 mm
$$P_b(0.2 \text{ mm}) = \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 1.2 \times 10^6 \times (0.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 0.048}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1.048} = 133{,}588 \text{ Pa} \approx 133.6 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Gain in the Range h = 0.1 to 0.3 mm

**At h = 0.1 mm:**

$$P_b(0.1 \text{ mm}) = \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 1.2 \times 10^6 \times (0.1 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 0.012} = 138{,}338 \text{ Pa} \approx 138.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**At h = 0.3 mm:**

$$P_b(0.3 \text{ mm}) = \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 1.2 \times 10^6 \times (0.3 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$= \frac{140{,}000}{1 + 0.108} = 126{,}467 \text{ Pa} \approx 126.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**Average Gain in the Range:**

$$G = \left\vert \frac{\Delta P_b}{\Delta h} \right\vert = \frac{\vert 126.5 - 138.3 \vert \text{ kPa}}{\vert 0.3 - 0.1 \vert \text{ mm}}$$

$$G = \frac{11.8 \text{ kPa}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = \frac{11{,}800 \text{ Pa}}{2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}} = 5.9 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa/m}$$

### Step 4: Final Answers
- **(a)** $P_b(0.5 \text{ mm}) = 107.7 \text{ kPa}$
- **(b)** $P_b(0.2 \text{ mm}) = 133.6 \text{ kPa}$
- **(c)** Average gain $G = 5.9 \times 10^7$ Pa/m (extremely high — characteristic of the flapper-nozzle)

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bellows vs. Flapper-Nozzle vs. Diaphragm

| Parameter | Bellows Actuator | Flapper-Nozzle | Diaphragm Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Force/Displacement generator | Pneumatic amplifier | Control valve actuator |
| **Output Type** | Linear displacement (5–25 mm) | Pressure signal (0–140 kPa) | Linear stem motion (10–80 mm) |
| **Output Force Range** | Low (1–100 N) | N/A (low flow) | High (100–50,000 N) |
| **Pressure Range** | 0–200 kPa typical | Supply = 140 kPa | 20–100 kPa (3–15 psi) |
| **Linearity** | Very linear (metallic) | Highly nonlinear (linearized with feedback) | Linear (with proper design) |
| **Friction** | Near zero (no sliding seals) | None (flexure pivot) | Low (with rolling diaphragm) |
| **Sensitivity/Gain** | Moderate | **Very high (10⁵–10⁷ Pa/m)** | Moderate (force-based) |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | Negligible | Small (elastomer hysteresis) |
| **Response Speed** | Slow (high stiffness) | Fast (low volume) | Moderate |
| **Failure Mode** | Spring returns it | No moving mass to fail | **Spring fail-safe** (inherent) |
| **Industrial Application** | Precision instruments, transducers | First stage of pneumatic controllers | **Globe valves, butterfly valves, dampers** |
| **Construction Material** | Brass, SS, phosphor bronze | Stainless steel flapper/nozzle | Neoprene, Viton, EPDM diaphragm |
| **Temperature Limit** | High (metallic) | High | Limited by elastomer (typically -40 to +200 °C) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Medium | High (theory questions) | **Very High (numericals)** |

```mermaid
    style EXT fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600
    style RET fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style EQUIL fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"With a neat sketch, explain the working of a diaphragm actuator used in industrial control valves."**

**Model Answer:**

A diaphragm actuator consists of a flexible elastomeric diaphragm clamped between two housings. The lower housing receives the pneumatic control signal (3–15 psig), while the upper housing is open to atmosphere. A rigid diaphragm plate is bonded to the center of the diaphragm and is connected to the valve stem.

**Working Principle:**
1. Pneumatic signal pressure is admitted to the lower chamber of the actuator.
2. The pressure acts on the **effective diaphragm area**, producing an axial force $F = P \cdot A_{eff}$.
3. This force pushes the diaphragm plate and the valve stem downward against the opposing **range spring**.
4. The spring deflection is **linearly proportional to the signal pressure**.
5. On signal failure (loss of air), the spring forces the stem to a predetermined **fail-safe position** (either fully open or fully closed).

**Key features:** Simple construction, inherent fail-safe action, suitable for throttling control, widely used in process industries. The effective area is typically calculated as $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_p^2$ where $D_p$ is the diaphragm plate diameter.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Explain the working of a flapper-nozzle assembly as a pneumatic amplifier."**

**Model Answer:**

The flapper-nozzle is a high-gain pneumatic amplifier used as the first stage of pneumatic controllers. It consists of a **fixed nozzle** and a **movable flapper** (pivoted plate) facing the nozzle tip, separated by a small gap $h$ (typically 0.1–0.5 mm).

**Working Principle:**
1. Compressed air at constant supply pressure (typically 20 psig or 140 kPa) flows through a fixed restriction into the nozzle chamber.
2. Air escapes through the gap between flapper and nozzle.
3. The **back-pressure in the nozzle** ($P_b$) is a sensitive function of the gap $h$.
4. When the flapper moves **closer** to the nozzle (smaller $h$), the back-pressure **rises sharply** (the orifice flow is restricted).
5. When the flapper moves **away** (larger $h$), the back-pressure **falls** toward atmospheric.

**Mathematical Model:** $P_b = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + K h^2}$

**Key feature:** Static gain can exceed $10^5$ Pa/m, making the flapper-nozzle the most sensitive pneumatic transducer. However, its flow capacity is too low to drive a valve directly, so its output is fed into a **pneumatic relay** (volume booster) for the final output stage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **bellows actuator**. Derive the expression for thrust force and equilibrium stroke. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A diaphragm actuator has a diaphragm plate diameter of 200 mm. The range spring has a stiffness of 12 kN/m. The signal range is 3 to 15 psig. Calculate: (i) Effective area, (ii) Spring pre-compression at 3 psig, (iii) Spring compression at 15 psig, and (iv) Full stroke of the actuator. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Construction:** A bellows actuator consists of a metallic bellows (brass, phosphor bronze, or stainless steel) made of nested convolutions, enclosed in a housing. The closed end of the bellows is connected to the output stem; the open end is sealed to the housing. A range spring provides the opposing force.

**Working:**
1. Regulated air pressure is admitted to the interior of the bellows.
2. Pressure acts on the **effective area** $A_{eff}$ producing thrust $F = P \cdot A_{eff}$.
3. The bellows elongates axially against the spring.
4. The stem (connected to the bellows top) moves to position the control valve.

**Derivation of Thrust Force:**
For an ideal frictionless bellows in equilibrium:

$$\sum F = 0 \implies P \cdot A_{eff} - F_{spring} = 0$$

$$F_{thrust} = P \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot x_{eq}$$

where $k$ is the spring stiffness and $x_{eq}$ is the equilibrium stroke.

**Derivation of Equilibrium Stroke:**

$$x_{eq} = \frac{P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$$

The effective area of the bellows is empirically given by:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{D_o^2 + D_i^2}{2}\right)$$

where $D_o$ and $D_i$ are the outer (crest) and inner (root) diameters of the convolutions, respectively.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $D_p = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $k = 12 \text{ kN/m} = 12{,}000 \text{ N/m}$
- $P_{min} = 3 \text{ psig} = 3 \times 6894.76 \text{ Pa} = 20{,}684 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{max} = 15 \text{ psig} = 15 \times 6894.76 \text{ Pa} = 103{,}421 \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Effective Area:**

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Spring Pre-compression at 3 psig:**
At $P_{min}$, the pneumatic force balances the spring pre-compression:

$$P_{min} \cdot A_{eff} = k \cdot x_0$$

$$x_0 = \frac{20{,}684 \times 0.03142}{12{,}000} = \frac{649.9}{12{,}000} = 0.0542 \text{ m} = 54.2 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Spring Compression at 15 psig:**

$$x_{max} = \frac{P_{max} \cdot A_{eff}}{k} = \frac{103{,}421 \times 0.03142}{12{,}000} = \frac{3249.5}{12{,}000} = 0.2708 \text{ m} = 270.8 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Full Stroke of the Actuator:**

$$\Delta x = x_{max} - x_0 = 270.8 - 54.2 = 216.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Note:** This is unrealistically large for a practical actuator. In real designs, the spring is pre-compressed well beyond the 3 psig balance point to limit stroke to 10–50 mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **flapper-nozzle assembly** with a suitable diagram. Discuss why it must be followed by a pneumatic relay for practical applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A flapper-nozzle system has a supply pressure of 140 kPa. The flapper-nozzle characteristic is given by $P_b = \dfrac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \cdot h^2}$ kPa, where $h$ is in meters. Calculate: (i) The back-pressure when the gap is 0.4 mm, (ii) The back-pressure when the gap is 0.15 mm, and (iii) The average static gain in the range $h = 0.1$ mm to $h = 0.3$ mm. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Construction:** The flapper-nozzle assembly consists of:
- A **nozzle** (small fixed orifice, 0.5–1.0 mm diameter)
- A **flapper** (a flat metal plate, ~6 mm wide) mounted on a flexure pivot
- A **fixed restriction** $R$ (a needle valve) in the supply line
- A **supply air** connection at 20 psig (140 kPa)

The flapper is positioned very close (~0.1–0.2 mm) to the nozzle tip. The output is the **back-pressure** $P_b$ in the nozzle chamber.

**Working:**
1. Air flows from supply → fixed restriction → nozzle chamber → escape through flapper-nozzle gap.
2. The back-pressure is a sensitive inverse function of the gap $h$.
3. Small movements of the flapper (input) cause large changes in $P_b$ (output).
4. The relationship is highly nonlinear: $P_b = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + K h^2}$.
5. Maximum sensitivity occurs at $h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3K}}$.

**Why Pneumatic Relay is Needed:**
- The flapper-nozzle, despite its high pressure gain, has **extremely low flow capacity** because the nozzle orifice is tiny.
- It cannot supply the volume of air needed to **charge the volume of a long pipeline** or to **move a large diaphragm actuator** quickly.
- A **pneumatic relay** (also called a volume booster or pneumatic power amplifier) takes the low-flow, high-pressure output of the flapper-nozzle and reproduces it with much higher flow capacity.
- Typical volume gain: $G_V = 10$ to $50$.
- The relay provides **unity pressure gain** (output pressure = input pressure) but **high flow gain** (output flow ≫ input flow).
- Together, the flapper-nozzle and relay form a **two-stage pneumatic amplifier** with high pressure sensitivity AND high flow capacity.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $P_s = 140$ kPa
- $K = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^{-2}$

**(i) Back-pressure at h = 0.4 mm = 0.0004 m:**

$$P_b = \frac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \times (0.0004)^2}$$

$$= \frac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \frac{140}{1 + 0.24}$$

$$= \frac{140}{1.24} = 112.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Back-pressure at h = 0.15 mm = 0.00015 m:**

$$P_b = \frac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \times (0.00015)^2}$$

$$= \frac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$= \frac{140}{1 + 0.03375}$$

$$= \frac{140}{1.03375} = 135.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Average Static Gain from h = 0.1 mm to h = 0.3 mm:**

**At h = 0.1 mm = 0.0001 m:**

$$P_b = \frac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \times (0.0001)^2} = \frac{140}{1 + 0.015} = \frac{140}{1.015} = 137.93 \text{ kPa}$$

**At h = 0.3 mm = 0.0003 m:**

$$P_b = \frac{140}{1 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \times (0.0003)^2} = \frac{140}{1 + 0.135} = \frac{140}{1.135} = 123.35 \text{ kPa}$$

**Average Gain:**

$$G = \left\vert \frac{\Delta P_b}{\Delta h} \right\vert = \frac{\vert 123.35 - 137.93 \vert \text{ kPa}}{\vert 0.3 - 0.1 \vert \text{ mm}}$$

$$G = \frac{14.58 \text{ kPa}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = \frac{14{,}580 \text{ Pa}}{2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}} = 7.29 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa/m}$$

This extremely high gain ($> 10^7$ Pa/m) is characteristic of the flapper-nozzle assembly and is why it is used as the front-end of every high-precision pneumatic controller.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Always convert psig to Pa ($1 \text{ psi} = 6894.76 \text{ Pa}$). Marks are heavily docked for mixing units (e.g., using $A_{eff}$ in mm² with pressure in Pa).
> 2. **Confusing Geometric vs. Effective Area:** Students often use $\pi D^2/4$ with the diaphragm outer diameter. The correct "effective" area uses the **diaphragm plate diameter** in the rigid-plate model, or the empirical formula $\frac{\pi}{12}(D_o^2 + D_o D_i + D_i^2)$ when both diameters are given.
> 3. **Forgetting the Live Zero:** When asked about "signal range", students sometimes write 0–15 psig. The correct answer is **3–15 psig** (with 3 psi = 0% and 15 psi = 100%).
> 4. **Spring Direction:** In a direct-acting diaphragm actuator, the spring pushes the stem UP and the air pushes the stem DOWN. Confusing this leads to wrong fail-safe direction answers.
> 5. **Flapper-Nozzle Nonlinearity:** Do not linearize $P_b = P_s / (1 + K h^2)$ as $P_b = P_s (1 - K h^2)$ — this approximation is only valid for $K h^2 \ll 1$.
> 6. **Pneumatic Relay vs. Flapper-Nozzle:** The flapper-nozzle has high **pressure** gain; the relay has high **flow (volume)** gain. Confusing these costs 2–3 marks in descriptive questions.
> 7. **Missing Negative Feedback Loop:** The pneumatic relay uses a feedback bellows to maintain the relationship $P_{out} = P_{back-pressure}$. Skipping this in the diagram loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Pneumatic Actuators**

### **A. Bellows Actuator**
- ☑ Made of corrugated thin-walled metal (brass, SS, phosphor bronze).
- ☑ Effective area: $A_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{D_o^2 + D_i^2}{2} \right)$ — between root and crest area.
- ☑ Thrust force: $F = P \cdot A_{eff} - kx$.
- ☑ Equilibrium stroke: $x_{eq} = \dfrac{P \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$.
- ☑ Used in: precision instruments, low-force applications, transducer elements.
- ☑ Advantage: frictionless, no sliding seals, high linearity.
- ☑ Limitation: low force output, small stroke (5–25 mm).

### **B. Flapper-Nozzle Assembly**
- ☑ A **pneumatic amplifier** — the heart of pneumatic controllers.
- ☑ Components: fixed restriction, nozzle (fixed), flapper (movable).
- ☑ Supply pressure: 20 psig (140 kPa).
- ☑ Back-pressure model: $P_b = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + K h^2}$.
- ☑ Maximum sensitivity gap: $h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3K}}$.
- ☑ Static gain can exceed $10^5$–$10^7$ Pa/m.
- ☑ Always followed by a **pneumatic relay (volume booster)** for practical use.
- ☑ Output of flapper-nozzle feeds into a **feedback bellows** for negative feedback.

### **C. Diaphragm Actuator (Most Important for KTU)**
- ☑ Construction: flexible elastomeric or fabric-reinforced diaphragm, diaphragm plate, range spring, two-piece housing.
- ☑ Force equation: $F = P \cdot A_{eff}$.
- ☑ Stem position equation: $x = \dfrac{A_{eff}}{k} \cdot P - x_0$.
- ☑ Effective area (rigid model): $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_p^2$.
- ☑ Effective area (empirical): $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{12} (D_o^2 + D_o D_i + D_i^2)$.
- ☑ Standard signal ranges: **3–15 psig** (NA) or **20–100 kPa** (IEC).
- ☑ Live zero: 3 psi / 20 kPa (failure detection).
- ☑ Spring pre-compression: $x_0 = \dfrac{P_{min} \cdot A_{eff}}{k}$.
- ☑ Spring rate: $k = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A_{eff}}{\Delta x_{full}}$.
- ☑ Two operating modes: **direct-acting** (air pushes stem down) and **reverse-acting** (air pulls stem up).
- ☑ Inherent **fail-safe** via spring (no air = spring returns stem to safe position).
- ☑ Used in: globe valves, butterfly valves, dampers — 70%+ of process control valves.

### **D. Key KTU Numerical Patterns**
- ☑ Find $A_{eff}$ from given $D_p$ (or $D_o, D_i$).
- ☑ Find $k$ from given stroke and pressure range.
- ☑ Find $x$ for a given $P$, or find $P$ for a given $x$.
- ☑ Find force, stroke, and verify fail-safe direction.
- ☑ For flapper-nozzle: find $P_b$ at given $h$, or compute gain over a range.

### **E. Industrial Significance**
- ☑ Pneumatic actuators dominate in **hazardous areas** (refineries, chemical plants) where electrical sparking is a fire/explosion risk.
- ☑ Diaphragm actuators are inherently **fail-safe** — a critical safety feature.
- ☑ Pneumatic signals (3–15 psi) are **immune to electromagnetic interference** (EMI/RFI).
- ☑ Modern "smart" valves combine pneumatic actuators with **digital valve controllers (DVCs)** for HART/Fieldbus communication.

### **F. Common KTU Acronyms**
- ☑ **DVC:** Digital Valve Controller
- ☑ **HART:** Highway Addressable Remote Transducer
- ☑ **IEC:** International Electrotechnical Commission
- ☑ **ISA:** International Society of Automation
- ☑ **PV:** Process Variable
- ☑ **SP:** Setpoint
- ☑ **MV:** Manipulated Variable
- ☑ **CV:** Control Valve

### **G. Last-Minute KTU Exam Tips**
- ☑ Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** — it earns 2–3 easy marks.
- ☑ Show **unit conversions** explicitly in numerical problems.
- ☑ Mention **"fail-safe action"** in any diaphragm actuator answer.
- ☑ For flapper-nozzle questions, **always mention the need for a relay**.
- ☑ Use the **effective area formula** appropriate to the problem (rigid vs. empirical).
- ☑ End with a **"real-world application"** sentence to demonstrate industrial awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Actuators - Solenoids, Stepper Motors, DC Motors, DC Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT523 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric Actuators - Solenoids, Stepper Motors, DC Motors, DC Servo Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electric Actuators — The Broader Picture

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** An *electric actuator* is a powered mechanism that converts electrical energy (voltage and current) into a controlled mechanical output — typically linear motion (solenoids) or rotary motion (motors) — to perform a physical task such as opening a valve, positioning a robotic arm, or driving a printer head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU focuses on this:** Every modern mechatronic system — from ABS braking in cars to CNC machines, automatic doors, and prosthetic limbs — uses an electric actuator as the final "muscle" that does useful work after the sensor and controller have made a decision.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Think of an electric actuator as the *electric muscle* of a machine. Your brain (controller) sends a nerve signal (electrical pulse) → your bicep (actuator) contracts → your hand moves. Replace the muscle with a solenoid or motor, and the nerve signal with a DC voltage, and you have an electric actuator. The *command* is electrical, the *output* is mechanical.

**Three key performance metrics KTU examiners love to test:**
- **Force / Torque Output** (Newtons / N·m)
- **Stroke / Angular Displacement** (mm or degrees)
- **Speed of Response** (seconds to full stroke / RPM)

### 1.2 Sub-Topic 1 — Solenoids (Linear Electric Actuator)

**Definition:** A solenoid is an electromechanical device consisting of a helical coil of insulated wire (the *solenoid coil*) wound around a movable ferromagnetic *plunger* (or *armature*). When current flows, a magnetic flux is established that pulls the plunger into the coil, producing a linear mechanical force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solenoid = "Push/Pull Magnet on Command."** Energise the coil → strong magnetic field → armature snaps inward. De-energise → a return spring pushes the armature back to its rest position.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine sucking a metal straw into a hollow tube by blowing air. In a solenoid, the magnetic field generated by the energized coil plays the role of the suction — pulling the iron core into the coil's hollow center.

**Constructional Components:**
- **Coil (Stator):** Many turns of enamelled copper wire; rated by resistance (Ω) and maximum continuous current (A).
- **Plunger / Armature (Rotor):** Soft iron or low-carbon steel — chosen for *high magnetic permeability* (μᵣ ≈ 5000–10000) and *low retentivity* (so it demagnetises cleanly).
- **Yoke / Frame:** Completes the magnetic circuit, concentrates flux, and provides a return path.
- **Return Spring:** Restores the plunger when the coil is de-energised.
- **Bobbin:** Insulating spool that holds the coil windings.
- **Lead Wires / Terminals:** Electrical input connections.

**KTU-Standard Operation Principle:** When DC voltage V is applied across the coil of resistance R, current I = V/R flows and produces a magnetomotive force (MMF) = **N·I** (ampere-turns). The resulting flux through the plunger tries to minimise the *reluctance* (magnetic resistance) of the magnetic path — it does this by pulling the plunger deeper into the coil where the air gap is shorter. This produces a **pull-in force F**.

**Key Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants):**
- **Pull Force (F):** 0.1 N to 500 N (depending on size).
- **Stroke Length (g):** 1 mm to 25 mm typical.
- **Operating Voltage:** 5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 110 V, 220 V DC (or AC for AC solenoids).
- **Duty Cycle:** 25%, 50%, 100% (continuous rating).

### 1.3 Sub-Topic 2 — Stepper Motors (Incremental Rotary Actuator)

**Definition:** A stepper motor is a *brushless, synchronous electric motor* that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete angular steps. Each input pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment called the **step angle (α)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper = "Digital Motor."** You send N pulses → rotor rotates by exactly N × α. No feedback sensor needed in *open-loop* position control. This is why steppers dominate printers, plotters, and 3D printers.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a compass needle that locks onto the nearest magnetic pole, then the magnetic field is rotated, and the needle *snaps* to the next pole, then the next, then the next. A stepper motor does exactly this — but with electromagnet "teeth" rotating the field electronically, and a permanent-magnet or toothed-iron rotor chasing the field one step at a time.

**Three Major Constructions (KTU-Favourite Question):**

| Type | Rotor | Torque | Cost | KTU Use Case |
|------|-------|--------|------|--------------|
| **PM (Permanent Magnet)** | Permanent magnet (radial magnetised) | Low–Medium | Low | Toys, small printers |
| **VR (Variable Reluctance)** | Toothed soft iron (no magnet) | Low | Lowest | High-speed, low-torque |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | PM + toothed iron stack | High | Highest | CNC, robotics, 3D printers |

**Key Engineering Metrics:**
- **Step Angle (α):** 1.8° (most common, 200 steps/rev), 0.9°, 7.5°.
- **Holding Torque (Tₕ):** 0.05 N·m to 50 N·m.
- **Operating Voltage:** 5 V, 12 V, 24 V DC.

### 1.4 Sub-Topic 3 — DC Motors (Continuous Rotary Actuator)

**Definition:** A DC (Direct Current) motor is an electromechanical energy converter that transforms DC electrical input into continuous rotary mechanical output, based on the interaction between a current-carrying armature conductor and a stationary magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **DC Motor = "Workhorse Actuator."** Not precise like a stepper, but cheap, simple, and powerful. Used in fans, pumps, toys, conveyor belts, and as the *base motor* inside DC servo systems.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a wire loop sitting between two magnets. Send current through the wire → a force (Fleming's Left-Hand Rule) pushes one side up and the other side down → the loop rotates. Keep swapping the current direction at just the right moment (via the **commutator**) and the loop spins continuously.

**KTU-Mandated Four-Way Classification:**
1. **Separately Excited DC Motor** — Field winding powered by an independent supply.
2. **Self-Excited DC Motor** — Field winding powered by the *armature supply itself*, further divided into:
   - (a) **Shunt** (field in parallel with armature).
   - (b) **Series** (field in series with armature).
   - (c) **Compound** (both shunt and series field windings).

### 1.5 Sub-Topic 4 — DC Servo Motors (Closed-Loop Rotary Actuator)

**Definition:** A DC servo motor is a specially designed, low-inertia DC motor (usually separately excited or permanent-magnet type) intended for use in a *closed-loop position/speed control system* where the output is continuously compared with the commanded reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Servo Motor = "DC Motor + Position Feedback."** A DC motor alone doesn't know where it is. Add an encoder/potentiometer, close the loop, and *now* it is a servo — capable of precise angular positioning, speed regulation, and disturbance rejection.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine driving a car with your eyes closed (open-loop DC motor — you steer, but cannot correct). Now open your eyes and watch the lane (closed-loop servo — the encoder "sees" the position and the controller corrects any deviation). Same steering wheel, dramatically better performance.

**Distinctive Constructional Features (Why servo motors differ from ordinary DC motors):**
- **Low Rotor Inertia:** Armature diameter reduced, length increased → *armature length-to-diameter ratio > 1* (vs. < 1 in normal DC motors).
- **Higher Starting Torque** (to accelerate the load quickly).
- **Wider Linear Torque-Speed Range** (no magnetic saturation up to rated load).
- **No Saturation in Field Poles** (operates in the linear region of the B-H curve).
- **Commutator designed for rapid switching** (used in start-stop-reverse cycles).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall:** *Solenoid → linear*; *Stepper → digital rotary*; *DC motor → continuous rotary*; *DC servo → closed-loop precision rotary*. Memorise this 4-way contrast — it shows up in every short-answer set.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Solenoid — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Coil Energisation:** A DC voltage V is applied across the coil terminals. The coil's DC resistance R determines the steady-state current:
$$I = \frac{V}{R}$$

**Step 2 — MMF Generation:** Each turn of the coil contributes to the total magnetomotive force. For N turns carrying current I:
$$\text{MMF} = N \cdot I \quad [\text{ampere-turns, AT}]$$

**Step 3 — Flux Production:** The MMF drives magnetic flux Φ through the magnetic circuit (plunger, yoke, air gap). By Hopkinson's Law (magnetic Ohm's law):
$$\Phi = \frac{\text{MMF}}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}}$$
where **ℛ** is the total reluctance of the magnetic circuit. The air gap dominates ℛ because air has μᵣ ≈ 1.

**Step 4 — Force on Plunger:** The flux crossing the air gap pulls the plunger in. The fundamental force equation for an electromagnetic actuator (derived from energy balance) is:
$$F = \frac{dW}{dg} = \frac{\Phi^2}{2\mu_0 A} = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

where:
- **F** = pull force in Newtons
- **Φ** = air-gap flux in Webers
- **B** = flux density in Tesla
- **A** = cross-sectional area of the air gap in m²
- **g** = air-gap length in metres
- **μ₀** = permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**

**Step 5 — Practical Force Equation (KTU-Favourite Form):** Substituting Φ = (N·I)/ℛ and ℛ = g/(μ₀A) for an air-gap-dominated magnetic circuit:
$$F = \frac{(N I)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Force is *inversely proportional to the SQUARE of the air gap* (g²). This means the force explodes to infinity as the gap closes — which is why solenoids are *inherently unstable* at the end of stroke unless cushioned.

**Step 6 — Return Stroke:** When the coil is de-energised, flux collapses, magnetic force vanishes, and a mechanical **return spring** pushes the plunger back to its rest position. The spring force is chosen to slightly *exceed* the residual friction but *be less than* the magnetic pull-in force.

### 2.2 Stepper Motor — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Pulse Command:** The driver circuit receives a digital pulse train (one pulse = one step command).

**Step 2 — Sequential Phase Excitation:** A ring counter or microcontroller activates the stator phase windings in a *fixed sequence* (e.g., A → B → A̅ → B̅ → A …).

**Step 3 — Rotor Alignment:** The rotor (PM rotor in PM type, toothed iron in VR/Hybrid) experiences a torque proportional to the *sine of the angle error* between rotor and stator teeth:
$$T = T_h \cdot \sin(\delta)$$
where **δ** is the *load angle* (angular misalignment) and **Tₕ** is the *holding torque*.

**Step 4 — Step Increment:** When the next phase is energised, the stator field vector jumps by the *step angle α*, the rotor follows, and the cycle repeats.

**Step 5 — Step Angle Geometry:** For a motor with **N_s** stator teeth (or pole pairs) and **N_r** rotor teeth:
$$\alpha = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} \quad \text{(Hybrid type)}$$

For a simple 2-phase PM stepper with p pole pairs:
$$\alpha = \frac{180°}{p}$$

**Microstepping (Advanced Concept KTU asks in 14-markers):**
By driving the phase currents with *sinusoidal* profiles instead of pure on/off pulses, the field vector rotates smoothly. With *m* microsteps per full step, the effective step angle becomes:
$$\alpha_{eff} = \frac{\alpha}{m}$$

Common microstepping: 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/256. Higher microstepping → smoother motion, finer resolution, but lower torque.

**Synchronous (Pull-Out) Torque Equation:**
$$T = T_h \cdot \sin\left(\frac{N_r \cdot \theta}{2}\right)$$
where θ is the electrical angle of stator excitation.

### 2.3 DC Motor — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Armature Reaction:** When armature current **I_a** flows through conductors placed in a magnetic field of flux density **B**, each conductor experiences a force:
$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot L$$
where L is the active conductor length.

**Step 2 — Torque Generation:** The collective force on all Z armature conductors at radius r creates a torque:
$$T = K_a \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \quad [\text{N·m}]$$
where:
- **K_a** = armature construction constant
- **Φ** = flux per pole in Webers
- **I_a** = armature current in Amperes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mantra:** *Torque is proportional to flux × armature current.* To control torque, control either Φ (field control) or I_a (armature control).

**Step 3 — Back-EMF (The Self-Regulating Mechanism):** As the armature rotates, conductors *cut* the magnetic flux and *generate* a voltage opposing the applied voltage (Lenz's Law):
$$E_b = K_a \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega \quad [\text{Volts}]$$
where **ω** is the angular speed in rad/s.

**Step 4 — Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff on the Armature Loop):**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$
$$\Rightarrow V = K_a \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 5 — Speed Equation (Solve for ω):**
$$\omega = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_a \Phi}$$

**Step 6 — Power Conversion:** Input electrical power = V·I_a. Output mechanical power = T·ω.
$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = K_a \Phi I_a \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$$

**Torque-Speed Characteristic (Linear):**
$$\omega = \omega_0 - \frac{R_a}{(K_a \Phi)^2} T$$
where **ω₀ = V/(K_aΦ)** is the *no-load speed* (intercept on speed axis) and the slope gives the *regulation*.

### 2.4 DC Servo Motor — Additional Theory

A DC servo motor follows the *same fundamental equations* as a DC motor, but its **transfer function** is what KTU tests.

**Transfer Function Derivation (from voltage equation and torque equation):**

Armature loop: 
$$V_a(s) = R_a I_a(s) + L_a s I_a(s) + E_b(s)$$

Back-EMF: 
$$E_b(s) = K_b \omega(s) = K_b s \theta(s)$$

Torque: 
$$T(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$

Mechanical load: 
$$T(s) = (J s^2 + B s) \theta(s) + T_L(s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU servomotor problems, we often assume L_a is negligible (low-inductance armature), so the transfer function from armature voltage to angular position is:
$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a)(J s^2 + B s) + K_t K_b s}$$

> In some KTU problems, viscous friction B is also neglected, simplifying to:
$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s(R_a J s + K_t K_b)}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Solenoid MMF | $\mathcal{F} = N I$ | AT | Flux generation |
| 2 | Solenoid Force (gap form) | $F = \dfrac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2g^2}$ | N | Pull force calculation |
| 3 | Solenoid Force (field form) | $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | N | Alternative form |
| 4 | Hopkinson's Law | $\Phi = \dfrac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Wb | Magnetic circuit |
| 5 | Air-gap Reluctance | $\mathcal{R}_{gap} = \dfrac{g}{\mu_0 A}$ | AT/Wb | Dominant in solenoids |
| 6 | Step Angle (PM, 2-phase) | $\alpha = \dfrac{180°}{p}$ | degrees | PM stepper |
| 7 | Step Angle (Hybrid) | $\alpha = \dfrac{360°}{N_s N_r}$ | degrees | Hybrid stepper |
| 8 | Stepper Torque | $T = T_h \sin(\delta)$ | N·m | Static torque |
| 9 | Steps per Revolution | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{\alpha}$ | — | Resolution |
| 10 | DC Motor Torque | $T = K_a \Phi I_a$ | N·m | Output torque |
| 11 | DC Motor Back-EMF | $E_b = K_a \Phi \omega$ | V | Self-induced voltage |
| 12 | DC Motor Voltage Eqn. | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | Armature KVL |
| 13 | DC Motor Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_a \Phi}$ | rad/s | No-load/loaded speed |
| 14 | No-Load Speed | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_a \Phi}$ | rad/s | T = 0 intercept |
| 15 | Power Conversion | $P_{mech} = T \omega = E_b I_a$ | W | Power balance |
| 16 | Servo Transfer Function | $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s(R_a J s + K_t K_b)}$ | rad/V | Position control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on symbol $K$:** In SI units, $K_a$ (torque constant, N·m/A·Wb) and $K_b$ (back-EMF constant, V·s/rad) are dimensionally consistent and numerically equal when SI units are used. KTU often writes $K_a = K_b = K$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solenoid Pull Force from Energy Principle

**Starting Point:** Energy stored in the magnetic field of an air gap of volume $V_{gap} = A \cdot g$ with flux density $B$:
$$W_{mag} = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot V_{gap} = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A \cdot g$$

**Step 1 — Express B in terms of Φ:**
$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B^2 = \frac{\Phi^2}{A^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$W_{mag} = \frac{\Phi^2}{2 \mu_0 A^2} \cdot A \cdot g = \frac{\Phi^2 g}{2 \mu_0 A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Principle of Virtual Work (Force = negative gradient of energy w.r.t. position):**
$$F = -\frac{dW_{mag}}{dg} \bigg|_{\Phi = \text{const}}$$

The negative sign indicates the force acts to *reduce* the gap (i.e., pull the plunger in).

**Step 4 — Differentiate (Φ held constant for current-controlled source):**
$$F = -\frac{d}{dg}\left[\frac{\Phi^2 g}{2 \mu_0 A}\right] = -\frac{\Phi^2}{2 \mu_0 A}$$

**Step 5 — Take the magnitude and rewrite in terms of MMF:**
Since $\Phi = \frac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$ and $\mathcal{R} = \frac{g}{\mu_0 A}$ for an air-gap-dominated circuit:
$$\Phi = \frac{NI \mu_0 A}{g}$$

Substituting back:
$$\Phi^2 = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0^2 A^2}{g^2}$$

Therefore:
$$F = \frac{1}{2 \mu_0 A} \cdot \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0^2 A^2}{g^2} = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,F = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}\,} \quad \text{(Pull-in force)}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):** A solenoid has $N = 1000$ turns, current $I = 1$ A, air-gap area $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, air-gap length $g = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m. Compute pull force.

**Solution Steps:**

$$F = \frac{(1000 \times 1)^2 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (10^{-4})}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

**Step (a) — Numerator:**
$$(NI)^2 = 10^6$$
$$(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(10^{-4}) = 4\pi \times 10^{-11} \approx 1.2566 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$\text{Numerator} = 10^6 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-10} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step (b) — Denominator:**
$$2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step (c) — Divide:**
$$F = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 15.7 \text{ N}$$

> **Answer:** $F \approx 15.7$ N.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Motor Torque and Back-EMF Relation (from Lorentz Force)

**Starting Point:** Each armature conductor of active length L, carrying current $I_c$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, experiences a force:
$$f_c = B \cdot I_c \cdot L \quad [\text{Newtons}]$$

**Step 1 — Force on All Conductors:** The armature has $Z$ total conductors arranged at an average radius $r$ from the shaft. Each conductor contributes to torque:
$$dT = f_c \cdot r = B \cdot I_c \cdot L \cdot r$$

For the full armature with total armature current $I_a$ (sum of all branch currents):
$$T = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot B \cdot L \cdot r \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right)$$
where $A$ is the number of parallel paths.

Wait — let's derive it more carefully. The total armature current $I_a$ is shared between $A$ parallel paths, so current per conductor is $I_c = I_a / A$. The total number of conductors is $Z$, but in a wave-wound machine $A = 2$ and in a lap-wound machine $A = P$ (number of poles). The torque becomes:

$$T = \frac{Z}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \cdot P \cdot \frac{1}{A}$$

Defining the **armature constant** $K_a = \dfrac{ZP}{2\pi A}$:

$$T = K_a \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

$$\boxed{\,T = K_a \Phi I_a\,} \quad \text{(DC motor torque equation)}$$

**Step 2 — Derive Back-EMF:** As the armature rotates at $\omega$ rad/s, each conductor cuts the flux at a rate $d\Phi/dt = B \cdot L \cdot v = B \cdot L \cdot \omega r$. The EMF induced per conductor is $e_c = B L \omega r$. Summing over all $Z$ conductors and accounting for parallel paths:

$$E_b = \frac{Z P}{2\pi A} \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega = K_a \Phi \omega$$

$$\boxed{\,E_b = K_a \Phi \omega\,} \quad \text{(Back-EMF equation)}$$

**Step 3 — Key Ratio:**
$$\frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{T}{I_a} = K_a \Phi \quad \text{(in SI units: V·s/rad = N·m/A)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — DC Servo Motor Transfer Function (Position Output)

**Starting Assumptions:** Neglect armature inductance $L_a$ (low-inertia servo has small $L_a$). Neglect load torque $T_L$. Neglect viscous friction $B$ (or include if asked).

**Step 1 — Armature Voltage Equation (in s-domain):**
$$V_a(s) = R_a I_a(s) + E_b(s)$$

(Since $L_a \approx 0$, the $L_a s$ term drops.)

**Step 2 — Back-EMF as Function of Angular Velocity:**
$$E_b(s) = K_b \omega(s) = K_b s \theta(s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Voltage Equation:**
$$V_a(s) = R_a I_a(s) + K_b s \theta(s)$$
$$\Rightarrow I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Torque Equation:**
$$T(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Load Equation (no $T_L$, no $B$):**
$$T(s) = J s^2 \theta(s)$$
$$K_t I_a(s) = J s^2 \theta(s)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I_a(s)$:**
$$K_t \cdot \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \theta(s)}{R_a} = J s^2 \theta(s)$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by $R_a$:**
$$K_t V_a(s) - K_t K_b s \theta(s) = R_a J s^2 \theta(s)$$

**Step 8 — Collect $\theta(s)$ terms on the right:**
$$K_t V_a(s) = R_a J s^2 \theta(s) + K_t K_b s \theta(s) = s(R_a J s + K_t K_b) \theta(s)$$

**Step 9 — Final Transfer Function:**
$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s(R_a J s + K_t K_b)}$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s(R_a J s + K_t K_b)}\,} \quad \text{(DC servo position TF, no L_a, no B)}$$

**Step 10 — System Type:** This transfer function has *one pole at the origin* (the 's' in the denominator) → it is a **Type-1 system** → zero steady-state error for a step input in position.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stepper Motor Step Angle (Hybrid Type)

**Geometry:** Hybrid stepper has $N_s$ stator teeth and $N_r$ rotor teeth. The full-step rotor advance is dictated by the tooth-pitch difference.

**Step 1 — Tooth Pitch:** The angular pitch of a tooth on the stator (in electrical degrees) is:
$$\theta_{s,pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

**Step 2 — Rotor Tooth Pitch:**
$$\theta_{r,pitch} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 3 — Rotor Must Align One Tooth Forward When Phase is Switched:** The smallest common angular increment at which rotor and stator re-align is:
$$\alpha = \frac{360°}{\text{LCM}(N_s, N_r)}$$

In practice, for hybrid steppers, manufacturers design $N_s$ and $N_r$ to differ by exactly 2 (or another small integer), so:
$$\alpha = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r} \quad \text{(simplified form)}$$

**Example (KTU):** A hybrid stepper has $N_s = 50$ stator teeth and $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth. Compute step angle.
$$\alpha = \frac{360°}{50 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{2500} = 0.144°$$
$$N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{0.144°} = 2500 \text{ steps/rev}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Standard industrial hybrid steppers have $\alpha = 1.8°$ (200 steps/rev), which corresponds to $N_s N_r = 200$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Torque-Speed Equation of DC Motor

**Starting Point:**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a = K_a \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$
$$T = K_a \Phi I_a$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $I_a$ from the torque equation:**
$$I_a = \frac{T}{K_a \Phi}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the voltage equation:**
$$V = K_a \Phi \omega + \frac{T R_a}{K_a \Phi}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\omega$ (in terms of $T$):**
$$K_a \Phi \omega = V - \frac{T R_a}{K_a \Phi}$$
$$\omega = \frac{V}{K_a \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K_a \Phi)^2} T$$

$$\boxed{\,\omega = \omega_0 - m T\,}$$
where:
- $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_a \Phi}$ is the **no-load (ideal) speed**
- $m = \dfrac{R_a}{(K_a \Phi)^2}$ is the **slope** of the torque-speed line

**Step 4 — Stall Torque (T at $\omega = 0$):**
$$T_{stall} = \frac{V K_a \Phi}{R_a}$$

---

### 3.6 Numerical Example — DC Motor Performance

**Given:** Separately excited DC motor with $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ $\Omega$, $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $K_a = 100$, no-load speed $\omega_0 = ?$

**Step 1 — No-Load Speed:**
$$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_a \Phi} = \frac{220}{100 \times 0.05} = \frac{220}{5} = 44 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$N_0 = \frac{44 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 420 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Stall Torque:**
$$T_{stall} = \frac{V \cdot K_a \Phi}{R_a} = \frac{220 \times 5}{0.5} = 2200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Torque-Speed Line:**
$$\omega = 44 - \frac{0.5}{(5)^2} T = 44 - 0.02 T$$

**Step 4 — At half-load torque $T = 1100$ N·m:**
$$\omega = 44 - 0.02 \times 1100 = 44 - 22 = 22 \text{ rad/s} \approx 210 \text{ RPM}$$

> **Answer:** No-load speed 44 rad/s (420 RPM); stall torque 2200 N·m; half-load speed 22 rad/s.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

### 4.1 Solenoid — Block Diagram of Magnetic Circuit and Force Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCV[DC Voltage Source V] -->|Current I flows| COIL["Solenoid Coil N turns"]
    COIL -->|MMF NI| GAP["Air Gap g"]
    GAP -->|Flux Phi crosses| PLUNGER["Soft Iron Plunger Armature"]
    PLUNGER -->|Pull Force F applied| LOAD["Mechanical Load Valve Lever"]
    COIL --> YOKE["Magnetic Yoke Closes Circuit"]
    YOKE --> PLUNGER
    SPRING["Return Spring"] -->|Restoring force when de-energised| PLUNGER
    PLUNGER -->|Stroke x mm| OUT["Linear Mechanical Output"]
    DCV -.Induced EMF.-> COIL
```

### 4.2 Stepper Motor — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PULSE["Pulse Train from Controller"] --> DRIVER["Stepper Driver IC Power Amplifier"]
    DRIVER -->|Phase A current| PA["Phase A Winding Stator Pole 1"]
    DRIVER -->|Phase B current| PB["Phase B Winding Stator Pole 2"]
    DRIVER -->|Phase Abar current| PAB["Phase A Complement Stator Pole 3"]
    DRIVER -->|Phase Bbar current| PBB["Phase B Complement Stator Pole 4"]
    PA --> FIELD["Rotating Magnetic Field Vector"]
    PB --> FIELD
    PAB --> FIELD
    PBB --> FIELD
    FIELD -->|Pulls rotor by step angle alpha| ROTOR["Hybrid or PM Rotor"]
    ROTOR -->|Discrete angular steps| SHAFT["Output Shaft"]
    SHAFT -->|Mechanical work| LOAD["Mechanical Load"]
    ROTOR -.Holding torque Th.-> LOAD
```

### 4.3 DC Motor — Block Diagram with Electrical and Mechanical Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VSUP["DC Supply V"] --> RA["Armature Resistance Ra"]
    RA --> LA["Armature Inductance La"]
    LA --> NODE["Summing Node"]
    NODE --> BACK["Back EMF Eb equals Ka Phi omega"]
    NODE --> IA["Armature Current Ia"]
    IA --> TORQUE["Torque Generator T equals Ka Phi Ia"]
    TORQUE --> MECH["Mechanical Domain J and B"]
    MECH -->|Angular speed omega| BACK
    MECH --> SHAFT["Output Shaft"]
    SHAFT --> LOAD["Mechanical Load"]
    FIELD["Field Winding Flux Phi"] -.-> TORQUE
    FIELD -.-> BACK
```

### 4.4 DC Servo Motor — Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference Input theta ref"] --> DIFF["Summing Junction e equals ref minus feedback"]
    DIFF --> AMP["Power Amplifier Gain Ka"]
    AMP -->|Voltage Va| SERVO["DC Servo Motor Plant Ka divided by s times RaJ s plus Kt Kb"]
    SERVO --> OUT["Output Shaft theta s"]
    OUT --> LOAD["Mechanical Load"]
    OUT --> FB["Feedback Sensor Encoder or Potentiometer Gain H"]
    FB --> DIFF
    DIFF -.Error signal.-> AMP
```

### 4.5 Comparison Matrix — All Four Actuators (KTU Quick Reference)

| Feature | Solenoid | Stepper Motor | DC Motor | DC Servo Motor |
|---------|----------|---------------|----------|----------------|
| **Output Type** | Linear | Discrete rotary | Continuous rotary | Precision rotary |
| **Position Feedback** | None (ON/OFF) | Open-loop (no feedback normally) | None (open-loop) | Mandatory (closed-loop) |
| **Control Signal** | DC voltage (binary) | Pulse train (digital) | DC voltage (analog) | DC voltage (analog) |
| **Key Equation** | $F = (NI)^2 \mu_0 A / 2g^2$ | $\alpha = 360°/N_s N_r$ | $T = K_a \Phi I_a$ | $\theta/V_a$ transfer function |
| **Typical Application** | Valve, lock, relay | Printer, CNC | Fan, pump | Robot joint, antenna positioner |
| **Cost** | Very low | Medium | Low | Medium-High |
| **Precision** | Low (ON/OFF) | High (open-loop) | Low (speed depends on load) | Very high (closed-loop) |
| **Hold at Rest?** | No (spring returns) | Yes (holding torque) | No (coasts to stop) | Yes (locks at commanded position) |
